title: Apache Airflow for Data Engineers: Mid-Level Track
description: Replace cron-based scheduling with data-aware Datasets, custom timetables, and event-driven triggers that react to data availability rather than clock time.

chapters.title: Data-Aware Scheduling
chapters.description: Replace cron-based scheduling with data-aware Datasets, custom timetables, and event-driven triggers that react to data availability rather than clock time.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's orchestration platform ran smoothly for five months after the junior-level deployment. Three core DAGs — Bronze ingestion, Silver transformation, and Gold aggregation — executed nightly on cron schedules staggered 90 minutes apart. Then the team added nine more DAGs: separate pipelines for solar generation telemetry, battery state-of-charge monitoring, weather forecast integration, regulatory tariff imports, and several downstream analytics workflows. Twelve DAGs, all scheduled by clock time, each assuming the previous DAG finished within a fixed window. On April 14th, a slow SFTP transfer delayed Bronze by 40 minutes. Silver started on schedule at 02:00, read yesterday's stale data, and produced a Silver dataset that looked clean but contained no readings from the current processing date. Gold aggregated the stale data. The billing team discovered the problem at 09:30 — seven hours after the cascade began.
The Timing Assumption Problem

In the junior-level track, you learned to schedule DAGs with cron expressions and preset strings like @daily. That approach works when the schedule has a comfortable buffer between dependent DAGs and the processing time is predictable. It breaks when either condition fails. At mid-level scale — 12 DAGs, 1.2 million meters, 10 regions, 3 consuming teams — processing time is no longer predictable. A Bronze DAG that normally takes 20 minutes can take 90 minutes when the SFTP server is under load, when a data provider sends a larger-than-usual file, or when the Celery workers are busy with a backlog from a previous failure. Every fixed time buffer is an assumption, and every assumption is a point of failure.

# SaulėGrid's original cron-based schedule for 12 DAGs
# Each DAG assumes the previous one finished within its time buffer

# Bronze layer — chain entry points
# dag_bronze_meters:       0 0 * * *   (00:00)
# dag_bronze_generation:   0 0 * * *   (00:00)
# dag_bronze_weather:      0 5 * * *   (05:00)

# Silver layer — 90-minute buffer after Bronze
# dag_silver_meters:       30 1 * * *  (01:30)
# dag_silver_generation:   30 1 * * *  (01:30)
# dag_silver_quality:      30 1 * * *  (01:30)

# Gold layer — 90-minute buffer after Silver
# dag_gold_billing:        0 3 * * *   (03:00)
# dag_gold_forecast:       0 3 * * *   (03:00)

# Problem: 12 DAGs × 90-minute buffers = 4.5 hours of assumed processing
# If ANY Bronze DAG runs 40 minutes late, the entire chain breaks

The staggered schedule looks orderly on paper. In practice, it encodes twelve assumptions about processing time — one per DAG — that are tested every night and fail silently when violated. The 90-minute buffer between Bronze and Silver was based on the maximum observed processing time during the first three months of production. By month six, data volume had grown 40%, the SFTP server had been migrated to a new host with different throughput characteristics, and the 90-minute buffer was no longer sufficient on high-volume days. No alert fired when the buffer was exceeded because cron does not know about buffers — it fires on the clock.

At junior-level scale with 3 DAGs, the time buffer approach was manageable — the buffer was large relative to the processing time, and failures were rare enough to handle manually. At 12 DAGs with cross-dependencies, the probability of at least one DAG exceeding its buffer on any given night is high. SaulėGrid tracked their scheduling incidents over a quarter and found that 23% of nights had at least one timing-related issue: a sensor timeout, a stale data read, or a Gold DAG processing incomplete data. The incident rate scaled with DAG count because each additional DAG added another timing assumption that could fail independently.
Why Cron Cannot Express Dependencies

The root cause is that cron expresses when to start, not when it is safe to start. A cron schedule of 0 2 * * * says 'start at 02:00' regardless of whether the upstream data has been produced, whether the Bronze DAG has completed, or whether the input files are valid. The junior-level workaround was the ExternalTaskSensor — a task that polls the metadata database waiting for an upstream DAG Run to reach the success state. Sensors work, but they introduce their own problems: they consume worker slots while waiting, they require precise schedule alignment between producer and consumer DAGs, and they fail silently when the upstream DAG's schedule changes. SaulėGrid used ExternalTaskSensors for the Bronze-to-Silver dependency but abandoned them after two incidents where a schedule change in Bronze caused the sensor to wait indefinitely.

blocks.title: Cron-based scheduling vs data-aware scheduling
blocks.content:
Cron-Based (time-driven):

00:00           02:00           03:30
  │               │               │
  ▼               ▼               ▼
[Bronze]        [Silver]        [Gold]
 dag_bronze      dag_silver      dag_gold
 @daily 00:00    @daily 02:00    @daily 03:30

 Problem: Silver starts at 02:00 even if Bronze is still running.

Data-Aware (event-driven):

00:00           Bronze done!     Silver done!
  │               │               │
  ▼               ▼               ▼
[Bronze]   ───▶ [Silver]   ───▶ [Gold]
 @daily 00:00    triggered by     triggered by
                 Dataset update   Dataset update

 Silver starts when Bronze's output is ready, not at a fixed time.
blocks.caption: Data-aware scheduling replaces clock-time assumptions with explicit data availability signals. The DAG chain runs as fast as the data allows, never too early.

visual-recommendation.description: Side-by-side comparison showing cron-based DAG chains with fixed time gaps versus Dataset-driven chains with event arrows, highlighting the timing fragility of the cron approach and the resilience of the data-aware approach.

blocks.content: The Scale Inflection Point

The scheduling problem at mid-level scale is not just more DAGs — it is the combinatorial explosion of dependencies between them. Three DAGs have 2 potential cross-dependencies. Twelve DAGs have up to 66. Each dependency managed by a time buffer is a latent failure. Each dependency managed by a sensor is a consumed worker slot. The inflection point comes when the number of scheduling dependencies exceeds what the team can monitor manually and what the executor can absorb in sensor overhead. For SaulėGrid, that inflection point was around 8 DAGs — the point where sensor slots began competing with processing task slots and timing failures became a weekly occurrence rather than a monthly surprise.
Why Sensors Are Not the Answer

ExternalTaskSensors were the junior-level tool for cross-DAG dependencies. They work by polling: the sensor checks every 60 seconds whether the upstream task instance has completed. This polling pattern has three costs. First, worker slot consumption: a sensor running in poke mode occupies a worker slot for the entire wait duration, which at SaulėGrid meant one of three Celery worker slots was consumed by a task doing nothing but sleeping and querying the database. Second, schedule coupling: the sensor must know the exact execution_date of the upstream DAG Run it is waiting for, which requires that both DAGs share the same schedule. When SaulėGrid changed Bronze from @daily to a custom timetable, every sensor pointing at Bronze broke because the execution dates no longer aligned. Third, timeout fragility: sensors have a timeout parameter, and when the upstream DAG is genuinely late, the sensor times out and the downstream DAG fails — even though the upstream data will arrive eventually.

left.label: ExternalTaskSensor (Junior Pattern)
points: Polling — sensor queries the metadata database every N seconds
Occupies a worker slot during the entire wait (poke mode) or periodically (reschedule mode)
Producer and consumer must share the same schedule for execution_date alignment
Sensor times out after a configured period; downstream DAG fails
Requires external_dag_id, external_task_id, execution_date_fn, and timeout

right.label: Dataset Scheduling (Mid-Level Pattern)
points: Event — scheduler detects Dataset update and triggers consuming DAG
Zero worker cost — the scheduler handles triggering internally
No schedule coupling — the consumer triggers when the Dataset is updated regardless of schedules
Consumer simply waits — no timeout, no failure from waiting
Requires only the Dataset URI in the schedule parameter

blocks.content: What This Module Covers

This module replaces cron-based scheduling with three modern alternatives. Datasets are the primary mechanism: a DAG declares which data it produces and which data it consumes, and the scheduler triggers consuming DAGs when all their input Datasets have been updated. Custom timetables replace cron for scheduling logic that cron cannot express — business-day schedules, holiday-aware schedules, and irregular calendar patterns. Event-driven triggers handle the remaining cases: API-triggered reprocessing, webhook-based notifications from external systems, and manual interventions. By the end of this module, SaulėGrid's scheduling architecture will be fully data-aware: no DAG depends on a clock-time assumption, and every cross-DAG dependency is explicit and observable.

blocks.title: The scheduling maturity shift
blocks.content: The shift from cron to Datasets is not a tool upgrade — it is a paradigm shift. Cron-based scheduling asks 'when should this run?' Dataset-based scheduling asks 'what data does this need, and is that data ready?' The first question produces fragile schedules based on timing assumptions. The second produces resilient schedules based on data availability. Every scheduling incident SaulėGrid experienced at junior-level scale was caused by answering the first question when they should have been answering the second.

A Dataset in Airflow is a logical identifier — a URI string — that represents a piece of data produced by one DAG and consumed by others. Datasets do not store data, do not know the format of the data, and do not verify that the data actually exists at the URI. They are labels that create a producer-consumer contract: a task declares that it updates a Dataset by listing it in its outlets parameter, and a DAG declares that it depends on a Dataset by including it in its schedule parameter. When all outlet-marked Datasets for a consuming DAG have been updated at least once, the scheduler creates a new DAG Run. This is the entire mechanism — no polling, no worker slots consumed while waiting, and no schedule alignment required between producer and consumer.
Creating a Dataset

A Dataset is created by instantiating the Dataset class with a URI string. The URI is a logical identifier, not a filesystem path or a database connection string. Airflow does not resolve the URI, does not check whether the location exists, and does not read from or write to it. The URI's sole purpose is to create a unique identifier that the scheduler can track. That said, using meaningful URIs that correspond to actual data locations is a strong convention because it makes the dependency graph self-documenting — when an engineer sees Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/meters/') in the code, the data's location and purpose are immediately clear without consulting any external documentation.

from airflow.datasets import Dataset

# Datasets are logical identifiers — URIs that Airflow tracks
# The URI should be meaningful but Airflow does not resolve it

bronze_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/meters/')
bronze_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/generation/')
bronze_weather = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/weather/')

silver_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/meters/')
silver_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/generation/')

gold_billing = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/gold/billing/')
gold_forecast = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/gold/forecast/')

# Bad practice — non-descriptive URIs that tell you nothing
# dataset_1 = Dataset('output_a')  # What is this? Where does it go?
Producing a Dataset

A task produces a Dataset by listing it in the outlets parameter. The outlets declaration is metadata — it tells the scheduler that when this task succeeds, the specified Dataset should be marked as updated. Outlets can be set on any operator: classic operators use the outlets keyword argument, and TaskFlow tasks use the outlets parameter in the @task decorator. When a task instance with outlets completes successfully, the scheduler records a Dataset event. This event is the trigger signal that consuming DAGs listen for.

Notice that only the ingest_meters task has the outlets parameter — not the log_completion task. The Dataset update event fires when ingest_meters succeeds, which is the moment the Bronze data is actually ready. The log_completion task is a downstream logging step that does not affect data availability. Placing outlets on the correct task matters: if outlets were on log_completion instead, and log_completion failed due to a logging error, the Dataset update would not fire even though the data was successfully written. Conversely, if outlets were on a task that runs before the data is fully written, consuming DAGs could be triggered prematurely.
Consuming a Dataset

A DAG consumes a Dataset by including it in the schedule parameter instead of a cron expression. When the schedule is a Dataset or a list of Datasets, the DAG has no time-based schedule — it runs only when the scheduler detects that all specified Datasets have been updated since the last DAG Run. This is the fundamental difference from cron scheduling: the consuming DAG does not run at a fixed time, it runs when the data is ready. The DAG has no cron expression, no time buffer, and no assumption about when upstream processing completes — the scheduler handles the coordination entirely.

The schedule=[bronze_meters] declaration replaces both the cron expression and the ExternalTaskSensor from the junior-level approach. There is no polling task consuming worker slots, no execution_date alignment to maintain, and no timeout to configure. When dag_bronze_meters finishes and the ingest_meters task updates the bronze_meters Dataset, the scheduler immediately creates a DAG Run for dag_silver_transform. The consuming DAG's start time depends entirely on when the data is ready, not on when the clock says to start. This is the scheduling paradigm shift that the entire module builds on.

blocks.title: Dataset URIs must match exactly
blocks.content: The producer and consumer must use the same URI string, character for character. Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/meters/') and Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/meters') (without the trailing slash) are different Datasets to the scheduler. SaulėGrid defines all Dataset URIs in a shared datasets.py module that both producer and consumer DAG files import, ensuring exact string match and providing a single inventory of all Datasets on the platform.
What Datasets Are Not

Datasets are not data validation tools — they do not check whether the data at the URI is correct, complete, or even present. A task with outlets can update a Dataset even if the actual data write failed silently. Datasets are not schema contracts — they carry no information about the structure or format of the data. Datasets are not access control mechanisms — any DAG can consume any Dataset without permission checks. Understanding these boundaries is important because it means data quality validation, schema enforcement, and access control must be handled by the pipeline logic itself, not delegated to the Dataset mechanism. Datasets solve the scheduling problem — when to trigger — and nothing more.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's convention: every task with outlets includes a data validation step before returning. If validation fails, the task raises an exception, the task instance fails, and the Dataset is not updated. This ensures that the Dataset update signal genuinely means 'the data is ready and valid,' not just 'the task ran.' The validation step is part of the task, not a separate downstream task, precisely because the outlet fires on the task's success.

The previous lesson introduced the Dataset abstraction with a single producer and a single consumer. Production platforms are more complex: a single producer may feed multiple consumers, a single consumer may depend on multiple producers, and the dependency graph can span many layers. SaulėGrid's 12-DAG platform has a dependency graph where Bronze DAGs produce Datasets consumed by Silver DAGs, Silver DAGs produce Datasets consumed by Gold DAGs, and Gold DAGs produce Datasets consumed by analytics and reporting workflows. This lesson covers the multi-Dataset patterns that govern real-world scheduling and introduces the Dataset tab in the Airflow UI that makes these relationships visible.
One Producer, Multiple Consumers

When a task updates a Dataset, every DAG that lists that Dataset in its schedule is evaluated for triggering. If the consuming DAG depends only on that single Dataset, it triggers immediately. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters produces the bronze_meters Dataset. Three downstream DAGs consume it: dag_silver_meters_transform, dag_silver_quality_report, and dag_analytics_raw_export. All three are triggered when Bronze meters completes — they do not compete for the trigger signal, and they can run in parallel if the executor has capacity. This fan-out pattern is natural for Dataset scheduling: the producer does not need to know how many consumers exist, and adding a new consumer requires no change to the producer.

The producer DAG (dag_bronze_meters from the previous lesson) has no knowledge of these three consumers. It declares outlets=[bronze_meters] on its ingestion task and nothing more. The scheduler handles the fan-out: when the Dataset event is recorded, it scans all DAGs whose schedule includes bronze_meters and creates DAG Runs for each one. This decoupling is a significant architectural advantage over ExternalTaskSensors, where adding a new consumer required modifying the consumer DAG to include the correct external_dag_id and execution_date_fn. With Datasets, the consumer simply declares its dependency and the scheduler does the rest.
Dataset Event Tracking

Every Dataset update creates an event record in the metadata database. The event includes the timestamp, the producing DAG ID, the producing task ID, and the DAG Run ID. This event history serves two purposes: the scheduler uses it to determine when to trigger consuming DAGs, and the operations team uses it to audit the data flow. If a question arises about when Gold billing last received fresh data, the Dataset event history provides a definitive answer: the silver_meters Dataset was last updated at 02:47 by dag_silver_meters_transform, task transform_meters, run manual__2026-03-24T00:00:00+00:00. The event is immutable — it records what actually happened, not what was scheduled to happen.
Multiple Producers, One Consumer

The more powerful pattern is multi-Dataset consumption: a DAG that waits for multiple upstream Datasets to be updated before triggering. When the schedule parameter contains a list of Datasets, the scheduler creates a DAG Run only when every Dataset in the list has been updated at least once since the last DAG Run. This is an AND condition — all Datasets must be updated, not just any one of them. SaulėGrid's Gold billing DAG requires both Silver meters and Silver generation data because the billing calculation combines consumption and generation readings for prosumer net metering.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.datasets import Dataset
from pendulum import datetime

silver_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/meters/')
silver_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/generation/')

# Gold billing requires BOTH Silver meters AND Silver generation
@dag(
    dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
    schedule=[silver_meters, silver_generation],  # AND logic — both must update
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['gold', 'billing', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_gold_billing():

    @task
    def compute_billing():
        """Compute net metering balance: consumption - generation.
        
        Requires both Silver meters (consumption) and Silver generation
        to be current before computing billing aggregates.
        """
        # ... billing logic combining both Silver datasets ...
        return {'prosumers_billed': 45_000, 'regions': 10}

    compute_billing()

dag_gold_billing()

If Silver meters updates at 02:15 and Silver generation updates at 02:40, the Gold billing DAG triggers at 02:40 — the moment the last required Dataset is updated. If Silver meters updates but Silver generation fails, Gold billing does not trigger at all. This AND behavior is exactly what SaulėGrid needs: billing calculations that use incomplete data produce incorrect net metering balances, which create regulatory compliance issues. The multi-Dataset schedule makes it structurally impossible for the billing DAG to run on partial data.
The Dataset Tab in the Airflow UI

Airflow provides a dedicated Datasets tab in the web UI that visualizes all Datasets, their producing tasks, and their consuming DAGs. The graph view shows Datasets as nodes with arrows from producing tasks and arrows to consuming DAGs, creating a complete dependency map of the platform's data flow. For SaulėGrid, this view shows the Bronze-Silver-Gold chain with every producer and consumer relationship visible in a single diagram. The UI also shows the event history for each Dataset: when it was last updated, which task updated it, and which DAG Runs were triggered by the update.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's Dataset dependency graph
blocks.content:
dag_bronze_meters ──▶ [bronze_meters] ──▶ dag_silver_meters_transform
                                       ──▶ dag_silver_quality_report
                                       ──▶ dag_analytics_raw_export

dag_bronze_generation ──▶ [bronze_generation] ──▶ dag_silver_generation_transform

dag_silver_meters_transform ──▶ [silver_meters] ──┐
                                                  ├──▶ dag_gold_billing
dag_silver_generation_transform ──▶ [silver_generation] ──┘
                                                  │
                                                  └──▶ dag_gold_forecast
blocks.caption: The Dataset dependency graph replaces the implicit timing assumptions with explicit data flow. Every arrow represents a declared producer-consumer relationship visible in the Airflow UI.

visual-recommendation.description: Annotated screenshot of the Airflow Datasets tab showing SaulėGrid's complete Bronze-Silver-Gold Dataset dependency graph with producing DAGs, Dataset nodes, and consuming DAGs in the graph view.

blocks.content: The dependency graph in the UI is interactive: clicking a Dataset node shows its event history, clicking a DAG node shows its DAG Runs triggered by that Dataset, and the edges show the update-to-trigger latency — the time between the Dataset event and the consumer DAG Run creation. SaulėGrid uses this latency metric as a platform health indicator: a consistent 2-second latency means the scheduler is processing Dataset events efficiently, while a growing latency trend suggests scheduler overload or metadata database performance degradation. The graph also makes orphaned Datasets visible — Datasets that are produced but have no consumers, or Datasets that are referenced in a schedule but have no producing task.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid reviews the Datasets graph weekly during the platform operations meeting. The graph reveals the platform's true dependency structure — which is sometimes different from the documented architecture when new DAGs are added without updating documentation. If a Dataset has no consumers, it may be unused and a candidate for removal. If a consumer depends on a Dataset that no task produces, the scheduling will never trigger — a silent misconfiguration that the graph makes immediately visible.

The previous two lessons covered Datasets as individual producer-consumer relationships. In production, these relationships form chains: Bronze produces data consumed by Silver, Silver produces data consumed by Gold, and Gold produces data consumed by analytics workflows. The chain's behavior — how triggers cascade, how failures propagate, and how the end-to-end latency is determined — depends on design decisions at each link. This lesson covers the architecture of Dataset-driven DAG chains, the migration path from sensor-based dependencies to Dataset-based dependencies, and the monitoring patterns that make chains observable.
Anatomy of a Chain

A Dataset-driven chain works like a relay: each DAG starts when its input Datasets are ready, does its work, updates its output Datasets, and the next DAG starts. The chain's entry point is still a cron-scheduled DAG — typically the Bronze ingestion that downloads data from external systems on a fixed schedule. From Bronze onward, every DAG in the chain is Dataset-scheduled. The end-to-end latency is the sum of individual DAG execution times, not a sum of fixed time buffers. If Bronze finishes in 15 minutes instead of the usual 30, the rest of the chain starts 15 minutes earlier. If Bronze takes 60 minutes, the chain waits. There is no wasted buffer time and no premature trigger.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.datasets import Dataset
from pendulum import datetime

# Shared Dataset definitions — imported from saulegrid_common.datasets
bronze_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/meters/')
silver_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/meters/')
gold_billing = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/gold/billing/')

# Link 1: Bronze (cron-scheduled entry point)
@dag(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    schedule='0 0 * * *',  # Only the chain entry uses cron
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'meters'],
)
def bronze():
    @task(outlets=[bronze_meters])
    def ingest():
        """Download and validate meter readings."""
        pass
    ingest()

# Link 2: Silver (Dataset-scheduled)
@dag(
    dag_id='dag_silver_meters',
    schedule=[bronze_meters],  # Triggered by Bronze completion
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['silver', 'meters'],
)
def silver():
    @task(outlets=[silver_meters])  # Produces Silver Dataset for Gold
    def transform():
        """Cleanse, validate, write Silver."""
        pass
    transform()

# Link 3: Gold (Dataset-scheduled, waits for Silver)
@dag(
    dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
    schedule=[silver_meters],  # Triggered by Silver completion
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['gold', 'billing'],
)
def gold():
    @task(outlets=[gold_billing])
    def aggregate():
        """Compute billing aggregates from Silver."""
        pass
    aggregate()

bronze()
silver()
gold()

The chain has exactly one cron trigger — the Bronze DAG at midnight. Every subsequent DAG runs in response to a Dataset update. This means the chain's schedule is defined by a single point of control (the Bronze cron) and the data flow determines the rest. If SaulėGrid needs to change the daily processing window, they change one cron expression. If they need to add a new Silver-to-Gold dependency, they add the Dataset to Gold's schedule list. Neither change requires modifying intermediate DAGs.
Chain Latency Characteristics

The end-to-end latency of a Dataset chain is the sum of individual DAG execution times plus the scheduler's Dataset event processing time (typically 2-5 seconds per link). Unlike cron-based chains where latency is fixed at the sum of time buffers regardless of actual processing time, Dataset chains compress latency on fast nights and extend it on slow nights. SaulėGrid's chain typically completes in 65 minutes (20 for Bronze, 25 for Silver, 20 for Gold), compared to the old cron chain's fixed 4-hour window. On slow nights, the chain extends to 100-110 minutes — still faster than the cron chain because there are no wasted buffer periods between DAGs.
Migrating from Sensors to Datasets

SaulėGrid's migration from ExternalTaskSensors to Datasets followed a three-phase approach. First, define Datasets for every inter-DAG data dependency and add outlets to producing tasks without changing any consuming DAGs — this is a non-breaking change because outlets are metadata that the scheduler records but that do not affect DAG execution. Second, convert consuming DAGs from sensor-based schedules to Dataset-based schedules one at a time, starting with the lowest-risk pipeline and verifying that triggering behavior matches the sensor-based behavior. Third, remove the now-unused sensor tasks and their associated configuration. The phased approach avoids a big-bang migration and provides a rollback path at each step.

rows: Change schedule from cron + sensor to [dataset]
Revert schedule to cron and restore sensor task
Remove sensor tasks and related configuration
Low — sensors are no longer active
Re-add sensor tasks from version control

blocks.content: The Shared Datasets Module

As the number of Datasets grows, maintaining URI consistency across producer and consumer DAG files becomes a maintenance burden. SaulėGrid centralizes all Dataset definitions in a shared Python module — saulegrid_common.datasets — that every DAG file imports. This guarantees exact URI match (because both sides reference the same Python object), provides a single inventory of all Datasets on the platform, and makes it easy to audit which Datasets exist without scanning every DAG file. The module also serves as documentation: each Dataset has a comment explaining what data it represents, which DAG produces it, and which DAGs consume it.

# File: saulegrid_common/datasets.py
# Central registry of all SaulėGrid Datasets
# Both producer and consumer DAGs import from this module

from airflow.datasets import Dataset

# Bronze layer (produced by dag_bronze_* DAGs)
bronze_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/meters/')
bronze_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/generation/')
bronze_weather = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/weather/')
bronze_battery = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/battery/')

# Silver layer (produced by dag_silver_* DAGs)
silver_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/meters/')
silver_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/generation/')
silver_quality_report = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/quality/')

# Gold layer (produced by dag_gold_* DAGs)
gold_billing = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/gold/billing/')
gold_forecast = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/gold/forecast/')
Chain Failure Propagation

When a DAG in a Dataset chain fails, the failure propagates naturally: the failed DAG does not update its output Dataset, so no downstream DAG triggers. This is the correct behavior — downstream DAGs should not process data from a failed upstream. However, the failure is not actively communicated to downstream consumers. If Bronze fails, Silver simply does not trigger. The absence of a trigger is not visible in the Silver DAG's UI unless you know to look for a missing DAG Run. SaulėGrid addresses this with a monitoring pattern: a separate DAG checks whether each expected DAG Run was created within the SLA window, and fires an alert when a run is missing. This turns the passive 'nothing happened' signal into an active notification.

A subtler failure mode is partial chain completion. SaulėGrid's Silver layer has two parallel transformations — meters and generation — triggered by separate Bronze Datasets. If Bronze meters succeeds but Bronze generation fails, the Silver meters DAG triggers while Silver generation does not. Gold billing, which depends on both Silver Datasets, does not trigger because one of its inputs is missing. The chain is in a partial state: Silver meters is fresh, Silver generation is stale, and Gold is waiting. This is operationally confusing because the Airflow UI shows Silver meters as green (success) and Gold as grey (no run), but the underlying cause is a Bronze generation failure. Clear logging, Dataset event tracking, and the SLA monitoring DAG help the on-call engineer trace from the symptom (Gold did not run) to the root cause (Bronze generation failed).

blocks.title: Chain observability gap
blocks.content: Dataset chains fail silently by design — a downstream DAG that was not triggered has no failed DAG Run to alert on. This is correct behavior (do not process incomplete data) but creates an observability gap. Every production Dataset chain should be accompanied by a freshness monitoring DAG that checks whether expected DAG Runs were created within the SLA window and alerts when runs are missing. SaulėGrid's dag_sla_monitor runs hourly and checks every Gold Dataset's last-updated timestamp against the expected freshness threshold.

Datasets solve the cross-DAG scheduling problem — they answer 'start when the data is ready.' But some DAGs do not depend on upstream data: they are chain entry points that download from external systems, generate reports on business schedules, or perform maintenance on calendars that cron cannot express. Cron syntax can represent 'every day at midnight' and 'every Monday at 06:00,' but it cannot represent 'every Lithuanian business day excluding public holidays,' 'the last business day of each month,' or 'every day except during SaulėGrid's annual maintenance window in August.' Custom timetables are the extension point for scheduling logic that requires more than five cron fields.
The Timetable Interface

A custom timetable is a Python class that implements Airflow's Timetable interface. The interface has two core methods: next_dagrun_info() returns the scheduling information for the next DAG Run given the current state, and infer_manual_data_interval() computes the data interval for a manually triggered run. The timetable replaces the cron parser in the scheduling loop: instead of evaluating '0 0 * * *' to determine the next run, the scheduler calls the timetable's next_dagrun_info() method, which can implement any logic — calendar lookups, holiday exclusions, business-day calculations, or even external API calls to determine the next valid run time.

from airflow.timetables.base import DagRunInfo, DataInterval, Timetable
from pendulum import DateTime, Duration, instance
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

# Lithuanian public holidays for 2026
# In production, this would be loaded from a configuration file
LITHUANIAN_HOLIDAYS = {
    date(2026, 1, 1),   # New Year's Day
    date(2026, 2, 16),  # Restoration of the State
    date(2026, 3, 11),  # Restoration of Independence
    date(2026, 4, 5),   # Easter Sunday
    date(2026, 4, 6),   # Easter Monday
    date(2026, 5, 1),   # Labour Day
    date(2026, 6, 24),  # St. John's Day
    date(2026, 7, 6),   # Statehood Day
    date(2026, 8, 15),  # Assumption of Mary
    date(2026, 11, 1),  # All Saints' Day
    date(2026, 11, 2),  # All Souls' Day
    date(2026, 12, 24),  # Christmas Eve
    date(2026, 12, 25),  # Christmas Day
    date(2026, 12, 26),  # Second Christmas Day
}


def is_lithuanian_business_day(d: date) -> bool:
    """Check if a date is a Lithuanian business day."""
    # Weekend check: Saturday = 5, Sunday = 6
    if d.weekday() >= 5:
        return False
    if d in LITHUANIAN_HOLIDAYS:
        return False
    return True


def next_business_day(d: date) -> date:
    """Find the next Lithuanian business day after the given date."""
    candidate = d
    while True:
        candidate = candidate + Duration(days=1)
        if is_lithuanian_business_day(candidate):
            return candidate


class LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable(Timetable):
    """Schedule DAG Runs on Lithuanian business days only.
    
    Skips weekends and Lithuanian public holidays.
    The data interval for each run covers the previous business day.
    """

    def next_dagrun_info(
        self,
        *,
        last_automated_data_interval: Optional[DataInterval],
        restriction,
    ) -> Optional[DagRunInfo]:
        if last_automated_data_interval is None:
            # First run: find the first business day >= earliest allowed
            start = restriction.earliest
            if start is None:
                return None
            start_date = start.date()
            if not is_lithuanian_business_day(start_date):
                start_date = next_business_day(start_date)
        else:
            # Subsequent runs: next business day after last interval end
            start_date = next_business_day(
                last_automated_data_interval.end.date()
            )

        # Data interval: this business day (00:00 to 00:00 next day)
        interval_start = DateTime(start_date.year, start_date.month, start_date.day, tz='Europe/Vilnius')
        interval_end = interval_start + Duration(days=1)

        if restriction.latest is not None and interval_start > restriction.latest:
            return None

        return DagRunInfo(
            data_interval=DataInterval(start=interval_start, end=interval_end),
            run_after=interval_end,  # Run after the business day ends
        )

    def infer_manual_data_interval(self, *, run_after: DateTime) -> DataInterval:
        """For manual triggers, use the current day as the data interval."""
        start = run_after.start_of('day')
        return DataInterval(start=start, end=start + Duration(days=1))

The LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable implements the core scheduling logic: find the next business day, compute its data interval, and tell the scheduler when to create the DAG Run. The next_dagrun_info() method is called by the scheduler during every scheduling loop iteration. It receives the last automated data interval (so it knows where the schedule left off) and a restriction object that defines the DAG's start and end dates. The method returns either a DagRunInfo with the next run's data interval and run-after time, or None if no more runs should be created.
Registering and Using the Timetable

Custom timetables must be registered with Airflow through the plugin system so the scheduler can instantiate them during DAG parsing. The registration involves creating an Airflow plugin class that lists the timetable in its timetables attribute. Once registered, the timetable can be used in the schedule parameter of any DAG definition. The plugin file must be placed in the Airflow plugins directory, and the timetable class must be importable from the DAG file. SaulėGrid keeps all custom timetable classes and their plugin registration in a single plugins/saulegrid_timetables.py file.

# File: plugins/saulegrid_timetables.py
from airflow.plugins_manager import AirflowPlugin

class SaulegridTimetablePlugin(AirflowPlugin):
    name = 'saulegrid_timetables'
    timetables = [LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable]


# File: dags/dag_regulatory_report.py
from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from pendulum import datetime
from saulegrid_timetables import LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_regulatory_report',
    schedule=LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable(),  # Runs on business days only
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['regulatory', 'vert', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_regulatory_report():

    @task
    def generate_vert_report():
        """Generate daily regulatory report for VERT (Lithuanian energy regulator).
        
        VERT requires business-day reporting. Weekend and holiday data
        is included in the next business day's report.
        """
        pass

    generate_vert_report()

dag_regulatory_report()
Testing Custom Timetables

Custom timetables are code, and code needs tests. The test strategy for a timetable is straightforward: call next_dagrun_info() with a known state and verify that the returned data interval and run-after time match expectations. Test boundary conditions explicitly: the first business day of the year, the day after a holiday, a holiday that falls on Monday (creating a three-day gap), and the edge case where multiple holidays are consecutive. SaulėGrid's test suite for the LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable covers 14 test cases including every Lithuanian holiday and the weekend-holiday combinations that create non-obvious gaps in the schedule.

# Testing the custom timetable
import pytest
from pendulum import DateTime
from airflow.timetables.base import DataInterval, TimeRestriction

def test_skips_weekend():
    """Friday's run should produce Monday as the next run."""
    timetable = LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable()
    friday = DataInterval(
        start=DateTime(2026, 3, 20, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),  # Friday
        end=DateTime(2026, 3, 21, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),
    )
    restriction = TimeRestriction(earliest=DateTime(2026, 1, 1, tz='Europe/Vilnius'), latest=None, catchup=True)
    result = timetable.next_dagrun_info(
        last_automated_data_interval=friday,
        restriction=restriction,
    )
    # Next business day is Monday March 23
    assert result.data_interval.start.day == 23

def test_skips_holiday():
    """Day before Restoration of Independence should skip to March 12."""
    timetable = LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable()
    march_10 = DataInterval(
        start=DateTime(2026, 3, 10, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),  # Tuesday
        end=DateTime(2026, 3, 11, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),
    )
    restriction = TimeRestriction(earliest=DateTime(2026, 1, 1, tz='Europe/Vilnius'), latest=None, catchup=True)
    result = timetable.next_dagrun_info(
        last_automated_data_interval=march_10,
        restriction=restriction,
    )
    # March 11 is Restoration of Independence — skip to March 12
    assert result.data_interval.start.day == 12

The tests import the timetable class directly and call its methods with constructed DataInterval and TimeRestriction objects. This is unit testing in its purest form — no Airflow scheduler, no metadata database, no running DAG. The tests run in milliseconds and catch logic errors in the holiday lookup, the weekend skip, and the boundary conditions before the timetable is deployed to any environment. SaulėGrid runs timetable tests as part of their CI pipeline alongside DAG integrity tests (covered in Module 6), ensuring that scheduling logic changes are validated automatically before reaching production.
When Custom Timetables Are Worth the Complexity

Custom timetables add code that must be tested, maintained, and deployed alongside DAG files. They are not the first tool to reach for — simpler alternatives should be considered first. A cron expression with a BranchPythonOperator that skips on non-business days achieves a similar result for simple cases. The timetable approach is worth the complexity when the skip logic affects the data interval (the DAG should not create a DAG Run at all, not create one and skip it), when the scheduling pattern is reused across multiple DAGs (SaulėGrid uses the Lithuanian business day timetable for 4 DAGs), or when the scheduling logic is complex enough that embedding it in branching operators would make the DAG code harder to understand than a dedicated timetable class.

A third consideration is the DAG's visibility in the Airflow UI. A cron-scheduled DAG with skip logic creates DAG Runs for every scheduled time — including holidays and weekends that are immediately skipped. These skipped runs appear in the Grid View as green cells with skipped tasks, creating visual noise that makes it harder to spot genuine failures in the UI. A timetable-scheduled DAG creates DAG Runs only on valid dates, so the Grid View shows only actual processing runs. For SaulėGrid's regulatory DAGs where the operations team reviews the Grid View daily, the cleaner UI presentation was a contributing factor in choosing the timetable approach over branching.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: The decision between a cron schedule with skip logic and a custom timetable comes down to whether the scheduling logic is a property of the DAG's calendar or a property of the DAG's execution flow. If the DAG should not have a DAG Run on holidays (no record in the metadata database, no UI entry, no scheduling overhead), use a timetable. If the DAG should have a DAG Run but skip its work on holidays (visible in the UI as a successful run with skipped tasks), use branching. SaulėGrid's regulatory DAG uses a timetable because VERT expects exactly one report per business day — a skipped run would create a gap in the audit trail.

Before building custom timetables, it is worth knowing what Airflow provides out of the box. The junior-level track covered cron expressions and preset strings like @daily. Airflow also ships several built-in timetable classes that handle scheduling patterns more complex than cron can express but simpler than a fully custom timetable. These built-in timetables cover fixed-interval schedules that do not align with calendar boundaries, cron schedules with explicit data interval control, and schedules defined by a list of specific datetimes. Knowing these exists prevents reinventing solutions for patterns that Airflow already handles.
DeltaDataIntervalTimetable

The DeltaDataIntervalTimetable schedules DAG Runs at fixed intervals — similar to a timedelta schedule but with proper data interval tracking. It is useful when the processing cadence does not align with calendar boundaries: every 4 hours, every 90 minutes, or every 18 hours. Cron cannot express intervals like 'every 90 minutes' because cron operates on clock positions (minute 0 of every other hour), not on durations from the last run. The DeltaDataIntervalTimetable runs at exact intervals from the start_date, producing predictable data intervals regardless of calendar alignment. This makes it the correct choice for monitoring and alerting DAGs that need consistent frequency without calendar dependency.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.timetables.interval import DeltaDataIntervalTimetable
from pendulum import datetime, duration

# SaulėGrid's battery state-of-charge monitoring: every 4 hours
@dag(
    dag_id='dag_battery_soc_monitor',
    schedule=DeltaDataIntervalTimetable(interval=duration(hours=4)),
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['battery', 'monitoring', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_battery_soc_monitor():
    @task
    def check_battery_levels():
        """Check state-of-charge across 120 battery sites.
        
        Runs every 4 hours to detect batteries below minimum
        charge threshold before the next tariff peak.
        """
        pass
    check_battery_levels()

dag_battery_soc_monitor()
CronTriggerTimetable

The CronTriggerTimetable combines cron-based triggering with explicit control over the data interval. The standard cron schedule infers the data interval from the gap between consecutive cron ticks — a @daily DAG processes the 24-hour window ending at the trigger time. The CronTriggerTimetable lets you specify the data interval independently of the trigger time. This is useful when the trigger time and the data coverage period are not aligned: SaulėGrid's weather forecast DAG runs at 05:00 but the data interval covers the previous calendar day because the forecast API publishes daily data at 04:30.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.timetables.trigger import CronTriggerTimetable
from pendulum import datetime

# Trigger at 05:00, but data interval covers the previous calendar day
@dag(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_weather',
    schedule=CronTriggerTimetable(
        cron='0 5 * * *',
        timezone='Europe/Vilnius',
        interval=None,  # Data interval = gap between consecutive triggers
    ),
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'weather', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_bronze_weather():
    @task
    def fetch_forecast():
        """Fetch weather forecast from external API.
        
        API publishes daily forecast at 04:30 Vilnius time.
        DAG runs at 05:00 to ensure data is available.
        """
        pass
    fetch_forecast()

dag_bronze_weather()
EventsTimetable

The EventsTimetable schedules DAG Runs on a specific list of datetimes rather than a repeating pattern. It is useful for irregular schedules: quarterly reporting dates, end-of-month processing, annual regulatory filings, or any pattern where the run dates are known in advance but do not follow a repeating calendar pattern. The dates are passed as a list to the constructor and the scheduler creates DAG Runs only at those specified times. SaulėGrid uses the EventsTimetable for its quarterly regulatory submission to VERT, where the submission dates are published by the Lithuanian energy regulator annually and change year to year based on the regulatory calendar.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.timetables.events import EventsTimetable
from pendulum import datetime

# VERT quarterly reporting dates for 2026
# Published annually by the Lithuanian energy regulator
vert_quarterly_dates = [
    datetime(2026, 1, 15),   # Q4 2025 report
    datetime(2026, 4, 15),   # Q1 2026 report
    datetime(2026, 7, 15),   # Q2 2026 report
    datetime(2026, 10, 15),  # Q3 2026 report
]

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_vert_quarterly_submission',
    schedule=EventsTimetable(events=vert_quarterly_dates),
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['regulatory', 'vert', 'quarterly', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_vert_quarterly_submission():
    @task
    def prepare_submission():
        """Prepare quarterly data submission for VERT.
        
        Aggregates 3 months of Gold data into the VERT-mandated
        format: prosumer counts, net metering balances, grid
        injection totals by region.
        """
        pass
    prepare_submission()

dag_vert_quarterly_submission()
Combining Built-in Timetables

The built-in timetable classes cover the most common non-cron scheduling patterns, but they are not composable — you cannot combine a DeltaDataIntervalTimetable with an EventsTimetable to create a schedule that runs every 4 hours except on certain dates. For composite patterns, the options are either a custom timetable that implements the combined logic or a branching strategy within the DAG that evaluates the additional conditions at runtime. SaulėGrid's convention is to use built-in timetables whenever one fits directly and reserve custom timetables for patterns that genuinely require calendar logic, keeping the codebase as simple as the scheduling requirements allow.
Choosing the Right Timetable

rows: Fixed interval not aligned to calendar
Cron trigger with explicit data interval control
Trigger at 05:00, data covers previous day
Calendar logic requiring code (holidays, business days)

blocks.content: The decision tree assumes the scheduling requirement is well-understood before the DAG is written. In practice, scheduling requirements evolve — a DAG that starts on a simple daily cron may later need business-day awareness when regulatory reporting is added, or may need Dataset scheduling when an upstream dependency is introduced. SaulėGrid's approach is to start with the simplest scheduling mechanism that meets the current requirement and upgrade to a more sophisticated mechanism only when the requirement changes. Each upgrade follows the migration pattern from lesson 4: add the new scheduling capability first, validate it in staging, then switch the production DAG.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's scheduling convention: use cron for simple repeating schedules, Datasets for cross-DAG dependencies, built-in timetables for non-standard intervals and event-based schedules, and custom timetables only when none of the above suffice. The convention is documented in the platform's SCHEDULING.md and reviewed during DAG design review. A developer proposing a custom timetable must demonstrate that none of the simpler options handle the requirement.

Datasets and timetables solve different scheduling problems: Datasets answer 'start when the data is ready' and timetables answer 'start on this calendar pattern.' Some DAGs need both. SaulėGrid's billing aggregation DAG should run when the Silver data is ready — but it must also run by 06:00 every morning regardless, because the billing team has an SLA that Gold data is available before business hours. If Bronze is delayed and Silver is not ready by 06:00, the billing DAG should run anyway using the most recent Silver data available, then be marked for reprocessing when the fresh data arrives. Airflow supports this combined scheduling through the DatasetOrTimeSchedule and the less common DatasetAndTimeSchedule conditions.
DatasetOrTimeSchedule

The DatasetOrTimeSchedule triggers a DAG Run when either the specified Datasets are updated or the time-based schedule fires — whichever comes first. This is the 'safety net' pattern: the DAG runs on data availability in the normal case, but the time-based schedule guarantees a minimum run frequency. If the data arrives at 03:00, the DAG runs at 03:00. If the data has not arrived by 06:00, the DAG runs at 06:00 anyway. This prevents SLA breaches caused by upstream delays while preserving the data-driven trigger for the happy path.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from airflow.datasets import Dataset
from airflow.timetables.datasets import DatasetOrTimeSchedule
from airflow.timetables.trigger import CronTriggerTimetable
from pendulum import datetime

silver_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/meters/')
silver_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/silver/generation/')

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
    schedule=DatasetOrTimeSchedule(
        timetable=CronTriggerTimetable('0 6 * * *', timezone='Europe/Vilnius'),
        datasets=[silver_meters, silver_generation],
    ),
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['gold', 'billing', 'sla-critical', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_gold_billing():

    @task
    def check_data_freshness():
        """Check whether Silver data is from the current processing date.
        
        If triggered by the time-based safety net (06:00) before Silver
        completes, log a staleness warning and flag for reprocessing.
        """
        # ... freshness check logic ...
        pass

    @task
    def compute_billing():
        """Compute billing aggregates from Silver data."""
        pass

    freshness = check_data_freshness()
    freshness >> compute_billing()

dag_gold_billing()

The check_data_freshness task is a critical component of the combined schedule pattern. When the DAG is triggered by a Dataset update, the data is fresh and the freshness check passes. When the DAG is triggered by the 06:00 time-based safety net, the Silver data may be from the previous day if Bronze was delayed. The freshness check detects this condition, logs a warning, and the billing computation runs on the most recent available data. The on-call engineer is alerted about the staleness, and when the delayed Bronze eventually completes and Silver updates, the DatasetOrTimeSchedule triggers a second run with fresh data.
Handling Double Triggers

The OR pattern can produce double triggers in edge cases: if the Dataset updates at 05:55 and the cron fires at 06:00, both conditions are met within minutes. Airflow handles this gracefully — the 05:55 Dataset trigger creates one DAG Run, and the 06:00 cron trigger creates another only if the Dataset trigger has not already satisfied the scheduling condition. In practice, if the Dataset-triggered run is already in progress or completed by 06:00, the cron trigger does not create a duplicate. However, if the Dataset trigger completes at 05:59 and the cron fires at 06:00, a second run can be created. SaulėGrid's billing DAG is idempotent (Module 3), so a double trigger produces the same correct result — but the second run wastes compute. The team monitors for double triggers and adjusts the cron time to 06:30 if they occur frequently.
Freshness Tracking for Combined Schedules

When a DAG uses DatasetOrTimeSchedule, the task code needs to know which condition triggered the run: was it the Dataset update (fresh data available) or the time-based safety net (deadline reached, data may be stale)? Airflow provides the triggering_dataset_events context variable that contains the Dataset events that triggered the current run. If this variable is empty, the run was triggered by the time-based schedule, not by a Dataset update. SaulėGrid's billing DAG uses this to set a freshness flag: if triggered by Datasets, the flag is fresh and the billing calculation proceeds normally. If triggered by the 06:00 cron, the flag is stale, a warning is logged, and the results include a staleness annotation that the billing team sees in their dashboard.

The freshness tracking pattern turns the ambiguity of a combined schedule into an observable, actionable signal. Without it, the billing team cannot distinguish between a normal run with current data and a safety-net run with yesterday's data. With it, every billing report carries a provenance annotation that downstream consumers can use to assess data quality. SaulėGrid's analytics dashboard shows a yellow indicator when billing data is flagged stale, alerting analysts to treat the numbers as preliminary until the reprocessing completes.
DatasetAndTimeSchedule

The DatasetAndTimeSchedule is the stricter sibling: the DAG triggers only when both the Dataset condition and the time condition are met. This is useful for DAGs that need data freshness and a specific processing window. SaulėGrid considered this for the VERT regulatory DAG: the report must be generated from the current day's Silver data (Dataset condition) and must be generated during business hours (time condition), because the VERT submission API is only available from 08:00 to 18:00. In practice, SaulėGrid chose the OR pattern for billing (SLA-critical, must run even on stale data) and reserves the AND pattern for cases where running on stale data would be worse than not running at all.

left.label: OR — Trigger on either condition
points: Dataset update OR time-based trigger — whichever fires first
SLA-critical DAGs that must run by a deadline, even on stale data
Guaranteed to run at least once per time period (data may be stale)
Gold billing — must produce results by 06:00 for the billing team

right.label: AND — Trigger when both conditions are met
points: Dataset update AND time-based window — both must be satisfied
DAGs that need both fresh data and a specific processing window
May not run if data is not ready during the time window
VERT submission — requires fresh data AND the VERT API to be available

blocks.content: The choice between OR and AND scheduling is a risk assessment: what is the cost of running on stale data versus the cost of not running at all? For SaulėGrid's billing DAG, not running means the billing team has no data for the morning — a guaranteed SLA breach that affects all downstream business processes. Running on stale data means the numbers are from yesterday — inconvenient but manageable because the billing team knows to look for the staleness flag and can reprocess when fresh data arrives. For the VERT regulatory submission, running on stale data means submitting incorrect figures to the regulator — a compliance violation that is worse than a delayed submission. The cost matrix determines the scheduling pattern.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: The most common mistake with combined scheduling is using the AND pattern when the OR pattern is needed. If the billing DAG used DatasetAndTimeSchedule with a 06:00 cron, it would only run when Silver data arrived before 06:00 — which is the normal case. But on the one morning where Silver is late, the DAG would not run at all, and the billing team would have no data. The OR pattern is the safe default for SLA-critical DAGs because it guarantees a run. Reserve the AND pattern for cases where stale data is worse than no data.

Datasets and timetables cover the two most common scheduling patterns: data-driven and time-driven. A third category exists: event-driven triggers from outside the Airflow platform. SaulėGrid's meter data provider occasionally sends correction files that replace a previous day's readings with updated values. These corrections arrive at unpredictable times — days or weeks after the original data — and the Bronze DAG must reprocess the corrected day. No cron schedule or Dataset dependency can handle this because the trigger originates from an external system's action, not from Airflow's internal data flow or calendar. Event-driven triggers — the REST API, the CLI, and webhook patterns — handle these ad-hoc, externally-initiated execution requests that cannot be anticipated by any schedule.
The REST API Trigger

Airflow's REST API provides a POST endpoint to trigger a DAG Run with optional configuration parameters. The calling system sends a JSON payload specifying the DAG ID, an optional logical date, and a conf dictionary with parameters that tasks can access at runtime. This is the most common event-driven trigger pattern because most external systems can make HTTP requests. SaulėGrid's meter data provider sends a webhook notification when a correction file is available, and SaulėGrid's integration service translates that webhook into an API call to trigger the Bronze reprocessing DAG.

The conf dictionary is the primary mechanism for passing context from the trigger source to the DAG's tasks. Tasks access the configuration via the params or dag_run.conf context variables at runtime. SaulėGrid's reprocessing DAG reads the correction_date and source_file from conf to determine which day to reprocess and which file to use as the data source. The note field provides human-readable context that appears in the Airflow UI's DAG Runs list, helping the on-call engineer understand why the DAG Run was created without needing to inspect the conf payload.
The Reprocessing DAG Pattern

The schedule=None parameter is the key signal that distinguishes event-driven DAGs from all other DAG types: this DAG has no automatic schedule and will never create DAG Runs on its own. It exists solely to be triggered by the API, the CLI, or a manual trigger from the Airflow UI. The params dictionary provides default values that the UI renders as a form when an operator triggers the DAG manually — useful for ad-hoc reprocessing when the correction does not arrive through the automated webhook path and the engineer needs to specify the parameters directly.
The Webhook Integration Pattern

External systems rarely call the Airflow API directly. Instead, they send webhooks to an integration service — a lightweight HTTP endpoint that validates the webhook payload, translates it into an Airflow API call, and logs the interaction for audit purposes. The integration service provides authentication isolation (the external system authenticates with the integration service, not with Airflow), payload validation (the service rejects malformed webhook payloads before they reach Airflow), and rate limiting (the service prevents a misbehaving external system from flooding Airflow with trigger requests). SaulėGrid's integration service is a small Flask application that receives meter provider webhooks, validates the correction file metadata, and calls the Airflow API to trigger dag_bronze_reprocess with the validated parameters.
CLI Triggers

The Airflow CLI provides the airflow dags trigger command for triggering DAGs from shell scripts, cron jobs on external machines, or CI/CD pipelines. The CLI accepts the same conf parameter as the API, passed as a JSON string on the command line. SaulėGrid uses CLI triggers in two primary contexts: their CI/CD pipeline triggers a test DAG Run after deploying new DAG code to staging to verify that the deployment did not break any DAG parsing, and their monthly maintenance script triggers the database cleanup DAG after completing the metadata database vacuum to remove orphaned records.

# Trigger from the command line with configuration
airflow dags trigger dag_bronze_reprocess \
  --conf '{"correction_date": "2026-03-10", "source_file": "corrections/meters_2026-03-10_v2.csv", "reason": "Manual correction"}'

# Trigger from CI/CD after staging deployment
airflow dags trigger dag_staging_smoke_test \
  --conf '{"deployment_id": "deploy-20260324-001", "triggered_by": "github_actions"}'
When to Use Event-Driven Triggers

rows: Ad-hoc reprocessing for data quality investigation

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's convention for event-triggered DAGs: always use schedule=None, always define params with sensible defaults, always include a validation task as the first step that verifies the trigger parameters before processing, and always log the trigger source and reason for audit trail. The params defaults ensure that manual triggers from the UI work without requiring the operator to remember the exact JSON format.

The previous lessons presented the tools: Datasets for data-driven scheduling, timetables for calendar-driven scheduling, and event triggers for ad-hoc execution. This lesson presents the mistakes — the scheduling patterns that appear reasonable but create fragility, waste resources, or cause subtle failures at mid-level scale. Each anti-pattern is drawn from SaulėGrid's operational experience or from common patterns observed in production Airflow deployments. Understanding these mistakes is as important as understanding the correct patterns, because the anti-patterns are often the first thing an engineer reaches for.
Anti-Pattern 1: Time Buffers as Dependencies

The most common scheduling anti-pattern is using time gaps between cron schedules as implicit dependencies. Bronze runs at 00:00, Silver at 02:00, Gold at 04:00 — with 2-hour buffers assuming each DAG completes within 90 minutes. This works until it does not: a slow source, a data quality issue that triggers retries, or a worker shortage during peak processing all extend execution time beyond the buffer. The fix is explicit data dependencies via Datasets. If migrating all at once is too risky, at minimum replace the most fragile dependency (the one with the smallest buffer relative to processing time) first.
Anti-Pattern 2: Sensor Chains at Scale

ExternalTaskSensors work for one or two cross-DAG dependencies. At mid-level scale — 12 DAGs with 8 cross-DAG dependencies — sensor chains create hidden worker slot consumption. Each sensor in poke mode occupies a worker slot continuously. Eight sensors consuming slots means eight fewer slots available for actual work. SaulėGrid discovered this when their 3-worker Celery cluster had 5 sensors running simultaneously, leaving only 1 available slot for data processing tasks. The queued processing tasks could not execute until the sensors released their slots, which happened only when the upstream DAGs completed — but the upstream DAGs could not complete because their downstream tasks were also waiting for slots. The result was a deadlock that required manual intervention.

blocks.title: Sensor deadlock risk
blocks.content: The sensor deadlock is a real production risk: sensors hold worker slots while waiting, reducing the pool available for actual tasks. If enough sensors run simultaneously to exhaust the worker pool, no tasks can execute — including the upstream tasks that the sensors are waiting for. The mitigation is threefold: use Datasets instead of sensors wherever possible, use reschedule mode (not poke mode) for any remaining sensors to release slots between checks, and configure a dedicated pool for sensor tasks with a slot limit that cannot exhaust the worker pool.
Anti-Pattern 3: Mismatched Dataset URIs

Dataset URIs must match exactly between producer and consumer. A trailing slash mismatch, a case difference, or a path prefix difference creates two separate Datasets in the scheduler's view. The producer updates one URI, the consumer waits on another, and the trigger never fires. This is a silent failure — no error message, no warning, no indication that anything is wrong. The consuming DAG simply never runs. SaulėGrid caught this when a developer copied a Dataset URI from documentation that used a different path prefix than the actual DAG code. The fix is centralizing all Dataset URIs in a shared module (saulegrid_common.datasets) that both producers and consumers import.
Anti-Pattern 4: Dataset Cascades

When every DAG in a chain produces Datasets and every downstream DAG consumes them, the chain triggers cascading runs automatically. This is usually desirable — Bronze completes, Silver triggers, Gold triggers. But if a single Bronze DAG update triggers 5 Silver DAGs, each producing a Dataset that triggers 3 Gold DAGs, one Bronze completion cascades into 15 Gold DAG Runs. If the cascade involves DAGs that are not idempotent, or if the executor does not have capacity for 15 simultaneous DAG Runs, the cascade overwhelms the platform. SaulėGrid experienced this when adding a new Silver-to-Gold dependency caused Gold to trigger twice for the same data — once from Silver meters and once from Silver generation — because Gold depended on each Dataset independently rather than on both together.
Anti-Pattern 5: Timetables with Side Effects

Custom timetables should be pure scheduling logic: given the current state, return the next run time. They should not make API calls, query databases, or write files. The scheduler calls next_dagrun_info() frequently — potentially every few seconds during the scheduling loop — and a timetable that makes an external call on every invocation creates latency, fragility, and unintended load on external systems. SaulėGrid's first custom timetable queried the Lithuanian holiday API on every call to determine whether the next day was a holiday. The API rate-limited Airflow after 1,000 calls in an hour. The fix was loading the holiday calendar once during timetable initialization and caching it.

The common signal for timetable side effects is scheduler log entries showing slow DAG file processing. The scheduler logs the parse time for each DAG file, and a custom timetable that makes external calls inflates the parse time because the timetable is instantiated during parsing. SaulėGrid monitors the DAG file parse time as part of their performance dashboard (Module 9). When a new timetable caused parse time to spike from 0.3 seconds to 4.2 seconds per scheduling loop, the anomaly was immediately visible. The fix — caching the holiday calendar at class level with a daily refresh — brought parse time back to 0.4 seconds.
Anti-Pattern Summary

rows: Downstream DAG processes stale or incomplete data
Migrate to Datasets; use reschedule mode for remaining sensors
Centralize URIs in a shared module
Executor overwhelmed by simultaneous DAG Runs
Use multi-Dataset AND triggers; control concurrency with max_active_runs
Cache external data at initialization; keep scheduling logic pure

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Every scheduling anti-pattern is a violation of the same principle: the scheduling mechanism should express dependencies, not assumptions. Time buffers assume processing time is predictable. Sensor chains assume worker capacity is unlimited. Mismatched URIs assume string matching is forgiving. Dataset cascades assume executor capacity scales with DAG count. Timetable side effects assume external systems are always available. Replace every assumption with an explicit mechanism, and the schedule becomes robust.

This module has covered four scheduling mechanisms — Datasets for data-driven triggers, custom and built-in timetables for calendar-driven schedules, event-driven triggers for ad-hoc execution, and combined schedules for SLA-critical DAGs. This lesson synthesizes every mechanism into SaulėGrid's complete scheduling architecture: how the 12 production DAGs are scheduled, why each scheduling decision was made, how the mechanisms interact, and how the architecture is monitored. Every design choice traces back to a concept from this module. The architecture is not a theoretical ideal — it is the result of migrating from the junior-level cron-based setup and fixing the scheduling failures that motivated the migration.
The Complete DAG Schedule

rows: Chain entry point — downloads from SFTP on a fixed schedule
Chain entry point — downloads solar generation telemetry
Weather API publishes at 04:30; timetable controls data interval
SLA-critical — must produce data by 06:00 even if Silver is late
Requires both Silver datasets; no time SLA
Quarterly regulatory submission on published dates
Battery monitoring on a fixed non-calendar interval

visual-recommendation.description: Styled reference table showing all 12 SaulėGrid DAGs with their scheduling types color-coded by mechanism: blue for cron, green for Dataset, orange for timetable, purple for event-driven.

blocks.content: The Dataset Dependency Graph

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's complete scheduling architecture
blocks.content:
CRON ENTRY POINTS                    DATASET-DRIVEN CHAIN                   CONSUMERS

dag_bronze_meters ─────▶ [bronze_meters] ─────▶ dag_silver_meters ─────▶ [silver_meters] ──┐
  @daily 00:00                                                                              ├─▶ dag_gold_billing
dag_bronze_generation ─▶ [bronze_generation] ─▶ dag_silver_generation ─▶ [silver_generation]─┘     (OR 06:00 SLA)
  @daily 00:00                                                                              │
                                                                                            └─▶ dag_gold_forecast
dag_bronze_weather                      [bronze_meters] ─────▶ dag_silver_quality_report
  CronTrigger 05:00

EVENT-DRIVEN                           TIMETABLE-DRIVEN

dag_bronze_reprocess                    dag_regulatory_report
  schedule=None (API)                     LithuanianBusinessDayTimetable

dag_battery_soc_monitor                 dag_vert_quarterly
  Delta 4h                                EventsTimetable (4 dates/year)
blocks.caption: SaulėGrid's scheduling architecture uses four mechanisms: cron for chain entry points, Datasets for inter-DAG dependencies, timetables for calendar patterns, and API triggers for ad-hoc execution. No DAG depends on a time buffer assumption.

visual-recommendation.description: Full-color architecture diagram showing SaulėGrid's 12 DAGs with their scheduling mechanisms, Dataset connections, and the flow from Bronze through Silver to Gold, with visual distinction between cron-scheduled, Dataset-scheduled, timetable-scheduled, and event-driven DAGs.

blocks.content: Scheduling Documentation

SaulėGrid maintains a SCHEDULING.md file in the DAG repository that documents every DAG's scheduling mechanism, the rationale for the choice, and the operational implications. The file is organized by scheduling type: cron-scheduled entry points, Dataset-driven DAGs, timetable-driven DAGs, and event-driven DAGs. Each entry includes the DAG ID, the schedule configuration, the upstream and downstream dependencies, the SLA (if any), and the monitoring pattern. The file is reviewed during every DAG design review and updated as part of the deployment checklist. Without this documentation, the scheduling architecture is only visible in the code and the Datasets UI — which shows the current state but not the reasoning behind the design decisions.

The documentation also includes a scheduling decision tree that new team members follow when adding a DAG: does the DAG depend on upstream data? Use Datasets. Does the DAG need a non-standard calendar? Use a timetable. Is the DAG triggered by an external event? Use schedule=None with API triggers. Does the DAG have an SLA that requires running even on stale data? Use DatasetOrTimeSchedule. Is the DAG a simple repeating schedule with no upstream dependency? Use cron. The decision tree ensures consistency across the team and prevents the ad-hoc scheduling decisions that created the junior-level timing problems.
The Migration Journey

SaulėGrid's scheduling architecture did not start this way. The junior-level deployment used cron schedules for all DAGs with ExternalTaskSensors for cross-DAG dependencies. The migration to the current architecture happened over six weeks in three phases. Phase 1 added Dataset outlets to all producing tasks without changing any consumers — a zero-risk change that enabled monitoring of Dataset events in the UI. Phase 2 converted consuming DAGs from sensor-based to Dataset-based scheduling, one DAG at a time, starting with the lowest-risk pipeline (Silver quality report). Phase 3 replaced the billing DAG's cron schedule with DatasetOrTimeSchedule after validating the pattern in staging for two weeks. The custom timetable for regulatory reporting was added in a separate sprint after the Dataset migration was complete.
Monitoring the Schedule

The scheduling architecture requires monitoring at two levels. The first level is individual DAG monitoring: did each DAG Run complete successfully within its SLA? This is handled by the SLA callbacks and the on-failure alerting configured on each DAG. The second level is chain monitoring: did the end-to-end chain from Bronze to Gold complete within the overall SLA? This requires a dedicated monitoring DAG — dag_sla_monitor — that runs hourly and checks the last-updated timestamp of each Gold Dataset against the expected freshness threshold. If Gold billing's last update is more than 6 hours old, the monitor fires an alert. This chain-level monitoring catches the silent failure mode where a Bronze failure prevents downstream triggers without generating any downstream failure alerts.

The Datasets tab in the Airflow UI provides the visual monitoring layer. During the weekly platform review, the engineering team inspects the Dataset dependency graph for anomalies: Datasets with no recent events (suggesting a producer failure), consumers with many pending triggers (suggesting executor capacity issues), and new Datasets that were added without updating the scheduling documentation. The graph is the single source of truth for how the platform's DAGs relate to each other — more accurate than any architecture document because it reflects the actual code, not the intended design.
Design Decisions and Trade-offs

Several design decisions in this architecture involve trade-offs that are worth making explicit. The decision to keep Bronze on cron rather than converting it to an event-driven trigger means that Bronze runs even when no new data is available — which is intentional because the absence of data on the SFTP server is itself a signal that should be logged and investigated. The decision to use DatasetOrTimeSchedule for billing rather than pure Dataset scheduling means that stale data may be served to the billing team on delayed mornings — which the team accepted because stale billing data is better than no billing data before business hours.
