The decision to use a custom timetable for regulatory reporting rather than a simpler branching approach means additional code to maintain — which is justified because the timetable is shared across 4 DAGs and VERT's audit requirements demand clean business-day scheduling with no skipped runs.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: Data-aware scheduling replaces timing assumptions with explicit data dependencies, calendar logic with timetable classes, and ad-hoc execution with parameterized triggers — transforming SaulėGrid's fragile cron chain into a resilient, observable, and self-documenting orchestration architecture where every DAG runs when it should and never when it should not.

chapters.title: Advanced Task Patterns
chapters.description: Master advanced task design patterns including custom operators, custom hooks, task groups for visual and logical organization, advanced XCom patterns with backend configuration, and the decorator extensions that make TaskFlow production-ready.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Bronze ingestion pipeline started as a single DAG with three TaskFlow tasks: download meter readings from SFTP, validate the file, and write to the Bronze layer. When the team expanded to 12 DAGs covering meters, solar generation, battery telemetry, weather forecasts, and regulatory tariff imports, they discovered that 8 of those DAGs contained nearly identical download-validate-write logic — the same SFTP connection handling, the same file validation checks, the same error logging pattern, copied and adapted across each DAG. When Ieva fixed a retry bug in the meter download task, she had to locate and patch the same logic in 7 other DAG files. Two of the patches were missed, and the generation and battery DAGs continued running with the broken retry behavior for three weeks before anyone noticed.
The Copy-Paste Problem

In the junior-level track, you learned to write tasks using PythonOperator and the @task decorator. Those tools are sufficient when each DAG is independent and the logic is unique. At mid-level scale — 12 DAGs maintained by a 3-person team — duplicated logic becomes a maintenance burden. Every copy of the download function is a copy of every bug in that function. Every copied validation check is an opportunity for the copies to diverge as engineers fix bugs in one copy without realizing others exist. The problem is not that PythonOperator is wrong — it is that PythonOperator solves the 'run this function' problem without solving the 'reuse this pattern across DAGs' problem.

The junior-level approach treats each task as a standalone function: the download logic lives inside the DAG file, the validation logic lives inside the DAG file, and the connection handling lives inside the DAG file. When the same pattern appears in multiple DAGs, the engineer copies the function and adjusts the parameters. This works for 3 DAGs. It breaks for 12 because the surface area for inconsistency grows with every copy. SaulėGrid tracked their DAG-related bugs over a quarter and found that 60% were caused by inconsistent copies of shared logic — a fix applied to some DAGs but not others.

blocks.title: Junior pattern vs mid-level pattern for shared logic
blocks.content:
Junior Pattern (copy per DAG):

dag_bronze_meters.py     dag_bronze_generation.py     dag_bronze_weather.py
├─ download_meters()     ├─ download_generation()     ├─ download_weather()
├─ validate_meters()     ├─ validate_generation()     ├─ validate_weather()
└─ write_bronze()        └─ write_bronze()            └─ write_bronze()

  Same logic copied 3x → bugs fixed inconsistently

Mid-Level Pattern (shared components):

saulegrid_common/                 DAG files:
├─ hooks/                         dag_bronze_meters.py
│   └─ sftp_hook.py               ├─ SaulegridSFTPHook()
├─ operators/                     ├─ MeterIngestionOperator()
│   └─ ingestion_operator.py      └─ BronzeWriteOperator()
└─ validators/
    └─ file_validator.py          Bug fix in one place → all DAGs fixed
blocks.caption: Custom hooks and operators centralize shared logic so that a bug fix or improvement applies everywhere. The DAG file becomes a composition of reusable components, not a collection of copied functions.

visual-recommendation.description: Side-by-side comparison showing scattered duplicated functions in the junior pattern versus centralized shared components in the mid-level pattern, with arrows showing how a single fix propagates.

blocks.content: What This Module Covers

This module teaches four patterns for building reusable, testable task components. Custom hooks encapsulate external system interaction — connection handling, retry logic, and protocol-specific behavior — so that DAG code calls hook.download_file() instead of managing SFTP sessions directly. Custom operators encapsulate task-level logic — the complete workflow of downloading, validating, and staging a file — so that DAG code declares MeterIngestionOperator(source='sftp', validation='strict') instead of writing the ingestion steps inline. Task groups organize related tasks visually in the Airflow UI, replacing the flat task list with collapsible groups that reflect the pipeline's logical structure. Advanced XCom patterns handle data passing at scale, replacing the default metadata database backend with object storage when payloads grow beyond what the database can handle efficiently.
The Four Reusable Layers

The solution to copy-paste is not more discipline — it is architecture. Instead of relying on engineers to keep copies in sync, the team restructures shared logic into reusable components with a single source of truth. This module teaches four types of reusable components, each solving a different layer of the task design problem. Hooks solve the connection layer — how to interact with an external system. Operators solve the task layer — what a complete unit of work does. Task groups solve the visual layer — how related tasks appear in the UI. XCom backends solve the data passing layer — how tasks exchange information. Together, these four layers transform DAG files from monolithic scripts into compositions of tested, maintained components.
The Component Hierarchy

rows: Connection handling and external system protocol
Used inside operators or tasks that interact with external systems
SaulegridSFTPHook — SFTP connection with retry and validation
Complete task logic: input → processing → output
Used in DAG definitions as task instances
MeterIngestionOperator — download, validate, stage in one task
Visual and logical grouping of related tasks
Used in DAG definitions to organize task sets
silver_meters group — transform, validate, write as a collapsible unit
Configured at the Airflow level, used transparently by all tasks
S3 XCom backend — stores task outputs in object storage

blocks.content: The hierarchy is deliberate: hooks are the lowest-level building blocks (connection and protocol), operators compose hooks into complete task logic (the workflow), task groups compose tasks into visual units (the UI), and XCom backends handle the data passing between all of them (the plumbing). Each layer builds on the one below. A custom operator typically uses a custom hook internally. A task group typically contains custom operator instances. The XCom backend serves all of them transparently. This lesson establishes the hierarchy; the following lessons teach each layer in depth.
The Cost of Duplication

Code duplication in DAGs has three measurable costs beyond the consistency bugs. First, onboarding time increases because new team members must learn the same pattern in multiple variations — the meter download in one file, the generation download in another, each with slightly different variable names and error handling. Second, code review becomes slower because reviewers cannot assume that a pattern in one DAG matches the pattern in another — every DAG must be reviewed independently even when the logic is conceptually identical. Third, refactoring becomes high-risk because changing a pattern requires coordinating changes across multiple files, each of which may have diverged in subtle ways from the original.

blocks.title: The reuse mindset shift
blocks.content: The shift from junior to mid-level task design is a shift from 'write the logic' to 'build the component.' A junior engineer writes a function that downloads meter files. A mid-level engineer builds a hook that handles SFTP connections for any file type, then builds an operator that uses that hook to perform ingestion for any Bronze source. The function solves today's DAG. The component solves every DAG that needs SFTP ingestion — including the ones that have not been written yet.

A hook in Airflow is a Python class that manages the connection to an external system. In the junior-level track, you used built-in hooks like PostgresHook and the SFTP provider's hook to interact with databases and file servers. Those built-in hooks handle generic use cases — any PostgreSQL query, any SFTP file transfer. At mid-level, SaulėGrid needs hooks that encode their specific operational patterns: retry logic tailored to their meter data provider's intermittent failures, file naming validation that catches malformed filenames before processing, and structured logging that includes the SaulėGrid context (region, data date, file type) in every log entry. Custom hooks wrap the generic provider hooks with this domain-specific behavior.
The Hook Contract

Every Airflow hook extends BaseHook or one of its subclasses. The core contract is simple: the hook receives a conn_id string, uses it to look up credentials from the Connections store (or Secrets Backend, as covered in Module 5), and provides methods that interact with the external system using those credentials. The hook manages the connection lifecycle — opening, using, and closing — so that task code never deals with raw credentials, connection pools, or session management. The hook is the boundary between Airflow and the outside world: everything inside the hook knows about the external system's protocol, and everything outside the hook knows only about the hook's methods.

from airflow.hooks.base import BaseHook
from airflow.providers.sftp.hooks.sftp import SFTPHook
import logging
import re
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class SaulegridSFTPHook(BaseHook):
    """SFTP hook with SaulėGrid-specific conventions.
    
    Wraps the standard SFTPHook with:
    - File naming validation (saulegrid_{type}_{date}.csv pattern)
    - Structured logging with SaulėGrid context
    - Retry logic tailored to the meter provider's intermittent failures
    - Download verification (file size and checksum)
    """
    
    conn_name_attr = 'sftp_conn_id'
    default_conn_name = 'sftp_saulegrid_meters'
    conn_type = 'sftp'
    hook_name = 'SaulėGrid SFTP'
    
    # SaulėGrid file naming convention
    FILENAME_PATTERN = re.compile(
        r'^saulegrid_(meters|generation|battery|weather)_\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}\.csv$'
    )
    
    def __init__(self, sftp_conn_id: str = default_conn_name, **kwargs):
        super().__init__()
        self.sftp_conn_id = sftp_conn_id
        self._sftp_hook: Optional[SFTPHook] = None
    
    def get_conn(self) -> SFTPHook:
        """Return the underlying SFTP connection, creating it if needed."""
        if self._sftp_hook is None:
            self._sftp_hook = SFTPHook(ssh_conn_id=self.sftp_conn_id)
            logger.info(f'SFTP connection established: {self.sftp_conn_id}')
        return self._sftp_hook
    
    def validate_filename(self, filename: str) -> bool:
        """Check if filename matches the SaulėGrid naming convention."""
        if not self.FILENAME_PATTERN.match(filename):
            logger.error(
                f'Invalid filename: {filename}. '
                f'Expected pattern: saulegrid_{{type}}_{{date}}.csv'
            )
            return False
        return True
    
    def download_file(
        self, 
        remote_path: str, 
        local_path: str,
        data_date: str,
        file_type: str,
    ) -> dict:
        """Download a file with SaulėGrid logging and validation.
        
        Returns metadata dict with file_size, download_duration, and status.
        """
        import os
        import time
        
        filename = os.path.basename(remote_path)
        if not self.validate_filename(filename):
            raise ValueError(f'Filename validation failed: {filename}')
        
        logger.info(
            f'Downloading {file_type} file for {data_date}: {remote_path}'
        )
        
        start = time.time()
        conn = self.get_conn()
        conn.retrieve_file(remote_path, local_path)
        duration = time.time() - start
        
        file_size = os.path.getsize(local_path)
        logger.info(
            f'Download complete: {file_type} for {data_date}, '
            f'{file_size:,} bytes in {duration:.1f}s'
        )
        
        return {
            'filename': filename,
            'file_size': file_size,
            'download_seconds': round(duration, 1),
            'data_date': data_date,
            'file_type': file_type,
            'status': 'success',
        }

The SaulegridSFTPHook wraps the standard SFTPHook with four SaulėGrid-specific behaviors. First, filename validation ensures that every downloaded file matches the naming convention agreed with the meter data provider — catching misnamed files before they enter the pipeline. Second, structured logging includes the file type and data date in every log entry, making it possible to filter Airflow logs by business context rather than just task ID. Third, the download metadata return value provides file size and duration for monitoring — SaulėGrid tracks download performance as a leading indicator of provider-side issues. Fourth, the connection lifecycle is managed by the hook: the underlying SFTPHook is created on first use and reused within the task, preventing redundant connection setup.
Hook Registration and Discovery

Custom hooks can be registered through the Airflow plugin system or simply imported from a Python package in the Airflow environment. The plugin approach adds the hook to the Connections UI, allowing operators to select the custom connection type when creating connections. The package approach is simpler — the hook is a regular Python class that tasks import directly. SaulėGrid uses the package approach for internal hooks because the connection types (SFTP, HTTP, PostgreSQL) already exist in the provider packages. The custom hooks add domain logic on top of existing connection types rather than defining new connection types.

# Using the custom hook in a task
from airflow.decorators import task
from saulegrid_common.hooks.sftp_hook import SaulegridSFTPHook

@task
def download_meters(data_date: str) -> dict:
    """Download meter readings using the SaulėGrid SFTP hook."""
    hook = SaulegridSFTPHook(sftp_conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_meters')
    
    metadata = hook.download_file(
        remote_path=f'/outbound/saulegrid_meters_{data_date}.csv',
        local_path=f'/tmp/bronze/meters/{data_date}.csv',
        data_date=data_date,
        file_type='meters',
    )
    
    return metadata  # Passed to downstream tasks via XCom

The task code is clean: it instantiates the hook, calls download_file with the business parameters, and returns the metadata. All SFTP protocol details, connection management, filename validation, and structured logging are encapsulated in the hook. If the meter data provider changes their server, the team updates the Connection credentials. If they change the file naming convention, the team updates the hook's FILENAME_PATTERN. Neither change requires modifying any DAG file. The separation between 'what to download' (DAG's responsibility) and 'how to download' (hook's responsibility) is the core value of the hook abstraction.
Testing Custom Hooks

Custom hooks are testable in isolation because they are regular Python classes. The test strategy involves mocking the underlying provider hook (SFTPHook) and verifying that the custom hook's domain logic — filename validation, structured logging, metadata collection — works correctly without an actual SFTP connection. SaulėGrid's hook tests cover five scenarios: valid filename acceptance, invalid filename rejection, download metadata accuracy, connection reuse within a single hook instance, and error handling when the underlying connection fails. These tests run in the CI pipeline in under 2 seconds.

# Testing the custom hook with mocked connections
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from saulegrid_common.hooks.sftp_hook import SaulegridSFTPHook


def test_valid_filename():
    hook = SaulegridSFTPHook()
    assert hook.validate_filename('saulegrid_meters_2026-03-24.csv') is True
    assert hook.validate_filename('saulegrid_generation_2026-01-01.csv') is True


def test_invalid_filename():
    hook = SaulegridSFTPHook()
    assert hook.validate_filename('meters_2026-03-24.csv') is False
    assert hook.validate_filename('saulegrid_meters_20260324.csv') is False
    assert hook.validate_filename('random_file.txt') is False


@patch.object(SaulegridSFTPHook, 'get_conn')
def test_download_returns_metadata(mock_conn):
    """Verify download_file returns correct metadata structure."""
    mock_sftp = MagicMock()
    mock_conn.return_value = mock_sftp
    
    hook = SaulegridSFTPHook()
    # Create a temp file to simulate download
    with patch('os.path.getsize', return_value=1024000):
        result = hook.download_file(
            remote_path='/outbound/saulegrid_meters_2026-03-24.csv',
            local_path='/tmp/test.csv',
            data_date='2026-03-24',
            file_type='meters',
        )
    
    assert result['file_type'] == 'meters'
    assert result['data_date'] == '2026-03-24'
    assert result['status'] == 'success'
    assert 'file_size' in result

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's convention: every custom hook includes a validate_ method for input validation and returns a metadata dictionary from every interaction method. The metadata dictionary has a consistent structure — status, duration, and domain-specific fields — so that monitoring dashboards can aggregate hook performance across all DAGs without custom parsing. The hook's logging uses Python's standard logging module with structured fields, not print statements, so that log aggregation tools can filter and search efficiently.

The test suite validates both the positive path (valid filenames are accepted, downloads complete successfully) and the negative path (invalid filenames raise errors, connection failures propagate correctly). The negative path tests are especially important for hooks because hooks sit at the boundary between the controlled Airflow environment and the unpredictable external world. SaulėGrid's SFTPHook test suite includes a timeout simulation test that verifies the hook raises a meaningful exception when the SFTP server does not respond within the configured timeout — catching the exact class of error that caused the three-week incident described in lesson 1.
When to Write a Custom Hook

Not every external system interaction needs a custom hook. The decision framework is straightforward: if the interaction is a single API call with no domain-specific validation or logging, use the built-in provider hook directly. If the same interaction pattern appears in 3 or more DAGs with domain-specific logic (validation, naming conventions, structured logging, retry strategies), extract a custom hook. If the external system uses a protocol that no Airflow provider supports, write a hook from scratch. SaulėGrid has 4 custom hooks: SaulegridSFTPHook for meter data, SaulegridWeatherAPIHook for the forecast provider, SaulegridVERTHook for the regulatory API, and SaulegridWarehouseHook for their analytics PostgreSQL database. Each wraps a standard provider hook with SaulėGrid-specific conventions.

A custom hook encapsulates how to interact with an external system. A custom operator encapsulates the complete task logic — the full workflow from inputs to outputs. In the junior-level track, you wrote task logic inline as Python functions passed to PythonOperator or decorated with @task. At mid-level, when the same task workflow appears across multiple DAGs, the pattern should be extracted into a custom operator that exposes the workflow's configuration as constructor parameters. The operator becomes a reusable building block: MeterIngestionOperator(source_type='meters', validation='strict') in one DAG and MeterIngestionOperator(source_type='generation', validation='relaxed') in another — same logic, different configuration.
The Operator Contract

Every Airflow operator extends BaseOperator. The contract has two parts: __init__() receives configuration parameters and stores them as instance attributes, and execute() performs the actual work when the scheduler runs the task. The scheduler calls execute() with a context dictionary that provides access to the DAG Run, the task instance, XCom methods, and template-rendered values. The separation between __init__ (configuration) and execute (runtime) is important because __init__ runs during DAG parsing (which happens on every scheduler heartbeat) while execute runs only when the task is actually scheduled. Heavy initialization logic in __init__ slows DAG parsing for every DAG that uses the operator.

from airflow.models import BaseOperator
from airflow.utils.context import Context
from saulegrid_common.hooks.sftp_hook import SaulegridSFTPHook
import logging
import os

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class MeterIngestionOperator(BaseOperator):
    """Downloads, validates, and stages SaulėGrid data files.
    
    Encapsulates the complete Bronze ingestion workflow:
    1. Download from SFTP using SaulegridSFTPHook
    2. Validate file structure (header check, row count minimum)
    3. Stage to the Bronze layer path
    
    Template fields allow runtime rendering of date-dependent paths.
    """
    
    # Fields that support Jinja templating at runtime
    template_fields = ('data_date', 'remote_path', 'bronze_path')
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        *,
        source_type: str,
        sftp_conn_id: str = 'sftp_saulegrid_meters',
        data_date: str = '{{ ds }}',
        remote_path: str = '',
        bronze_path: str = '',
        min_row_count: int = 1000,
        validation_mode: str = 'strict',
        **kwargs,
    ):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.source_type = source_type
        self.sftp_conn_id = sftp_conn_id
        self.data_date = data_date
        self.remote_path = remote_path or f'/outbound/saulegrid_{source_type}_{{{{ ds }}}}.csv'
        self.bronze_path = bronze_path or f'/data/bronze/{source_type}/{{{{ ds }}}}/'
        self.min_row_count = min_row_count
        self.validation_mode = validation_mode
    
    def execute(self, context: Context) -> dict:
        """Execute the complete ingestion workflow."""
        logger.info(
            f'Starting {self.source_type} ingestion for {self.data_date}'
        )
        
        # Step 1: Download
        hook = SaulegridSFTPHook(sftp_conn_id=self.sftp_conn_id)
        local_path = f'/tmp/bronze/{self.source_type}/{self.data_date}.csv'
        os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(local_path), exist_ok=True)
        
        download_meta = hook.download_file(
            remote_path=self.remote_path,
            local_path=local_path,
            data_date=self.data_date,
            file_type=self.source_type,
        )
        
        # Step 2: Validate
        row_count = self._count_rows(local_path)
        if row_count < self.min_row_count:
            if self.validation_mode == 'strict':
                raise ValueError(
                    f'{self.source_type} file has {row_count} rows, '
                    f'minimum is {self.min_row_count}'
                )
            else:
                logger.warning(
                    f'Low row count: {row_count} < {self.min_row_count}'
                )
        
        # Step 3: Stage to Bronze
        os.makedirs(self.bronze_path, exist_ok=True)
        staged_path = os.path.join(self.bronze_path, os.path.basename(local_path))
        os.rename(local_path, staged_path)
        
        result = {
            **download_meta,
            'row_count': row_count,
            'staged_path': staged_path,
            'validation_mode': self.validation_mode,
        }
        logger.info(f'Ingestion complete: {result}')
        return result
    
    def _count_rows(self, filepath: str) -> int:
        """Count data rows (excluding header)."""
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            return sum(1 for _ in f) - 1  # Subtract header

The MeterIngestionOperator encapsulates the entire Bronze ingestion workflow: download via the custom SFTP hook, validate the file structure, and stage to the Bronze layer. The template_fields tuple declares which parameters support Jinja templating — data_date, remote_path, and bronze_path can contain template expressions like {{ ds }} that Airflow renders at runtime with the actual execution date. This means the operator's paths are dynamic without requiring runtime Python logic to compute dates. The validation_mode parameter provides flexibility: 'strict' raises an exception on low row counts (appropriate for meter data where a short file indicates a provider error), while 'relaxed' logs a warning (appropriate for weather data where some days genuinely have fewer stations reporting).
Using the Operator in a DAG

from airflow.decorators import dag
from airflow.datasets import Dataset
from pendulum import datetime
from saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion import MeterIngestionOperator

bronze_meters = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/meters/')
bronze_generation = Dataset('s3://saulegrid-data/bronze/generation/')

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    schedule='0 0 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_bronze_meters():
    # One line replaces 40+ lines of inline download-validate-stage logic
    MeterIngestionOperator(
        task_id='ingest_meters',
        source_type='meters',
        sftp_conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_meters',
        min_row_count=100_000,  # Meters file should have at least 100K rows
        validation_mode='strict',
        outlets=[bronze_meters],  # Dataset update on success
    )

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_generation',
    schedule='0 0 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'generation', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_bronze_generation():
    # Same operator, different configuration
    MeterIngestionOperator(
        task_id='ingest_generation',
        source_type='generation',
        sftp_conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_generation',
        min_row_count=5_000,  # Generation has fewer rows than meters
        validation_mode='relaxed',
        outlets=[bronze_generation],
    )

dag_bronze_meters()
dag_bronze_generation()

Both DAGs use the same operator class with different parameters. The meters DAG configures strict validation with a 100,000-row minimum because a short meter file always indicates a provider error. The generation DAG configures relaxed validation with a 5,000-row minimum because solar generation files legitimately vary in size depending on cloud cover and daylight hours. If the team discovers a bug in the download logic, the fix is applied once in the MeterIngestionOperator class and propagates to every DAG that uses it — eliminating the copy-paste consistency problem that motivated this module.
Template Fields and Runtime Rendering

The template_fields attribute is the mechanism that makes operators dynamic without requiring Python date arithmetic in the DAG file. When the scheduler creates a task instance, it renders every field listed in template_fields through Jinja, replacing template expressions with actual values. The data_date field defaults to '{{ ds }}' which renders to the logical date of the DAG Run — for a DAG Run processing March 24th data, {{ ds }} becomes '2026-03-24'. The remote_path and bronze_path fields contain the rendered date automatically because they reference {{ ds }} in their default values. This means the operator works correctly for backfills, reruns, and normal scheduled runs without any date logic in the DAG file.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: A frequent error when writing custom operators is putting heavy imports or computations in __init__. The scheduler calls __init__ on every DAG parse cycle to build the task graph — which means __init__ runs every 30 seconds (by default) on every DAG that uses the operator. Importing pandas, connecting to a database, or reading a configuration file in __init__ slows parsing for the entire Airflow instance. Keep __init__ lightweight: store parameters as attributes and defer all heavy work to execute(). SaulėGrid enforces this with a lint rule that flags imports inside __init__ methods of BaseOperator subclasses.
Operator vs TaskFlow

left.label: Custom Operator (BaseOperator)
points: Reusable task logic shared across multiple DAGs
Constructor parameters — visible in Airflow UI and serialized DAG
template_fields enables Jinja rendering of specified fields
Testable as a standalone class with mocked context
Separate module in a shared package
Ingestion, validation, and cross-DAG operations

right.label: @task Decorator (TaskFlow)
points: DAG-specific logic that is not reused
Function parameters — passed via XCom from upstream tasks
Automatic for all parameters via Airflow's rendering
Testable as a regular Python function
Inline in the DAG file or imported as a function
DAG-specific transformations and one-off logic

blocks.content: Error Handling in Custom Operators

Custom operators must handle errors at two levels: expected failures (file not found, validation rejection, network timeout) and unexpected failures (disk full, memory exhaustion, code bugs). Expected failures should be caught, logged with context, and re-raised as descriptive exceptions that help the on-call engineer diagnose the problem without reading the full stack trace. Unexpected failures should propagate naturally — catching all exceptions with a bare except clause masks bugs and produces misleading 'success' results. SaulėGrid's operator design rules require that every except clause specifies the exception type and logs the full context before re-raising.

The choice between a custom operator and a @task function is not about preference — it is about reuse scope. If the logic appears in one DAG and is unlikely to be reused, @task is simpler and keeps the code in the DAG file where it is easy to find. If the logic appears in 3 or more DAGs, or if the logic is complex enough to warrant a dedicated test suite, a custom operator is the correct abstraction. SaulėGrid's guideline: if you are about to copy a @task function from one DAG to another, stop and extract a custom operator instead.

Writing a custom operator that works is straightforward — subclass BaseOperator, implement execute(), and the scheduler will run it. Writing a custom operator that is production-quality requires applying design principles that prevent subtle failures. SaulėGrid's first custom operator was a monolith: the BronzePipelineOperator handled downloading, validating, staging, logging, and alerting in a single 200-line execute() method. It worked for meter data. When the team tried to reuse it for weather data — which arrives via HTTP API instead of SFTP — they discovered that the operator's download logic was hardcoded for SFTP. The operator was not reusable because it violated the single responsibility principle by combining protocol-specific logic with generic pipeline logic.
Single Responsibility

An operator should do one thing well. The MeterIngestionOperator from the previous lesson handles the Bronze ingestion workflow — but not the Silver transformation, not the quality reporting, and not the alerting. Each of those is a separate operator or task. When an operator tries to handle the entire pipeline, it becomes impossible to reuse parts of it: you cannot use the download logic without the transformation logic, and you cannot use the validation logic without the staging logic. SaulėGrid's refactored approach: SaulegridSFTPHook handles downloading, FileValidationOperator handles validation, and BronzeStageOperator handles staging. Each component is reusable independently.
Idempotent Execution

An operator's execute() method must be safe to run multiple times for the same data interval. If a task fails after downloading the file but before staging it, the retry must not create a duplicate download or a corrupted staging directory. The MeterIngestionOperator achieves idempotency through deterministic paths: the staging path is computed from the data_date (bronze/{source_type}/{date}/), so a re-execution overwrites the same path. If the operator used timestamp-based paths (bronze/{source_type}/{timestamp}/), every retry would create a new directory, leaving orphaned partial downloads. Idempotent operators are a prerequisite for safe retries, backfills, and manual reruns.

def execute(self, context: Context) -> dict:
    """Idempotent execution: re-running produces the same result.
    
    Key idempotency guarantees:
    - Download path is deterministic (based on data_date, not timestamp)
    - Staging uses overwrite semantics (os.rename replaces existing file)
    - No append operations (no risk of duplicate data on retry)
    - Return value is deterministic (same inputs → same metadata)
    """
    # Deterministic local path — same on every execution for same date
    local_path = f'/tmp/bronze/{self.source_type}/{self.data_date}.csv'
    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(local_path), exist_ok=True)
    
    # Download overwrites any previous partial download
    download_meta = self.hook.download_file(
        remote_path=self.remote_path,
        local_path=local_path,
        data_date=self.data_date,
        file_type=self.source_type,
    )
    
    # Stage with overwrite — no risk of duplicates
    staged_path = os.path.join(self.bronze_path, f'{self.data_date}.csv')
    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(staged_path), exist_ok=True)
    os.replace(local_path, staged_path)  # Atomic replace
    
    return {**download_meta, 'staged_path': staged_path}
Meaningful Return Values

An operator's return value from execute() is automatically stored in XCom and available to downstream tasks. A return value of None wastes this mechanism — downstream tasks have no programmatic insight into what happened. A meaningful return value includes the metrics that downstream tasks and monitoring systems need: row counts, file sizes, processing durations, validation outcomes, and the paths where output was written. SaulėGrid's convention is that every operator returns a dictionary with at minimum a status field ('success', 'partial', 'warning'), a row_count field, and domain-specific metadata. This allows monitoring DAGs to aggregate operator outcomes without parsing log files.
Clear Failure Modes

An operator should fail loudly and specifically. A generic 'Task failed' error forces the on-call engineer to read the full log to diagnose the problem. A specific error — 'Meter file has 0 rows: expected minimum 100,000. Source: sftp_saulegrid_meters, file: saulegrid_meters_2026-03-24.csv' — tells the engineer exactly what happened, where it happened, and what the expected behavior was. SaulėGrid's operator design rules require that every exception raised by execute() includes the source system, the specific failure condition, the expected condition, and a suggested remediation: 'Check the SFTP server status' or 'Verify the data provider sent today's file.'
Proper Logging

Operators should log at the INFO level for normal operation and WARNING or ERROR for anomalies. Every log entry should include enough context to identify the task without consulting the Airflow UI: the source type, the data date, the connection ID, and the operation being performed. SaulėGrid's logging convention uses structured fields: logger.info('Download complete', extra={'source_type': 'meters', 'data_date': '2026-03-24', 'file_size': 1024000}). This structured approach enables log aggregation tools to filter, search, and dashboard operator behavior across all DAGs. Print statements are prohibited in operators — they bypass the logging framework and cannot be aggregated.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's operator design checklist: Does __init__ only store parameters? Does execute() produce the same result when called twice for the same date? Does execute() return a metadata dictionary with at minimum status, row_count, and duration? Do all exceptions include the source system and a suggested action? Do all log entries include source_type and data_date? If any answer is no, the operator is not ready for production. The checklist is part of the code review template for PRs that introduce new operators.
The Monolith Anti-Pattern

The monolith anti-pattern is the most common operator design mistake: a single operator that handles the entire pipeline from download through Gold aggregation. SaulėGrid's original BronzePipelineOperator was 200 lines with nested try-except blocks, conditional logic for different source types, and mixed concerns of downloading, validating, transforming, and alerting. The operator could not be reused for any individual step, could not be tested without mocking six external systems, and could not be debugged without reading every line. The refactored design uses three operators composed through DAG dependencies: MeterIngestionOperator for download and staging, FileValidationOperator for quality checks, and BronzeWriteOperator for final Bronze layer persistence. Each operator is under 80 lines, testable in isolation, and reusable across DAGs.

SaulėGrid's Silver transformation DAG processes three data domains — meters, solar generation, and battery state-of-charge — each with its own transformation, validation, and write tasks. At junior-level scale with 3 tasks per domain, the DAG had 9 tasks plus start and end sentinels — manageable in the Graph View. When the team added quality reporting and dead-letter handling tasks for each domain, the task count grew to 18. The Graph View became a dense web of nodes and edges where identifying which tasks belonged to the meter processing pipeline versus the generation pipeline required reading every task ID. Task groups solve this by organizing related tasks into collapsible visual units that the Airflow UI renders as nested subgraphs.
The TaskGroup Context Manager

A TaskGroup is created using a context manager. Tasks defined inside the with TaskGroup() block are visually grouped in the Graph View and their task IDs are automatically prefixed with the group name. The group appears as a single collapsible node that expands to show its internal tasks when clicked. This provides two benefits: the Graph View at the top level shows the DAG's logical structure (meters → generation → battery → merge) rather than a flat list of 18 tasks, and the task ID prefixing creates a namespace that prevents ID collisions when different groups contain tasks with the same logical name (each group can have a 'validate' task without conflict).

In the Graph View, this DAG shows two collapsible nodes — 'meters' and 'generation' — rather than six individual tasks. Clicking on the 'meters' group expands it to show transform_meters → validate_meters → write_meters. The task IDs are automatically prefixed: meters.transform_meters, meters.validate_meters, meters.write_meters. This prefixing means that both groups can have a 'validate' task without conflict because the full IDs are meters.validate_meters and generation.validate_generation. The parallel placement of the groups in the graph makes the pipeline's structure immediately visible: meters and generation are independent parallel paths.
Nested Task Groups

Task groups can be nested for complex DAGs. SaulėGrid's Gold aggregation DAG has three top-level groups — billing, forecasting, and regulatory — where the billing group itself contains sub-groups for residential, commercial, and prosumer billing calculations. Nesting provides three levels of visual hierarchy: the top level shows the three business domains, the second level shows the billing sub-categories, and the leaf level shows the individual tasks. The Graph View renders nested groups as nested boxes, creating a visual hierarchy that mirrors the business logic. In practice, two levels of nesting is sufficient for most DAGs — three levels makes the expanded view difficult to navigate.
Dependencies Between Groups

Dependencies can be set between task groups using the same >> operator used for individual tasks. When a dependency is set on a group, Airflow creates dependencies between the group's root tasks (tasks with no upstream within the group) and the group's leaf tasks (tasks with no downstream within the group). This means meters_group >> merge_task creates a dependency from the last task in the meters group to the merge task. Dependencies between groups create the correct execution order without requiring the DAG author to know which specific tasks are at the group's boundaries — the abstraction handles the wiring.

# Setting dependencies between task groups and individual tasks
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator

@dag(dag_id='dag_gold_aggregate', ...)
def dag_gold_aggregate():
    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    with TaskGroup('meters') as meters_group:
        # ... meter aggregation tasks ...
        pass

    with TaskGroup('generation') as gen_group:
        # ... generation aggregation tasks ...
        pass

    @task
    def merge_gold():
        """Merge all domain aggregations into final Gold output."""
        pass

    # Dependencies between groups and individual tasks
    start >> [meters_group, gen_group]  # Parallel groups after start
    [meters_group, gen_group] >> merge_gold()  # Fan-in to merge
    merge_gold() >> end

The dependency start >> [meters_group, gen_group] creates edges from start to the root tasks of both groups. The dependency [meters_group, gen_group] >> merge_gold() creates edges from the leaf tasks of both groups to the merge task. The graph shows a clean diamond pattern: start fans out to two parallel groups, and both groups fan in to merge. This pattern is common in Gold layer DAGs where multiple data domains are aggregated independently and then combined. The task group abstraction makes the diamond visible at the top level of the graph without expanding the groups.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's task group convention: every task group has a tooltip parameter that describes the group's purpose in one sentence. Group names use snake_case and match the business domain: 'meters', 'generation', 'billing_residential'. Groups never span business domains — a group should contain tasks that belong to the same logical processing unit. If a task does not fit naturally into any group, it stays at the top level. The convention is documented in the team's DAG style guide and enforced during code review.
When to Use Task Groups

rows: DAG has 3-5 tasks with no repeated structure
Flat structure is clear enough; groups add unnecessary nesting
DAG has 10+ tasks with 2-3 parallel processing paths
Groups collapse parallel paths into readable top-level nodes
DAG processes multiple data domains (meters, generation, battery)
Each domain becomes a visual unit in the graph
DAG has stages (download → validate → transform → write)
Groups by stage can help, but linear DAGs are often clear without groups
DAG has nested parallel processing (billing → residential + commercial)
Nested groups mirror the business hierarchy

blocks.content: The decision to use task groups is driven by the Graph View's readability, not by code organization. A DAG with 5 sequential tasks does not need groups — the linear graph is already clear. A DAG with 15 tasks across 3 parallel domains becomes unreadable without groups. SaulėGrid's threshold is practical: if the Graph View requires scrolling to see all tasks, add groups. If a team member cannot identify which tasks belong to the meter processing pipeline within 3 seconds of looking at the graph, add groups. The groups exist for the humans who operate the pipeline, not for the scheduler that executes it.

In the junior-level track, you learned that TaskFlow passes data between tasks through return values: task_a() returns a dictionary, task_b(data) receives it, and Airflow handles the serialization through XCom. That pattern works cleanly for small payloads — file paths, row counts, configuration dictionaries, and status messages. At mid-level scale, SaulėGrid's tasks started passing larger payloads: quality reports with per-region statistics, validation results with lists of rejected meter IDs, and transformation summaries with schema information. When the quality report task returned a 15MB dictionary containing rejection details for 20,000 meters across 10 regions, the XCom write took 8 seconds and the metadata database grew by 15MB per DAG Run — 450MB per month from a single task's return value.
How XCom Works Under the Hood

XCom stores task return values in the Airflow metadata database by default. When a task returns a value, Airflow serializes it to JSON and writes a row to the xcom table with the DAG ID, task ID, execution date, key, and the serialized value. When a downstream task pulls the value, Airflow queries the xcom table and deserializes the result. This database-backed storage is simple and reliable for small values — but every XCom value occupies space in the metadata database, every read is a database query, and every DAG Run accumulates more rows. The xcom table is the fastest-growing table in most Airflow deployments because it receives a write for every task return value in every DAG Run.

blocks.title: XCom data flow between tasks
blocks.content:
Task A (execute)                  Metadata Database                Task B (execute)
      │                                 │                                │
      │  return {'rows': 1200000}       │                                │
      ├──── serialize to JSON ──────────▶│  INSERT INTO xcom              │
      │                                 │  (dag_id, task_id, key, value) │
      │                                 │                                │
      │                                 │         SELECT value    ◀──────┤
      │                                 │  FROM xcom                     │
      │                                 │  WHERE dag_id=... AND task=... │
      │                                 ├────── deserialize JSON ────────▶│
      │                                 │                                │
      │                                 │  {'rows': 1200000}             │
blocks.caption: Every XCom value passes through the metadata database. For small values (file paths, counts), this is efficient. For large values (data frames, detailed reports), this becomes a performance bottleneck.

blocks.content: XCom Size Limits and Performance

The default XCom backend uses the metadata database's TEXT or BLOB column to store serialized values. PostgreSQL's TEXT type can store values up to 1GB, but storing multi-megabyte values in the metadata database causes three problems. First, write latency: serializing and inserting a 15MB JSON string takes seconds, during which the task's worker slot is occupied doing database I/O instead of data processing. Second, database bloat: the xcom table grows with every DAG Run, and large values accelerate the growth. SaulėGrid's metadata database grew from 2GB to 42GB in six months, with the xcom table accounting for 38GB. Third, read latency: downstream tasks that pull large XCom values experience multi-second query times, especially when the xcom table is not regularly vacuumed.
The Right Data for XCom

rows: Yes — small string, critical for downstream tasks
Yes — small JSON, useful for monitoring and branching
Yes — small JSON, needed by downstream tasks
Quality reports with per-region detail (15MB JSON)
No — too large for the metadata database
Write to S3/GCS, pass the path via XCom
Never — serialization is slow and bloats the database
Write to storage, pass the path via XCom
Lists of rejected record IDs (20,000 items)
Borderline — consider writing to a dead-letter table

blocks.content: The pattern for handling large data is indirect passing: the producing task writes the large payload to external storage (S3, GCS, a database table) and returns the storage path via XCom. The consuming task reads the path from XCom and retrieves the data from storage. This keeps XCom values small (a path string is under 200 bytes) while allowing arbitrarily large data transfer between tasks. SaulėGrid's quality report task now writes the full per-region report to S3 and returns {'report_path': 's3://saulegrid-data/quality/meters/2026-03-24.json', 'summary': {'total_rejected': 20000, 'regions_affected': 4}} — the summary is small enough for XCom, and the full report is available in S3 for tasks that need it.

# Indirect passing: large data to storage, path via XCom
@task
def validate_meters() -> dict:
    """Validate meter readings and produce quality report.
    
    Full report → S3 (large, detailed)
    Summary → XCom return value (small, actionable)
    """
    # ... validation logic produces detailed_report (15MB) ...
    
    # Write large payload to S3
    report_path = f's3://saulegrid-data/quality/meters/{data_date}.json'
    write_to_s3(report_path, detailed_report)
    
    # Return small summary via XCom
    return {
        'report_path': report_path,
        'total_rows': 1_200_000,
        'valid_rows': 1_180_000,
        'rejected_rows': 20_000,
        'regions_affected': ['VILNIUS', 'KAUNAS', 'KLAIPEDA', 'SIAULIAI'],
        'status': 'partial',  # Some rejections but pipeline continues
    }

@task
def generate_quality_dashboard(validation_summary: dict):
    """Generate quality dashboard from validation summary.
    
    Uses the summary for dashboard metrics.
    Reads the full report from S3 only if detailed analysis is needed.
    """
    if validation_summary['rejected_rows'] > 50_000:
        # Threshold exceeded — read full report for detailed analysis
        full_report = read_from_s3(validation_summary['report_path'])
        # ... detailed analysis ...
    else:
        # Normal operation — summary is sufficient
        print(f"Quality OK: {validation_summary['rejected_rows']} rejections")
Key-Based XCom Retrieval

TaskFlow's return-value pattern stores all data under a single XCom key ('return_value'). Classic operators can push multiple XCom values with different keys using ti.xcom_push(key='metrics', value=...) and ti.xcom_push(key='paths', value=...). Downstream tasks pull specific keys with ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='upstream', key='metrics'). This key-based approach is useful when a task produces logically distinct outputs that different downstream tasks consume independently — one task needs the metrics, another needs the paths, and neither needs both. SaulėGrid uses key-based XCom for the Bronze ingestion operator, which pushes 'download_metadata' and 'validation_result' as separate keys so that the monitoring DAG can pull download_metadata without deserializing the larger validation_result.
Cross-DAG XCom Retrieval

XCom values can be pulled across DAG boundaries using the include_prior_dates parameter in xcom_pull(). This is useful when a monitoring or reporting DAG needs to access metrics from production DAGs without being part of the same DAG definition. SaulėGrid's monitoring DAG pulls download_metadata XCom values from all Bronze DAGs to compute daily download performance metrics — average download duration, total file sizes, and failure rates — without requiring the Bronze DAGs to push data to a separate monitoring system. The cross-DAG pull uses task_ids and dag_id parameters to specify the exact source, and include_prior_dates=True retrieves the most recent value even if the source DAG ran on a different schedule. This pattern should be used sparingly because it creates an implicit dependency that is not visible in the Datasets graph.

blocks.title: XCom cleanup matters
blocks.content: The xcom table is not cleaned automatically. Without explicit maintenance, it grows indefinitely. SaulėGrid's weekly maintenance DAG (Module 9) runs airflow db clean --table xcom to remove XCom values older than 30 days. Before implementing this cleanup, the xcom table had grown to 38GB and metadata database queries were visibly slow. After cleanup, the table stabilized at 3GB. The retention period should be long enough to support backfill operations (which may need XCom values from older runs) but short enough to prevent database bloat.

The previous lesson's indirect passing pattern — write large data to storage, pass the path via XCom — works but requires every task author to implement the pattern manually. If a developer forgets and returns a large dictionary directly, the metadata database absorbs the cost. Custom XCom backends solve this by intercepting the serialization layer: instead of writing XCom values to the metadata database, a custom backend writes them to S3, GCS, or another object store automatically. The task code does not change — return values are still returned normally — but the storage location changes from the database to object storage. This moves the decision from every individual task to the platform configuration.
How Custom Backends Work

Airflow's XCom system uses a backend class that implements serialize_value() and deserialize_value() methods. The default backend serializes to JSON and stores in the metadata database. A custom backend overrides these methods to store values elsewhere. When a task returns a value, Airflow calls the backend's serialize_value() method, which can write the value to S3 and return a reference string that gets stored in the database instead. When a downstream task pulls the value, Airflow calls deserialize_value(), which reads the reference from the database, fetches the actual value from S3, and returns it. The task code is completely unaware of the backend — the indirection is transparent.

blocks.title: Custom XCom backend data flow
blocks.content:
Task A (return value)             Custom Backend                S3 / Object Store
      │                                │                              │
      │  return {'rows': 1200000,      │                              │
      │          'details': <15MB>}    │                              │
      │                                │                              │
      ├── serialize_value() ──────────▶│                              │
      │                                │── upload to S3 ─────────────▶│
      │                                │                              │  s3://xcom/dag/task/key.json
      │                                │◀── return S3 path ───────────┤
      │                                │                              │
      │   Metadata DB stores only:     │                              │
      │   's3://xcom/dag/task/key.json'│                              │
      │   (small reference string)     │                              │

Task B (xcom_pull)                     │                              │
      │                                │                              │
      ├── deserialize_value() ────────▶│                              │
      │                                │── download from S3 ─────────▶│
      │                                │◀── return full value ────────┤
      │                                │                              │
      │  Receives: {'rows': 1200000,   │                              │
      │             'details': <15MB>} │                              │
blocks.caption: The custom backend intercepts XCom serialization. Large values are stored in object storage while the metadata database holds only a small reference string. Task code does not change.

visual-recommendation.description: Data flow diagram showing the custom XCom backend intercepting the serialize/deserialize process, with the metadata database storing only reference strings and S3 storing the actual values.

blocks.content: Configuring an S3 XCom Backend

# airflow.cfg configuration for S3 XCom backend
# [core]
# xcom_backend = saulegrid_common.xcom.S3XComBackend

# Implementation: saulegrid_common/xcom.py
from airflow.models.xcom import BaseXCom
import json
import boto3
import os


class S3XComBackend(BaseXCom):
    """Store XCom values in S3 instead of the metadata database.
    
    Values smaller than THRESHOLD_BYTES are stored in the database
    as usual (no S3 overhead for small values like file paths).
    Values larger than the threshold are uploaded to S3.
    """
    
    BUCKET = os.getenv('XCOM_S3_BUCKET', 'saulegrid-airflow-xcom')
    THRESHOLD_BYTES = 10_000  # 10KB — values below this stay in the DB
    
    @staticmethod
    def serialize_value(
        value,
        key=None,
        task_id=None,
        dag_id=None,
        run_id=None,
        map_index=None,
    ):
        """Serialize value — small values to DB, large values to S3."""
        serialized = json.dumps(value)
        
        if len(serialized) < S3XComBackend.THRESHOLD_BYTES:
            # Small value — store directly in the database
            return BaseXCom.serialize_value(value)
        
        # Large value — upload to S3, store reference in DB
        s3_key = f'xcom/{dag_id}/{run_id}/{task_id}/{key or "return_value"}.json'
        
        s3 = boto3.client('s3')
        s3.put_object(
            Bucket=S3XComBackend.BUCKET,
            Key=s3_key,
            Body=serialized.encode('utf-8'),
        )
        
        # Store reference string in the database
        reference = json.dumps({'__s3_xcom__': True, 's3_key': s3_key})
        return BaseXCom.serialize_value(reference)
    
    @staticmethod
    def deserialize_value(result):
        """Deserialize value — detect S3 references and fetch from S3."""
        value = BaseXCom.deserialize_value(result)
        
        if isinstance(value, str):
            try:
                parsed = json.loads(value)
                if isinstance(parsed, dict) and parsed.get('__s3_xcom__'):
                    # This is an S3 reference — fetch the actual value
                    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
                    response = s3.get_object(
                        Bucket=S3XComBackend.BUCKET,
                        Key=parsed['s3_key'],
                    )
                    return json.loads(response['Body'].read())
            except (json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError):
                pass
        
        return value

The S3XComBackend uses a threshold strategy: values smaller than 10KB are stored in the metadata database as usual (adding S3 overhead for a 50-byte file path is wasteful), while values larger than 10KB are uploaded to S3 with a reference string stored in the database. The threshold is configurable via the THRESHOLD_BYTES class attribute. SaulėGrid set theirs to 10KB after analyzing their XCom usage: 92% of XCom values were under 1KB (file paths, row counts, status dictionaries), and the remaining 8% accounted for 95% of the xcom table's storage. The threshold strategy optimizes for both cases: small values stay fast (no S3 roundtrip), and large values stay out of the database.
Backend Configuration

The custom backend is activated by a single configuration change in airflow.cfg: setting the xcom_backend property to the fully qualified class name of the backend. This configuration change takes effect on the next Airflow restart — there is no rolling update mechanism for XCom backends because the backend must be consistent across all components (scheduler, workers, web server) to avoid deserialization failures. SaulėGrid deploys backend changes during maintenance windows with a coordinated restart of all Airflow components. The deployment checklist includes verifying that the S3 bucket exists, the IAM permissions are configured, and the backend class is importable in the Airflow environment.

# airflow.cfg — switching to the S3 XCom backend
[core]
xcom_backend = saulegrid_common.xcom.S3XComBackend

# Environment variable alternative (overrides airflow.cfg)
# AIRFLOW__CORE__XCOM_BACKEND=saulegrid_common.xcom.S3XComBackend

# Verify the backend is configured correctly
airflow config get-value core xcom_backend
# Output: saulegrid_common.xcom.S3XComBackend
Migration and Rollback

Switching to a custom XCom backend is a configuration change in airflow.cfg — no DAG code modifications required. However, the migration has operational implications. Existing XCom values in the metadata database remain readable because the custom backend's deserialize_value() falls through to the default deserialization for non-S3 references. New values are written to S3. A rollback involves reverting the configuration and accepting that S3-stored values become inaccessible to the default backend (though they remain in S3 for manual retrieval). SaulėGrid tested the migration in staging for two weeks before applying it to production, verifying that all DAGs continued to function and that the metadata database stopped growing.
S3 Lifecycle Management

S3-stored XCom values need lifecycle management just like database-stored values. SaulėGrid configures an S3 lifecycle rule that transitions XCom objects to Glacier after 30 days and deletes them after 90 days. This aligns with their metadata database retention policy: XCom values older than 30 days are rarely needed for operational purposes, but keeping them for 90 days supports quarterly audit requirements. The lifecycle rule is defined in Terraform as part of the Airflow infrastructure configuration, ensuring that XCom storage costs remain proportional to the 30-day active window rather than growing indefinitely.
Monitoring the Backend

After deploying the custom backend, SaulėGrid monitors two metrics: metadata database xcom table size (should stabilize or shrink) and S3 XCom bucket size (should grow proportionally to DAG Run count). A dashboard shows both metrics on the same chart, making it visually clear that large values moved from the database to S3. The team also monitors XCom read/write latency: S3 reads add 50-200ms of network latency compared to database reads, which is acceptable for task-to-task data passing but would be noticeable if a task pulled XCom values in a tight loop. SaulėGrid's convention is that a task pulls XCom at most once per upstream dependency — never inside a loop.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: The custom XCom backend embodies the principle that platform-level decisions should not depend on individual developer discipline. The indirect passing pattern from the previous lesson works correctly but requires every developer to implement it consistently. The custom backend enforces the pattern transparently: large values go to S3 regardless of whether the developer remembered the convention. Platform-level guardrails are more reliable than code review conventions because they cannot be forgotten, bypassed, or applied inconsistently.

The junior-level track covered the @task decorator as the modern alternative to PythonOperator. The @task decorator handles the most common pattern — a Python function that runs in the Airflow worker's Python environment and passes data through return values. At mid-level, SaulėGrid needs decorator variants that handle more complex patterns: branching logic that chooses which downstream path to follow, conditional pipeline termination that skips remaining tasks, isolated Python environments for tasks with conflicting dependencies, and task group creation through decorators instead of context managers. These variants extend the TaskFlow API from simple function execution to production-ready workflow control.
@task.branch — Branching as a Decorated Function

The choose_parser function receives the upstream detection result as a parameter through TaskFlow's automatic XCom passing and returns a task_id string identifying which downstream branch should execute. Airflow marks the returned task as the chosen branch and automatically skips the other tasks by setting their state to 'skipped.' This is significantly cleaner than the classic BranchPythonOperator pattern because the branching logic receives typed input data rather than requiring manual XCom pulls. SaulėGrid uses @task.branch for file format routing, conditional regulatory reporting that skips VERT tasks on non-business days, and environment-specific processing paths that apply different quality thresholds in development versus production.

The choose_parser function receives the upstream detection result as a parameter (via TaskFlow's automatic XCom passing) and returns a task_id string. Airflow marks the returned task as the chosen branch and skips the other tasks. This is cleaner than the classic BranchPythonOperator pattern because the branching logic receives typed input data rather than pulling raw XCom values. SaulėGrid uses @task.branch for file format routing (some providers send CSV, others send JSON), for conditional regulatory reporting (skip VERT reporting on non-business days), and for environment-specific processing (different logic paths for development versus production data).
@task.short_circuit — Conditional Pipeline Termination

The @task.short_circuit decorator creates a task that evaluates a boolean condition: if the decorated function returns True, downstream tasks execute normally. If it returns False, all downstream tasks are skipped. This is the decorator equivalent of ShortCircuitOperator from the junior track. SaulėGrid uses short-circuit tasks for pre-condition checks: verify that the Bronze layer has data for today before running Silver transformation, verify that Silver is fresh before running Gold aggregation, and verify that the VERT API is available before running the regulatory submission DAG.

@task.short_circuit
def check_bronze_freshness(data_date: str) -> bool:
    """Skip Silver transformation if Bronze data is not fresh.
    
    Returns True to continue pipeline, False to skip all downstream.
    """
    bronze_timestamp = get_latest_bronze_timestamp('meters')
    expected_date = parse_date(data_date)
    
    if bronze_timestamp.date() < expected_date:
        print(f'Bronze data is stale: latest is {bronze_timestamp.date()}, '
              f'expected {expected_date}. Skipping Silver transformation.')
        return False  # Skip all downstream tasks
    
    return True  # Continue with Silver transformation
@task_group — Decorator-Based Task Grouping

The @task_group decorator provides an alternative to the TaskGroup context manager for creating task groups. Instead of defining tasks inside a with TaskGroup() block, you define a function decorated with @task_group that returns the group's tasks. This approach is useful when the group's internal structure is complex enough to warrant its own function, or when the same group pattern is reused across multiple DAGs. SaulėGrid uses @task_group for their standard Silver transformation pattern — a transform-validate-write sequence that appears in every Silver DAG with different parameters.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's decorator selection guideline: use @task for standard Python tasks that pass data through return values. Use @task.branch when a task must choose between 2-4 downstream paths. Use @task.short_circuit for pre-condition checks that should skip the entire remaining pipeline. Use @task_group when the same multi-task pattern appears in 3 or more DAGs. Use classic operators (BaseOperator subclass) when the task pattern needs template_fields, custom UI rendering, or provider-level integration. The guideline is documented in the team's DAG style guide and reviewed during onboarding.
@task.virtualenv — Isolated Python Environments

The @task.virtualenv decorator runs a task inside a temporary Python virtual environment with its own dependencies. This solves the dependency conflict problem: if one task needs pandas 1.5 and another needs pandas 2.0, they cannot coexist in the same Python environment. The virtualenv decorator creates a fresh environment for each task execution, installs the specified requirements, runs the function, and destroys the environment. SaulėGrid uses this for their weather forecast task, which depends on a meteorological library that conflicts with the pandas version used by the Silver transformation tasks. The trade-off is startup latency — creating the virtualenv and installing packages adds 15-30 seconds to task execution.
Combining Decorators

The decorator variants can be combined within a single DAG: a @task.short_circuit checks the pre-condition, a @task.branch routes to the correct processing path, @task functions handle the processing, and a @task_group organizes the processing tasks into a visual unit. The decorators compose naturally because they all participate in TaskFlow's automatic dependency inference and XCom passing. SaulėGrid's production Silver DAG uses all four: short_circuit checks Bronze freshness, branch routes to full-refresh versus incremental processing, task functions handle the transformation logic, and task_group organizes each data domain into a collapsible UI group. The resulting DAG reads like a workflow specification rather than a collection of operator configurations.

Custom hooks, operators, and task groups are the building blocks. This lesson covers how to organize, package, and distribute those building blocks across the DAG repository so that every DAG can use them without code duplication. SaulėGrid's first attempt at sharing code was a utils.py file in the DAGs directory that grew to 2,000 lines — a grab-bag of functions, constants, and classes that every DAG imported. The file was modified so frequently that merge conflicts became a daily occurrence. The team restructured their shared code into a proper Python package with a clear module hierarchy, and the merge conflicts dropped to near zero because different modules are modified by different teams.
The Package Approach

The recommended approach for sharing code across Airflow DAGs is a Python package installed in the Airflow environment. The package is a standard Python project with a setup.py or pyproject.toml, a clear module hierarchy, unit tests, and a versioned release process. DAG files import from the package just as they would import any third-party library. The package is installed into the Airflow worker environment during deployment — either baked into the Docker image or installed via pip in the initialization script. This approach provides proper dependency management, versioning, and test isolation.

saulegrid-airflow/
├── dags/                          # DAG definitions — deployed to the DAGs folder
│   ├── dag_bronze_meters.py
│   ├── dag_bronze_generation.py
│   ├── dag_silver_transform.py
│   ├── dag_gold_billing.py
│   └── ...
├── plugins/                       # Airflow plugins — timetables, UI extensions
│   └── saulegrid_timetables.py
├── saulegrid_common/              # Shared package — installed as a library
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── hooks/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── sftp_hook.py           # SaulegridSFTPHook
│   │   ├── weather_hook.py        # SaulegridWeatherAPIHook
│   │   └── warehouse_hook.py      # SaulegridWarehouseHook
│   ├── operators/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── ingestion.py           # MeterIngestionOperator
│   │   └── validation.py          # FileValidationOperator
│   ├── datasets.py                # Central Dataset URI registry
│   ├── xcom.py                    # S3XComBackend
│   └── validators/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── file_validator.py      # Shared validation functions
├── tests/
│   ├── test_hooks/
│   ├── test_operators/
│   ├── test_dag_integrity.py
│   └── test_validators/
├── pyproject.toml                 # Package metadata and dependencies
└── README.md
Installation and Deployment

The saulegrid_common package is installed into the Airflow environment during the Docker image build process. The Dockerfile includes a pip install -e ./saulegrid_common command that installs the package in development mode, allowing rapid iteration during development while maintaining proper package semantics. For production, the package is installed in standard mode with a pinned version. The same package is installed in the CI environment for testing, in the development environment for local development, and in the staging and production Airflow instances — ensuring that the same code runs in all environments.

# Dockerfile excerpt — installing the shared package
FROM apache/airflow:2.8.1-python3.10

# Copy the shared package
COPY saulegrid_common/ /opt/airflow/saulegrid_common/
COPY pyproject.toml /opt/airflow/

# Install the package
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -e /opt/airflow/

# Copy DAGs and plugins
COPY dags/ /opt/airflow/dags/
COPY plugins/ /opt/airflow/plugins/

The repository has three top-level directories with distinct purposes. The dags/ directory contains DAG definition files that are deployed to the Airflow DAGs folder — these are pure composition files that instantiate operators and set dependencies, with minimal inline logic. The plugins/ directory contains Airflow plugin classes (custom timetables, UI extensions) that need Airflow's plugin discovery mechanism. The saulegrid_common/ directory is the shared package that contains hooks, operators, validators, the Dataset registry, and the XCom backend — this is installed as a Python library in the Airflow environment. The tests/ directory mirrors the package structure with corresponding test modules.
The Plugins Folder Alternative

Airflow's plugins folder provides a simpler alternative for sharing code: Python files placed in the plugins directory are automatically added to the Python path and importable from DAG files. This approach avoids the overhead of a proper Python package — no setup.py, no installation step, no version management. However, the plugins folder lacks dependency management, cannot be versioned independently, and does not support proper test isolation. SaulėGrid uses the plugins folder only for Airflow-specific extensions that require the plugin discovery mechanism (custom timetables, custom views) and the package approach for everything else. The general guideline: if the code works as a standalone Python library, it should be a package. If it requires Airflow's plugin infrastructure, it goes in plugins.
Import Performance

Shared packages create a parsing performance consideration: every import statement in a DAG file executes during the scheduler's DAG parse cycle. If the shared package imports heavy libraries at the top level — pandas, boto3, or a large data processing framework — those imports slow down every parse cycle for every DAG that uses the package. SaulėGrid's convention is that the shared package's top-level __init__.py imports nothing. Each module imports only what it needs, and heavy imports are deferred to method bodies rather than module level. The hook's import of boto3 happens inside the download_file() method, not at the module level, so the import occurs only when the task actually executes — not when the scheduler parses the DAG file.
Versioning Shared Code

The shared package should be versioned alongside the DAG repository. SaulėGrid uses a monorepo approach where the package and DAGs live in the same Git repository and are versioned together — a change to the shared package and the DAGs that use it are committed and deployed atomically. The alternative — a separate package repository with independent versioning — provides better isolation but adds deployment complexity: the package must be released and installed before the DAGs that depend on new features can be deployed. SaulėGrid chose the monorepo because their team is small (3 engineers) and the deployment simplicity outweighs the isolation benefit.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: The shared package is the team's API contract with itself. Every hook, operator, and utility function in saulegrid_common is a commitment: it will be maintained, tested, and backward-compatible. Functions that are experimental or DAG-specific should stay in the DAG file until they prove their value across multiple DAGs. Promoting code to the shared package is a deliberate decision, not a reflexive one. SaulėGrid's rule: a function must be used by at least 3 DAGs before it is promoted to saulegrid_common. This prevents the shared package from becoming a dumping ground for every utility function.

This module has covered four layers of task design: custom hooks for external system interaction, custom operators for reusable task logic, task groups for visual organization, and XCom backends for inter-task data passing. This lesson synthesizes every layer into SaulėGrid's complete task architecture: how the saulegrid_common package is structured, which components exist at each layer, how they compose into production DAGs, and how the architecture evolved from the junior-level copy-paste approach. Every design choice traces back to a concept from this module, and every component was motivated by a specific maintenance or performance problem.
The Component Inventory

rows: SFTP with naming validation and structured logging
Weather API with token rotation and rate limiting
PostgreSQL with query timing and connection pooling
Schema and quality validation with rejection routing
Residential + commercial + prosumer billing with merge
Large values to S3, small values to metadata DB
File structure and naming validation functions

blocks.content: How Components Compose

blocks.title: Component composition in a Bronze DAG
blocks.content:
dag_bronze_meters.py
       │
       │  imports
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│  MeterIngestionOperator       │  (saulegrid_common.operators)
│  ├─ task_id='ingest_meters'  │
│  ├─ source_type='meters'     │
│  └─ validation_mode='strict' │
└──────────┬───────────────────┘
           │  uses internally
           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│  SaulegridSFTPHook            │  (saulegrid_common.hooks)
│  ├─ validate_filename()      │
│  ├─ download_file()          │
│  └─ structured logging       │
└──────────┬───────────────────┘
           │  uses internally
           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│  SFTPHook (provider)          │  (airflow.providers.sftp)
│  └─ retrieve_file()          │
└──────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The DAG file composes high-level components. Each component delegates to the layer below. A bug fix at any layer propagates to every DAG that uses it.

visual-recommendation.description: Layered component diagram showing the composition hierarchy from DAG file through custom operator to custom hook to provider hook, with arrows indicating delegation.

blocks.content: The architecture's value is in how the components compose.
