A Bronze DAG file is under 20 lines: it imports MeterIngestionOperator from saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion, instantiates it with source-specific parameters, and declares the Dataset outlet. The operator internally uses SaulegridSFTPHook for downloading and the file validator for structure checks. A Silver DAG file uses the silver_processing_group task group, which internally uses FileValidationOperator for quality gates. The XCom backend operates transparently underneath all of it. The DAG file is a composition specification — it declares what should happen and with what parameters. The how is encapsulated in the shared components.

# A production Bronze DAG — 18 lines because the complexity
# is encapsulated in the shared package
from airflow.decorators import dag
from airflow.datasets import Dataset
from pendulum import datetime
from saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion import MeterIngestionOperator
from saulegrid_common.datasets import bronze_meters

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    schedule='0 0 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_bronze_meters():
    MeterIngestionOperator(
        task_id='ingest_meters',
        source_type='meters',
        sftp_conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_meters',
        min_row_count=100_000,
        validation_mode='strict',
        outlets=[bronze_meters],
    )

dag_bronze_meters()

Compare this to the junior-level equivalent: 60+ lines of inline download logic, validation logic, staging logic, and error handling. The mid-level DAG is shorter not because it does less, but because the work is encapsulated in tested, reusable components. The MeterIngestionOperator has 90 lines of code and 14 unit tests. The SaulegridSFTPHook has 75 lines and 8 tests. The file validator has 50 lines and 12 tests. The total code is more than the junior-level inline version — but it is tested, documented, and shared across 8 DAGs instead of duplicated 8 times.
The Migration Journey

SaulėGrid migrated from the junior-level architecture over four sprints. Sprint 1 extracted the SFTP hook and the meter ingestion operator from dag_bronze_meters, wrote tests, and converted the meters DAG to use the new components — keeping all other DAGs unchanged. Sprint 2 generalized the ingestion operator to handle generation and battery data via the source_type parameter, converted 5 more Bronze DAGs, and extracted the file validator. Sprint 3 introduced task groups for the Silver DAGs and the shared datasets.py registry. Sprint 4 deployed the S3 XCom backend and migrated the remaining DAGs. Each sprint delivered working DAGs to production, so the migration was incremental rather than a risky big-bang rewrite.
Testing the Architecture

The shared package has three layers of tests. Unit tests verify individual components in isolation — each hook, operator, and validator has its own test module with mocked external dependencies. Integration tests verify that components compose correctly — a test that instantiates MeterIngestionOperator with SaulegridSFTPHook and verifies the end-to-end download-validate-stage workflow against a mock SFTP server. DAG integrity tests verify that every DAG file imports correctly, has no circular dependencies, and uses only registered operators and connections. The test suite runs in under 90 seconds in CI and catches the majority of issues before deployment.

SaulėGrid tracks test coverage as a quality metric: the saulegrid_common package maintains 85% line coverage, with 100% coverage on the hook and operator execute() methods. The coverage target is not arbitrary — it reflects the team's experience that untested code in hooks and operators is the primary source of production incidents, while untested code in utility functions is rarely the root cause of outages. The coverage requirement is enforced by CI: a PR that reduces coverage below 85% is flagged for review.
Maintenance Impact

The architecture's return on investment is measured in maintenance metrics. Before the migration, SaulėGrid tracked 14 copy-paste consistency bugs in a quarter — bugs where a fix was applied to some DAGs but not others. After the migration, that number dropped to zero because shared logic exists in one place. DAG file changes per sprint dropped from 35 to 12 because most changes are now in the shared package rather than in individual DAG files. Code review time per DAG decreased because reviewers only need to verify the composition (correct parameters, correct dependencies) rather than the implementation (which is tested separately in the package's test suite).

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: Custom hooks encapsulate how to interact with external systems, custom operators encapsulate what a task does, task groups organize tasks visually, and XCom backends manage data passing — together, they transform SaulėGrid's DAGs from collections of duplicated functions into compositions of tested, reusable components where a bug fix in one place fixes every DAG that uses the component.

chapters.title: Pipeline Design Patterns
chapters.description: Master production pipeline design patterns including idempotency, incremental processing, fault tolerance, SLA management, dead-letter handling, and the graceful failure recovery strategies that keep SaulėGrid's orchestration reliable.

blocks.content: On February 17th, SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters failed at 00:22 due to a transient SFTP timeout. The on-call engineer cleared the failed task at 00:35 and let it retry. The task succeeded on retry — it downloaded the meter file and wrote 1.2 million rows to the Bronze layer. The next morning, the billing team reported that regional consumption totals were exactly double the expected values. The retry had appended 1.2 million rows to the Bronze layer without removing the 600,000 rows that the first attempt had already written before the timeout. The pipeline was correct in the sense that it performed the write operation. It was catastrophically wrong in the sense that running it twice produced a different result than running it once.
What Idempotency Means

An operation is idempotent if performing it multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. For a data pipeline, this means: running the same DAG for the same data interval — whether once, twice, or ten times — must produce identical output. No duplicate rows, no missing rows, no accumulated side effects. Idempotency is not a performance optimization or a best practice that can be deferred. It is the foundational requirement that makes retries safe, backfills possible, and manual reruns trustworthy. Without idempotency, every pipeline failure becomes a manual investigation: did the retry corrupt the data, or is the output correct?

In the junior-level track, pipelines were written to run once successfully. Retries were configured (retries=3, retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5)), but the task logic assumed that each execution was a fresh start. At mid-level scale — 12 DAGs, 1.2 million meters, 3 consuming teams relying on the output — the assumption of single execution is dangerous. Tasks fail for transient reasons (network timeouts, temporary disk full, external service hiccups) and are retried automatically. DAGs are backfilled when historical data needs reprocessing. Engineers manually clear and rerun tasks during incident response. Every one of these scenarios executes the task logic more than once for the same data interval.
The February 17th Failure Analysis

blocks.title: Non-idempotent write: first attempt vs retry
blocks.content:
First attempt (partial success before timeout):

SFTP Server ──── download ────▶ /tmp/meters.csv (1.2M rows)
                                      │
                                      ▼
                               Bronze Layer (append mode)
                               ├── 600K rows written
                               └── TIMEOUT at row 600,001
                                   Task fails ✗

Retry (runs the full task again):

SFTP Server ──── download ────▶ /tmp/meters.csv (1.2M rows)
                                      │
                                      ▼
                               Bronze Layer (append mode)
                               ├── 600K rows (from first attempt)
                               └── 1.2M rows (from retry)
                                   Total: 1.8M rows ✗✗
                                   Expected: 1.2M rows
blocks.caption: Append mode makes retries unsafe: each execution adds rows without removing the partial results from previous attempts. The Bronze layer accumulates duplicates silently.

visual-recommendation.description: Side-by-side diagram showing the non-idempotent append creating 1.8M rows after retry versus the idempotent overwrite producing exactly 1.2M rows regardless of retry count.

blocks.content: The root cause was a single design decision: the Bronze write used append mode. The task opened the output file in append mode and wrote rows as they were downloaded from the SFTP server, streaming data to disk as it arrived over the network. When the task failed after writing 600,000 rows due to the SFTP timeout, those 600,000 rows persisted in the Bronze layer because append mode does not clean up partial writes on failure. When the retry ran the task from the beginning, it appended another 1.2 million rows on top of the existing partial write, producing 1.8 million rows where 1.2 million were expected. The fix was straightforward — switch from append to overwrite — but the incident exposed a systemic vulnerability across all of SaulėGrid's DAGs.
The Three Enemies of Idempotency

rows: Each execution adds rows to the output without clearing previous results
Bronze CSV writer in append mode: open(path, 'a')
Overwrite mode: write to a deterministic path and replace
Each execution writes to a different location (timestamp-based paths)
staging/meters_{timestamp}.csv creates a new file per retry
Date-based paths: staging/meters/{ds}.csv always overwrites the same file
Each execution triggers an external action (email, API call) that cannot be undone
on_success_callback sends a Slack notification — retry sends a duplicate notification
Guard notifications with a check: only send if not already sent for this DAG Run

blocks.content: The Idempotency Test

Every task should pass the idempotency test: if you run the task twice for the same execution date, does the output match the output of running it once? This is not a theoretical exercise — it is a literal test that SaulėGrid runs as part of their integration test suite (Module 6). The test executes the task once, records the output, executes the task again for the same date, records the output, and asserts that both outputs are identical. Tasks that fail this test are flagged as non-idempotent and cannot be deployed to production until the write pattern is corrected.

# SaulėGrid's idempotency test pattern
# Run as part of the integration test suite (Module 6)

def test_bronze_meters_idempotent(test_dag, test_date):
    """Verify that running the Bronze meters task twice
    produces the same result as running it once."""
    
    # First execution
    run_task(test_dag, 'ingest_meters', test_date)
    output_first = read_bronze_output('meters', test_date)
    
    # Second execution (same date — simulates a retry)
    run_task(test_dag, 'ingest_meters', test_date)
    output_second = read_bronze_output('meters', test_date)
    
    # The outputs must be identical
    assert output_first.row_count == output_second.row_count, (
        f'Row count mismatch: first={output_first.row_count}, '
        f'second={output_second.row_count}. Task is not idempotent.'
    )
    assert output_first.checksum == output_second.checksum, (
        f'Data checksum mismatch after re-execution. '
        f'Task is not idempotent — output changed on retry.'
    )

The test is simple but powerful. It catches append-mode writes where the row count doubles on second execution, non-deterministic paths where the output location changes between runs, and any other pattern where re-execution produces a different result. SaulėGrid runs this test for every task that writes data — Bronze ingestion, Silver transformation, Gold aggregation, and all quality report writers. Tasks that only read data or compute metrics without writing persistent output are exempt from the idempotency test because they have no persistent side effects that would differ between executions. The test adds approximately 10 seconds per task to the CI pipeline but has prevented three duplication incidents since its introduction.

blocks.title: The foundational guarantee
blocks.content: Idempotency is not one pattern among many — it is the prerequisite for every other pattern in this module. Incremental processing assumes that reprocessing a day's data does not create duplicates. Fault tolerance assumes that retries are safe. SLA recovery assumes that clearing and rerunning a failed task produces correct results. Dead-letter handling assumes that rejected records can be reprocessed without affecting the main pipeline output. Every subsequent lesson in this module builds on the guarantee that running a task twice produces the same result as running it once.
When Idempotency Is Difficult

Some operations are inherently difficult to make idempotent. Sending emails, posting to Slack, calling external APIs that create records, and triggering downstream systems all produce side effects that cannot be 'overwritten' the way a file can. SaulėGrid handles these cases with a guard pattern: before performing the side effect, the task checks whether the effect has already been performed for this DAG Run (by querying a state table or checking the external system). If the effect already exists, the task skips it. This adds complexity but is necessary for tasks where idempotency cannot be achieved through simple overwrite semantics. The guard pattern is covered in detail in lesson 7.

The previous lesson established that idempotency is the foundational requirement for production pipelines. This lesson covers the three strategies for achieving idempotent writes in practice: overwrite, delete-then-write, and upsert. Each strategy has different trade-offs in terms of simplicity, atomicity, and compatibility with downstream consumers. SaulėGrid uses all three strategies across different layers of the pipeline: overwrite for Bronze (raw data replacement is always safe), delete-then-write for Silver (ensures clean partition state before writing), and upsert for Gold (incremental updates to aggregation tables without full recomputation).
Strategy 1: Overwrite

The overwrite strategy writes output to a deterministic path and replaces whatever was there before. The path is computed from the data interval — typically the logical date — so that every execution for the same date writes to the same location. If the task runs once, the file is written. If the task runs again (retry, backfill, manual rerun), the file is overwritten with identical content. The previous version is gone, but since the new version is identical, no data is lost. This is the simplest idempotent write strategy and the one SaulėGrid uses for all Bronze ingestion tasks.

import os
import shutil
from airflow.decorators import task

@task
def write_bronze_meters(data_date: str, source_path: str) -> dict:
    """Write meter data to Bronze layer with overwrite semantics.
    
    Idempotent: writing to the same date partition always produces
    the same result regardless of how many times the task runs.
    """
    # Deterministic path based on data date — same date = same path
    bronze_path = f'/data/bronze/meters/date={data_date}/'
    
    # Remove existing data for this date (if any from a previous run)
    if os.path.exists(bronze_path):
        shutil.rmtree(bronze_path)
    
    # Write fresh data
    os.makedirs(bronze_path, exist_ok=True)
    target_file = os.path.join(bronze_path, 'meters.csv')
    shutil.copy2(source_path, target_file)
    
    row_count = sum(1 for _ in open(target_file)) - 1  # Exclude header
    
    return {
        'bronze_path': bronze_path,
        'row_count': row_count,
        'data_date': data_date,
        'write_mode': 'overwrite',
    }

The key design element is the deterministic path: bronze/meters/date={data_date}/. The data_date comes from the DAG Run's logical date ({{ ds }} in Airflow templates), which is the same for every execution of the same DAG Run — including retries, cleared reruns, and backfills. The shutil.rmtree() call before writing ensures that partial results from a failed previous attempt are removed before the fresh write begins. This two-step pattern — delete existing, then write fresh — makes the overwrite atomic from the consumer's perspective: the output either contains the complete fresh data or does not exist at all.
Strategy 2: Delete-Then-Write

The delete-then-write strategy is a refinement of overwrite for partitioned datasets. Instead of replacing an entire directory, the task deletes only the partition being processed and writes fresh data to that partition. This is important when the output directory contains data from multiple dates and the task should only replace one date's partition. SaulėGrid's Silver layer uses this pattern: the Silver directory contains date-partitioned Parquet files for the entire history, and the daily Silver transformation task replaces only today's partition without affecting historical partitions.

from airflow.decorators import task
import os
import shutil

@task
def write_silver_meters(data_date: str, transformed_data: dict) -> dict:
    """Write transformed meters to Silver layer.
    
    Delete-then-write: removes only the target date partition
    before writing, preserving all other dates in the Silver table.
    """
    silver_base = '/data/silver/meters/'
    partition_path = os.path.join(silver_base, f'date={data_date}/')
    
    # Step 1: Delete only the target partition
    if os.path.exists(partition_path):
        shutil.rmtree(partition_path)
        print(f'Cleared existing partition: {partition_path}')
    
    # Step 2: Write fresh data to the partition
    os.makedirs(partition_path, exist_ok=True)
    output_file = os.path.join(partition_path, 'part-00000.parquet')
    write_parquet(transformed_data, output_file)
    
    return {
        'silver_path': partition_path,
        'row_count': transformed_data['row_count'],
        'data_date': data_date,
        'write_mode': 'delete-then-write',
    }

The critical difference from simple overwrite is scope: the delete operation targets only the date={data_date} partition directory, not the entire Silver table. If the Silver meters table contains 365 days of history, a retry of March 24th's transformation deletes and rewrites only the March 24th partition. The other 364 days remain untouched. This partition-scoped delete is essential for any table where downstream consumers query historical data — a full table overwrite would briefly make the table empty (between delete and write), causing concurrent queries to return no results.
Strategy 3: Upsert

The upsert strategy merges new data into an existing table using a natural key: if a row with the same key exists, it is updated; if no matching row exists, the new row is inserted. This is the most complex idempotent write strategy but is necessary for tables that accumulate data across multiple runs — like Gold aggregation tables where each day's run adds or updates rows for that day's regions without replacing the entire table. SaulėGrid's Gold billing table uses upsert on the composite key (region, data_date): each run updates or inserts billing totals for the processed date's regions without affecting other dates' data.

from airflow.decorators import task

@task
def upsert_gold_billing(data_date: str, billing_data: dict) -> dict:
    """Upsert billing aggregates into Gold table.
    
    Uses the composite key (region, data_date) for merge logic:
    - If a row exists for this region and date: UPDATE the totals
    - If no row exists: INSERT a new row
    
    Idempotent: running twice for the same date updates the same rows
    with the same values, producing an identical table state.
    """
    from saulegrid_common.hooks.warehouse_hook import SaulegridWarehouseHook
    
    hook = SaulegridWarehouseHook(postgres_conn_id='postgres_warehouse')
    
    upsert_sql = """
        INSERT INTO gold.billing (
            region, data_date, total_consumption_kw, total_generation_kw,
            net_balance_kw, prosumer_count, meter_count, updated_at
        )
        VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, NOW())
        ON CONFLICT (region, data_date)
        DO UPDATE SET
            total_consumption_kw = EXCLUDED.total_consumption_kw,
            total_generation_kw = EXCLUDED.total_generation_kw,
            net_balance_kw = EXCLUDED.net_balance_kw,
            prosumer_count = EXCLUDED.prosumer_count,
            meter_count = EXCLUDED.meter_count,
            updated_at = NOW();
    """
    
    rows_upserted = 0
    for region_data in billing_data['regions']:
        hook.run(upsert_sql, parameters=(
            region_data['region'], data_date,
            region_data['consumption_kw'], region_data['generation_kw'],
            region_data['net_balance_kw'], region_data['prosumer_count'],
            region_data['meter_count'],
        ))
        rows_upserted += 1
    
    return {
        'rows_upserted': rows_upserted,
        'data_date': data_date,
        'write_mode': 'upsert',
    }

The ON CONFLICT clause is the PostgreSQL mechanism for upsert: if a row with the same (region, data_date) already exists, the existing row is updated with the new values. If no conflict exists, a new row is inserted. Running this task twice for the same date updates the same rows with the same values — producing an identical table state. The updated_at timestamp changes on each execution, but the business data (consumption, generation, balance) remains identical. SaulėGrid accepts the timestamp difference because monitoring queries filter on data_date, not updated_at.
Choosing the Right Strategy

left.label: Overwrite / Delete-then-Write
points: Simple — delete and rewrite the entire partition or file
Brief window where partition is empty between delete and write
File-based layers (Bronze Parquet, Silver Parquet) where the full partition is recomputed
Concurrent readers may see empty partition during rewrite (usually acceptable for batch)
Bronze (overwrite), Silver (delete-then-write by date partition)

right.label: Upsert
points: Complex — requires a natural key and merge logic (ON CONFLICT or MERGE)
No empty window — rows are updated in place
Database tables (Gold, analytics) where rows are updated incrementally
Concurrent readers always see complete data (important for serving layers)
Gold billing, Gold forecast (upsert on region + date key)

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's idempotency convention by layer: Bronze uses overwrite (simplest, raw data is always replaced entirely), Silver uses delete-then-write (partition-scoped, preserves historical data while replacing the current date), Gold uses upsert (row-level merge, supports concurrent reads from the analytics team). The convention is documented in the pipeline design standards and enforced during code review. A new DAG that writes to Bronze using append mode is rejected at review.

SaulėGrid's junior-level Silver transformation task read the entire Bronze layer — all historical meter readings — applied the quality filters, and wrote the complete Silver table. This full-refresh approach was correct and simple: every Silver run produced the complete, up-to-date dataset. At 400,000 meters with three months of history, the full refresh took 8 minutes. At mid-level scale — 1.2 million meters with twelve months of history — the same approach took 2 hours and 40 minutes. The Silver task processed 1.2 billion rows to update one day's 1.2 million rows. The remaining 1.199 billion rows were read, filtered, and rewritten identically to the previous run. Incremental processing eliminates this waste by processing only the new or changed data.
Full Refresh vs Incremental

left.label: Full Refresh (Junior Pattern)
points: Entire Bronze table (1.2 billion rows, 12 months)
2 hours 40 minutes
Proportional to total data volume — grows with history
Always correct — recomputes everything from scratch
Self-healing — tomorrow's run fixes today's errors

right.label: Incremental (Mid-Level Pattern)
points: Only the new partition (1.2 million rows, 1 day)
8 minutes
Proportional to daily data volume — constant regardless of history
Correct only if the incremental logic is correct — bugs compound over time
Requires explicit backfill to fix historical errors

blocks.content: The trade-off is clear: incremental processing is dramatically faster but shifts the correctness burden from the data to the code. A full refresh is self-correcting — if today's run has a bug, tomorrow's run reprocesses everything and produces the correct output. An incremental pipeline's output is the accumulation of every daily run, so a bug in one day's processing persists until someone detects it and triggers a backfill. This is why idempotency (lesson 1) is a prerequisite for incremental processing: the backfill that fixes historical errors must be safe to run without creating duplicates.
The Data Interval as the Increment

Airflow's data interval model provides the natural boundary for incremental processing. Every DAG Run has a data_interval_start and data_interval_end that define the time window the run is responsible for. The incremental task reads only the data that falls within this interval — typically one day's worth of Bronze data — processes it, and writes it to the corresponding Silver partition. The task does not need to know about any data outside its interval. This alignment between Airflow's scheduling model and the pipeline's data scope is what makes incremental processing natural in Airflow.

from airflow.decorators import task
from datetime import datetime

@task
def transform_meters_incremental(**context) -> dict:
    """Incrementally transform only the current day's meter data.
    
    Uses the data interval to scope the read to exactly one day.
    Writes to a date-partitioned Silver path using delete-then-write.
    """
    # Extract the data interval from the Airflow context
    data_interval_start = context['data_interval_start']
    data_interval_end = context['data_interval_end']
    data_date = data_interval_start.format('YYYY-MM-DD')
    
    # Read ONLY the Bronze partition for this date
    bronze_path = f'/data/bronze/meters/date={data_date}/'
    raw_data = read_parquet(bronze_path)
    
    print(
        f'Incremental read: {raw_data.row_count:,} rows '
        f'from {data_date} (interval: {data_interval_start} to {data_interval_end})'
    )
    
    # Apply quality filters (same logic as full refresh)
    filtered = apply_quality_filters(raw_data)
    
    # Write to Silver partition (delete-then-write for idempotency)
    silver_path = f'/data/silver/meters/date={data_date}/'
    write_with_overwrite(filtered, silver_path)
    
    return {
        'data_date': data_date,
        'rows_read': raw_data.row_count,
        'rows_written': filtered.row_count,
        'rows_rejected': raw_data.row_count - filtered.row_count,
        'processing_mode': 'incremental',
    }

The task reads from bronze/meters/date={data_date}/ — exactly one partition. Whether the Bronze table contains 30 days or 3 years of history, the Silver transformation reads the same 1.2 million rows. The processing time is constant: 8 minutes today, 8 minutes next year. The Silver output is written to a date-partitioned path using delete-then-write semantics, so retries and backfills overwrite the same partition without creating duplicates. The quality filter logic is identical to the full-refresh version — the only change is the scope of the read.
Handling Late-Arriving Data

Incremental processing assumes that each day's Bronze partition contains all of that day's data. In practice, data arrives late: a meter that was offline sends its readings the next day, a correction file replaces a previous day's data, or an external API delivers yesterday's weather forecast 6 hours after the expected time. Late-arriving data creates a gap: the Silver partition for March 24th was processed at 02:00, but a correction file arriving at 14:00 means the Bronze partition for March 24th has changed. The Silver partition is now stale.

SaulėGrid handles late-arriving data with two mechanisms. First, the correction reprocessing DAG (dag_bronze_reprocess from Module 1, lesson 8) triggers a Bronze rewrite for the affected date, which updates the Bronze Dataset and cascades through the Dataset chain to re-trigger Silver and Gold for that date. Second, a nightly reconciliation task compares Bronze and Silver row counts for the last 7 days and flags any date where Silver has fewer rows than Bronze — indicating that Silver needs reprocessing. The reconciliation catch provides a safety net for cases where the correction file arrives without triggering the formal reprocessing workflow.
Watermarks for Non-Partitioned Sources

Some data sources are not date-partitioned — they arrive as a continuous stream of records with embedded timestamps. SaulėGrid's battery state-of-charge data arrives as a single file containing the latest readings from all 120 battery sites, with each record carrying a timestamp. The incremental task must determine which records are new since the last processing. A watermark — a stored timestamp representing the most recent record processed — provides this boundary. The task reads all records with timestamps after the watermark, processes them, writes the output, and advances the watermark.

from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.models import Variable

@task
def process_battery_incremental() -> dict:
    """Incrementally process battery state-of-charge data.
    
    Uses a watermark stored in Airflow Variables to track
    the timestamp of the last processed record.
    """
    # Read the watermark (last processed timestamp)
    watermark = Variable.get(
        'battery_soc_watermark', 
        default_var='2026-01-01T00:00:00'
    )
    
    # Read only records newer than the watermark
    battery_data = read_battery_file('/data/bronze/battery/latest.csv')
    new_records = [
        r for r in battery_data 
        if r['timestamp'] > watermark
    ]
    
    if not new_records:
        print(f'No new battery records since {watermark}')
        return {'new_records': 0, 'watermark': watermark}
    
    # Process and write new records
    processed = transform_battery_data(new_records)
    append_to_silver(processed, '/data/silver/battery/')  # Append is OK here
    
    # Advance the watermark to the latest processed timestamp
    new_watermark = max(r['timestamp'] for r in new_records)
    Variable.set('battery_soc_watermark', new_watermark)
    
    return {
        'new_records': len(new_records),
        'watermark': new_watermark,
        'processing_mode': 'watermark-incremental',
    }

blocks.title: Watermark idempotency caveat
blocks.content: The watermark pattern uses append writes (new records are added to the Silver battery table) rather than overwrite. This is acceptable because the watermark ensures that the same records are never processed twice under normal operation. However, if the watermark is manually reset (to reprocess historical data), the append will create duplicates. SaulėGrid's watermark-based tasks include a deduplication step: after appending, a SQL MERGE removes any duplicate records based on the natural key (site_id, timestamp). This makes the watermark pattern idempotent even when the watermark is reset.
When to Use Full Refresh Instead

Incremental processing is not always the right choice. Some transformations are inherently global — computing a percentile rank across all meters requires reading all meters, not just today's. Some datasets are small enough that the full-refresh cost is negligible — SaulėGrid's substation reference table has 200 rows and refreshes in under 1 second regardless of approach. Some pipelines need the self-healing property of full refresh more than they need the performance of incremental — a pipeline that feeds regulatory reports may prioritize correctness guarantees over processing speed. SaulėGrid's guideline: use incremental processing when the dataset grows over time and the transformation is partition-scoped. Use full refresh when the dataset is small, the transformation is global, or the correctness guarantee of recomputation is more valuable than the performance of incrementality.

In the junior-level track, you configured retries on tasks: retries=3, retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5). The task failed, waited 5 minutes, and tried again. At mid-level, retries need more sophistication. SaulėGrid's SFTP provider rate-limits clients that reconnect too frequently — 3 retries at 5-minute intervals caused the provider to block SaulėGrid's IP for 30 minutes, turning a 1-minute transient failure into a 35-minute outage. The weather API returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) and expects exponential backoff. The PostgreSQL warehouse has connection limits that make rapid retries counterproductive when the database is under load. Each external system has different failure characteristics, and the retry strategy must match.
Exponential Backoff

Exponential backoff increases the delay between retries exponentially: the first retry waits 1 minute, the second waits 2 minutes, the third waits 4 minutes, the fourth waits 8 minutes, and so on. This progressively increasing delay gives the external system time to recover between retry attempts and avoids the thundering-herd problem where multiple retrying tasks simultaneously overwhelm a recovering system. Airflow supports exponential backoff natively through the retry_exponential_backoff parameter on task definitions. When combined with max_retry_delay, the exponential growth is capped to prevent unreasonably long waits — without the cap, the tenth retry would wait over 8 hours.

from airflow.decorators import task
from datetime import timedelta

# SaulėGrid's tiered retry strategy
# Different external systems get different retry configurations

@task(
    retries=5,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=1),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
    max_retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=30),
    # Retry delays: 1min, 2min, 4min, 8min, 16min (capped at 30min)
)
def download_meters_from_sftp():
    """SFTP download with exponential backoff.
    
    The meter provider rate-limits aggressive reconnections.
    Exponential backoff avoids triggering the rate limit.
    """
    pass

@task(
    retries=8,
    retry_delay=timedelta(seconds=30),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
    max_retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=15),
    # Retry delays: 30s, 1min, 2min, 4min, 8min, 15min, 15min, 15min
)
def fetch_weather_forecast():
    """Weather API with aggressive retry for HTTP 429.
    
    The API returns 429 with a Retry-After header.
    More retries at shorter initial intervals because the API
    recovers quickly from rate limiting.
    """
    pass

@task(
    retries=3,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=2),
    retry_exponential_backoff=False,  # Fixed interval for database
    # Retry delays: 2min, 2min, 2min
)
def write_to_warehouse():
    """PostgreSQL write with fixed retry interval.
    
    Database connection failures are typically transient (connection pool
    exhaustion) and resolve within 1-2 minutes. Exponential backoff is
    unnecessary — fixed intervals are sufficient.
    """
    pass

SaulėGrid's retry configuration is not uniform across tasks. The SFTP download gets 5 retries with exponential backoff because the provider's rate limiting is cumulative — rapid retries make the problem worse. The weather API gets 8 retries with aggressive initial timing because the API recovers quickly from rate limiting and the data has a tight freshness window. The warehouse write gets 3 retries at fixed intervals because database connection issues are binary — either the pool has capacity or it does not, and waiting longer does not help faster than waiting a fixed 2 minutes.
Retry-Specific Logging

When a task retries, the on-call engineer needs to know three things: what failed, why it failed, and what the retry strategy will do about it. Default Airflow logging records the exception and the retry count, but production tasks need more context. SaulėGrid's tasks log the external system involved, the specific error code or message, the retry number and delay, and whether the failure is expected to be transient (worth retrying) or permanent (should fail immediately). This structured logging enables the monitoring dashboard to distinguish between transient retries (normal operations) and persistent failures (requires investigation).

import logging
from airflow.decorators import task
from datetime import timedelta

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@task(
    retries=5,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=1),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
    max_retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=30),
)
def download_meters(**context):
    """Download with retry-aware logging."""
    attempt = context['ti'].try_number
    max_tries = context['ti'].max_tries + 1  # max_tries is 0-indexed
    
    try:
        hook = SaulegridSFTPHook(sftp_conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_meters')
        result = hook.download_file(
            remote_path='/outbound/saulegrid_meters_{{ ds }}.csv',
            local_path='/tmp/bronze/meters/{{ ds }}.csv',
            data_date='{{ ds }}',
            file_type='meters',
        )
        logger.info(
            f'Download succeeded on attempt {attempt}/{max_tries}',
            extra={'attempt': attempt, 'status': 'success'},
        )
        return result
        
    except TimeoutError as e:
        logger.warning(
            f'SFTP timeout on attempt {attempt}/{max_tries}. '
            f'Transient failure — retry expected to succeed. '
            f'Error: {e}',
            extra={'attempt': attempt, 'status': 'transient_failure'},
        )
        raise  # Re-raise to trigger Airflow's retry mechanism
        
    except PermissionError as e:
        logger.error(
            f'SFTP permission denied on attempt {attempt}/{max_tries}. '
            f'Permanent failure — retries will not help. '
            f'Action: Check SFTP credentials in Connection sftp_saulegrid_meters. '
            f'Error: {e}',
            extra={'attempt': attempt, 'status': 'permanent_failure'},
        )
        raise  # Will retry but log indicates retries are futile
The Retry Ceiling

More retries are not always better. SaulėGrid learned this when a misconfigured SFTP connection with the wrong password triggered 5 retries with exponential backoff, consuming 31 minutes of wall time before the task finally failed. The error was permanent — no amount of retrying would fix a wrong password — but the retry logic had no way to distinguish permanent from transient failures at the configuration level. The retry ceiling principle states that the total retry duration should not exceed the task's SLA window — if the Bronze SLA is 90 minutes, the retry budget is 90 minutes minus the expected execution time, leaving a defined window for investigation if retries are exhausted.
Distinguishing Transient and Permanent Failures

rows: Network timeout, HTTP 429/503, connection pool exhaustion
Yes — the system is temporarily unavailable
Wrong credentials, file not found, schema mismatch
No — retrying will produce the same error
Disk full, memory exhaustion, external system maintenance
Maybe — depends on whether the condition resolves within the SLA window
Retry 2-3 times, then fail with escalation alert
Empty file, row count below threshold, invalid values
No — the data itself is the problem, not the system
Route to dead-letter (lesson 6), alert data team

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's tasks use a try-except pattern that catches specific exception types: TimeoutError and ConnectionError are treated as transient (retry is appropriate), ValueError and PermissionError are treated as permanent (fail fast), and generic Exception is treated as unknown (retry conservatively). This exception-type classification is imperfect — some ConnectionError instances are permanent (wrong hostname) and some ValueError instances are transient (temporary data corruption in the source file). But the classification catches the common cases correctly and the monitoring dashboard flags unusual retry patterns for investigation.
Retry Monitoring

SaulėGrid monitors retry behavior as a platform health indicator. The monitoring dashboard tracks three metrics: daily retry count across all tasks (trending upward indicates systemic instability), retry-to-success ratio (the percentage of retried tasks that eventually succeed — a ratio below 80% suggests permanent failures are consuming retry budget), and average retry duration (the wall-clock time consumed by retries — trending upward indicates that failure recovery is taking longer). These metrics are reviewed in the weekly platform operations meeting. A spike in retry count after a deployment is an early signal that the deployment introduced a regression affecting external system interaction. A sustained high retry count without a deployment trigger suggests an infrastructure or external system degradation.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: The goal of fault tolerance is not to prevent failures — it is to contain them. A well-designed retry strategy contains transient failures within the task's SLA window without human intervention. A well-designed failure classification alerts the right person when intervention is needed. The worst outcome is not a failed task — it is a task that retries silently for 30 minutes on a permanent failure, consuming worker slots and delaying the escalation that would have resolved the issue in 5 minutes.

SaulėGrid's billing team needs Gold data before 06:00 every morning. The analytics team needs Silver data before 04:00 for their daily dashboards. The VERT regulatory submission requires data by 09:00 on business days. These are Service Level Agreements — commitments about when data will be available. Without SLA monitoring, breaches are discovered when a downstream consumer complains: the billing team emails at 09:30 asking why their dashboard is empty, and the investigation reveals that Bronze failed at 00:15 and nobody was notified. SLA management transforms these implicit time expectations into explicit, monitored contracts with automated alerting when breaches occur or are at risk of occurring.
Defining SLAs in Airflow

Airflow provides an sla parameter on tasks that defines the maximum expected duration. If a task instance exceeds its SLA duration (measured from the DAG Run's logical date, not from the task's start time), Airflow calls the sla_miss_callback function. The callback receives the task instance, the DAG, and the amount by which the SLA was exceeded, allowing the callback to send targeted alerts. SaulėGrid defines SLAs on the terminal task of each layer's DAG chain — the last task in Bronze, Silver, and Gold — because the terminal task's completion time represents the moment when the layer's data is available to consumers.
SLA Measurement Semantics

A common source of confusion with Airflow's SLA mechanism is the measurement baseline. The SLA duration is measured from the DAG Run's logical date (also called the data interval start), not from the task's actual start time. For a daily DAG with a midnight logical date, an SLA of timedelta(hours=6) means the task must complete by 06:00 — regardless of when the task actually started executing. If the DAG Run is created at 00:00 but the task does not start until 01:30 (waiting for upstream Datasets), the SLA clock has already consumed 90 minutes before the task begins. This is intentional: the SLA represents a commitment to the consumer about when data will be available, not about how long the task takes to run.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from datetime import timedelta
from pendulum import datetime

def sla_alert(dag, task_list, blocking_task_list, slas, blocking_tis):
    """SLA miss callback — sends alert to the appropriate channel."""
    for sla in slas:
        severity = 'critical' if 'gold' in sla.dag_id else 'warning'
        message = (
            f'SLA BREACH [{severity.upper()}]: '
            f'{sla.dag_id}/{sla.task_id} exceeded SLA by '
            f'{sla.timedelta - sla.sla}. '
            f'Expected completion: {sla.sla} after logical date. '
            f'Action: Check task logs and upstream dependencies.'
        )
        send_alert(channel='#saulegrid-alerts', message=message, severity=severity)


@dag(
    dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
    schedule='0 0 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    sla_miss_callback=sla_alert,
    tags=['gold', 'billing', 'sla-critical'],
)
def dag_gold_billing():

    @task(sla=timedelta(hours=6))  # Must complete within 6 hours of logical date
    def compute_billing():
        """Gold billing must be ready before 06:00.
        
        Logical date is midnight (00:00), so sla=6h means
        the task must complete by 06:00.
        """
        pass

    compute_billing()

dag_gold_billing()

The sla=timedelta(hours=6) on the compute_billing task means the task must complete within 6 hours of the DAG Run's logical date. For a daily DAG with a midnight logical date, this translates to a 06:00 completion deadline. If the task finishes at 06:15, Airflow calls sla_alert with the details of the breach. The alert includes the DAG ID, the task ID, the amount of the overshoot, and a suggested action. SaulėGrid configures the callback to send critical alerts for Gold DAGs (billing team SLA) and warning alerts for Silver DAGs (analytics team SLA).
SLA Architecture at SaulėGrid

rows: On-call engineer + billing lead + team lead

blocks.content: Proactive SLA Monitoring

Airflow's built-in SLA mechanism alerts after a breach — the SLA has already been missed when the callback fires. SaulėGrid supplements this with proactive monitoring: a separate monitoring DAG that checks progress at regular intervals and predicts whether the chain will complete before the SLA deadline. The monitor runs every 30 minutes and checks two conditions: has the expected DAG Run been created (Dataset chains can fail silently if the upstream did not trigger), and is the elapsed time on track to meet the SLA? If Bronze has not completed by 01:00, the monitor fires a warning because the remaining chain (Silver + Gold) needs at least 90 minutes, leaving only 30 minutes of buffer before the Bronze 01:30 SLA.

The proactive monitor catches two failure modes that Airflow's built-in SLA mechanism misses. First, missing DAG Runs: if a Dataset chain fails silently (Bronze did not trigger Silver), there is no Silver DAG Run to breach an SLA — the absence of a run is invisible to the SLA callback. The monitor detects this by checking whether the expected DAG Run exists. Second, slow progress: if Bronze is running but taking longer than usual, the monitor can predict that the downstream chain will miss the Gold SLA and fire a warning before the breach occurs, giving the on-call engineer time to investigate and potentially intervene.
SLA Reporting

SaulėGrid tracks SLA achievement as a monthly metric: the percentage of days where every layer completed within its SLA window. The target is 99.5% — allowing roughly 1-2 breaches per month. The monthly report breaks down breaches by root cause (upstream delay, infrastructure issue, code bug, data quality), layer (Bronze, Silver, Gold), and severity (warning vs critical). This data feeds the quarterly platform retrospective where the team decides whether to adjust SLA thresholds, add capacity, or improve specific pipeline stages. A declining SLA achievement trend is an early indicator that the platform is approaching its capacity limits.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid reviews SLA performance weekly. The monitoring dashboard shows SLA achievement rate (target: 99.5% per month), average completion time by layer, and the distribution of completion times over the past 30 days. SLA thresholds are adjusted quarterly based on observed performance: if Bronze consistently completes by 00:45 and the SLA is 01:30, the buffer is healthy. If Bronze starts averaging 01:15, the buffer is eroding and the team investigates before breaches occur. SLAs are living targets that evolve with data volume and platform capacity, not fixed numbers set once and forgotten.

SaulėGrid's Silver transformation applies quality filters: meter readings with null meter_id, negative daily_kw values (except for prosumer generation), and future dates are rejected. In the junior-level implementation, rejected records were logged and discarded — a print statement recorded the rejection reason and the pipeline continued with the valid records. At mid-level scale, 20,000 records per day were being rejected, and the only evidence was buried in task logs that were rotated after 7 days. When the analytics team asked why Klaipėda region's monthly totals were consistently 3% lower than expected, the investigation required reconstructing which records had been rejected — information that no longer existed in the logs. Dead-letter patterns solve this by routing rejected records to a durable, queryable location.
What a Dead Letter Is

A dead letter is a record that failed a quality check and was diverted from the main pipeline into a separate storage location — the dead-letter table or dead-letter directory. The dead letter includes the original record, the rejection reason, the timestamp of rejection, and the pipeline stage where the rejection occurred. The name comes from postal systems: a letter that cannot be delivered is not destroyed — it is routed to a dead-letter office where it can be inspected, corrected, and potentially re-delivered. In data pipelines, dead letters serve the same purpose: rejected records are preserved for investigation, correction, and potential reprocessing.
The Dead-Letter Table

The dead-letter record contains everything needed for investigation and reprocessing: the original record (so the analyst can see the raw data), the rejection reason (so they understand why it failed), the rejection code (for automated categorization), the pipeline stage (for tracing which quality gate triggered the rejection), and the timestamp and source DAG (for audit compliance). SaulėGrid writes dead letters to a PostgreSQL table (dead_letter.meters) using upsert on the composite key (meter_id, data_date) so that reprocessing the same day's data overwrites previous dead-letter entries rather than creating duplicates.
Validation Function Design

The validation function returns structured rejection data with a code and a human-readable reason — not just a boolean pass-or-fail indicator. The rejection code enables automated categorization and trend analysis: the monitoring dashboard can show that 60% of today's rejections were NULL_METER_ID, 30% were NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER, and 10% were UNKNOWN_REGION. This distribution helps the data quality team identify root causes efficiently: a spike in NULL_METER_ID rejections suggests a provider-side file generation issue, while UNKNOWN_REGION rejections after adding a new operating region suggests a configuration update is needed in the validation function.
Monitoring Dead-Letter Volume

Dead letters are not just an error archive — they are a real-time data quality signal that provides quantitative insight into pipeline health. SaulėGrid monitors the daily rejection rate and fires alerts when it exceeds defined thresholds. A rejection rate under 2% is normal and expected because data quality issues are endemic in large-scale meter readings — occasional null IDs, sensor malfunctions producing negative readings, and timezone issues causing future dates. A rate between 2% and 5% triggers a warning for the data quality team to investigate the distribution of rejection codes. A rate above 5% triggers a critical alert because it indicates a systemic issue requiring immediate attention.

# Dead-letter monitoring query
# Run by SaulėGrid's monitoring DAG after each Silver transformation

MONITOR_QUERY = """
    SELECT 
        data_date,
        rejection_code,
        COUNT(*) as rejection_count,
        COUNT(*) * 100.0 / (
            SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver.meters WHERE date = %(data_date)s
        ) as rejection_rate_pct
    FROM dead_letter.meters
    WHERE data_date = %(data_date)s
    GROUP BY data_date, rejection_code
    ORDER BY rejection_count DESC;
"""

# Alert thresholds
WARNING_THRESHOLD = 2.0   # Percent
CRITICAL_THRESHOLD = 5.0  # Percent

The monitoring query aggregates dead letters by rejection code, providing a distribution that reveals the root cause pattern. If 80% of today's rejections are NULL_METER_ID, the provider likely sent a corrupt file with missing identifiers. If rejections are evenly distributed across codes, the issue is probably a general data quality degradation rather than a specific error. SaulėGrid's monitoring dashboard shows the rejection distribution as a daily bar chart, with historical trends that reveal whether rejection rates are stable, improving, or degrading over time. A gradual increase in UNKNOWN_REGION rejections after SaulėGrid expanded into new operating areas was the signal that the validation configuration needed updating.
Reprocessing Dead Letters

Dead letters are not permanently rejected — they are candidates for reprocessing after the root cause is fixed. If 5,000 records were rejected because of an UNKNOWN_REGION code after SaulėGrid expanded into a new operating region, the fix is to add the new region to the validation rules and reprocess the affected dates. SaulėGrid's reprocessing workflow reads dead letters for the specified date range, applies the updated validation logic, and routes newly-valid records back into the Silver pipeline. Records that still fail validation remain in the dead-letter table with an updated rejected_at timestamp. The dead-letter table's upsert semantics make this reprocessing idempotent.

blocks.title: Dead-letter table maintenance
blocks.content: The dead-letter table grows with every DAG Run that produces rejections. SaulėGrid's maintenance policy: dead letters older than 90 days are archived to cold storage (S3 Glacier) and removed from the active table. The 90-day window covers the quarterly regulatory audit period during which VERT may request data lineage documentation including rejection records. Dead letters younger than 90 days remain in the active table for investigation and reprocessing. The archival is automated as a monthly maintenance task.

Retries handle transient failures automatically. SLA monitoring detects when pipelines are running late. Dead letters preserve records that fail quality checks. But some failures require human intervention — a task exhausts its retries, a DAG fails on permanent errors, or an upstream system is down for an extended period. Graceful failure recovery is the set of patterns that make human intervention efficient, safe, and predictable. The goal is not to prevent the need for intervention — it is to ensure that when intervention is needed, the engineer knows exactly what to do, the actions are safe to perform, and the pipeline returns to a consistent state.
Clearing and Rerunning Failed Tasks

The most common recovery action is clearing a failed task — resetting its state so the scheduler reruns it. Airflow provides the 'Clear' action in the UI and the airflow tasks clear CLI command. When you clear a task, Airflow resets its state to 'none' and the scheduler picks it up for re-execution on the next scheduling loop. Because all SaulėGrid tasks are idempotent (lesson 1), clearing is always safe: the rerun produces the same output as a fresh run. Without idempotency, clearing a task that had partially written data could create duplicates or corrupted output.

# Clear a single failed task for a specific date
airflow tasks clear dag_bronze_meters \
  --start-date 2026-03-24 \
  --end-date 2026-03-24 \
  --task-regex ingest_meters \
  --yes

# Clear all failed tasks in a DAG for a date range (backfill recovery)
airflow tasks clear dag_silver_meters \
  --start-date 2026-03-20 \
  --end-date 2026-03-24 \
  --only-failed \
  --yes

# Clear a task and its downstream dependencies
# (useful when the failed task's output feeds downstream tasks)
airflow tasks clear dag_bronze_meters \
  --start-date 2026-03-24 \
  --end-date 2026-03-24 \
  --task-regex ingest_meters \
  --downstream \
  --yes

The --downstream flag is critical for chain recovery. If Bronze fails and is cleared, its output Dataset triggers Silver, and Silver's output triggers Gold — the entire chain reruns from the point of failure. Without --downstream, only the cleared task reruns, but downstream tasks that already ran (perhaps on stale data) are not re-triggered. SaulėGrid's recovery procedure always uses --downstream for chain failures because a failure at any point in the chain means all downstream outputs are suspect and should be recomputed from the corrected upstream data.
The Monday Morning Recovery Pattern

SaulėGrid's meter data provider has a known maintenance window every Saturday night. The SFTP server is unreliable from 22:00 Saturday to 06:00 Sunday. The Bronze DAG fails on Saturday night, retries exhaust on Sunday morning, and by Monday morning, three days of data have accumulated on the SFTP server — Saturday's, Sunday's, and Monday's files. The on-call engineer's recovery procedure is documented in a runbook: clear the failed Saturday DAG Run, trigger manual runs for Sunday and Monday, and verify that the Dataset chain cascades correctly through Silver and Gold for all three days.

# SaulėGrid's Monday morning recovery procedure
# Run from the Airflow CLI or scripted in a recovery DAG

# Step 1: Clear the failed Saturday run and downstream chain
airflow tasks clear dag_bronze_meters \
  --start-date 2026-03-21 \
  --end-date 2026-03-21 \
  --downstream \
  --yes

# Step 2: Trigger Sunday's run (was never created because Bronze failed Saturday)
airflow dags trigger dag_bronze_meters \
  --exec-date 2026-03-22 \
  --conf '{"reason": "Monday recovery — Sunday data"}'

# Step 3: Trigger Monday's run
airflow dags trigger dag_bronze_meters \
  --exec-date 2026-03-23 \
  --conf '{"reason": "Monday recovery — Monday data"}'

# Step 4: Verify all three days cascade through Silver and Gold
# (Monitor the Datasets tab — all three dates should trigger Silver)
Backfill Recovery

When a pipeline bug is discovered that affected multiple days of output, the fix requires a backfill: reprocessing the affected date range with the corrected logic. Airflow's backfill command creates DAG Runs for a date range, executing them sequentially or in parallel. SaulėGrid's backfill procedure is three steps: deploy the fix to the DAG code, run the backfill for the affected date range, and verify the output by comparing row counts and checksums against expected values. The idempotent write patterns from lesson 2 make backfills safe: each date's output is overwritten with the corrected version, and downstream consumers see the corrected data on their next read.

# Backfill a date range after deploying a bug fix
airflow dags backfill dag_silver_meters \
  --start-date 2026-03-01 \
  --end-date 2026-03-24 \
  --reset-dagruns \
  --yes

# Note: --reset-dagruns clears existing DAG Runs before creating new ones
# This ensures the backfill overwrites any previous (buggy) output
The Guard Pattern for Side Effects

Some tasks have side effects that cannot be overwritten: sending emails, posting Slack messages, calling billing APIs, and submitting regulatory reports. These tasks need a guard that checks whether the side effect has already been performed for this DAG Run before performing it again. SaulėGrid implements guards using a state table: before sending a Slack alert, the task queries the state table for an entry matching (dag_id, task_id, data_date, side_effect_type). If an entry exists, the side effect is skipped. If not, the side effect is performed and the entry is created. This makes side-effect tasks idempotent without preventing the side effect from occurring on the first execution.

The guard pattern introduces a state table dependency: the task must be able to read and write guard records. SaulėGrid uses a dedicated PostgreSQL table (pipeline_state.side_effect_guards) with columns for guard_key, created_at, dag_id, task_id, and data_date. The guard check is a simple SELECT query, and the guard record is a simple INSERT. If the state table is unavailable (database connection failure), the task fails rather than risking a duplicate side effect — this is the conservative default. The guard table is small (one row per side effect per date) and is included in the weekly database maintenance that cleans records older than 90 days.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's recovery decision framework: if the failure is transient and retries are exhausted, clear and rerun. If the failure is caused by missing upstream data (weekend outage), trigger manual runs for the missing dates. If the failure is caused by a code bug, deploy the fix and backfill the affected range. If the failure is in a side-effect task, check the guard table before manual intervention to avoid duplicate notifications. The framework is documented in the on-call runbook and the first thing the on-call engineer consults when paged.

SaulėGrid's pipeline has three layers — Bronze, Silver, Gold — each processed by separate DAGs. When all three succeed for the same date, the pipeline is consistent: Gold reflects the same data that Bronze ingested. When a failure occurs partway through the chain — Bronze succeeds but Silver fails, or Silver succeeds for meters but fails for generation — the pipeline is in an inconsistent state. Gold may serve data from yesterday's Silver run while Bronze has today's data. The billing team sees aggregates that do not match the detail-level data in Silver. This lesson covers the patterns that detect, prevent, and resolve cross-DAG inconsistencies.
The Inconsistency Window

Every multi-DAG pipeline has an inherent inconsistency window: the period between when the first DAG in the chain completes and the last DAG completes. During this window, different layers contain data from different processing dates. Bronze has today's fresh data, Silver is actively processing today's data, and Gold still contains yesterday's aggregates. In the normal case, this window lasts only minutes and is invisible to consumers because they query Gold at fixed times well after the chain completes. The window becomes a critical problem when a failure extends it from minutes to hours or days — the layers remain out of sync long enough that downstream consumers notice stale or inconsistent data.

blocks.title: Consistency states in the Bronze-Silver-Gold chain
blocks.content:
Normal flow (consistent within minutes):

00:00   00:20   00:45   01:05
  │       │       │       │
  ▼       ▼       ▼       ▼
[Bronze] ─▶ [Silver] ─▶ [Gold]
  ✓ done    ✓ done    ✓ done

  Inconsistency window: 00:00 to 01:05 (65 minutes)
  Consumers query Gold after 06:00 — well after consistency

Failure case (extended inconsistency):

00:00   00:20        ?:??      ?:??
  │       │           │         │
  ▼       ▼           ▼         ▼
[Bronze] ─▶ [Silver] ─✗ FAIL   [Gold]
  ✓ done    ✗ failed            stale (yesterday's data)

  Bronze has today's data
  Silver has partial/no today's data
  Gold has yesterday's data
  Consumers see stale Gold data — may not realize it's stale
blocks.caption: The inconsistency window is invisible during normal operation but becomes a data quality risk when failures extend it beyond the consumer's query time.

visual-recommendation.description: Timeline diagram showing the normal brief inconsistency window versus the extended window during a Silver failure, with consumer query time marked to show when staleness becomes visible.

blocks.content: Detecting Inconsistency

SaulėGrid detects cross-DAG inconsistency with a freshness comparison query that checks the latest data date present in each pipeline layer. For each processing date, the query compares the most recent timestamp or partition date in Bronze, Silver, and Gold. If Bronze shows March 24th data but Gold still shows March 23rd data, the pipeline is inconsistent for March 24th — Gold has not yet processed the data that Bronze has already ingested. The freshness check runs as part of the SLA monitoring DAG and fires alerts when any layer's freshness lags behind the layer above it by more than one processing cycle, indicating a stalled or failed intermediate pipeline.

The consistency check compares the latest data date across all three layers. In normal operation, all three layers show the same date (today). When Bronze has March 24th data but Silver has March 23rd, the check fires a warning indicating a one-day gap in the Bronze-to-Silver link. When the gap exceeds one day, the severity escalates to critical because multi-day inconsistency suggests an unresolved failure rather than a transient delay. SaulėGrid runs this check hourly during the processing window (00:00 to 06:00) and every 4 hours during the rest of the day, providing continuous visibility into the pipeline's consistency state.
Preventing Partial Chain Execution

Dataset scheduling (Module 1) naturally prevents the most common inconsistency: Gold does not trigger until both Silver meters and Silver generation are complete. If Silver meters succeeds but Silver generation fails, Gold does not run. However, this protection only works when the DAG chain uses Dataset scheduling for all inter-layer dependencies. If any link in the chain uses cron scheduling (time-based) instead of Dataset scheduling (data-based), the downstream DAG runs on schedule regardless of whether the upstream completed — which is exactly the inconsistency scenario that Dataset scheduling was designed to prevent.

SaulėGrid's architecture review checklist includes a consistency verification step that confirms every inter-layer dependency uses Dataset scheduling rather than cron-based time assumptions. This verification is performed during the quarterly architecture review and whenever a new DAG is added to the platform. The only permitted exception to the Dataset requirement is the chain entry point — the Bronze ingestion DAGs that download from external systems on fixed cron schedules because the external system's data availability is time-based rather than event-based. From Bronze onward, every dependency is expressed as a Dataset relationship, making it structurally impossible for a downstream DAG to process data before its upstream dependency has completed successfully.
Partial Chain Completion

The most operationally confusing inconsistency occurs when parallel Silver DAGs have different completion states. SaulėGrid's Silver layer processes meters and generation as separate DAGs triggered by separate Bronze Datasets. If Bronze meters succeeds but Bronze generation fails, Silver meters triggers and completes while Silver generation does not. Gold billing, which requires both Silver Datasets, does not trigger. The pipeline is in a partial state: one Silver domain is fresh, another is stale, and Gold is waiting. The Airflow UI shows Silver meters as green (success) and Gold as grey (no run), but the root cause is a Bronze generation failure — two layers removed from the visible symptom. The freshness monitoring DAG detects this by checking all layers independently, not just the terminal layer.
Recovery from Inconsistency

When inconsistency is detected, the recovery procedure depends on which layer failed. If Silver failed, clearing Silver and triggering a rerun for the affected date restores consistency — Silver reprocesses Bronze data, produces the Silver Dataset, and Gold triggers automatically. If Bronze failed for one source but succeeded for another (meters succeeded, generation failed), the recovery is more complex: Bronze generation must be rerun, then Silver must reprocess both meters and generation for that date, then Gold must rerun. The --downstream flag on the clear command propagates the rerun through the entire chain.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Cross-DAG consistency is not a feature to add — it is a property to preserve. The Dataset scheduling model provides consistency by default: downstream DAGs run only when upstream data is ready. Every deviation from Dataset scheduling (cron dependencies, manual triggers, sensor-based chains) creates a potential consistency gap. SaulėGrid's architecture treats Dataset scheduling as the consistency mechanism and the freshness monitoring DAG as the verification layer that detects any gaps the scheduling model did not prevent.

SaulėGrid's data engineering team deploys DAG changes weekly. Some changes are simple — adjusting a quality threshold, adding a new tag, updating a connection parameter. Others are structural — adding a new task to a DAG, changing the output schema, or modifying the transformation logic that affects downstream consumers. Every deployment carries risk: the new code may introduce a bug, the schema change may break downstream queries, or the deployment may coincide with a running DAG Run that was created under the old code. Pipeline versioning is the set of practices that make DAG evolution safe, traceable, and reversible.
The Safe Deployment Window

SaulėGrid's pipeline has a natural quiet period: between Gold completion (typically 01:30) and the next Bronze start (00:00 the next day). During this window, no DAG Runs are in progress and no data is being written. Deploying during this window eliminates the risk of a code change affecting a running task — the new code takes effect before the next processing cycle begins. Deploying outside this window risks a race condition: a running Silver task may execute under the old code while the scheduler has already loaded the new DAG definition, causing state inconsistencies.

SaulėGrid's deployment schedule targets 10:00-14:00 on weekdays — well within the quiet period. Emergency deployments outside this window require team lead approval and must follow the hotfix procedure: deploy the fix, verify that no DAG Runs are currently in progress, and manually trigger a test run to verify the fix before the next automatic cycle. The deployment window is documented in the team's operational standards and enforced by the CI/CD pipeline (Module 7), which gates production deployments to the approved window unless an emergency override is applied.
DAG Versioning Strategies

When a DAG change is significant enough that old and new versions cannot coexist, SaulėGrid uses a versioned DAG ID strategy: the new version is deployed as a separate DAG (dag_silver_meters_v2) alongside the original (dag_silver_meters). Both DAGs run in parallel for a validation period — typically one week — during which the team compares outputs to verify that the new version produces identical or improved results. After validation, the old DAG is paused and eventually removed. This blue-green deployment approach eliminates the risk of a bad deployment affecting production immediately, at the cost of temporarily doubling the DAG count and compute resources for the affected pipeline.
Schema Change Management

The most dangerous pipeline change is a schema modification — adding, removing, or renaming columns in the output of a pipeline stage. Schema changes propagate downstream: if Silver adds a column, Gold's transformation must handle the new column, and the analytics team's queries must not break on the schema difference. SaulėGrid manages schema changes with a backward-compatibility requirement: new columns are added as nullable (so old code that does not reference the column still works), existing columns are never removed (they are deprecated with a null fill), and column renames are implemented as add-new-then-deprecate-old over two deployment cycles.

# Schema change: adding a new column to Silver meters
# Phase 1 (this deployment): add the column with null default
# Phase 2 (next deployment): populate the column, update downstream

# Phase 1: Backward-compatible addition
def transform_meters_v2(raw_data):
    """Silver transformation with new net_balance_kw column.
    
    Version 2 adds net_balance_kw for prosumer net metering.
    Column is nullable — downstream queries that don't reference
    it continue to work without modification.
    """
    transformed = apply_quality_filters(raw_data)
    
    # New column: net_balance_kw = daily_kw (consumption) - generation_kw
    for record in transformed:
        if record['customer_type'] == 'PROSUMER':
            record['net_balance_kw'] = (
                record['daily_kw'] - record.get('generation_kw', 0)
            )
        else:
            record['net_balance_kw'] = None  # Non-prosumers: null
    
    return transformed

# DAG changelog (committed alongside the code change)
# 2026-03-24: Added net_balance_kw to Silver meters schema
#   - Nullable column, null for non-prosumer records
#   - Backward compatible: no downstream changes required
#   - Phase 2 (2026-04-01): Gold billing will use net_balance_kw

The two-phase deployment separates the schema change from the logic change. Phase 1 adds the column with null values, which is backward-compatible — every existing query continues to work because null columns are invisible to queries that do not reference them. Phase 2, deployed in the next cycle after verifying that the column is being populated correctly, updates the downstream Gold transformation to use the new column. If Phase 1 introduces a bug, the rollback removes only the new column logic without affecting any downstream code. If Phase 2 introduces a bug, the rollback reverts the Gold logic to the previous version that does not reference the column.
DAG Changelog Convention

Every DAG file in SaulėGrid's repository includes a changelog comment at the top of the file, documenting every significant change: new tasks added, schema changes, scheduling changes, and dependency changes. The changelog is not a substitute for Git history — it is a human-readable summary that the on-call engineer can read in 10 seconds to understand what changed recently. When a DAG starts failing after a deployment, the first diagnostic step is reading the changelog for recent changes. SaulėGrid's changelog convention uses the format: date, author, change description, backward compatibility assessment, and rollback plan.

# SaulėGrid DAG changelog convention
# Top of every DAG file, below imports

"""
CHANGELOG:
2026-03-24 (Ieva): Added net_balance_kw column to Silver output
  - Backward compatible: nullable column, null for non-prosumers
  - Rollback: Remove net_balance_kw computation, redeploy
  
2026-03-17 (Matas): Switched scheduling from cron to Dataset
  - Breaking: Silver no longer runs at fixed 02:00
  - Rollback: Revert schedule parameter to '0 2 * * *'

2026-03-10 (Ieva): Added dead-letter routing for rejected records
  - Backward compatible: valid records unaffected
  - Rollback: Remove dead-letter logic, rejected records logged only
"""
Rollback Procedures

Every deployment must have a documented rollback plan. For code changes, the rollback is a Git revert of the deployment commit followed by a redeployment. For schema changes, the rollback depends on the phase: Phase 1 (column addition) is rolled back by removing the column computation and reprocessing the affected dates. Phase 2 (downstream adoption) is rolled back by reverting the downstream code. For scheduling changes, the rollback is reverting the schedule parameter. SaulėGrid tests the rollback plan quarterly by performing a simulated rollback in the staging environment and verifying that the pipeline returns to a consistent state.

SaulėGrid tests their rollback procedures quarterly by performing a simulated rollback in the staging environment. The test deploys a code change to staging, verifies it works, then rolls back to the previous version and verifies that the pipeline returns to the pre-change state. This rehearsal ensures that the rollback documentation is accurate, that the team remembers the procedure, and that no environmental changes (new dependencies, configuration drift) have made the rollback path invalid since the last test. The quarterly rollback test has caught documentation errors twice — once when a configuration file was renamed and the rollback instructions referenced the old name, and once when a new dependency was not included in the rollback's requirements.txt.

blocks.title: Irreversible changes
blocks.content: Some changes are difficult or impossible to roll back: removing a column from the output schema (downstream consumers that reference the column will break), changing a partition key (all historical data is organized under the old key), and modifying a Dataset URI (all consumers referencing the old URI lose their scheduling trigger). SaulėGrid classifies changes as reversible or irreversible during the design review. Irreversible changes require additional approval, a longer staging period, and a communication plan for downstream consumers before deployment.

This module has covered seven production pipeline patterns: idempotency guarantees that retries are safe, overwrite-delete-upsert strategies that make writes repeatable, incremental processing that keeps execution time constant, fault tolerance that matches retry strategies to failure types, SLA management that detects breaches before consumers notice, dead-letter routing that preserves rejected records for investigation, and graceful recovery procedures that restore consistency after failures. This lesson synthesizes every pattern into SaulėGrid's pipeline design standards — the document that every new DAG is reviewed against before deployment.
The Pipeline Design Standards

rows: Every write task must produce identical output when run multiple times for the same data interval
Idempotency integration test in CI (run twice, compare outputs)
Bronze: overwrite. Silver: delete-then-write by date partition. Gold: upsert on natural key.
Code review checks write mode against layer convention
Silver and Gold tasks must read only the current data interval, not the full table
Execution plan review verifying partition-scoped reads
Every external-system task must have retry parameters matching the system's failure profile
Code review checks retry config against the system profile table
Every production DAG must have an SLA defined on its terminal task
DAG integrity test asserts sla parameter is set
Every quality gate must route rejected records to a dead-letter table with rejection codes
Unit test verifies rejection routing for known-bad records
Every production DAG must have a recovery section in the operations runbook
Deployment checklist verifies runbook entry exists
Every inter-layer dependency must use Dataset scheduling
Architecture review verifies no cron-based inter-layer dependencies
