New columns must be nullable. Existing columns must not be removed or renamed without a two-phase plan.
Code review checks schema changes against compatibility rules

blocks.content: The Standards in Practice

The standards document is not aspirational — it is enforced. SaulėGrid's deployment checklist requires that every new DAG or DAG modification is reviewed against each standard before promotion to production. The checklist is a pull request template: the developer checks each box, the reviewer verifies each claim, and the PR cannot merge until all boxes are checked. For automated standards (idempotency tests, SLA assertions, dead-letter routing tests), the CI pipeline runs the verification. For design standards (write strategy, retry configuration, schema compatibility), the code reviewer checks manually using the standard's verification column as a guide.

# SaulėGrid PR checklist for DAG changes (template)

## Pipeline Design Standards Review

- [ ] **Idempotency**: All write tasks produce identical output on re-execution
      CI evidence: link to idempotency test results
- [ ] **Write strategy**: Write mode matches layer convention (Bronze=overwrite,
      Silver=delete-then-write, Gold=upsert)
- [ ] **Incremental scope**: Tasks read only the current data interval
- [ ] **Retry config**: External-system tasks have appropriate retry parameters
      System: _______ Retries: ___ Backoff: ___
- [ ] **SLA defined**: Terminal task has sla parameter set
- [ ] **Dead letters**: Quality gates route rejections to dead-letter table
- [ ] **Recovery runbook**: Runbook entry exists for this DAG
- [ ] **Dataset scheduling**: All inter-layer dependencies use Datasets
- [ ] **Schema compatibility**: Schema changes are backward-compatible

## Deployment Plan
- Deployment window: _______ (must be 10:00-14:00 weekday)
- Rollback plan: _______
- Downstream impact: _______

The PR checklist template is enforced by GitHub's required reviewers configuration: a PR that modifies DAG files cannot merge without at least one approved review from a team member who has verified each checklist item. The reviewer does not trust the developer's self-assessment — they independently verify the idempotency test results, inspect the write mode in the code, confirm the SLA parameter exists, and check the operations runbook for the corresponding entry. This dual-verification process adds 15-20 minutes to each review but has prevented 100% of the standards violations that previously reached production.
How the Standards Evolved

Every standard in the document traces to a production incident. The idempotency requirement was added after the February 17th duplication incident (lesson 1). The SLA definition requirement was added after the billing team's 09:30 complaint that exposed a silent failure. The dead-letter requirement was added after the Klaipėda analytics investigation that required reconstructing rejected records from expired logs. The schema compatibility rules were added after a column rename broke the analytics team's dashboards without warning. The standards document is a living record of lessons learned — each entry includes a link to the incident post-mortem that motivated it.

The standards are reviewed quarterly at the platform operations retrospective. The review checks three things: are the standards still relevant (have any patterns been replaced by better approaches), are they being followed (review a sample of recent PRs against the checklist), and are they complete (have any new incident types occurred that the standards do not cover). SaulėGrid added the cross-DAG consistency standard after the quarterly review in Q1 2026, when the freshness monitoring DAG caught two inconsistency incidents that the existing standards did not address.
Enforcing Standards Automatically

Where possible, SaulėGrid automates standards enforcement through CI pipeline checks rather than relying solely on code review. The DAG integrity test suite (Module 6) includes automated checks for SLA parameters on terminal tasks, Dataset scheduling on inter-layer dependencies, and retry configuration on external-system tasks. These automated checks catch approximately 70% of standards violations before the reviewer sees the PR. The remaining 30% — write strategy appropriateness, schema compatibility assessment, and recovery runbook quality — require human judgment and are verified during the manual code review. The balance between automated and manual enforcement is reviewed quarterly alongside the standards themselves.
The Standards as Team Culture

The pipeline design standards serve a purpose beyond technical quality — they establish a shared vocabulary and a shared set of expectations within the team. When a developer says 'this task is idempotent,' every team member understands the specific guarantee: re-execution for the same data interval produces identical output. When a reviewer says 'the retry configuration does not match the system profile,' the developer knows exactly what to fix. The standards compress the team's collective experience into a reference that new team members can absorb in their first week, rather than learning each pattern through their own production incidents over months.

The pipeline design standards represent the team's collective answer to the question: what does a production-ready DAG look like? Before the standards existed, this answer was implicit — encoded in individual engineers' experience and applied inconsistently. After the standards, the answer is explicit, documented, and enforceable. A junior engineer joining the team can read the standards document, review the referenced lesson for each standard, and understand the full set of production requirements within their first week. The standards compress months of operational learning into a reference that is accessible to anyone who needs it.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: Production pipelines are not pipelines that work — they are pipelines that work repeatedly (idempotency), work on the right data (incrementality), recover from failures (fault tolerance and retries), meet their deadlines (SLA management), preserve evidence of problems (dead letters), return to consistency after failures (recovery patterns), and evolve safely over time (versioning) — and SaulėGrid's pipeline design standards codify every one of these requirements into a checklist that every DAG must pass before reaching production.

chapters.title: Executors and Runtime Architecture
chapters.description: Understand how Airflow executes tasks at runtime, master the executor models (Local, Celery, Kubernetes), learn pools and queues for resource management, and build the judgment for selecting the right executor for a given scale and workload.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, you defined DAGs and tasks, and Airflow ran them. The scheduler decided when to run, the executor ran the code, and the result appeared in the UI. At mid-level, you need to understand what happens between 'the scheduler decides to run this task' and 'the task code executes on a worker.' This lifecycle — from DAG definition to running Python process — involves five components interacting through the metadata database, and misunderstanding any step leads to debugging blind spots. SaulėGrid's team spent two days investigating a task that was 'stuck in queued' before discovering that the issue was not in the DAG code, not in the scheduler, but in the executor's worker pool configuration. Understanding the runtime lifecycle would have narrowed the diagnosis to 15 minutes.
The Five Components

Airflow's runtime architecture has five interacting components. The scheduler parses DAG files, creates DAG Runs and task instances, and determines which task instances are ready to execute. The executor receives ready task instances from the scheduler and dispatches them to workers. The worker is the process (or container, or pod) that actually runs the task's Python code. The metadata database stores the state of every DAG Run and task instance, serving as the coordination mechanism between all components. The web server reads from the metadata database to render the UI. These five components can run on a single machine (development) or across dozens of machines (production), but the interaction pattern is always the same.

blocks.title: The task execution lifecycle
blocks.content:
DAG File (Python)                Scheduler                    Metadata Database
      │                              │                              │
      │  parse DAG file              │                              │
      ├─────────────────────────────▶│                              │
      │                              │  create DAG Run              │
      │                              ├─────────────────────────────▶│
      │                              │  create task instances       │
      │                              ├─────────────────────────────▶│
      │                              │  set state: 'scheduled'     │
      │                              ├─────────────────────────────▶│
      │                              │                              │
                                     │  dispatch to executor        │
                             Executor ◀──────────────────────────────┤
                                │                                   │
                                │  send to worker                   │
                         Worker ◀                                   │
                           │                                        │
                           │  execute task code                     │
                           │  set state: 'running'                  │
                           ├───────────────────────────────────────▶│
                           │                                        │
                           │  task completes                        │
                           │  set state: 'success' or 'failed'     │
                           ├───────────────────────────────────────▶│
                           │                                        │
                                     │  Web Server reads state     │
                              UI ◀───┤◀────────────────────────────┤
blocks.caption: Every task instance passes through the same lifecycle regardless of which executor is configured. The metadata database is the single source of truth for task state.

visual-recommendation.description: Full-color architecture diagram showing the five components (scheduler, executor, worker, metadata database, web server) with numbered arrows showing the task instance lifecycle from DAG parsing through execution to state recording.

blocks.content: The Task Instance Lifecycle

A task instance progresses through a defined sequence of states. The scheduler creates it in the 'none' state during DAG parsing. When the task's dependencies are met (upstream tasks succeeded, the data interval is valid, the schedule triggers), the scheduler moves it to 'scheduled.' The executor picks up scheduled task instances and moves them to 'queued' — they are waiting for a worker. When a worker begins executing the task code, the state changes to 'running.' When the code completes, the state becomes 'success' or 'failed.' If retries are configured and the task failed, the state moves to 'up_for_retry' and eventually back to 'scheduled' for the next attempt.

rows: Task instance created, dependencies not yet evaluated
Sent to worker pool, waiting for available worker
Seconds to minutes (depends on worker availability)
Seconds to hours (depends on task logic)
Task code raised an unhandled exception
Failed task waiting for retry delay to elapse
Task skipped by branching or short-circuit logic

blocks.content: Where Tasks Get Stuck

Understanding the lifecycle reveals where tasks get stuck and why. A task stuck in 'scheduled' means the scheduler has marked it ready but the executor has not picked it up — check executor health, parallelism configuration, and whether the scheduler process is running. A task stuck in 'queued' means the executor dispatched it but no worker is available — check worker count, pool slot availability, and whether workers are consuming existing tasks too slowly. A task stuck in 'running' for longer than expected may be genuinely slow or may be a zombie — a task whose worker died without updating the state. Each stuck state points to a different component and a different diagnostic approach.

SaulėGrid's 'stuck in queued' incident had a specific root cause: the CeleryExecutor's worker pool was configured for 8 concurrent tasks, but 5 ExternalTaskSensors (from the pre-Dataset era) were occupying 5 of those slots in poke mode, leaving only 3 slots for actual data processing tasks. When the Bronze, Silver, and Gold tasks all needed to run simultaneously, they queued behind the sensors. The diagnosis was visible in the metadata database: 5 task instances in 'running' state (the sensors) and 3 in 'queued' state (the processing tasks). The fix was migrating sensors to Datasets (Module 1) and increasing the worker pool size.
The Scheduling Loop

The scheduler runs a continuous loop that drives the entire lifecycle. On each iteration, the loop parses DAG files to discover new or modified DAGs, evaluates which task instances have their dependencies satisfied and should transition from 'none' to 'scheduled,' dispatches scheduled tasks to the executor, checks for zombie tasks that have stopped heartbeating, and processes callbacks for completed tasks. The loop interval is controlled by the scheduler_heartbeat_sec configuration — SaulėGrid runs the loop every 5 seconds, which means a task can wait up to 5 seconds after its dependencies are met before the scheduler marks it as scheduled. At 12 DAGs, the loop iteration takes approximately 0.5 seconds, leaving 4.5 seconds of idle time per cycle. If DAG parsing slows down (due to heavy top-level code in DAG files), the loop iteration time increases and scheduling latency grows.
The Executor's Role

The executor is the component that determines how tasks are dispatched to workers. In the junior-level track, the executor was transparent — tasks ran and the executor's identity did not matter. At mid-level, the executor becomes a critical architectural decision because it determines the platform's concurrency model, isolation model, and scaling model. A LocalExecutor runs tasks as subprocesses on the scheduler machine — simple but limited to one machine's resources. A CeleryExecutor distributes tasks to a pool of remote workers via a message broker — scalable but requires broker infrastructure. A KubernetesExecutor launches a fresh pod for each task — maximally isolated but adds pod startup latency. The next three lessons cover each executor in detail.

The metadata database serves as the coordination layer between all components. The scheduler writes task states, the executor reads and updates them, the worker updates them during execution, and the web server reads them for display. No component communicates directly with another — all coordination happens through database state. This design makes the system resilient to component restarts: if the scheduler restarts, it reads the current state from the database and resumes where it left off. If a worker restarts, the scheduler detects the zombie via heartbeat and reschedules the task. The database is the single source of truth, and every component's view of the world is derived from it.

blocks.title: Architecture principle
blocks.content: The executor is the only component that differs between Airflow deployment models. The scheduler, metadata database, and web server work identically regardless of the executor. DAG code is executor-agnostic — the same DAG runs on LocalExecutor in development and CeleryExecutor in production without modification. This abstraction is what makes it possible to develop locally and deploy to a distributed cluster. The executor choice is an infrastructure decision, not a code decision.

The LocalExecutor is the simplest production-grade executor in Airflow. It runs each task as a separate subprocess on the same machine as the scheduler. When the scheduler marks a task instance as scheduled, the LocalExecutor forks a new Python process, runs the task's execute() method, and waits for the process to complete. There is no message broker, no remote worker fleet, and no network communication between the scheduler and the worker — everything happens on a single machine. This simplicity makes LocalExecutor the natural choice for development environments, small production deployments, and teams that do not have the infrastructure expertise to operate a distributed executor.
How LocalExecutor Works

The LocalExecutor maintains a process pool that controls how many tasks can run simultaneously. When a task becomes scheduled, the executor submits it to the pool. If a pool slot is available, a subprocess is immediately spawned and the task begins running. If all slots are occupied, the task waits in the queue until a slot frees up. The pool size is controlled by the parallelism configuration in airflow.cfg — this is the maximum number of concurrent task subprocesses. Each subprocess is a complete Python interpreter that imports the DAG file, locates the task, and calls its execute() method with the Airflow context.

blocks.title: LocalExecutor architecture
blocks.caption: LocalExecutor runs everything on one machine. The process pool limits concurrency to prevent resource exhaustion. Simple to operate but limited to the machine's CPU, memory, and disk.

visual-recommendation.description: Architecture diagram showing all Airflow components on a single machine with the LocalExecutor's process pool containing task subprocesses, with resource meters showing CPU and memory utilization.

blocks.content: Configuration

# airflow.cfg — LocalExecutor configuration
[core]
executor = LocalExecutor
parallelism = 8          # Maximum concurrent task subprocesses
dag_concurrency = 4       # Maximum concurrent tasks per DAG
max_active_runs_per_dag = 2  # Maximum concurrent DAG Runs per DAG

# The relationship between these settings:
# - parallelism is the global ceiling (total across all DAGs)
# - dag_concurrency limits tasks within a single DAG
# - max_active_runs limits concurrent DAG Runs for backfills
#
# Example: parallelism=8, dag_concurrency=4
# Two DAGs can each run 4 tasks simultaneously (total: 8)
# One DAG cannot run more than 4 tasks even if the other has 0

The three concurrency settings interact as nested limits. The parallelism setting is the global ceiling — no more than 8 tasks can run simultaneously across all DAGs. The dag_concurrency setting limits a single DAG to 4 concurrent tasks, preventing one DAG from monopolizing the entire process pool. The max_active_runs_per_dag setting limits backfills: if you backfill 30 days, only 2 DAG Runs execute concurrently, preventing the backfill from starving today's scheduled run. SaulėGrid's LocalExecutor development environment uses parallelism=4 to match the developer's laptop resources while keeping the production configuration at parallelism=8 on a dedicated server.
When LocalExecutor Reaches Its Limits

SaulėGrid ran LocalExecutor in production for the first six months. The single-machine deployment handled 5 DAGs with a total of 30 tasks running sequentially through the Bronze-Silver-Gold chain. The limits became apparent when the DAG count grew to 12: the machine's 16GB of RAM was not enough for 8 concurrent Python processes each loading pandas and the SaulėGrid common library, and CPU contention between task processes and the scheduler caused scheduling delays. The scheduler needs CPU time to parse DAGs and dispatch tasks; when task processes consume all available CPU, the scheduler falls behind and tasks queue up even though the process pool has empty slots.
Resource Contention

The fundamental limitation of LocalExecutor is resource contention: the scheduler and all tasks compete for the same CPU, memory, and disk I/O. A data-intensive task that consumes 4GB of RAM and 100% of one CPU core directly impacts the scheduler's ability to parse DAGs and dispatch tasks. SaulėGrid's meter ingestion task downloads a 3GB file and processes it in memory — during this task's execution, the scheduler's DAG parsing time increased from 0.3 seconds to 2.1 seconds because the Python process pool left insufficient memory for the scheduler. This resource sharing is the primary reason production deployments above 8-10 concurrent tasks migrate to a distributed executor.

left.label: Advantages
points: Single machine — no broker, no workers, no network
Minimal operational overhead — one process to manage
All processes on one machine — easy to inspect with ps, top, htop
Simple — install Airflow, configure, start
Low — one server or VM

right.label: Limitations
points: Limited to one machine's CPU, RAM, and disk
No horizontal scaling — adding capacity means a bigger machine
Resource contention between scheduler and tasks is hard to isolate
No task isolation — a memory-hungry task affects all other tasks
Vertical scaling is expensive (bigger machines cost disproportionately more)

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's LocalExecutor production deployment ran on a dedicated t3.xlarge EC2 instance (4 vCPUs, 16GB RAM) with parallelism set to 8. The deployment served 5 DAGs with a peak of 8 concurrent tasks for six months. The migration trigger was not a crash or failure — it was the observation that scheduler DAG parsing time increased from 0.3 to 2.1 seconds under peak load, and that adding a sixth DAG would push peak concurrent tasks above the machine's capacity. The migration was proactive, driven by monitoring metrics, not reactive to an outage. This is the ideal migration pattern: detect the approaching limit before hitting it.
When to Use LocalExecutor

LocalExecutor is the correct choice in three scenarios. First, development environments where simplicity outweighs scalability — every developer on SaulėGrid's team runs LocalExecutor on their laptop. Second, small production deployments with fewer than 10 concurrent tasks and predictable resource usage — if the total memory and CPU requirements of all concurrent tasks fit comfortably on one machine, distributed execution adds complexity without benefit. Third, staging environments that mirror the production DAG structure but process smaller datasets — the staging environment does not need the same capacity as production but should use the same Airflow version and configuration for compatibility testing.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's guideline for LocalExecutor sizing: the machine should have at least 2x the memory required by the most concurrent tasks, leaving headroom for the scheduler, web server, and metadata database. If the peak concurrent workload is 8 tasks each requiring 1GB of RAM, the machine needs at least 16GB — 8GB for tasks and 8GB for Airflow overhead, OS, and buffer. When the required machine size exceeds what is economically reasonable for a single server, it is time to evaluate CeleryExecutor.

The CeleryExecutor distributes task execution across a fleet of worker machines using Celery, a Python distributed task queue. When the scheduler marks a task as scheduled, the CeleryExecutor sends a message to a message broker (Redis or RabbitMQ). Worker processes on separate machines listen for messages, pick up tasks, execute them, and report results back through the broker. This separation means the scheduler does not run tasks — it only dispatches them. Workers do not parse DAGs — they only execute tasks. The scheduler and workers can scale independently: add more workers when tasks queue up, without touching the scheduler.
Architecture Components

blocks.title: CeleryExecutor distributed architecture
blocks.caption: The message broker decouples the scheduler from workers. Workers can be added or removed without restarting the scheduler. Each worker machine has its own CPU, memory, and disk — no resource contention with the scheduler.

visual-recommendation.description: Multi-node architecture diagram showing the scheduler, Redis broker, three worker nodes, and metadata database with message flow arrows illustrating the task dispatch and result reporting cycle.

blocks.content: How Task Dispatch Works

The dispatch sequence has four steps. First, the scheduler places a task message on the broker's queue — the message contains the DAG ID, task ID, execution date, and enough context for the worker to locate and execute the task. Second, a worker listening on the queue picks up the message and updates the task state to 'running' in the metadata database. Third, the worker imports the DAG file, locates the task class, and calls its execute() method. Fourth, when the task completes, the worker updates the metadata database with the final state (success or failed) and reports the result to the broker's result backend. The scheduler observes the state change on its next heartbeat and proceeds with downstream scheduling.

# airflow.cfg — CeleryExecutor configuration
[core]
executor = CeleryExecutor

[celery]
broker_url = redis://redis-host:6379/0
result_backend = db+postgresql://airflow:password@db-host:5432/airflow
worker_concurrency = 8        # Tasks per worker process
worker_autoscale = 8,2        # Max,Min concurrent tasks per worker

# Start a Celery worker (run on each worker machine)
# airflow celery worker --concurrency 8 --queues default

The broker_url points to the Redis instance that holds the task queue. The result_backend stores task results — SaulėGrid uses the metadata database itself as the result backend, which simplifies the architecture by eliminating a separate result store. The worker_concurrency setting controls how many tasks a single worker process handles simultaneously — similar to LocalExecutor's parallelism but scoped to one worker machine. With 3 worker machines each running concurrency=8, SaulėGrid can execute 24 tasks simultaneously — 3x the capacity of their previous LocalExecutor deployment on a single machine.
SaulėGrid's Migration to Celery

SaulėGrid migrated from LocalExecutor to CeleryExecutor when the platform grew to 12 DAGs with peak concurrent task counts exceeding 10. The migration involved three infrastructure additions: a Redis server for the message broker (a small t3.medium EC2 instance), two worker nodes (t3.large instances with 8GB RAM each), and network configuration to allow workers to reach the metadata database and the Redis broker. The scheduler machine remained the same — it no longer ran tasks, so its resource requirements actually decreased. The migration took one sprint to implement and test, with a week of parallel running (both executors active in staging) before cutting over production.
Worker Requirements

Every Celery worker must have access to three things: the DAG files (so it can import the DAG and locate the task), the Python environment with all required packages (so the task code can execute), and network access to the metadata database and the message broker (so it can report state changes and receive messages). SaulėGrid ensures DAG file consistency across workers using Git-sync — a sidecar process that pulls the latest DAG files from the Git repository every 60 seconds. The Python environment is consistent because all workers run the same Docker image, which includes the Airflow installation, provider packages, and the saulegrid_common library.
Failure Modes

CeleryExecutor introduces failure modes that do not exist with LocalExecutor. A broker disconnection prevents the scheduler from dispatching tasks and workers from picking them up — all tasks queue indefinitely until the broker recovers. A worker crash leaves tasks in 'running' state permanently because the worker died before updating the metadata database — Airflow's zombie detection mechanism catches these after a timeout and marks them as failed. Network partition between workers and the metadata database means workers execute tasks but cannot report results — the scheduler sees tasks stuck in 'queued' while the workers have already completed them. Each failure mode requires different monitoring and mitigation.

blocks.title: Broker as single point of failure
blocks.content: The Redis broker is the single point of failure in a CeleryExecutor deployment. If Redis goes down, no tasks can be dispatched and no results can be reported. SaulėGrid mitigates this with Redis replication (a primary and a replica with automatic failover using Redis Sentinel) and monitoring that alerts the on-call engineer within 60 seconds of broker unavailability. The broker does not store persistent data — it is a message queue — so a brief broker outage does not lose task state (that is in the metadata database). But a sustained outage stalls the entire pipeline until the broker recovers.
When to Choose CeleryExecutor

CeleryExecutor is the correct choice when the workload exceeds a single machine's capacity, when tasks need dedicated resources without contending with the scheduler, or when the team needs horizontal scaling — adding workers to handle growing workloads without replacing the scheduler machine. SaulėGrid's CeleryExecutor deployment handles 12 DAGs with a peak of 18 concurrent tasks across 3 worker nodes, with headroom for growth to 24 concurrent tasks by adding a fourth worker. The operational overhead — managing Redis, monitoring worker health, maintaining DAG file sync — is the cost of distributed execution, but at mid-level scale, the capacity benefit outweighs the operational complexity.

The KubernetesExecutor represents a fundamentally different execution model: instead of maintaining a persistent worker fleet, it launches a fresh Kubernetes pod for each task. When the scheduler marks a task as scheduled, the executor creates a pod specification, submits it to the Kubernetes API, and the Kubernetes cluster schedules the pod on a node with available resources. The pod starts, runs the task code, reports the result, and terminates. No persistent workers, no message broker, no shared process pool — each task gets its own isolated container with dedicated CPU, memory, and a clean Python environment.
How KubernetesExecutor Works

The execution sequence is different from Celery. The scheduler creates a pod spec that includes the Docker image, resource requests and limits, environment variables, and the command to execute the task. The Kubernetes API accepts the pod spec and schedules it on a node. The pod starts, which involves pulling the Docker image (if not cached), initializing the container, and starting the Airflow task runner process. The task runner imports the DAG file, locates the task, executes it, updates the metadata database with the result, and exits. The Kubernetes cluster detects the pod completion and garbage-collects it. The entire lifecycle — from pod creation to garbage collection — is per-task and per-execution.

blocks.title: KubernetesExecutor task lifecycle
blocks.caption: Each task gets its own pod lifecycle. No persistent workers exist between task executions. The Kubernetes cluster handles scheduling, resource allocation, and pod cleanup.

blocks.content: Pod Templates

# KubernetesExecutor pod template — defines how task pods are created
# airflow.cfg
# [kubernetes]
# pod_template_file = /opt/airflow/pod_template.yaml

# pod_template.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  labels:
    app: saulegrid-airflow-task
spec:
  containers:
    - name: base
      image: saulegrid/airflow-worker:2.8.1
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: "500m"       # 0.5 CPU cores minimum
          memory: "1Gi"     # 1GB RAM minimum
        limits:
          cpu: "2000m"      # 2 CPU cores maximum
          memory: "4Gi"     # 4GB RAM maximum
      env:
        - name: AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: airflow-secrets
              key: sql-alchemy-conn
  restartPolicy: Never       # Tasks should not auto-restart

The pod template defines the default container configuration for all task pods. Resource requests guarantee a minimum allocation (the pod will not be scheduled on a node with insufficient resources), and resource limits cap the maximum consumption (the pod is killed if it exceeds the limit). The restartPolicy: Never is critical — Airflow manages retries through its own mechanism, so Kubernetes should not restart failed pods. SaulėGrid's pod template requests 0.5 CPU and 1GB RAM as the default, which is sufficient for lightweight tasks. Tasks that need more resources override the defaults using the executor_config parameter.
Per-Task Resource Configuration

The executor_config is evaluated at runtime, not at DAG parse time. This means the pod specification can reference Airflow Variables or template expressions that resolve to different values depending on the environment. SaulėGrid uses this to vary resource requests between environments: development pods get 512MB RAM (sufficient for small test datasets), staging pods get 2GB (matching a fraction of production data), and production pods get 8-16GB (matching the full meter file size). The environment-specific configuration is stored in Airflow Variables and referenced in the pod override, keeping the DAG code identical across environments.

The executor_config parameter allows individual tasks to override the pod template's defaults. SaulėGrid's meter ingestion task needs 8GB of RAM to process the meter file in memory, while the quality reporting task needs only 512MB. With CeleryExecutor, both tasks share the same worker resources and the large task can starve the small one. With KubernetesExecutor, each task gets exactly the resources it requests — the Kubernetes scheduler places the large task on a node with sufficient memory and the small task on any available node. This per-task resource isolation is the primary advantage of KubernetesExecutor over CeleryExecutor.
The Startup Latency Trade-off

KubernetesExecutor's per-task isolation comes with a latency cost: every task execution includes pod creation time (1-2 seconds), image pull time (5-30 seconds if the image is not cached on the node), and container initialization time (2-5 seconds). For SaulėGrid's meter ingestion task that runs for 20 minutes, the 10-30 second startup overhead is negligible. For a lightweight quality check task that takes 5 seconds to execute, the startup overhead can be 5x the actual processing time. This makes KubernetesExecutor less suitable for workloads with many short-lived tasks — the per-task overhead accumulates and slows the overall pipeline.
When to Choose KubernetesExecutor

rows: Each pod can run a different Docker image with different packages
Per-task resource requests and limits via executor_config
Leverages existing infrastructure and team expertise
Pod startup overhead dominates execution time
Operational complexity of Kubernetes exceeds the benefit
SaulėGrid's current workload (12 DAGs, mixed task durations)
CeleryExecutor meets current needs; Kubernetes planned for workloads requiring dependency isolation

blocks.title: Architecture principle
blocks.content: KubernetesExecutor trades simplicity for isolation. CeleryExecutor runs tasks on persistent workers that share a Python environment — simple to operate but tasks cannot have conflicting dependencies. KubernetesExecutor runs each task in its own container — maximum isolation but with startup overhead and Kubernetes operational complexity. The choice depends on whether dependency isolation or operational simplicity is more valuable for the team's workload. SaulėGrid chose CeleryExecutor because their tasks share the same Python environment (the saulegrid_common library) and their team has more experience with Redis than Kubernetes.

The previous three lessons covered the mechanics of each executor in isolation. This lesson provides the decision framework for choosing between them — and equally important, for knowing when to migrate from one to another. SaulėGrid evaluated all three executors when planning their mid-level platform upgrade. The decision was not purely technical: it considered the team's infrastructure expertise, the operational overhead of each option, the budget for infrastructure, and the growth trajectory that would determine when the next migration would be needed. The right executor is the simplest one that meets the current requirements with reasonable headroom for growth.
The Decision Matrix

rows: Full — each task gets its own pod

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Evaluation

SaulėGrid evaluated the three executors against five weighted criteria. Criterion 1 (weight 30%): does it support 12 DAGs with up to 18 concurrent tasks? LocalExecutor could handle 12-16 concurrent tasks on a large machine, but with resource contention between tasks and the scheduler. CeleryExecutor with 3 workers handles 24 concurrent tasks comfortably. KubernetesExecutor handles any number of concurrent tasks but introduces pod startup latency. Criterion 2 (weight 25%): does the team have the expertise to operate it? The team has strong Python and Linux skills, moderate Redis experience, and no Kubernetes experience.

Criterion 3 (weight 20%): what is the ongoing operational cost? LocalExecutor costs one server. CeleryExecutor costs the scheduler server, a Redis instance, and 2-3 worker servers. KubernetesExecutor costs a Kubernetes cluster (managed or self-hosted) and the scheduler server. Criterion 4 (weight 15%): how long before we outgrow it? CeleryExecutor can scale to 50+ concurrent tasks by adding workers, providing 2-3 years of headroom. KubernetesExecutor scales further but the team does not project needing more than 30 concurrent tasks within 18 months. Criterion 5 (weight 10%): does it require dependency isolation? Currently all tasks share the same Python environment. Only the weather API task has a conflicting dependency, handled with @task.virtualenv.
The Scoring

CeleryExecutor scored highest because it provides the needed capacity without the operational overhead of Kubernetes, aligns with the team's existing skills, and offers sufficient growth headroom. LocalExecutor was eliminated because the resource contention at 18 concurrent tasks would degrade scheduler performance. KubernetesExecutor scored lowest because the team's lack of Kubernetes expertise would create operational risk, and the primary advantage (dependency isolation) was not a current requirement. The evaluation was documented and shared with the engineering manager, establishing a clear rationale that can be revisited when requirements change.
Migration Triggers

The executor selection is not permanent. SaulėGrid defined explicit triggers for the next migration. If concurrent task demand exceeds 40 tasks (CeleryExecutor's practical limit with the current 5-worker fleet), the team will evaluate KubernetesExecutor or a larger Celery fleet. If more than 3 tasks require conflicting Python dependencies (currently handled by @task.virtualenv), the dependency isolation benefit of KubernetesExecutor becomes compelling. If the team hires a platform engineer with Kubernetes expertise, the operational risk of KubernetesExecutor decreases significantly. These triggers are reviewed quarterly alongside the platform capacity metrics.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's executor selection principle: choose the simplest executor that meets the current requirements with 12-18 months of growth headroom. Do not choose KubernetesExecutor because it is the most powerful — choose it because the team needs its specific capabilities (dependency isolation, autoscaling, per-task resource control) and has the expertise to operate it. An executor that the team cannot debug during a 2AM incident is not a good executor, regardless of its technical capabilities.
The CeleryWorkerExecutor Alternative

Airflow 2.7+ introduced the CeleryKubernetesExecutor, a hybrid that uses CeleryExecutor for most tasks and KubernetesExecutor for tasks that specifically request pod-level isolation via executor_config. This hybrid approach lets SaulėGrid run their standard tasks on the Celery worker fleet (fast startup, shared environment) while running the weather API task — which has conflicting dependencies — in an isolated Kubernetes pod (slow startup, dedicated environment). The hybrid is a pragmatic middle ground that avoids a full migration to KubernetesExecutor while providing isolation for the specific tasks that need it. SaulėGrid is evaluating this approach as an alternative to their current @task.virtualenv workaround for dependency conflicts.
The Migration Path

Airflow's executor abstraction makes migration straightforward: change the executor configuration in airflow.cfg, set up the new infrastructure (broker, workers, or cluster), and restart. DAG code does not change. SaulėGrid's migration from LocalExecutor to CeleryExecutor required zero DAG modifications — the same DAGs that ran on a single machine ran on the distributed cluster without any code changes. The only DAG-visible difference was performance: tasks that previously queued behind the scheduler's single-machine bottleneck now executed on dedicated worker machines with no resource contention. The migration from CeleryExecutor to KubernetesExecutor would similarly require no DAG code changes, only executor_config additions for tasks that need non-default resources.

The executor controls how many tasks can run simultaneously across the platform. Pools provide finer-grained control: they limit concurrent task execution by resource type. SaulėGrid's SFTP provider allows a maximum of 2 simultaneous connections — if 5 Bronze DAGs try to download files concurrently, 3 connections are rejected and the download tasks fail. The PostgreSQL analytics warehouse has a connection limit of 20 — if every Gold task opens a connection simultaneously, the pool is exhausted and the billing team's dashboard queries time out. Pools solve these problems by limiting concurrent tasks that access the same constrained resource, regardless of which DAG they belong to.
How Pools Work

A pool is a named resource with a fixed number of slots. When a task is assigned to a pool, it can only execute when a slot in that pool is available. If all slots are occupied, the task waits in the 'queued' state until a slot frees up. Pools are global — they limit concurrency across all DAGs, not within a single DAG. The default pool has 128 slots, which means tasks not assigned to a specific pool are effectively unlimited (128 concurrent tasks far exceeds most platforms' capacity). Creating a custom pool with fewer slots constrains the specific resource that the pool represents.

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from pendulum import datetime

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    schedule='0 0 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'meters'],
)
def dag_bronze_meters():

    @task(pool='sftp_pool')  # Limited to 2 concurrent SFTP connections
    def download_meters():
        """Download meters from SFTP.
        
        Assigned to the sftp_pool (2 slots) to prevent
        overwhelming the provider's SFTP server.
        """
        pass

    @task  # No pool — uses the default pool (128 slots)
    def validate_meters():
        """Validate meter data — CPU only, no external dependency."""
        pass

    @task(pool='warehouse_pool')  # Limited to 5 concurrent DB connections
    def write_to_warehouse():
        """Write to PostgreSQL analytics warehouse.
        
        Assigned to the warehouse_pool (5 slots) to stay within
        the database's connection limit.
        """
        pass

    data = download_meters()
    valid = validate_meters()
    write_to_warehouse()

dag_bronze_meters()

The download_meters task is assigned to the sftp_pool with 2 slots. Even if 5 Bronze DAGs run simultaneously, only 2 SFTP download tasks execute at the same time — the other 3 wait in 'queued' state until a slot opens. The validate_meters task has no pool assignment and uses the default pool — validation is CPU-only and does not contend for any shared resource. The write_to_warehouse task is assigned to the warehouse_pool with 5 slots, matching the connection budget that SaulėGrid allocates to Airflow (5 of the warehouse's 20 total connections, leaving 15 for dashboards and other consumers).
Creating and Managing Pools

# Create pools via the Airflow CLI
airflow pools set sftp_pool 2 'SFTP connections to meter provider (max 2)'
airflow pools set warehouse_pool 5 'PostgreSQL warehouse connections (max 5 for Airflow)'
airflow pools set api_pool 3 'External API calls (weather, VERT regulatory)'

# List all pools
airflow pools list

# Output:
# Pool            | Slots | Description
# default_pool    | 128   | Default pool
# sftp_pool       | 2     | SFTP connections to meter provider (max 2)
# warehouse_pool  | 5     | PostgreSQL warehouse connections (max 5 for Airflow)
# api_pool        | 3     | External API calls (weather, VERT regulatory)

Pools are created through the CLI, the REST API, or the Airflow UI. The slot count should match the external system's capacity limit — not Airflow's capacity. SaulėGrid's sftp_pool has 2 slots because the provider allows 2 concurrent connections, regardless of whether Airflow has the capacity to run 20 concurrent download tasks. The pool description is mandatory in SaulėGrid's convention because it documents why the limit exists — a future engineer who sees a pool with 2 slots needs to know whether increasing it is safe (it is not, because the limit is the provider's, not Airflow's).
Pools and Executor Interaction

Pools and executor concurrency settings interact as independent limits. The executor's parallelism setting controls the total number of tasks that can run simultaneously. Pools control how many of those tasks can access a specific resource. If parallelism is 24 (3 workers × 8 concurrent tasks) and the sftp_pool has 2 slots, at most 2 of those 24 running tasks can be SFTP downloads — even if the executor has capacity for more. The pool is the tighter constraint for resource-limited external systems. SaulėGrid's configuration ensures that pool slot counts are always smaller than the executor's capacity, so the pool is the effective limit, not the executor.
Pool Priority

When multiple tasks compete for the same pool, Airflow uses a priority system to determine which task executes first. The priority_weight parameter on tasks sets the relative priority — higher weights execute before lower weights. SaulėGrid assigns priority_weight=10 to SLA-critical tasks (Gold billing) and priority_weight=1 to non-critical tasks (quality reports). When the warehouse_pool has 5 slots and 8 tasks are waiting, the 5 tasks with the highest priority_weight execute first. This ensures that SLA-critical tasks are not blocked by lower-priority tasks when pool slots are scarce.
The Sensor Slot Problem Revisited

Pools provide a second mitigation for the sensor slot consumption problem discussed in Module 1. If the team still uses ExternalTaskSensors for any dependencies, assigning them to a dedicated sensor_pool with a limited slot count prevents them from consuming slots in the default pool. SaulėGrid's sensor_pool has 3 slots — enough for the remaining legacy sensors but not enough to starve the default pool. When combined with the reschedule mode (which releases the slot between sensor pokes), the dedicated pool ensures that sensor tasks never consume more than 3 of the platform's execution slots regardless of how many sensors are running.
Pool Monitoring

SaulėGrid monitors pool utilization as a capacity planning metric. The monitoring dashboard shows, for each pool, the number of slots in use versus total slots and the number of tasks waiting for a slot. A pool that is consistently at capacity during the processing window indicates that the external system's concurrency limit is a pipeline bottleneck. The sftp_pool showed 100% utilization for 45 minutes every night — meaning download tasks were queuing behind each other. SaulėGrid addressed this by requesting a connection limit increase from the SFTP provider (from 2 to 4 connections), then updating the pool to 4 slots. Pool utilization metrics drove the provider conversation with concrete data rather than a vague 'we need more connections.'

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's pool design principle: create a pool for every external system that has a concurrency limit, with the slot count set to the system's documented capacity (or the portion of that capacity allocated to Airflow). Document the external system's limit in the pool description. Review pool utilization monthly — if a pool is consistently at capacity, either the external system needs capacity increase or the pipeline needs to reduce concurrent access. If a pool is never full, it may be unnecessary overhead. The pool inventory is maintained alongside the connection registry (Module 5).

Pools limit how many tasks of a given type run concurrently. Queues determine which workers execute which tasks. In a CeleryExecutor deployment, all tasks are sent to the 'default' queue by default, and all workers listen on the 'default' queue. This means any worker can execute any task. At mid-level scale, this uniform distribution is often suboptimal: SaulėGrid's Bronze ingestion tasks are network-heavy (downloading large files from SFTP), while Silver transformation tasks are CPU-heavy (data cleansing and quality filtering). Running both on the same workers means the network-heavy downloads contend with CPU-heavy transformations for the same resources.
How Queues Work

A queue in CeleryExecutor is a named channel in the message broker. When a task is assigned to a queue (via the queue parameter), the scheduler places its message on that specific queue. Workers subscribe to one or more queues and only process messages from their subscribed queues. This allows worker specialization: network-optimized workers handle download tasks, CPU-optimized workers handle transformation tasks, and memory-optimized workers handle large-file processing tasks. Each worker type can be sized appropriately for its workload without over-provisioning resources for the most demanding task type.

Each task is routed to a queue matching its resource profile. On the worker side, each worker subscribes to one or more queues based on the machine's capabilities. SaulėGrid's worker fleet has one network worker (high bandwidth, moderate CPU) listening on the 'network' queue, two compute workers (high CPU, 16GB RAM) listening on the 'compute' queue, and one database worker (low-latency network to the PostgreSQL server) listening on the 'database' queue. The database worker is deliberately co-located in the same availability zone as the PostgreSQL server to minimize query latency.
Queue Isolation Benefits

Queue-based routing provides isolation beyond resource affinity. When a compute worker crashes, only tasks on the 'compute' queue are affected — network and database tasks continue executing on their dedicated workers. Without queue routing, a single worker failure can affect tasks of all types if the failed worker was running a mixed workload. SaulėGrid's queue isolation reduced the blast radius of worker failures: a compute worker outage on March 15th affected only Silver transformation tasks, while Bronze downloads and Gold writes continued uninterrupted on their dedicated workers. The mean time to recovery was 8 minutes (restart the compute worker) rather than the 25 minutes it would have taken to recover all task types.

# Starting workers with queue subscriptions
# Each worker listens only on its assigned queues

# Network worker (handles SFTP downloads, API calls)
airflow celery worker \
  --queues network,default \
  --concurrency 4 \
  --hostname network-worker-01

# Compute workers (handle transformations, quality checks)
airflow celery worker \
  --queues compute,default \
  --concurrency 8 \
  --hostname compute-worker-01

airflow celery worker \
  --queues compute,default \
  --concurrency 8 \
  --hostname compute-worker-02

# Database worker (handles warehouse writes, near DB server)
airflow celery worker \
  --queues database,default \
  --concurrency 4 \
  --hostname database-worker-01
Queue Assignment Conventions

SaulėGrid assigns queues based on the task's dominant resource requirement, not its pipeline layer. A Bronze task that downloads files from SFTP goes to the 'network' queue. A Bronze task that validates downloaded files locally goes to the 'compute' queue (it is CPU-bound, not network-bound). A Silver task that reads from Bronze and writes to the warehouse goes to the 'compute' queue for the transformation phase and the 'database' queue for the write phase — but since Airflow assigns a single queue per task, the task goes to 'compute' because the transformation dominates the execution time. When a task's resource profile is ambiguous, SaulėGrid defaults to the 'default' queue and lets any available worker handle it.

Each worker subscribes to both its specialized queue and the 'default' queue. The default queue subscription ensures that tasks without an explicit queue assignment are still processed. The --concurrency setting is tuned per worker: the network worker handles 4 concurrent downloads (limited by SFTP provider connections), the compute workers handle 8 concurrent transformations each (limited by CPU cores), and the database worker handles 4 concurrent writes (limited by database connection pool). The --hostname parameter provides a readable name in the Airflow UI's worker status display.
Queue Design Principles

SaulėGrid's queue design follows three principles. First, queues represent resource affinity, not business logic — 'network' and 'compute' are better queue names than 'bronze' and 'silver' because the resource profile is the reason for routing, not the pipeline layer. A Silver task that downloads a reference file belongs on the 'network' queue, not the 'silver' queue. Second, every worker subscribes to the 'default' queue as a fallback — if a task is deployed without a queue assignment, it should still execute rather than sit in an unmonitored queue indefinitely. Third, queue count should be small — SaulėGrid uses 3 queues (network, compute, database) rather than creating a queue per DAG or per task, keeping the routing model simple enough for any team member to understand.
Monitoring Queue Depth

Queue depth — the number of messages waiting in each queue — is a key operational metric. A growing queue depth indicates that tasks are arriving faster than workers can process them. SaulėGrid monitors queue depth through the Celery Flower dashboard (a web UI for monitoring Celery workers) and through direct Redis queries. Alert thresholds are configured per queue: the 'network' queue alerts if depth exceeds 5 (indicating SFTP bottleneck or worker failure), and the 'compute' queue alerts if depth exceeds 15 (indicating insufficient compute workers for the current workload). Queue depth trends over time reveal whether the worker fleet is keeping pace with pipeline growth.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: The most common queue-related failure is assigning a task to a queue that no worker listens on. The task sits in the queue indefinitely — it is not failed, not timed out, just waiting. The Airflow UI shows it as 'queued' with no indication that the queue is orphaned. SaulėGrid prevents this with a CI check that validates all queue assignments in DAG code against a known list of active queues. If a task references queue='gpu' but no worker is configured for the 'gpu' queue, the CI check fails before the DAG reaches production.

SaulėGrid's pipeline workload is not constant. Between 00:00 and 02:00, all Bronze DAGs execute simultaneously — 5 download tasks, 5 validation tasks, and the Silver transformations that cascade from completed Bronze DAGs. During business hours (08:00 to 18:00), only monitoring and quality reporting DAGs run — 2-3 lightweight tasks at most. Running 3 worker machines 24 hours a day to handle a 2-hour peak is wasteful: 22 hours of the day, the fleet is 80% idle. Worker scaling — adjusting the number of active workers to match the current workload — reduces this waste.
Manual Scaling

The simplest scaling approach is manual: start additional workers before the peak window and stop them after the peak passes. SaulėGrid's initial approach used a cron job on the infrastructure server that started 2 additional workers at 23:50 (10 minutes before the midnight Bronze trigger) and stopped them at 04:00 (after the typical chain completion). This is not dynamic — it does not respond to actual workload — but it matches SaulėGrid's predictable daily pattern. The cron-based approach works when the peak window is consistent and the cost of over-provisioning during the window is acceptable.

# SaulėGrid's cron-based worker scaling
# Infrastructure server crontab

# Start 2 additional workers at 23:50 for the overnight processing window
50 23 * * *  docker-compose -f /opt/airflow/docker-compose-workers.yml up -d worker-4 worker-5

# Stop additional workers at 04:00 after chain completion
0 4 * * *   docker-compose -f /opt/airflow/docker-compose-workers.yml stop worker-4 worker-5

# Weekend: start workers for the full weekend backlog window
50 23 * * 5  docker-compose -f /opt/airflow/docker-compose-workers.yml up -d worker-4 worker-5 worker-6
0 8 * * 1   docker-compose -f /opt/airflow/docker-compose-workers.yml stop worker-4 worker-5 worker-6

Celery provides built-in autoscaling at the individual worker level: the worker_autoscale configuration dynamically adjusts the number of concurrent task processes within a single worker based on the depth of the message queue. The setting worker_autoscale = 8,2 means the worker starts with a minimum of 2 concurrent task processes during idle periods and scales up to a maximum of 8 processes when pending messages accumulate in the queue. This is vertical scaling within a single worker machine — it adjusts how many of the machine's CPU cores are dedicated to task execution versus being held in reserve for the operating system and the Airflow worker management process.
Celery Autoscaling

Celery provides built-in autoscaling at the worker level: the worker_autoscale configuration adjusts the number of concurrent processes within a single worker based on queue depth. The setting worker_autoscale = 8,2 means the worker starts with 2 processes and scales up to 8 when the queue has pending tasks. This is not horizontal scaling (adding machines) — it is vertical scaling within a single worker machine. SaulėGrid uses Celery autoscale on their compute workers: during off-peak hours, each worker runs 2 processes (low resource usage), and during the overnight peak, each worker scales to 8 processes (full utilization).

# Celery worker with autoscaling
# Starts with 2 processes, scales up to 8 when tasks are queued
airflow celery worker \
  --autoscale 8,2 \
  --queues compute,default \
  --hostname compute-worker-01

# airflow.cfg equivalent
# [celery]
# worker_autoscale = 8,2
Scaling Decisions

The distinction between vertical scaling (more processes per worker) and horizontal scaling (more workers) matters for cost and performance. Vertical scaling is free in the short term — it uses existing machine capacity that was being held in reserve. But it has a ceiling: a machine with 8 CPU cores cannot productively run 16 concurrent CPU-bound tasks. Horizontal scaling adds capacity beyond a single machine's ceiling but costs money for each additional machine. SaulėGrid's strategy is to exhaust vertical scaling first (Celery autoscale from 2 to 8 processes per worker) before adding horizontal capacity (starting additional worker containers). This approach uses existing resources efficiently before incurring additional infrastructure cost.
Kubernetes-Based Autoscaling

For KubernetesExecutor deployments, scaling is inherent: the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler adds nodes when pods cannot be scheduled due to insufficient resources, and removes nodes when utilization drops below a threshold. For CeleryExecutor on Kubernetes, KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) provides horizontal scaling: KEDA monitors the Celery queue depth in Redis and scales the worker Deployment's replica count based on the number of pending messages. When the queue depth exceeds a threshold, KEDA adds worker pods. When the queue empties, KEDA scales the workers back down. SaulėGrid has not yet adopted KEDA but plans to evaluate it when they migrate their CeleryExecutor fleet from VMs to Kubernetes.
Scaling Metrics

rows: Tasks arriving faster than workers can process
Add workers or increase autoscale maximum
Use larger worker instances or reduce concurrency
Insufficient worker capacity for the workload
Add workers to the affected queue

blocks.content: SaulėGrid monitors these metrics through their Grafana dashboard. The queue depth panel shows real-time depth for each queue, with alert lines at the warning and critical thresholds. The worker utilization panel shows CPU and memory per worker host. The task queue time panel shows the average time tasks spend in 'queued' state before a worker picks them up — this is the most direct indicator of worker capacity: if tasks wait more than 60 seconds on average, the fleet is undersized for the workload. The dashboard is reviewed during the weekly platform operations meeting, and scaling decisions are made based on the trailing 7-day trends.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's scaling strategy is hybrid: a fixed baseline of 3 persistent workers that handle the steady-state daytime workload, plus 2 additional workers started by cron for the overnight peak window, plus Celery autoscale within each worker for burst capacity. This three-layer approach (persistent + scheduled + autoscale) provides cost efficiency (no over-provisioning during the day), reliability (persistent workers are always available), and burst capacity (additional workers for the peak). The layers will be replaced with KEDA-based autoscaling when the fleet moves to Kubernetes.

Every executor model has failure modes that are specific to its architecture. LocalExecutor fails when the single machine runs out of resources. CeleryExecutor fails when the broker goes down, workers crash, or network partitions isolate components. KubernetesExecutor fails when the cluster cannot schedule pods, images fail to pull, or pod evictions kill running tasks. Understanding these failure modes is essential for building monitoring that detects them and runbooks that resolve them. SaulėGrid's on-call engineer needs to diagnose whether a 'stuck task' is caused by a scheduler bug, a worker crash, a broker outage, or a resource exhaustion — and each cause has a different fix.
Zombie Tasks

A zombie task is a task instance that the metadata database shows as 'running' but that is not actually being executed by any worker. This happens when a worker process dies without updating the task state — a machine crash, an out-of-memory kill, or a network partition between the worker and the metadata database. The task appears to be running in the Airflow UI, but no progress is being made. Airflow detects zombies through a heartbeat mechanism: running tasks send periodic heartbeat updates to the metadata database, and the scheduler marks tasks as zombies when the heartbeat stops. The scheduler_zombie_task_threshold configuration controls how long the scheduler waits before declaring a task a zombie (default: 300 seconds).

# airflow.cfg — zombie detection configuration
[scheduler]
scheduler_zombie_task_threshold = 300  # Seconds without heartbeat before zombie
job_heartbeat_sec = 5                  # How often running tasks send heartbeats

# When a zombie is detected, the scheduler:
# 1. Marks the task as failed in the metadata database
# 2. Logs a zombie detection event
# 3. If retries remain, the task is rescheduled for retry
# 4. The on_failure_callback fires (if configured)

SaulėGrid reduced the zombie threshold from the default 300 seconds to 120 seconds after an incident where a worker crash left 3 tasks in zombie state for 5 minutes each. During those 15 combined minutes, downstream tasks could not start because their upstream dependencies appeared to still be running. The 120-second threshold means zombies are detected within 2 minutes of the worker crash, and the tasks are either retried or marked as failed — either way, the pipeline can proceed. The trade-off is false positives: a task that legitimately stops heartbeating for 2 minutes (due to a long blocking I/O operation) could be falsely marked as a zombie.
Broker Failures (CeleryExecutor)

A Redis broker outage has two effects: the scheduler cannot dispatch new tasks (messages cannot be sent to the queue), and workers cannot pick up queued tasks (messages cannot be read from the queue). Tasks already running on workers are unaffected — they continue executing because they have already been dispatched. But new task dispatches and completions reports stall. SaulėGrid monitors Redis with a health check that pings the broker every 10 seconds and alerts if the ping fails for 3 consecutive checks (30 seconds). The runbook for a broker outage is: check Redis process status, check memory usage (Redis evicts data when memory is full), and restart if necessary.
Worker Failures (CeleryExecutor)

Worker failures are more nuanced than broker failures because they affect only the tasks running on the failed worker. If a worker machine crashes, the tasks it was executing become zombies (detected by the heartbeat mechanism). If a worker process hangs but the machine is still running, the heartbeat may continue (because the process is alive, just stuck), making the zombie undetectable. SaulėGrid mitigates process-level hangs by setting execution_timeout on all long-running tasks — a task that exceeds its timeout is killed by the worker process, preventing indefinite hangs.

# Execution timeout as a safety net against hung tasks
from airflow.decorators import task
from datetime import timedelta

@task(
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),  # Kill if running > 60 minutes
    retries=2,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
)
def transform_silver_meters():
    """Silver transformation with execution timeout.
    
    Normal execution: 8-12 minutes.
    Timeout at 60 minutes catches hung processes.
    If the task is killed by timeout, it is retried (if retries remain).
    """
    pass

# SaulėGrid's timeout convention by task type:
# Bronze downloads: 30 minutes (network-dependent)
# Silver transforms: 60 minutes (CPU-dependent, data-volume-dependent)
# Gold aggregations: 45 minutes (database-dependent)
# Quality reports: 15 minutes (lightweight)

The execution_timeout setting provides a last line of defense against tasks that hang indefinitely. Without it, a hung task occupies a worker slot forever — or until the zombie detection threshold triggers, which can take 5 minutes. With the timeout, the worker kills the task process after the specified duration and marks it as failed. If retries are configured, the task is rescheduled. SaulėGrid sets timeouts on every task as a mandatory practice: the timeout is set to 3x the task's typical execution time, providing generous headroom for slow days while catching genuine hangs. A task that normally takes 10 minutes gets a 30-minute timeout — long enough to accommodate slow SFTP servers but short enough to release the worker slot within a reasonable time.
Pod Failures (KubernetesExecutor)

KubernetesExecutor introduces pod-specific failure modes. Pod eviction occurs when the Kubernetes node runs out of resources and the cluster evicts pods to free capacity — running tasks are killed without warning. Image pull failures prevent the pod from starting, leaving the task in 'queued' state indefinitely. Resource limit violations (exceeding the pod's memory limit) cause the container to be OOM-killed. Each failure mode appears differently in the Kubernetes events log and requires different remediation: eviction requires node capacity increase, image pull failures require image registry troubleshooting, and OOM kills require increasing the task's resource limits.
Monitoring the Executor

rows: Check scheduler process, restart if needed
Check worker host, restart worker process
Check worker health, increase heartbeat frequency
Add workers or investigate pool exhaustion

blocks.title: Architecture principle
blocks.content: Every distributed system fails differently than a single-machine system. LocalExecutor fails simply: the machine is down, nothing runs. CeleryExecutor fails partially: the broker may be down while workers are healthy, or one worker may be down while others continue. KubernetesExecutor fails ephemerally: pods are evicted and rescheduled, nodes are added and removed, and the failure is often self-healing — but only if the cluster has capacity. The monitoring strategy must match the executor's failure model: single-point monitoring for LocalExecutor, component-level monitoring for CeleryExecutor, and cluster-level monitoring for KubernetesExecutor.

This module has covered the executor lifecycle, three executor models, the selection framework, resource management with pools and queues, worker scaling strategies, and executor-specific failure modes. This lesson synthesizes every concept into SaulėGrid's complete runtime architecture: how the CeleryExecutor deployment is configured, how workers are organized by queue, how pools protect external systems, how scaling handles the overnight peak, and how monitoring detects failures. Every design choice traces back to a concept from this module, and every configuration value was determined by the platform's specific requirements.
The Deployment Topology

blocks.content: Pool and Queue Configuration

rows: Shares the api_pool with weather API
No external limit — use default pool
Prevents sensors from consuming worker slots

blocks.content: The Scaling Strategy

SaulėGrid's scaling operates on three layers. The baseline layer is 3 persistent workers that run continuously, providing capacity for the daytime workload of monitoring DAGs and ad-hoc reprocessing requests. The scheduled layer adds 2 burst workers at 23:50 for the overnight processing peak (all Bronze DAGs trigger at midnight, cascading through Silver and Gold by 02:00). The burst workers are stopped at 04:00 after chain completion. The autoscale layer adjusts concurrency within each worker: compute workers autoscale from 2 to 8-12 processes based on queue depth, providing intra-worker burst capacity without adding machines. The three layers provide cost efficiency (no idle workers during the day), reliability (persistent workers are always available), and peak capacity (scheduled + autoscale for the overnight window).
Failure Recovery Procedures

The runtime architecture's failure recovery procedures are documented in the on-call runbook with a decision tree for each failure type. A broker failure: check Redis process status, verify memory availability, restart if necessary, and monitor task dispatch resumption. A worker failure: identify the failed worker from the monitoring dashboard, check the worker host's health (SSH access, disk space, memory), restart the worker process, and verify that tasks resume on the queue. A zombie spike: check which worker is producing zombies, inspect the worker's resource utilization (memory exhaustion is the most common cause of zombie-producing crashes), and address the root cause before the zombie threshold triggers cascading failures. Each procedure includes the expected resolution time and the escalation path if the procedure does not resolve the issue within the time budget.
Monitoring and Alerting

The runtime monitoring dashboard has five panels. The scheduler health panel shows the scheduler heartbeat lag and DAG parsing duration — a spike in either indicates scheduler overload. The broker health panel shows Redis memory usage, connection count, and ping latency — any degradation risks task dispatch failures. The worker fleet panel shows per-worker CPU, memory, process count, and task completion rate — a worker with zero completions despite queued tasks indicates a stuck or crashed worker. The queue depth panel shows pending messages per queue with alert thresholds — the earliest indicator of capacity shortage. The zombie detection panel shows the hourly zombie count — a proxy for worker stability.

Alert routing follows the severity model established in Module 3: broker failures are critical (all dispatching stops), worker failures are warning (other workers compensate), queue depth breaches are warning (temporary capacity issues), and zombie spikes are informational (usually self-resolving through retries). The on-call engineer receives all alerts through PagerDuty, with critical alerts requiring acknowledgment within 15 minutes and warnings within 1 hour. The alert routing is documented in the on-call runbook alongside the diagnosis and remediation steps for each alert type.
Evolution Path

SaulėGrid's runtime architecture is not static — it has a planned evolution path. The next phase (6-12 months) involves migrating the CeleryExecutor fleet from EC2 instances to Kubernetes Deployments, which enables KEDA-based autoscaling to replace the cron-based burst worker pattern. The following phase (12-18 months) evaluates whether the KubernetesExecutor should replace CeleryExecutor entirely, driven by the anticipated need for per-task dependency isolation when the platform integrates with external ML model serving systems. Each evolution phase has defined triggers (capacity metrics, dependency isolation requirements, team expertise), a staging validation period, and a rollback plan. The evolution path is documented in the platform roadmap and reviewed quarterly.

The evolution path illustrates a broader principle: the runtime architecture should grow with the platform. SaulėGrid started with LocalExecutor (1 machine, 5 DAGs), migrated to CeleryExecutor (5 machines, 12 DAGs), and plans for KubernetesExecutor (cluster autoscaling, 30+ DAGs). Each migration was driven by concrete capacity or capability requirements, not by technology trends. The team resisted the temptation to adopt Kubernetes early ('because everyone uses it') and instead made a documented, evidence-based decision that CeleryExecutor was the right choice for their current scale. When the triggers for the next migration are met, the documentation trail will support the decision just as clearly.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: The executor is the engine that transforms DAG definitions into running code — LocalExecutor for simplicity, CeleryExecutor for distributed capacity, KubernetesExecutor for isolation — and pools, queues, scaling, and monitoring are the controls that ensure the engine runs efficiently, within the constraints of external systems, and recovers gracefully when components fail.

chapters.title: Secrets, Connections, and Configuration at Scale
chapters.description: Master secrets management with Secrets Backends, dynamic connection resolution, environment-specific configuration, and the separation of DAG code from runtime configuration that enables safe multi-environment deployment.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, you created Connections through the Airflow web UI: navigate to Admin → Connections, fill in the form with the conn_id, host, port, login, and password, and save. SaulėGrid's team created 4 connections this way — sftp_saulegrid_meters, api_weather_forecast, api_vert_regulatory, and postgres_warehouse. The approach worked for a single environment. When the platform expanded to three environments — development, staging, and production — the team had to recreate all 4 connections in each environment's Airflow UI, manually entering credentials from a shared password manager. On April 3rd, an engineer copying the SFTP password into the production Connection form accidentally pasted the staging password instead. The Bronze meters DAG failed at midnight with an authentication error that took 45 minutes to diagnose.
The Three-Environment Problem

SaulėGrid's three environments exist for a reason: development uses mock data sources, staging connects to the provider's test endpoints, and production connects to the live systems. Each environment has different credentials, different hostnames, and different configuration values. With UI-based Connection management, every credential change requires a human to log into each environment's Airflow UI and manually update the Connection form. The meter data provider rotates their SFTP password quarterly — that is 3 environments × 1 connection = 3 manual updates, each with the risk of a typo, a copy-paste error, or a forgotten environment. With 12 connections across 3 environments, the team performs 36 manual credential entries per rotation cycle.

blocks.title: Manual Connection management across environments
blocks.content:
Password Manager          Dev Airflow UI           Staging Airflow UI       Production Airflow UI
       │                       │                         │                         │
       │  copy password         │                         │                         │
       ├──────────────────────▶│  paste into form         │                         │
       │                       │  ✓ correct               │                         │
       │                       │                         │                         │
       │  copy password         │                         │                         │
       ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────▶│  paste into form        │
       │                       │                         │  ✓ correct              │
       │                       │                         │                         │
       │  copy password         │                         │                         │
       ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶│
       │                       │                         │  paste into form        │
       │                       │                         │  ✗ wrong password!      │
       │                       │                         │  (staging password      │
       │                       │                         │   pasted by mistake)    │
blocks.caption: Manual credential management across three environments is error-prone. Each manual step is an opportunity for a copy-paste error that produces a silent misconfiguration — the Connection looks valid in the UI but contains the wrong credentials.

visual-recommendation.description: Flow diagram showing the manual credential copy process across three environments with a highlighted error in the production step, contrasted with the automated Secrets Backend approach.

blocks.content: The Five Problems with UI Connections

rows: Wrong credentials cause authentication failures at runtime
Staging SFTP password pasted into production — 45-minute outage
No audit trail of who changed what credential and when
Weather API token updated without team notification — debugging confusion
Dev, staging, and production configurations drift apart
