Staging had an old connection format that passed tests but failed in production
Credential rotation requires manual intervention per environment
Quarterly SFTP rotation missed in staging — staging tests unreliable for 2 weeks
Credentials stored in a database that may be backed up or replicated insecurely
Database backup contained unencrypted connection passwords — security audit finding

blocks.content: The Security Audit Trigger

The final trigger for SaulėGrid’s configuration migration was a security audit finding. The audit team discovered that the Airflow metadata database backup contained unencrypted connection passwords in the connection table. Any engineer with access to the backup storage bucket could read every production credential by restoring the backup and querying the database. The audit classified this as a high-severity finding requiring remediation within 30 days. Moving credentials to HashiCorp Vault satisfied the requirement because Vault encrypts secrets at rest, controls access through fine-grained policies, and logs every secret read in an audit trail.
What This Module Covers

The migration from UI-based connections to automated configuration management follows a clear progression through this module. Lessons 2 and 3 cover the Secrets Backend — the mechanism that replaces manual credential entry with centralized secrets management in Vault. Lesson 4 covers dynamic connections for systems that require short-lived tokens. Lesson 5 covers environment-specific configuration for non-credential values that differ between environments. Lesson 6 covers managing the entire configuration as version-controlled code. Lessons 7 through 9 cover the operational practices — variable management conventions, credential rotation procedures, and connection auditing — that keep the configuration architecture healthy over time. Lesson 10 synthesizes everything into SaulėGrid's complete configuration stack.

This module replaces manual UI Connection management with a production-grade configuration architecture. Secrets Backends (lessons 2-3) externalize credentials to a dedicated secrets management system — HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault — so that credentials are never stored in the Airflow metadata database and never entered manually. Dynamic Connections (lesson 4) handle credentials that change at runtime, like short-lived tokens. Environment-specific configuration (lesson 5) separates values that differ between environments from DAG code that is identical across environments. Configuration as code (lesson 6) manages the entire Airflow configuration in version-controlled files. The remaining lessons cover variable management, credential rotation, connection auditing, and SaulėGrid's complete configuration architecture.
The Scale of Manual Management

The goal is a configuration architecture where no human ever types a password into a web form, no credential is stored in the Airflow metadata database where it might appear in backups or logs, every configuration change is version-controlled and auditable through the same Git-based review process as DAG code, and deploying to a new environment requires changing a configuration file rather than manually recreating dozens of UI entries. This is not gold-plating — it is the minimum standard for a platform that three downstream teams depend on for production data that drives billing, regulatory reporting, and business forecasting.
The Migration Path

The migration from UI-based connections to a Secrets Backend is incremental — it does not require a big-bang cutover. The lookup chain (Secrets Backend → environment variables → metadata database) means that connections can be migrated one at a time: move the SFTP connection to Vault on Monday, verify it works for a week, then move the PostgreSQL connection. Connections not yet migrated continue to work through the metadata database fallback. SaulėGrid migrated their 12 connections over three weeks, starting with the least critical (monitoring connections) and ending with the most critical (SFTP and warehouse connections), verifying each migration with the airflow connections test command.

blocks.title: The configuration maturity shift
blocks.content: The shift from UI-based connections to Secrets Backends is parallel to the shift from cron to Datasets in Module 1. Both replace manual, error-prone processes with automated, auditable mechanisms. Both seem like unnecessary overhead when the platform is small (3 connections, 1 environment) and become essential when the platform grows (12 connections, 3 environments, quarterly credential rotation). The best time to adopt Secrets Backends is before the first credential incident — the second-best time is immediately after.

A Secrets Backend is an Airflow abstraction that delegates credential storage to an external secrets management system. When a task requests a Connection (by referencing a conn_id), Airflow queries the Secrets Backend before checking the metadata database. If the backend returns credentials for the requested conn_id, those credentials are used. If the backend does not have the conn_id, Airflow falls back to the metadata database. This lookup chain means the migration to a Secrets Backend is non-breaking: existing connections in the metadata database continue to work, and new connections can be added to the backend without removing the old ones.
How the Lookup Chain Works

blocks.title: Connection resolution with Secrets Backend
blocks.content:
Task requests: conn_id = 'sftp_saulegrid_meters'
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. Secrets Backend (Vault)       │
│     Path: secret/airflow/         │
│           connections/             │
│           sftp_saulegrid_meters   │
│                                  │
│     Found? ──▶ YES ──▶ Use these │
│                        credentials│
│     Found? ──▶ NO  ──▶ Continue  │
└──────────────┬───────────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│  2. Environment Variables         │
│     AIRFLOW_CONN_SFTP_SAULEGRID_ │
│     METERS=sftp://user:pass@host │
│                                  │
│     Found? ──▶ YES ──▶ Use this  │
│     Found? ──▶ NO  ──▶ Continue  │
└──────────────┬───────────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│  3. Metadata Database             │
│     SELECT * FROM connection      │
│     WHERE conn_id = '...'        │
│                                  │
│     Found? ──▶ YES ──▶ Use this  │
│     Found? ──▶ NO  ──▶ Error:   │
│                        Connection │
│                        not found  │
└──────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The lookup chain tries each source in order. The Secrets Backend has highest priority — if it has the connection, the metadata database is never queried. This allows a gradual migration: move connections to the backend one at a time without removing them from the database.

visual-recommendation.description: Flow diagram showing the three-level connection resolution chain with Vault, environment variables, and metadata database, highlighting the priority order and the fallback mechanism.

blocks.content: Why External Secrets Management

Storing credentials in the Airflow metadata database has three security weaknesses. First, the database may be backed up to a location with weaker access controls — SaulėGrid's database backup contained unencrypted connection passwords in a storage bucket accessible to the entire engineering team. Second, the database credentials are the keys to all other credentials — anyone with database access can read every connection's password with a simple SQL query. Third, there is no audit trail of credential access — the database does not log which process read which connection's credentials at what time. A dedicated secrets management system like HashiCorp Vault addresses all three: secrets are encrypted at rest with managed keys, access is controlled by fine-grained policies, and every secret read is logged in an audit trail.
Supported Backends

rows: Selected — team has Vault experience from other projects
Good fit for AWS-native deployments; SaulėGrid uses AWS but prefers vendor-neutral
Simpler than Secrets Manager but limited versioning and rotation features
Not applicable — SaulėGrid is on AWS
Not applicable — SaulėGrid is on AWS
Used for Airflow system config; not suitable for connection credentials at scale

blocks.content: SaulėGrid chose HashiCorp Vault because the team already operated a Vault instance for other application secrets, the Airflow Vault provider is mature and well-documented, and Vault provides cross-cloud portability — if SaulėGrid later migrates from AWS to another cloud, the secrets management layer does not need to change. The choice between backends is primarily an infrastructure decision: use the secrets system that the organization already operates and the team already knows. Learning a new secrets management system while simultaneously migrating Airflow's credential management adds unnecessary risk.
Authentication Methods

Vault supports multiple authentication methods for the Airflow service account. AppRole is the recommended method for automated services: it uses a role ID that identifies the application and a secret ID that authenticates it to generate a Vault token. Token authentication uses a pre-generated Vault token stored in the Airflow environment, which is simpler but less secure because the token must be manually rotated. Kubernetes authentication uses the pod’s service account token to authenticate with Vault automatically, which is ideal for KubernetesExecutor deployments. SaulėGrid uses AppRole because their CeleryExecutor runs on EC2 instances where Kubernetes authentication is not available, and AppRole provides better auditability than static token authentication.
The Connection URI Format

Secrets Backends store connections as URI strings or JSON objects. The URI format encodes the connection type, credentials, host, port, and schema in a single string: scheme://login:password@host:port/schema. The JSON format provides the same fields as separate key-value pairs, which is more readable and easier to maintain in version control. SaulėGrid uses the JSON format in Vault because it makes credential rotation straightforward — updating the password field in the JSON is less error-prone than editing a URI string where the password is embedded between the login and the @ symbol.
Security Benefits

Moving credentials from the metadata database to Vault provides three concrete security improvements that SaulėGrid's security team required. First, encryption at rest with managed keys — Vault encrypts all stored secrets using its transit engine, while the metadata database stores connection passwords as plaintext in the connection table. Second, fine-grained access policies — Vault policies restrict which service accounts can read which secrets, while the metadata database grants access to all connections to anyone with database credentials. Third, comprehensive audit logging — Vault logs every secret read with the requesting identity, timestamp, and source IP, while the metadata database does not log connection reads at the application level. These three improvements resolved the security audit findings that prompted the migration.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Vault path convention: secret/airflow/connections/{conn_id} for connections and secret/airflow/variables/{variable_key} for variables. The path convention mirrors the Airflow namespace: a developer who knows the conn_id can predict the Vault path without consulting documentation. The convention is enforced by the Vault policy — the Airflow service account can only read paths matching secret/airflow/*, preventing access to other applications' secrets on the same Vault instance.

The previous lesson explained what Secrets Backends are and why they exist. This lesson covers the practical configuration: how to install the provider package, configure the backend in airflow.cfg, set up the Vault path structure, and verify that connections resolve correctly. The configuration has three components: the Airflow configuration that tells the scheduler which backend to use, the Vault configuration that defines the path structure and access policies, and the connection data stored in Vault that the backend retrieves at runtime. Getting all three aligned is the key to a working Secrets Backend — a misconfiguration in any component produces a 'connection not found' error that can be difficult to diagnose.
Step 1: Install the Provider Package

# Install the HashiCorp Vault provider for Airflow
pip install apache-airflow-providers-hashicorp

# Verify the installation
python -c "from airflow.providers.hashicorp.secrets.vault import VaultBackend; print('Vault provider installed')"

# The provider includes:
# - VaultBackend class for the secrets backend
# - VaultHook for direct Vault interaction from tasks
# - Connection types for Vault server configuration
Step 2: Configure airflow.cfg

# airflow.cfg — Secrets Backend configuration
[secrets]
backend = airflow.providers.hashicorp.secrets.vault.VaultBackend
backend_kwargs = {
    "connections_path": "connections",
    "variables_path": "variables",
    "mount_point": "secret",
    "url": "https://vault.saulegrid.internal:8200",
    "auth_type": "approle",
    "role_id": "airflow-secrets-reader",
    "secret_id_env_var": "VAULT_SECRET_ID"
}

# The backend_kwargs tell the VaultBackend:
# - Where to find connections: secret/connections/{conn_id}
# - Where to find variables: secret/variables/{var_key}
# - How to authenticate: AppRole with a role ID and secret ID
# - The secret_id is read from an environment variable (not stored in config)

The backend_kwargs configure the Vault client that the backend uses to fetch secrets. The connections_path and variables_path define the Vault path structure — with mount_point='secret' and connections_path='connections', the backend looks for connection data at secret/data/connections/{conn_id}. The authentication uses Vault's AppRole method: the role_id identifies the Airflow service account, and the secret_id (stored in an environment variable, not in airflow.cfg) authenticates it. This two-part authentication ensures that even if airflow.cfg is leaked, the attacker cannot access Vault without the secret_id from the environment.
Step 3: Store Connections in Vault

# Store a connection in Vault using the Vault CLI
# This is done once per connection, then managed through Vault's UI or API

# SFTP connection for meter data
vault kv put secret/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters \
    conn_type=sftp \
    host=sftp.meterai.saulegrid.lt \
    port=22 \
    login=saulegrid_airflow \
    password='<quarterly-rotated-password>' \
    extra='{"key_file": "/opt/airflow/keys/sftp_saulegrid.pem"}'

# PostgreSQL warehouse connection
vault kv put secret/connections/postgres_warehouse \
    conn_type=postgres \
    host=warehouse.saulegrid.internal \
    port=5432 \
    login=airflow_writer \
    password='<quarterly-rotated-password>' \
    schema=analytics

# Weather API connection
vault kv put secret/connections/api_weather_forecast \
    conn_type=http \
    host=api.weather-provider.eu \
    port=443 \
    password='<api-key>' \
    extra='{"Content-Type": "application/json"}'

Each vault kv put command stores a connection as key-value pairs at the specified Vault path. The field names used in the command must match the Airflow Connection model exactly: conn_type, host, port, login, password, schema, and extra. When a task requests conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_meters', the VaultBackend reads the key-value pairs from secret/data/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters and constructs an Airflow Connection object from them. The extra field is a JSON string that holds additional connection parameters beyond the standard fields, such as the SSH key file path for SFTP connections or the default request headers for HTTP connections.
Step 4: Verify the Configuration

# Verify that Airflow can resolve connections through the Secrets Backend
# Run from the Airflow CLI on any node (scheduler or worker)

# Test connection resolution
airflow connections get sftp_saulegrid_meters

# Expected output (password masked):
# Id: None
# Connection Id: sftp_saulegrid_meters
# Connection Type: sftp
# Host: sftp.meterai.saulegrid.lt
# Port: 22
# Login: saulegrid_airflow
# Password: ********
# Extra: {"key_file": "/opt/airflow/keys/sftp_saulegrid.pem"}

# Test the actual connection (attempts to connect to the host)
airflow connections test sftp_saulegrid_meters

# Expected: Connection successfully tested

The airflow connections get command verifies that the backend can resolve the conn_id — it reads from Vault and displays the connection fields with the password masked. The airflow connections test command goes further: it instantiates the appropriate hook for the connection type and attempts an actual connection to the host. Both commands should be run after any configuration change — backend setup, Vault path modification, or credential rotation — to verify end-to-end connectivity before the next scheduled DAG Run depends on it. SaulėGrid includes these verification commands in their deployment runbook and credential rotation procedure.
Common Configuration Errors

rows: Vault path does not match backend_kwargs configuration
Verify mount_point + connections_path + conn_id matches the Vault path
Vault policy does not allow the Airflow role to read the path
Update the Vault policy to grant read access on secret/data/connections/*
AppRole secret_id expired or environment variable not set
Regenerate the secret_id and update the VAULT_SECRET_ID environment variable
Network configuration prevents Airflow from reaching the Vault server
Verify network connectivity, security groups, and Vault URL in backend_kwargs
Connection data in Vault has wrong field names or values
Verify field names match Airflow's model (conn_type, not connection_type)

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's deployment checklist includes a verification section for the Secrets Backend that runs after every deployment: verify Vault connectivity (can the Airflow process reach the Vault URL), verify authentication (can the AppRole credentials authenticate successfully), verify path access (can the authenticated session read the connections path), and verify connection resolution (does airflow connections get return the expected connection). The four-step verification catches misconfiguration at the specific point of failure rather than discovering it when the first scheduled task fails at midnight.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: The most common configuration error is a path mismatch between backend_kwargs and the actual Vault path. With mount_point='secret' and connections_path='connections', the backend reads from secret/data/connections/{conn_id} — note the 'data' segment that Vault's KV v2 engine adds automatically. If you stored the connection at secret/connections/{conn_id} (without 'data'), the backend will not find it. The 'data' segment is a Vault KV v2 implementation detail that is invisible in the Vault CLI (vault kv put handles it automatically) but visible in the Vault HTTP API. Always verify with airflow connections get after setup.

Static connections — credentials stored in Vault and resolved at task runtime — handle the majority of external system interactions. Some systems require dynamic credentials: short-lived tokens that expire after minutes or hours, OAuth tokens that must be refreshed before each use, and credentials that are generated on-demand from a master credential. SaulėGrid's weather API switched from a static API key to OAuth 2.0 client credentials in early 2026. The access token expires every 60 minutes, and tasks that run longer than 60 minutes (or tasks that start more than 60 minutes after the token was issued) need a fresh token. Static Vault storage cannot handle this — the token would expire before the next task uses it.
The Token Refresh Problem

With static connections, the credential in Vault is valid for months (until the next quarterly rotation). With OAuth, the credential has two parts: the client ID and client secret (long-lived, stored in Vault) and the access token (short-lived, generated from the client credentials). The access token must be generated fresh for each task execution — or at least refreshed if it is about to expire. Storing the access token in Vault is not a viable solution because Vault does not automatically refresh OAuth tokens, and the token would expire between Vault writes. The solution is to generate the token at task execution time using the long-lived client credentials from Vault.

from airflow.hooks.base import BaseHook
import requests
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class SaulegridWeatherAPIHook(BaseHook):
    """Weather API hook with OAuth 2.0 token management.
    
    Client credentials (client_id, client_secret) are stored in Vault.
    Access token is generated dynamically at connection time.
    """
    
    def __init__(self, weather_conn_id='api_weather_forecast'):
        super().__init__()
        self.weather_conn_id = weather_conn_id
        self._token = None
    
    def get_conn(self):
        """Get connection and generate a fresh OAuth token."""
        # Step 1: Read client credentials from Vault (via Secrets Backend)
        conn = self.get_connection(self.weather_conn_id)
        client_id = conn.login
        client_secret = conn.password
        token_url = conn.extra_dejson.get('token_url', 
            'https://auth.weather-provider.eu/oauth2/token')
        
        # Step 2: Generate a fresh access token
        response = requests.post(token_url, data={
            'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
            'client_id': client_id,
            'client_secret': client_secret,
        })
        response.raise_for_status()
        
        self._token = response.json()['access_token']
        expires_in = response.json().get('expires_in', 3600)
        logger.info(
            f'OAuth token generated for weather API, '
            f'expires in {expires_in} seconds'
        )
        
        return self._token
    
    def fetch_forecast(self, region: str, date: str) -> dict:
        """Fetch weather forecast with auto-refreshed token."""
        token = self.get_conn()  # Fresh token for each call
        
        response = requests.get(
            f'https://api.weather-provider.eu/v2/forecast',
            headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'},
            params={'region': region, 'date': date},
        )
        response.raise_for_status()
        return response.json()

The hook reads the long-lived client credentials from Vault through the standard Connection mechanism (get_connection calls the Secrets Backend). The short-lived access token is generated fresh on every get_conn() call by exchanging the client credentials for a token at the OAuth server. The token is never stored in Vault, never written to the metadata database, and never persisted anywhere — it exists only in the hook instance's memory for the duration of the task execution. When the task completes, the token is garbage-collected. This pattern keeps long-lived secrets in Vault (where they belong) while handling short-lived tokens dynamically (where they must be).
Token Caching Within a Task

For tasks that make multiple API calls within a single execution, generating a new token for each call is wasteful — the token is valid for 60 minutes and the task typically completes in 5 minutes. The SaulegridWeatherAPIHook caches the token in the hook instance: the first get_conn() call generates the token, and subsequent calls within the same task reuse it. Since the hook instance exists only for the duration of the task execution, the cached token is automatically discarded when the task completes. This cache is safe because the token's 60-minute lifetime far exceeds the task's expected duration. If a task were to run longer than the token lifetime, the hook would need a refresh mechanism — but SaulėGrid's weather tasks complete in under 3 minutes.
Connection Lookup Hooks

Airflow 2.8+ supports connection lookup hooks — custom functions that intercept the connection resolution process and modify the returned Connection object before it reaches the task. A lookup hook can generate a fresh token, inject a computed field, or transform the connection based on runtime context (for example, selecting a read replica during business hours and the primary during off-peak maintenance). SaulėGrid does not currently use lookup hooks because their dynamic credential needs are handled within custom hooks (like SaulegridWeatherAPIHook). Lookup hooks become valuable when dynamic credential generation must be transparent to the task code — the task calls get_connection() and receives a Connection with a valid token already populated.
Short-Lived Credentials with AWS IAM

For AWS services, Airflow supports IAM role-based authentication that generates short-lived credentials automatically. Instead of storing an AWS access key and secret key in Vault, the Airflow worker assumes an IAM role that grants temporary credentials via the AWS Security Token Service. The credentials expire after 1 hour and are refreshed automatically by the AWS SDK. SaulėGrid uses this pattern for their S3 access (Bronze data storage, XCom backend): no AWS credentials are stored anywhere — the worker's IAM role provides temporary credentials at runtime. This eliminates an entire class of credential rotation problems for AWS services.

# AWS IAM-based authentication — no stored credentials
# The Airflow Connection for S3 has no login/password fields
# Authentication happens via the worker's IAM role

# Vault path: secret/connections/aws_s3_saulegrid
# {
#   "conn_type": "aws",
#   "extra": {
#     "role_arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/saulegrid-airflow-s3",
#     "region_name": "eu-central-1"
#   }
# }
# Note: no login or password fields — IAM role provides credentials

# In DAG code, the S3Hook uses the Connection and the IAM role handles auth
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.hooks.s3 import S3Hook

hook = S3Hook(aws_conn_id='aws_s3_saulegrid')
hook.load_file(
    filename='/tmp/bronze/meters/2026-03-24.csv',
    key='bronze/meters/date=2026-03-24/meters.csv',
    bucket_name='saulegrid-data',
)
When to Use Dynamic Credentials

rows: Manual rotation via Vault CLI or API
Client ID/secret in Vault, token generated dynamically
Hook generates token on each get_conn() call
Role ARN in Connection, no secrets stored
Manual rotation via Vault CLI or API
Master credentials in Vault, token generated dynamically
Hook generates token via database's token endpoint

blocks.content: Credential Hierarchy Summary

SaulėGrid's external systems span the full credential hierarchy. The S3 storage uses IAM roles with zero stored credentials — the most secure option, available because AWS supports role-based authentication natively. The weather API uses OAuth with client credentials in Vault and dynamic token generation — the second-best option, available because the provider supports OAuth 2.0. The SFTP server and PostgreSQL warehouse use static passwords in Vault with quarterly rotation — the fallback option, used because these systems do not support dynamic authentication. The VERT regulatory API uses a static API key in Vault with annual rotation — the longest-lived credential, accepted because the regulatory API changes slowly and the key is rate-limited to SaulėGrid's registered IP.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: The hierarchy of credential management follows a clear priority: eliminate credentials entirely when possible (IAM roles for AWS services), generate credentials dynamically when elimination is not possible (OAuth tokens from client credentials), and store credentials in Vault as the last resort (static passwords for external systems that do not support dynamic authentication). Each step up the hierarchy reduces the surface area for credential-related incidents: IAM roles have zero credential rotation events, OAuth has per-task token generation with no stored tokens, and static Vault credentials require quarterly rotation with manual verification. SaulėGrid aims to move every connection up this hierarchy as external systems add support for dynamic authentication.

Connections handle credentials. But production pipelines also depend on configuration values that are not credentials: file paths, processing thresholds, feature flags, endpoint URLs, and performance tuning parameters. These values differ between environments — the Bronze path is /data/bronze/ in production and /tmp/test-data/bronze/ in development, the quality rejection threshold is 2% in production and 10% in development (relaxed for testing), and the SFTP remote path prefix is /outbound/production/ versus /outbound/staging/. This lesson covers the patterns for separating environment-specific configuration from DAG code so that the same DAG code runs in all three environments without modification.
The Configuration Hierarchy

SaulėGrid's configuration lives in three layers, ordered by specificity. The first layer is the DAG code itself — the logic, task structure, dependencies, and Dataset definitions that are identical across all environments. The second layer is Airflow Variables — key-value pairs that hold environment-specific values like paths, thresholds, and feature flags. The third layer is the Secrets Backend — credentials that are environment-specific and security-sensitive. The separation principle: DAG code should never contain environment-specific values. If a value changes between environments, it belongs in Variables or the Secrets Backend, not in the DAG file.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's configuration hierarchy
blocks.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 1: DAG Code (identical across environments)   │
│  • Task definitions and dependencies                 │
│  • Dataset URIs and scheduling logic                 │
│  • Operator configurations (except env-specific)     │
│  • Stored in Git, deployed via CI/CD                 │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                      │ reads from
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 2: Airflow Variables (env-specific values)    │
│  • File paths: saulegrid.bronze.path                │
│  • Thresholds: saulegrid.quality.rejection_threshold│
│  • Feature flags: saulegrid.features.dead_letter    │
│  • Managed via CLI, API, or init script             │
└─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                      │ reads from
                      ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 3: Secrets Backend (credentials)              │
│  • SFTP passwords, API keys, database credentials   │
│  • Stored in Vault, per-environment paths           │
│  • Accessed via conn_id resolution                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The three-layer hierarchy separates code (identical everywhere) from configuration (environment-specific) from credentials (security-sensitive). DAG code never contains values from layers 2 or 3.

blocks.content: Using Variables for Environment Configuration

from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.models import Variable

@task
def write_bronze_meters(data_date: str, source_file: str) -> dict:
    """Write meter data to Bronze layer.
    
    The Bronze path comes from a Variable, not from hardcoded code.
    Dev: /tmp/test-data/bronze/meters/
    Staging: /data/staging/bronze/meters/
    Production: /data/bronze/meters/
    """
    # Read environment-specific path from Variable
    bronze_base = Variable.get('saulegrid.bronze.path', default_var='/data/bronze/')
    bronze_path = f'{bronze_base}meters/date={data_date}/'
    
    # Read environment-specific quality threshold
    rejection_threshold = float(Variable.get(
        'saulegrid.quality.rejection_threshold', 
        default_var='2.0'  # Production default: 2%
    ))
    
    # ... write logic using bronze_path ...
    # ... validation logic using rejection_threshold ...
    
    return {
        'bronze_path': bronze_path,
        'rejection_threshold': rejection_threshold,
        'environment': Variable.get('saulegrid.environment', default_var='production'),
    }

The DAG code references Variable.get('saulegrid.bronze.path') instead of hardcoding '/data/bronze/'. In development, this Variable is set to '/tmp/test-data/bronze/'. In staging, it is '/data/staging/bronze/'. In production, it is '/data/bronze/'. The same DAG code — the same Git commit, the same Docker image — runs in all three environments with different behavior controlled entirely by Variables. The default_var parameter provides a fallback for the production case (where the Variable should always exist) and prevents the task from crashing if the Variable is accidentally deleted.
The Variable Performance Warning

Variables accessed at the top level of a DAG file (outside of task functions) are evaluated during DAG parsing, which happens on every scheduler heartbeat — typically every 30 seconds. A Variable.get() call at the top level of a DAG file triggers a metadata database query (or a Vault query if Variables are in the Secrets Backend) on every parse cycle. With 12 DAG files each calling Variable.get() at the top level, the scheduler makes 12 extra database queries every 30 seconds. SaulėGrid experienced this: moving 4 Variable.get() calls from the top level of DAG files into task functions reduced the scheduler's metadata database query count by 40% and improved DAG parsing time by 0.3 seconds per cycle.

The correct placement of Variable.get() inside the task function means the database query executes only when the task actually runs — once per DAG Run, not once per scheduler heartbeat. For a daily DAG, this is the difference between 1 query per day (correct) and 2,880 queries per day (incorrect — 2 per minute × 1,440 minutes). SaulėGrid enforces this convention with a custom AST-based lint rule that scans DAG files for Variable.get() calls outside of function definitions. The lint rule runs in the CI pipeline and blocks merges that violate the convention, catching the performance issue before it reaches production.
Environment Initialization

Variables must be set in each environment before DAGs can run. SaulėGrid manages this with an initialization script that runs during the deployment process: the script reads a YAML configuration file specific to the target environment and creates or updates the corresponding Airflow Variables using the Airflow CLI. The YAML files are version-controlled alongside the DAG code in the Git repository, so the configuration for each environment is auditable, reviewable through the same pull request process as DAG changes, and reproducible from the repository without any manual intervention.

# config/production.yaml — production environment configuration
saulegrid:
  environment: production
  bronze:
    path: /data/bronze/
  silver:
    path: /data/silver/
  gold:
    path: /data/gold/
  quality:
    rejection_threshold: '2.0'
    dead_letter_enabled: 'true'
  monitoring:
    sla_alert_channel: '#saulegrid-alerts'
    freshness_check_interval_minutes: '30'

The environment YAML files are the single source of truth for non-credential configuration in each environment. The initialization script reads the YAML file during deployment and creates or updates the corresponding Airflow Variables. If a variable exists in the YAML file, it is set. If a variable exists in the database but not in the YAML file, it is left unchanged — the script does not delete variables, which allows temporary debugging variables to persist between deployments. SaulėGrid runs the initialization script as a Kubernetes init container that executes before the scheduler starts, ensuring that all variables are set before any DAG parsing begins.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Variable naming convention uses dot-separated namespaces: saulegrid.{domain}.{key}. The saulegrid prefix prevents collisions with Variables from other teams or Airflow plugins. The domain groups related variables: saulegrid.bronze.path, saulegrid.quality.rejection_threshold, saulegrid.monitoring.sla_alert_channel. The convention makes it possible to list all variables for a domain (airflow variables list | grep saulegrid.bronze) and to audit the complete configuration for an environment by dumping all saulegrid.* variables.

The previous lessons covered three configuration mechanisms: Secrets Backends for credentials, Variables for environment-specific values, and DAG code for pipeline logic. This lesson covers the practice of managing all three as code — version-controlled, reviewable, and deployable through CI/CD. Configuration as code means that the state of the Airflow platform (which connections exist, what their parameters are, which variables are set, what the scheduler configuration is) can be reconstructed from the Git repository without manual intervention. If the production Airflow instance is destroyed, the team can rebuild it from the repository and have the same connections, variables, pools, and settings.
The Connections-as-Code Pattern

Storing connection definitions in version-controlled files — rather than only in Vault or the UI — provides audit trail, review process, and disaster recovery. SaulėGrid maintains a connections.yaml file that defines the structure of every connection: the conn_id, conn_type, host, port, and extra fields. Crucially, the YAML file does not contain passwords or secrets — it contains the connection structure with placeholder references to Vault paths. The file serves as documentation ('what connections exist and what are their parameters?') and as the input for the initialization script that creates connections in Vault during deployment.

# config/connections.yaml — connection definitions (no secrets!)
# Passwords and API keys are stored in Vault, not in this file.
# This file defines the connection STRUCTURE for each environment.

connections:
  sftp_saulegrid_meters:
    conn_type: sftp
    host:
      dev: localhost
      staging: sftp.staging.meterai.saulegrid.lt
      production: sftp.meterai.saulegrid.lt
    port: 22
    login: saulegrid_airflow
    # password: stored in Vault at secret/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters
    extra:
      key_file: /opt/airflow/keys/sftp_saulegrid.pem

  postgres_warehouse:
    conn_type: postgres
    host:
      dev: localhost
      staging: warehouse.staging.saulegrid.internal
      production: warehouse.saulegrid.internal
    port: 5432
    login: airflow_writer
    # password: stored in Vault at secret/connections/postgres_warehouse
    schema: analytics

  api_weather_forecast:
    conn_type: http
    host:
      dev: mock-weather.localhost
      staging: api.staging.weather-provider.eu
      production: api.weather-provider.eu
    port: 443
    # password (API key): stored in Vault
    extra:
      token_url: https://auth.weather-provider.eu/oauth2/token

The YAML file uses per-environment host values so that the deployment script can select the correct host for the target environment. The password fields are intentionally absent — the comment documents where the credential is stored (Vault), but the file itself contains no secrets. This means the file can be committed to Git, reviewed in pull requests, and shared with new team members without exposing credentials. The initialization script reads this file, merges the environment-specific values with the credentials from Vault, and creates the complete Connection in Airflow.
The Initialization Script

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Initialize Airflow connections and variables from config files.

Run during deployment to set up the target environment.
Usage: python init_config.py --env production
"""
import yaml
import subprocess
import argparse

def init_connections(env: str):
    """Create connections from connections.yaml + Vault credentials."""
    with open(f'config/connections.yaml') as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    
    for conn_id, conn_def in config['connections'].items():
        # Select environment-specific host
        host = conn_def['host']
        if isinstance(host, dict):
            host = host[env]
        
        # Build the connection URI or JSON
        # Credentials come from Vault (already configured via Secrets Backend)
        # This creates the connection structure in the metadata database
        # as a fallback — the Secrets Backend takes priority at runtime
        cmd = [
            'airflow', 'connections', 'add', conn_id,
            '--conn-type', conn_def['conn_type'],
            '--conn-host', host,
            '--conn-port', str(conn_def.get('port', '')),
            '--conn-login', conn_def.get('login', ''),
        ]
        if conn_def.get('schema'):
            cmd.extend(['--conn-schema', conn_def['schema']])
        if conn_def.get('extra'):
            import json
            cmd.extend(['--conn-extra', json.dumps(conn_def['extra'])])
        
        subprocess.run(cmd, check=True)
        print(f'Created connection: {conn_id} ({env})')

def init_variables(env: str):
    """Create variables from environment-specific YAML."""
    with open(f'config/{env}.yaml') as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    
    def flatten(d, prefix=''):
        for k, v in d.items():
            key = f'{prefix}.{k}' if prefix else k
            if isinstance(v, dict):
                flatten(v, key)
            else:
                subprocess.run([
                    'airflow', 'variables', 'set', key, str(v)
                ], check=True)
                print(f'Set variable: {key} = {v}')
    
    flatten(config)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--env', required=True, choices=['dev', 'staging', 'production'])
    args = parser.parse_args()
    
    init_connections(args.env)
    init_variables(args.env)

The initialization script runs as part of the deployment process: after the Airflow containers start but before the scheduler begins processing DAGs. The script reads the YAML configuration files, selects the environment-specific values, and creates the connections and variables using the Airflow CLI. The script is idempotent: running it twice produces the same result (the CLI's add command updates existing entries). SaulėGrid's CI/CD pipeline (Module 7) runs this script automatically during deployment to staging and production, ensuring that the configuration matches the committed YAML files.
Pool and Configuration Management

The same configuration-as-code pattern extends to pools, Airflow system settings, and any other platform configuration. SaulėGrid's config/ directory contains connections.yaml, variables for each environment (dev.yaml, staging.yaml, production.yaml), pools.yaml (pool definitions with slot counts and descriptions), and airflow-overrides.yaml (non-default Airflow configuration properties). Every configuration change goes through a pull request, is reviewed by a team member, and is applied by the deployment pipeline. No configuration exists only in the UI — if the Airflow instance is rebuilt from scratch, the initialization script recreates the complete configuration.

# config/pools.yaml — pool definitions managed as code
pools:
  sftp_pool:
    slots: 2
    description: 'SFTP connections to meter provider (max 2 concurrent)'
  warehouse_pool:
    slots: 5
    description: 'PostgreSQL warehouse connections (5 of 20 total allocated to Airflow)'
  api_pool:
    slots: 3
    description: 'External API calls — weather and VERT regulatory (rate limited)'
  sensor_pool:
    slots: 3
    description: 'Legacy ExternalTaskSensors (prevents sensor slot exhaustion)'

The pools.yaml file follows the same pattern as connections.yaml and the environment YAML files: it defines the desired state, and the initialization script creates or updates the pools during deployment. The pool descriptions are mandatory because they document the external system's constraint that determines the slot count — a reviewer who sees slots: 2 without explanation cannot assess whether the value is correct. The description 'SFTP connections to meter provider (max 2 concurrent)' tells the reviewer that 2 is the provider's limit, not an arbitrary choice.
Disaster Recovery

The configuration-as-code architecture provides a clear disaster recovery path. If the production Airflow instance is destroyed — database corruption, infrastructure failure, or accidental deletion — the team rebuilds it by deploying the Git repository to a fresh Airflow installation and running the initialization script. The script recreates all connections (structure), variables, and pools from the YAML files. Vault credentials remain intact because Vault is a separate service. The only manual step is verifying that the Secrets Backend can reach Vault and that the airflow connections test commands pass. SaulėGrid tested this disaster recovery procedure in staging and confirmed that a complete rebuild from Git + Vault takes approximately 45 minutes.
Configuration Drift Detection

Configuration drift occurs when the running Airflow instance's configuration diverges from the committed YAML files — typically because someone created a connection or variable through the UI without updating the YAML files. SaulėGrid detects drift with a weekly comparison job: the job reads the current connections, variables, and pools from the metadata database and compares them against the YAML files. Any entry that exists in the database but not in the YAML files is flagged as drift. Any entry in the YAML files with different values in the database is flagged as a value mismatch. The drift report is reviewed in the weekly platform meeting, and the resolution is either updating the YAML files to match the UI changes or reverting the UI changes on the next deployment.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Configuration as code is not about eliminating the Airflow UI — it is about ensuring that the UI reflects the committed configuration, not the other way around. The YAML files in the Git repository are the source of truth. The Airflow UI displays the current state. If they disagree, the repository wins and the next deployment corrects the drift. This principle prevents 'snowflake configurations' where the production environment has settings that exist nowhere in version control, were created by a former team member, and nobody knows whether they can be safely changed.

The junior-level track introduced Variables as simple key-value pairs for non-credential configuration: file paths, threshold values, and feature flags stored in the metadata database and accessed through the Airflow UI or the Variable.get() API. At mid-level, Variables need structure, naming conventions, and performance awareness to remain manageable. SaulėGrid's variable inventory grew from 4 keys in the junior deployment to 35 keys covering paths, thresholds, feature flags, team contacts, monitoring parameters, and environment metadata. Without naming conventions, the variable list became a flat namespace of cryptic keys where each required separate documentation to understand what it controlled and which DAGs consumed it.
Namespaced Variable Keys

SaulėGrid's variable naming convention uses dot-separated namespaces following the pattern {company}.{domain}.{key}. The convention provides three concrete benefits: logical grouping means all bronze-related variables start with saulegrid.bronze and can be listed together, collision avoidance means saulegrid.bronze.path cannot conflict with another team's path variable on a shared Airflow instance, and discoverability means listing all variables with the saulegrid.quality prefix shows every quality-related configuration parameter at once. The namespace structure mirrors the team's mental model of the platform domains: bronze, silver, gold, quality, monitoring, and features.

# SaulėGrid's namespaced variable inventory
airflow variables list | grep saulegrid

# Output:
# saulegrid.environment              = production
# saulegrid.bronze.path              = /data/bronze/
# saulegrid.bronze.sftp_remote_path  = /outbound/production/
# saulegrid.silver.path              = /data/silver/
# saulegrid.gold.path                = /data/gold/
# saulegrid.quality.rejection_threshold = 2.0
# saulegrid.quality.dead_letter_enabled = true
# saulegrid.quality.min_row_count_meters = 100000
# saulegrid.quality.min_row_count_generation = 5000
# saulegrid.monitoring.sla_alert_channel = #saulegrid-alerts
# saulegrid.monitoring.freshness_check_minutes = 30
# saulegrid.features.dataset_scheduling = true
# saulegrid.features.s3_xcom_backend = true
JSON Variables for Complex Configuration

The JSON Variable pattern reduces the number of Variable.get() calls required to retrieve semantically related configuration values. One call retrieves all SLA thresholds as a dictionary instead of six separate calls for individual threshold values, which cuts metadata database query volume when Variables are accessed at task execution time. The pattern also provides update atomicity: modifying the SLA thresholds means editing one JSON document in a single Variable.set() call rather than coordinating updates across six separate variables that could be temporarily inconsistent. SaulėGrid uses JSON Variables for SLA threshold mappings, region configuration sets, file pattern dictionaries, and quality gate parameter bundles.

from airflow.models import Variable
import json

# JSON Variable: complex configuration in a single key
# Stored as: saulegrid.sla_thresholds = '{"bronze_meters": 1.5, ...}'

sla_config = Variable.get(
    'saulegrid.sla_thresholds',
    deserialize_json=True,
    default_var={
        'bronze_meters': 1.5,
        'bronze_generation': 1.5,
        'silver_meters': 3.0,
        'silver_generation': 3.0,
        'gold_billing': 6.0,
        'gold_forecast': 7.0,
    }
)

# Access individual thresholds
bronze_sla = sla_config['bronze_meters']  # 1.5 hours
gold_sla = sla_config['gold_billing']     # 6.0 hours

The JSON Variable pattern reduces the number of Variable.get() calls — one call retrieves all SLA thresholds instead of six separate calls — which improves performance when Variables are accessed at task execution time. It also keeps related values together: updating the SLA thresholds means editing one JSON value rather than coordinating updates across six separate variables. SaulėGrid uses JSON Variables for SLA thresholds, region configurations, file pattern mappings, and quality gate parameters. Scalar values (paths, single thresholds, feature flags) remain as simple string variables for readability.
The Parsing Performance Trap

# Detecting top-level Variable.get() calls with a lint rule
# SaulėGrid uses this as a pre-commit hook and CI check

import ast
import sys

def check_top_level_variable_get(filepath):
    """Flag Variable.get() calls outside of function definitions."""
    with open(filepath) as f:
        tree = ast.parse(f.read())
    
    violations = []
    for node in ast.walk(tree):
        if isinstance(node, ast.Call):
            # Check for Variable.get() at module level
            func = node.func
            if (isinstance(func, ast.Attribute) and 
                func.attr == 'get' and
                isinstance(func.value, ast.Name) and
                func.value.id == 'Variable'):
                # Check if this call is inside a function
                # (simplified — production version uses parent tracking)
                violations.append(node.lineno)
    
    return violations

# Usage in CI:
# python lint_variables.py dags/dag_bronze_meters.py
# ERROR: Variable.get() at line 12 is outside a function definition

The most common Variable performance mistake is accessing Variables during DAG parsing rather than during task execution. DAG files are parsed by the scheduler on every heartbeat cycle (default: every 30 seconds). A Variable.get() call at the module level of a DAG file executes on every parse, generating a metadata database query or Vault request every 30 seconds. Multiply by 12 DAG files and the scheduler makes 24 extra queries per minute — negligible individually, but measurable in aggregate. SaulėGrid's convention is strict: Variable.get() is only permitted inside task functions, never at the DAG file's top level. This convention is enforced by a custom lint rule that flags Variable.get() calls outside of function definitions.
Variables vs Secrets Backend for Variables

The Secrets Backend can store both connections and variables. When variables_path is configured in the backend_kwargs, the backend resolves Variable.get() calls against Vault before checking the metadata database — the same lookup chain as connections. SaulėGrid stores sensitive variables (API rate limit keys, internal system tokens) in Vault alongside connections, and non-sensitive variables (paths, thresholds, feature flags) in the metadata database via the YAML initialization script. The split keeps the Vault request volume manageable — Vault handles the 12 connections and 3 sensitive variables, while the metadata database handles the remaining 32 non-sensitive variables.

SaulėGrid's Secrets Backend configuration stores 3 sensitive variables in Vault and 32 non-sensitive variables in the metadata database. The split is deliberate: putting all 35 variables in Vault would generate 35 Vault API calls on every task that reads variables, adding latency and load to the Vault server. Putting sensitive variables in the metadata database would expose them in backups and to anyone with database access. The split optimizes for both security (sensitive values are encrypted and access-controlled in Vault) and performance (non-sensitive values are read from the local database with minimal latency). The initialization script enforces the split by checking whether a variable key is marked as sensitive in the YAML configuration before deciding where to store it.
Variable Storage Split

The variable management patterns in this lesson interact with the deployment patterns from lesson 6. The YAML configuration files define the non-sensitive variables, the initialization script creates them in the metadata database during deployment, and the Secrets Backend resolves the sensitive variables from Vault at runtime. The complete variable resolution chain is: task calls Variable.get('key') → Secrets Backend checks Vault (for sensitive keys) → if not found, check the metadata database (for non-sensitive keys) → if not found, use the default_var parameter. The chain is transparent to the task code — the task does not know whether the variable came from Vault or the database, maintaining the separation between DAG logic and configuration infrastructure.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: A frequent source of confusion is the interaction between Variables stored in Vault and Variables stored in the metadata database. When the Secrets Backend is configured for variables, Vault is checked first. If a variable exists in both Vault and the database, the Vault version wins — the database version is never seen. SaulėGrid avoids this ambiguity by maintaining a clear split: sensitive variables are in Vault only, non-sensitive variables are in the database only, and no variable exists in both places. The initialization script enforces this split by checking Vault before creating a database variable and raising an error if the key already exists in Vault.

Credential rotation — replacing passwords, API keys, and tokens on a regular schedule — is a security requirement that creates an operational challenge. SaulėGrid rotates all external system credentials quarterly: SFTP passwords, database passwords, API keys, and OAuth client secrets. With UI-based connections, each rotation required a human to update 12 connections across 3 environments (36 manual updates), verify each update, and coordinate the timing so that the old credential was not invalidated before all environments were updated. With the Secrets Backend, rotation is simpler but still requires careful orchestration to avoid pipeline downtime during the transition.
The Rotation Window

The rotation window must be long enough to cover the entire deployment process: updating Vault, verifying the connection, and waiting for the next DAG Run to confirm success. If the rotation window is 1 hour and the deployment takes 30 minutes plus a 45-minute wait for the next Bronze run, the total verification time exceeds the window and the old credential expires before verification completes. SaulėGrid negotiated 24-hour rotation windows with all providers specifically because their overnight processing window means verification can take up to 12 hours — from the 10:00 update to the 00:00 Bronze run. Providers that offer shorter rotation windows require the team to trigger a manual DAG Run for immediate verification.

The rotation window is the period during which both the old and new credentials are valid. The external system must accept both credentials simultaneously so that running tasks using the old credential complete successfully while new tasks use the new credential. SaulėGrid negotiates a 24-hour rotation window with each external system provider: the new password is activated at 10:00, the old password remains valid until 10:00 the next day. The platform team updates Vault during the 10:00-14:00 deployment window, and all tasks that start after the update use the new credentials. Tasks that started before the update and are still running use the cached old credentials from their Connection resolution at task start time.
The Rotation Procedure

# SaulėGrid's credential rotation procedure (quarterly)
# Executed by the platform engineer during the 10:00-14:00 deployment window

# Step 1: Receive the new credential from the provider
# (via secure channel — encrypted email, shared vault, or provider portal)

# Step 2: Update the credential in Vault
vault kv put secret/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters \
    conn_type=sftp \
    host=sftp.meterai.saulegrid.lt \
    port=22 \
    login=saulegrid_airflow \
    password='<new-quarterly-password>' \
    extra='{"key_file": "/opt/airflow/keys/sftp_saulegrid.pem"}'

# Step 3: Verify the new credential works
airflow connections test sftp_saulegrid_meters
# Expected: Connection successfully tested

# Step 4: Repeat for all connections being rotated
# Step 5: Document the rotation in the rotation log
# Step 6: Verify the next scheduled DAG Run succeeds with new credentials

The rotation procedure updates the credential in a single location — Vault — and the change propagates to all environments that use the same Vault instance (staging and production share a Vault instance with separate paths). Development uses mock credentials that do not require rotation. The critical verification step is airflow connections test, which attempts an actual connection using the new credential. If the test fails, the old credential is still valid (within the rotation window), and the platform engineer can debug without time pressure.
Automated Rotation Detection

Even with a documented procedure, rotation can be missed — a busy quarter, a staff change, or a miscommunication with the provider. SaulėGrid's monitoring DAG includes a credential freshness check that verifies when each connection's credential was last updated in Vault. Vault's KV v2 engine stores metadata including the update timestamp for each secret. The monitoring task reads this metadata and fires a warning alert when a connection's credential has not been updated within the expected rotation period (90 days for quarterly rotation). The alert gives the platform engineer a two-week reminder before the credential is likely to expire.

from airflow.decorators import task
import hvac  # HashiCorp Vault client
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

ROTATION_PERIOD_DAYS = 90  # Quarterly rotation
WARNING_BUFFER_DAYS = 14   # Alert 2 weeks before expected expiry

CONNECTIONS_TO_MONITOR = [
    'sftp_saulegrid_meters',
    'postgres_warehouse',
    'api_weather_forecast',
    'api_vert_regulatory',
]

@task
def check_credential_freshness() -> dict:
    """Alert when credentials are approaching their rotation deadline."""
    client = hvac.Client(url='https://vault.saulegrid.internal:8200')
    alerts = []
    
    for conn_id in CONNECTIONS_TO_MONITOR:
        # Read secret metadata (not the secret itself)
        metadata = client.secrets.kv.v2.read_secret_metadata(
            path=f'connections/{conn_id}',
            mount_point='secret',
        )
        
        last_updated = datetime.fromisoformat(
            metadata['data']['updated_time'].replace('Z', '+00:00')
        )
        days_since_update = (datetime.now(last_updated.tzinfo) - last_updated).days
        days_until_deadline = ROTATION_PERIOD_DAYS - days_since_update
        
        if days_until_deadline <= WARNING_BUFFER_DAYS:
            alerts.append({
                'conn_id': conn_id,
                'last_updated': str(last_updated.date()),
                'days_until_deadline': days_until_deadline,
                'action': 'Rotate credential before deadline',
            })
    
    if alerts:
        send_alert(
            channel='#saulegrid-platform',
            message=f'Credential rotation due: {len(alerts)} connections approaching deadline',
            details=alerts,
            severity='warning',
        )
    
    return {'checked': len(CONNECTIONS_TO_MONITOR), 'alerts': len(alerts)}
Post-Rotation Verification

The rotation is not complete until the next scheduled DAG Run succeeds with the new credentials. SaulėGrid's rotation procedure includes a mandatory verification step: after updating Vault, the engineer waits for the next scheduled Bronze DAG Run and confirms that the SFTP download task succeeds. If the next natural run is hours away, the engineer triggers a manual run with a test date to verify immediately. The rotation is logged in the team's rotation registry — a simple table in the operations wiki that records the connection, the rotation date, the engineer who performed it, and the verification status. The registry provides an audit trail for security reviews.
Rotation Failure Recovery

If the new credential fails verification (the airflow connections test command returns an error), the recovery procedure depends on whether the old credential is still valid. Within the rotation window, the old credential works — the team can revert the Vault entry to the old credential and investigate the new credential's issue without pipeline impact. After the rotation window, the old credential has been invalidated — the team must work with the provider to issue a corrected credential or extend the old credential's validity. SaulėGrid's rotation procedure includes a mandatory 'revert test' step: after updating Vault with the new credential, the engineer also stores the old credential in a separate Vault path (secret/connections-rollback/{conn_id}) as a backup for the duration of the rotation window.

# Rotation with rollback safety net
# Step 1: Backup the old credential before rotating
vault kv put secret/connections-rollback/sftp_saulegrid_meters \
    password='<old-password>' \
    backup_date='2026-03-24' \
    reason='Quarterly rotation backup'

# Step 2: Update with new credential
vault kv put secret/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters \
    conn_type=sftp host=sftp.meterai.saulegrid.lt port=22 \
    login=saulegrid_airflow password='<new-password>' \
    extra='{"key_file": "/opt/airflow/keys/sftp_saulegrid.pem"}'

# Step 3: Verify
airflow connections test sftp_saulegrid_meters

# If verification fails:
# vault kv put secret/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters \
#     ... password='<old-password-from-rollback>' ...

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's rotation schedule is synchronized with the provider's rotation cycle. The SFTP provider issues new credentials on the first Monday of each quarter. SaulėGrid schedules the Vault update for the same day during the 10:00-14:00 window. The weather API provider issues new OAuth client secrets via their portal with a 7-day overlap window. SaulėGrid checks the portal on the first day of the new secret's validity and updates Vault immediately. Synchronizing the rotation schedule with the provider's cycle eliminates the risk of using expired credentials because the update happens on the same day the new credential becomes available.

SaulėGrid's platform has 12 connections across 6 external system types. Over time, connections accumulate: a test connection created during debugging, a deprecated connection from an old provider integration, a connection created for a one-time data migration that was never cleaned up. Without auditing, the connection inventory drifts from the documented set — there are connections that exist but are not used, connections that are used but are not documented, and connections whose owners have left the team. Connection auditing is the practice of maintaining a complete, accurate inventory of every connection on the platform, verifying that each connection has a purpose, an owner, and a current credential.
The Connection Registry

The registry serves a different purpose than Vault's secret storage. Vault stores the credentials — the technical data needed to connect. The registry stores the operational context — who owns the connection, when it was last rotated, which DAGs depend on it, and who to contact at the external provider when issues arise. A new team member who needs to debug a failing SFTP connection finds the conn_id in the error log, looks it up in the registry, sees that Ieva owns it, that the provider's support email is meterai-support@saulegrid.lt, and that the last rotation was 6 weeks ago. This context turns a 30-minute investigation into a 5-minute lookup.

SaulėGrid maintains a connection registry — a structured document that lists every production connection with its metadata: the conn_id, the external system it connects to, the owner (which team member is responsible for the credential), the rotation schedule, the consuming DAGs, the Vault path, and the last verification date. The registry is stored in the platform's Git repository alongside the connections.yaml file. The distinction between the two files is that connections.yaml defines the connection structure (for automated deployment) while the registry documents the operational context (for human understanding).

# config/connection_registry.yaml — operational metadata for all connections
# Updated during credential rotation, connection changes, and quarterly audits

registry:
  sftp_saulegrid_meters:
    external_system: SaulėGrid meter data SFTP server
    owner: Ieva
    rotation_schedule: quarterly (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 first Monday)
    vault_path: secret/connections/sftp_saulegrid_meters
    consuming_dags:
      - dag_bronze_meters
      - dag_bronze_reprocess
    last_verified: 2026-03-24
    notes: Provider contact for rotation — meterai-support@saulegrid.lt

  postgres_warehouse:
    external_system: Analytics PostgreSQL warehouse
    owner: Matas
    rotation_schedule: quarterly
    vault_path: secret/connections/postgres_warehouse
    consuming_dags:
      - dag_gold_billing
      - dag_gold_forecast
      - dag_silver_quality_report
      - dag_monitoring
    last_verified: 2026-03-24
    notes: Connection pool limit 20 — Airflow allocated 5 (warehouse_pool)

  api_weather_forecast:
    external_system: Weather forecast provider API
    owner: Matas
    rotation_schedule: on provider notification (OAuth client secret)
    vault_path: secret/connections/api_weather_forecast
    consuming_dags:
      - dag_bronze_weather
    last_verified: 2026-03-24
    notes: OAuth 2.0 — client credentials in Vault, token generated dynamically
Detecting Unused Connections

An unused connection is a security risk — it has credentials that no one monitors, rotates, or needs. SaulėGrid detects unused connections by querying the metadata database for connections that have no associated task instances in the last 90 days. The query joins the connection table with the task_instance and xcom tables to find connections that were never referenced. The quarterly audit reviews this list and either removes the unused connection (if it was temporary) or documents the reason it exists (if it is used infrequently, like the VERT API that runs only on quarterly submission dates).

from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.models import Connection
from airflow.utils.session import provide_session

@task
@provide_session
def audit_connections(session=None) -> dict:
    """Identify connections that exist but may not be in active use."""
    # Get all connections from the metadata database
    all_connections = session.query(Connection).all()
    conn_ids = {c.conn_id for c in all_connections}
    
    # Load the connection registry to find documented connections
    import yaml
    with open('/opt/airflow/config/connection_registry.yaml') as f:
        registry = yaml.safe_load(f)['registry']
    
    documented = set(registry.keys())
    
    # Find discrepancies
    undocumented = conn_ids - documented  # In DB but not in registry
    missing = documented - conn_ids       # In registry but not in DB
    
    result = {
        'total_connections': len(conn_ids),
        'documented': len(documented),
        'undocumented': list(undocumented),
        'missing_from_db': list(missing),
    }
    
    if undocumented:
        send_alert(
            channel='#saulegrid-platform',
            message=f'Connection audit: {len(undocumented)} undocumented connections found',
            details=list(undocumented),
            severity='warning',
        )
    
    return result
Stale Connection Detection

Beyond undocumented connections, the audit detects stale connections — connections that exist and are documented but whose credentials may be expired or whose external systems may have changed. The staleness check compares the registry's last_verified date against the current date: connections not verified within the expected rotation period (90 days for quarterly rotation) are flagged as potentially stale. The distinction between unused and stale is important: an unused connection should be removed, while a stale connection should be re-verified and potentially rotated. SaulėGrid's audit report separates the two categories with different recommended actions.

The audit also checks for connections that are documented in the registry but have no corresponding entry in either Vault or the metadata database — indicating a documentation-only connection that was created in the registry but never actually configured. This reverse check catches a common oversight: an engineer documents a planned connection during sprint planning but forgets to create it in Vault. The consuming DAG would fail at runtime with a 'connection not found' error — the audit catches this gap before the DAG is deployed.
Reverse Audit Check

The audit task compares the metadata database's connection inventory against the documented registry. Connections that exist in the database but not in the registry are flagged as undocumented — they were likely created manually through the UI and need to be either documented or removed. Connections that exist in the registry but not in the database are flagged as missing — they may have been accidentally deleted or not yet created in this environment. SaulėGrid runs this audit monthly as part of the platform maintenance DAG.
Vault Audit Logs

Vault provides an audit log that records every secret access: which service account read which secret at what time. SaulėGrid forwards Vault audit logs to their central logging platform (ELK stack) and uses them for two purposes. First, security monitoring: detecting unusual access patterns, such as a connection being read outside of the normal pipeline execution window or by an unexpected service account. Second, compliance reporting: demonstrating to auditors that credential access is logged, that access is restricted to authorized service accounts, and that credentials are rotated on schedule. The audit logs complement the connection registry by providing evidence of how connections are used in practice, not just how they are documented.
Compliance Reporting

SaulėGrid's security team requires quarterly compliance reports that demonstrate credential management practices: all credentials are stored in an approved secrets system (Vault), all credentials are rotated within the defined period, all credential access is logged, and no credentials are stored in the source code or the metadata database. The connection audit generates the data for this report: the number of connections in Vault versus the metadata database (target: 100% in Vault), the rotation compliance rate (percentage of connections rotated within the rotation period), and the audit log summary showing which service accounts accessed which secrets. The report is generated automatically by the quarterly audit task and delivered to the security team.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's quarterly connection audit checklist: (1) Run the connection audit task and review undocumented/missing connections. (2) Verify that every documented connection has a current owner on the team. (3) Check the credential freshness monitoring for overdue rotations. (4) Review Vault audit logs for unusual access patterns. (5) Remove connections that have no consuming DAGs and no documented purpose. (6) Update the connection registry with any changes. The audit takes approximately 2 hours per quarter and has caught 3 stale connections and 1 orphaned test connection in the first two quarters.

This module has covered six configuration patterns: Secrets Backends for externalizing credentials to Vault, dynamic connections for short-lived tokens, environment-specific Variables for non-credential configuration, configuration as code for version-controlled deployment, namespaced variable management for organized key-value storage, credential rotation for ongoing security, and connection auditing for inventory accuracy. This lesson synthesizes every pattern into SaulėGrid's complete configuration architecture: how the configuration layers interact, how they are deployed, how they are maintained, and how they evolved from the junior-level UI-based approach.
The Configuration Stack

rows: environment YAML in Git → metadata DB
API rate limit keys, internal tokens

blocks.content: The Deployment Flow

When SaulėGrid deploys to production, the deployment pipeline executes three configuration steps after the Airflow containers start. First, the initialization script reads connections.yaml, the environment YAML, and pools.yaml, and creates or updates the corresponding entries in the metadata database. Second, the Secrets Backend configuration in airflow.cfg points to the production Vault path, ensuring that runtime connection resolution reads production credentials. Third, the Helm values file configures the Airflow system settings (executor type, scheduler parameters, worker concurrency). After these three steps, the Airflow instance has the complete configuration: code from Git, non-credential config from YAML files, credentials from Vault, and system settings from Helm values.

The three deployment steps execute in a specific order because each step depends on the previous one. The initialization script must run after the containers start (because it uses the Airflow CLI, which requires a running Airflow process) but before the scheduler begins parsing DAGs (because DAGs reference connections and variables that must exist before parsing). SaulėGrid implements this ordering using Kubernetes init containers: the init container runs the initialization script, waits for confirmation that all connections and variables are created, and only then allows the main scheduler container to start. This startup dependency prevents the race condition where the scheduler begins parsing DAGs before the configuration is ready.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's configuration deployment flow
blocks.content:
Git Repository
├── dags/              ──── deployed to ────▶ Airflow DAGs folder
├── saulegrid_common/  ──── installed as ───▶ Python package in worker env
├── config/
│   ├── connections.yaml ─── init script ──▶ Metadata DB (structure)
│   ├── production.yaml  ─── init script ──▶ Metadata DB (variables)
│   └── pools.yaml       ─── init script ──▶ Metadata DB (pools)
├── helm/
│   └── values-prod.yaml ─── Helm deploy ──▶ Airflow system settings
└── plugins/           ──── deployed to ────▶ Airflow plugins folder

HashiCorp Vault
├── secret/connections/  ──── Secrets Backend ──▶ Runtime credential resolution
└── secret/variables/    ──── Secrets Backend ──▶ Runtime sensitive variable resolution
blocks.caption: Every configuration artifact has a defined source (Git or Vault) and a defined deployment target. No configuration exists only in the UI.

visual-recommendation.description: Architecture diagram showing the configuration deployment flow from Git repository and Vault through the deployment pipeline to the Airflow instance, with clear separation between code, config, and credentials.

blocks.content: The Zero-Manual-Entry Guarantee

SaulėGrid's configuration architecture achieves a zero-manual-entry guarantee: no human ever types a credential into the Airflow web UI, no configuration value is created through the UI without a corresponding entry in the YAML files, and no Vault secret is updated without following the documented rotation procedure. The guarantee is enforced through three mechanisms: the initialization script overwrites any UI-created entries on each deployment (ensuring the YAML files win), the connection audit detects undocumented connections (flagging UI-created entries for removal), and the credential freshness monitor detects stale Vault entries (flagging missed rotations). The three mechanisms form a closed loop: every configuration has a documented source, every change is detectable, and every drift is correctable.

The zero-manual-entry guarantee eliminates the class of incidents that motivated this module: the April 3rd staging-password-in-production incident could not happen because no human enters passwords into the Airflow UI. The missed rotation incident could not happen because the credential freshness monitor alerts two weeks before the deadline. The undocumented connection incident could not happen because the audit detects connections without registry entries. Each incident prevention traces back to a specific pattern from this module — and the patterns work together as a system, not as isolated improvements.
The Migration Journey

SaulėGrid's configuration architecture was built incrementally over two months. Week 1: installed the Vault provider and configured the Secrets Backend for one connection (SFTP) while leaving other connections in the metadata database. Week 2-3: migrated the remaining 11 connections to Vault, verifying each with airflow connections test.
