Week 4: created the connections.yaml and environment YAML files from the current configuration, established the initialization script, and verified that a fresh deployment from Git + Vault produces an identical configuration. Week 5-6: implemented the credential freshness monitor and connection audit tasks. Week 7-8: documented the rotation procedures and the connection registry, then ran the first quarterly rotation using the new process. Each week delivered a working configuration that was strictly better than the previous week — no week required a big-bang migration.
Maintenance Schedule

The maintenance schedule is deliberately distributed across different cadences to prevent maintenance overload. The weekly credential freshness check runs automatically and requires only alert review — 5 minutes per week. The monthly connection audit runs automatically but requires human review of the results — 30 minutes per month. The quarterly credential rotation is the most labor-intensive activity — 4 hours per quarter including Vault updates, verification, and documentation. SaulėGrid schedules the quarterly rotation during the same sprint as the quarterly platform retrospective, combining two platform maintenance activities into one focused work period. The maintenance activities are tracked as recurring calendar events shared across the platform team.

The configuration architecture requires ongoing maintenance on three cadences. Weekly: the credential freshness monitor runs as part of the monitoring DAG and alerts on approaching rotation deadlines. Monthly: the connection audit runs and the results are reviewed by the platform team, with undocumented or unused connections investigated and resolved. Quarterly: credential rotation is performed for all connections, the connection registry is updated, and the configuration YAML files are reviewed for accuracy. The maintenance activities are documented in the operations runbook with checklists, responsible parties, and expected time investment (weekly: 5 minutes of alert review, monthly: 30 minutes of audit review, quarterly: 4 hours of rotation and verification).

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: Production configuration management separates credentials from code through Secrets Backends, separates environment-specific values from pipeline logic through Variables and YAML files, and maintains the configuration's accuracy through rotation procedures, freshness monitoring, and connection auditing — transforming SaulėGrid's configuration from a collection of manually-entered UI fields into a version-controlled, auditable, and automatically-deployed architecture where every value has a documented source and every change is traceable.

chapters.title: Testing Airflow Pipelines
chapters.description: Master systematic testing of Airflow DAGs and tasks: DAG integrity validation, unit testing of task logic, integration testing with test utilities, mocking external systems, and the testing pyramid that catches failures before deployment.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid deployed a new DAG on a Thursday afternoon. The DAG passed code review — the logic was correct, the operators were properly configured, and the Dataset scheduling followed the team's conventions. At midnight, the scheduler logged an error: 'DAG import error — circular import detected.' The DAG file imported a utility module that imported the DAG file back, creating a circular dependency that Python detected at import time. The scheduler could not parse the DAG, which meant no DAG Run was created, which meant no Dataset was updated, which meant every downstream DAG in the chain was never triggered. The entire Bronze-Silver-Gold chain was silently stalled. The on-call engineer discovered the problem at 06:30 when the Gold SLA monitor fired — six and a half hours after the failure began.
The Categories of DAG Failures

DAG code can fail in ways that application code cannot. A Python web application with a circular import fails at startup — the developer sees the error immediately. A DAG with a circular import fails during scheduler parsing — silently, without any visible indication unless the engineer checks the scheduler logs. DAG failures fall into four categories, each invisible in a different way: structural errors (circular imports, duplicate DAG IDs, syntax errors in the DAG file), configuration errors (referencing a connection that does not exist, using a deprecated API), logic errors (a transformation that produces wrong results, a quality gate with an inverted condition), and integration errors (the task works locally but fails when the external system is unavailable or returns unexpected data).

rows: Circular import, duplicate DAG ID, missing operator import
Midnight — scheduler logs an import error, no DAG Run created
CI pipeline — DAG integrity test fails, PR blocked
Midnight — task fails with 'connection not found' on first run
CI pipeline — connection reference validation fails
Quality gate rejects valid records (inverted condition)
Next morning — data team reports missing data in dashboards
CI pipeline — unit test with known-good data catches the inversion
SFTP hook works in dev but provider's prod server has different behavior
Production — task fails on unfamiliar error from the provider
Staging — integration test against provider's test endpoint

blocks.content: The Cost of Untested DAGs

SaulėGrid tracked their DAG-related production incidents over the first two quarters. Of 14 incidents, 9 would have been caught by tests that did not exist at the time: 4 structural errors (import failures, missing dependencies), 3 logic errors (wrong filter conditions, incorrect column references), and 2 configuration errors (typos in connection IDs, missing Variable keys). The remaining 5 incidents were caused by external system changes that no test could have predicted. The 9 preventable incidents consumed a combined 28 hours of engineering time — time spent diagnosing failures at 2 AM that could have been caught in a 3-minute CI pipeline run before the code was deployed.

The cost calculation is not just engineering hours. Each midnight failure delayed Gold data delivery to the billing team, triggered the SLA escalation protocol, and eroded the downstream teams' trust in the data platform. After the third incident in a month, the billing team started maintaining their own backup data extraction process — a shadow pipeline that duplicated effort and diverged from the official numbers. SaulėGrid's investment in testing was motivated not just by engineering efficiency but by the need to rebuild trust with consuming teams.
The Shadow Pipeline Problem

The cost of untested DAGs extends beyond engineering hours. Each production incident triggers the SLA escalation protocol, which involves notifying the on-call engineer, the team lead, and the affected consuming team. Repeated incidents erode the consuming teams' confidence in the data platform's reliability, leading them to build workarounds — shadow pipelines, manual data extractions, and cached copies — that duplicate effort and diverge from the official data. SaulėGrid's billing team maintained a backup extraction process for two months after the third incident in a month, consuming 4 hours per week of an analyst's time. Restoring trust required not just fixing the incidents but demonstrating that a testing system prevented future incidents.
The Testing Investment

Building a test suite requires upfront engineering time — SaulėGrid invested 3 weeks to create their initial 123-test suite. The investment pays off through incident prevention: each prevented incident saves 2-4 hours of on-call engineering time, avoids the downstream impact of late data, and maintains the consuming teams' confidence in the platform. The return-on-investment calculation is straightforward: if the test suite prevents one incident per month and each incident costs 3 hours of engineering time plus business impact, the 3-week investment pays for itself within the first quarter. SaulėGrid's actual prevention rate exceeded this estimate — 18 blocked PRs in two quarters.
What This Module Covers

This module teaches a systematic testing approach organized as a testing pyramid. The base of the pyramid is DAG integrity tests (lesson 2) — fast, numerous tests that verify every DAG file loads without errors. The second layer is structure validation tests (lesson 3) — tests that verify DAG properties like task existence, dependency graphs, and required parameters. The third layer is unit tests for task logic (lesson 4) — tests that verify transformation functions, validation logic, and business rules in isolation from Airflow. The fourth layer is mock-based tests (lesson 5) — tests that verify task behavior with simulated external systems. The top of the pyramid is integration tests (lesson 6) — slower, fewer tests that verify end-to-end behavior against real or realistic test environments.

blocks.title: The Airflow testing pyramid
blocks.content:
/\
                   /  \
                  / IT \
                 /______\          Integration Tests (lesson 6)
                /        \         Few, slow, high confidence
               / Mock Tests\       against real systems
              /______________\
             /                \    Mock-Based Tests (lesson 5)
            /   Unit Tests     \   Moderate count, fast, mock
           /____________________\  external dependencies
          /                      \
         /  Structure Validation  \ Unit Tests (lesson 4)
        /__________________________\ Pure logic, no Airflow
       /                            \
      /   DAG Integrity Tests        \ Structure Tests (lesson 3)
     /________________________________\ DAG properties, params
    /                                  \
   /    Many, fast, catch import errors  \ Integrity Tests (lesson 2)
  /______________________________________\ Every DAG loads correctly
blocks.caption: The testing pyramid for Airflow: many fast tests at the base catch structural errors, fewer targeted tests in the middle catch logic and configuration errors, and a small number of integration tests at the top verify end-to-end behavior.

visual-recommendation.description: Testing pyramid diagram with five labeled layers showing the Airflow-specific test types, their relative count, speed, and confidence level, with example test names for each layer.

blocks.content: The pyramid shape is deliberate: the bottom layers run in seconds and catch the most common errors (structural and configuration), while the top layers take minutes and catch subtler errors (integration and edge cases). SaulėGrid's test suite has 45 integrity tests, 30 structure tests, 25 unit tests, 15 mock-based tests, and 8 integration tests — 123 tests total that run in under 90 seconds in CI. The investment in the base layers pays the highest return: the 45 integrity tests alone would have prevented 4 of the 9 preventable incidents.

The pyramid structure determines the investment priority. SaulėGrid built the integrity tests first (1 day of effort, prevents 4 of 9 historical incidents), then structure tests (2 days, prevents 2 more), then unit tests (1 week, prevents the logic errors), and finally integration tests (1 week, prevents the end-to-end failures). If the team had only 2 days for testing, they would build only integrity and structure tests — and that minimal investment would still prevent 6 of the 9 historical incidents. The pyramid guides not just the test architecture but the development sequence.

blocks.title: The testing mindset shift
blocks.content: Testing DAGs is different from testing application code. Application tests verify that functions return correct values. DAG tests verify that the orchestration platform can parse, schedule, and execute the pipeline correctly. The circular import that caused SaulėGrid's Thursday incident was not a logic error — the transformation code was correct. It was an infrastructure error — the Python module structure prevented the scheduler from loading the DAG. DAG testing must cover both the logic layer (does the code produce correct results?) and the infrastructure layer (can Airflow actually run this code?).

DAG integrity tests are the foundation of the testing pyramid — fast, simple tests that verify every DAG file in the repository can be imported by the Airflow scheduler without errors. An integrity test does not check whether the DAG's logic is correct. It checks whether the DAG can be loaded at all: no circular imports, no syntax errors, no missing module dependencies, no duplicate DAG IDs, and no Python exceptions during the top-level execution of the DAG file. These tests run in under 5 seconds for SaulėGrid's 12 DAG files and catch the most common class of deployment failures — the structural errors that prevent the scheduler from parsing the DAG.
The DagBag Import Test

Airflow's DagBag class is the scheduler's mechanism for loading DAG files. When the scheduler parses the DAGs directory, it creates a DagBag that imports each Python file, executes it, and collects the DAG objects it finds. If a file raises an exception during import, the DagBag records the error but continues loading other files. The integrity test uses the same DagBag mechanism: it loads all DAG files and asserts that the import_errors dictionary is empty. If any file fails to import, the test fails with the specific error message, catching the problem in CI before deployment.

# tests/test_dag_integrity.py
# The most important test file in SaulėGrid's test suite

import pytest
from airflow.models import DagBag


@pytest.fixture(scope='session')
def dag_bag():
    """Load all DAGs once for the entire test session."""
    return DagBag(dag_folder='dags/', include_examples=False)


def test_no_import_errors(dag_bag):
    """Verify that ALL DAG files load without import errors.
    
    This single test catches:
    - Circular imports
    - Missing module dependencies
    - Syntax errors in DAG files
    - Exceptions in top-level DAG code
    - Invalid operator instantiation
    """
    assert dag_bag.import_errors == {}, (
        f'DAG import errors detected:\n'
        + '\n'.join(
            f'  {filepath}: {error}'
            for filepath, error in dag_bag.import_errors.items()
        )
    )


def test_dag_count(dag_bag):
    """Verify the expected number of DAGs are loaded.
    
    Catches accidental DAG deletion or files that define
    no DAG objects (empty or misconfigured files).
    """
    expected_dag_count = 12  # SaulėGrid's production DAG count
    actual_dag_count = len(dag_bag.dags)
    assert actual_dag_count == expected_dag_count, (
        f'Expected {expected_dag_count} DAGs, found {actual_dag_count}. '
        f'DAGs loaded: {sorted(dag_bag.dags.keys())}'
    )

The test_no_import_errors test is deceptively simple — a single assertion that the import_errors dictionary is empty. But this single test catches every structural error that would prevent the scheduler from loading the DAG: circular imports produce an ImportError, missing packages produce a ModuleNotFoundError, syntax errors produce a SyntaxError, and invalid operator configuration produces whatever exception the operator's __init__ raises. The error message includes the filepath and the exception, so the developer knows exactly which file failed and why. SaulėGrid considers this the single most valuable test in their entire suite.
Testing for Duplicate DAG IDs

def test_no_duplicate_dag_ids(dag_bag):
    """Verify that every DAG has a unique ID.
    
    Duplicate DAG IDs cause one DAG to silently overwrite another
    in the scheduler — the last-loaded DAG wins and the first
    disappears without any error message.
    """
    dag_ids = [dag.dag_id for dag in dag_bag.dags.values()]
    duplicates = [dag_id for dag_id in dag_ids if dag_ids.count(dag_id) > 1]
    assert len(duplicates) == 0, (
        f'Duplicate DAG IDs found: {set(duplicates)}. '
        f'Each DAG must have a unique dag_id.'
    )

Duplicate DAG IDs are a particularly insidious problem because Airflow does not raise an error — it silently uses the last-loaded DAG with that ID and ignores the earlier one. If two developers independently create DAGs with the ID 'dag_bronze_meters' in different files, the scheduler loads whichever file it parses last. The 'lost' DAG disappears from the UI with no error, no warning, and no log entry. The duplicate DAG ID test catches this by counting occurrences of each ID and failing if any appears more than once. SaulėGrid has a naming convention (dag_{layer}_{domain}) that reduces the risk, but the test provides the definitive check.
Testing for Top-Level Code Performance

import time

def test_dag_parsing_time(dag_bag):
    """Verify that DAG parsing completes within the time budget.
    
    Slow DAG parsing (>3 seconds per file) indicates heavy
    top-level code that runs on every scheduler heartbeat.
    """
    max_parse_seconds = 3.0  # Per-file parse time budget
    
    for filepath, dag in dag_bag.dags.items():
        # Re-parse the individual file and measure time
        start = time.time()
        test_bag = DagBag(dag_folder='dags/', include_examples=False)
        duration = time.time() - start
        
        if duration > max_parse_seconds:
            pytest.fail(
                f'DAG file parsing too slow: {duration:.1f}s > {max_parse_seconds}s. '
                f'Check for heavy imports or computations at the top level of DAG files.'
            )

The parsing time test catches the performance anti-pattern from Module 5: heavy imports or computations at the top level of DAG files that execute on every scheduler heartbeat. A DAG file that imports pandas at the top level adds 0.5-1.0 seconds to every parse cycle. A DAG file that calls Variable.get() at the top level adds a database query to every parse cycle. The test measures the total parse time for all DAG files and fails if it exceeds the budget, catching performance regressions before they reach the scheduler. SaulėGrid's budget is 3 seconds total for all 12 files — an average of 250ms per file.
Running Integrity Tests in CI

# .github/workflows/dag-tests.yml (excerpt)
# DAG integrity tests run on every push and pull request

jobs:
  dag-integrity:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install apache-airflow==2.8.1
          pip install -e ./saulegrid_common
          pip install pytest
      - name: Run integrity tests
        run: pytest tests/test_dag_integrity.py -v --tb=short
        env:
          AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES: 'false'
          AIRFLOW_HOME: /tmp/airflow

The CI configuration installs the same Airflow version as production and the saulegrid_common package, then runs the integrity tests. The AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES environment variable is set to 'false' to prevent Airflow's example DAGs from interfering with the DAG count test. The AIRFLOW_HOME is set to a temporary directory to avoid conflicts with any local Airflow installation. SaulėGrid's integrity tests complete in under 5 seconds in CI — fast enough to run on every push without slowing the development workflow. This approach ensures consistency across all environments and eliminates the class of failures caused by environment-specific differences.

The integrity test suite is the first test a new team member encounters and the last line of defense before deployment. SaulėGrid treats test_dag_integrity.py as the most critical file in the test suite — it is reviewed with the same rigor as production DAG code, and any modification requires team lead approval. The file is intentionally simple and stable: it rarely changes because the assertions (no import errors, correct DAG count, no duplicates, parse time budget) represent fundamental invariants that apply to every DAG regardless of what it does. A change to the integrity test file is a signal that a fundamental platform convention has changed — which merits broader discussion than a typical DAG modification.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's rule: the DAG integrity test suite must pass before any DAG change can be merged. The GitHub repository is configured with branch protection that requires the dag-integrity CI job to pass before a PR can be approved. This single configuration prevents every structural error — circular imports, syntax errors, missing dependencies, duplicate IDs — from ever reaching production. The rule was implemented after the Thursday circular import incident and has prevented 11 similar errors in the following quarter.

Integrity tests verify that DAGs load without errors. Structure validation tests go further: they verify that DAGs conform to the team's conventions and production requirements. A DAG can load perfectly (passing integrity tests) while missing an SLA parameter, using a non-standard naming convention, or lacking the required Dataset outlets. Structure validation tests encode the pipeline design standards from Module 3 as automated assertions — transforming documented conventions into enforced rules that cannot be bypassed by rushing through code review. This approach ensures consistency across all environments and eliminates the class of failures caused by environment-specific differences.
Testing DAG Properties

The parametrized tests check specific DAG IDs against specific requirements. The test_dag_has_tags test verifies that every production DAG has tags for UI filtering — a convention from the junior-level track that structure tests enforce automatically. The test_gold_dags_have_sla test verifies that Gold DAGs have the SLA callback configured — a requirement from Module 3. Each test failure message references the relevant module and lesson, helping the developer understand not just what to fix but why the requirement exists. SaulėGrid's structure tests encode 8 of the 9 pipeline design standards from Module 3 as automated assertions.
Testing Task Existence and Dependencies

The dependency and retry tests encode production standards as code. The test_bronze_meters_task_structure test verifies that the required tasks exist and have Dataset outlets — catching the scenario where a developer removes the outlets parameter during a refactoring. The test_bronze_dags_have_retries test verifies that tasks assigned to external system pools have adequate retry configuration — catching the scenario where a new task is added without configuring retries. Each test acts as a guardrail that prevents standards violations from reaching production regardless of code review thoroughness.
Testing Naming Conventions

Naming convention tests prevent the gradual erosion of consistency that happens when different engineers use different naming styles. Without the test, one developer might name their DAG 'bronze_weather_pipeline' while another uses 'dag_bronze_weather' — both work, but the inconsistency makes the Airflow UI harder to navigate and the command-line harder to script. The regex-based assertions are strict but documented: the error message includes the expected pattern and examples, so the developer knows exactly what to change. This approach ensures consistency across all environments and eliminates the class of failures caused by environment-specific differences.
Connection Reference Validation

One of the most valuable structure tests verifies that every connection ID referenced in the DAGs actually exists in the connection registry. SaulėGrid's test reads the connection_registry.yaml file and compares it against the conn_id values used in operators and hooks across all DAG files. A new DAG that references conn_id='sftp_meters' (missing the 'saulegrid_' prefix) would be caught by this test before deployment, preventing the midnight 'connection not found' error that disrupted the first three months of production. This approach ensures consistency across all environments and eliminates the class of failures caused by environment-specific differences.

import yaml

def test_connection_references_exist(dag_bag):
    """Every conn_id referenced in DAGs must exist in the connection registry."""
    # Load the known connections from the registry
    with open('config/connection_registry.yaml') as f:
        registry = yaml.safe_load(f)['registry']
    known_conn_ids = set(registry.keys())
    
    # Extract conn_id references from all tasks
    referenced_conn_ids = set()
    for dag in dag_bag.dags.values():
        for task in dag.tasks:
            # Check common conn_id parameter names
            for attr in ('conn_id', 'ssh_conn_id', 'postgres_conn_id',
                         'http_conn_id', 'aws_conn_id', 'sftp_conn_id'):
                conn_id = getattr(task, attr, None)
                if conn_id:
                    referenced_conn_ids.add(conn_id)
    
    # Find references to non-existent connections
    unknown = referenced_conn_ids - known_conn_ids
    assert not unknown, (
        f'DAGs reference unknown connection IDs: {unknown}. '
        f'Add these to config/connection_registry.yaml or fix the conn_id.'
    )

The connection reference test is particularly valuable because connection typos are silent until runtime. A DAG that references conn_id='sftp_meters' instead of 'sftp_saulegrid_meters' loads without errors (the conn_id is just a string parameter), passes all integrity tests, and looks correct in code review. The error only manifests when the task executes and Airflow tries to resolve the connection — typically at midnight when the Bronze DAG runs. The connection reference test catches this class of error by cross-referencing every conn_id in the DAG code against the known connection registry, turning a midnight failure into a CI failure.

SaulėGrid's structure tests are organized by concern rather than by DAG: test_dag_naming.py covers naming conventions for all DAGs, test_dag_parameters.py covers required parameters like SLA callbacks and retry counts, test_dag_connections.py covers connection reference validation against the registry, and test_dag_dependencies.py covers Dataset outlets and scheduling configuration. This organization by concern makes it straightforward to find the test that enforces a specific convention and to add new tests when new conventions are established. Each test file has a docstring that explains the conventions it enforces and links to the relevant section of the team's standards document.

blocks.title: Test maintenance
blocks.content: Structure validation tests must evolve with the platform. When SaulėGrid adds a new DAG, the parametrized test lists must be updated. When a new convention is established, a new test must be written. When a convention changes, the corresponding test must be updated. SaulėGrid treats test updates as part of the same PR that introduces the convention change — a new pipeline design standard is not considered complete until the corresponding test exists. The test suite is the executable specification of the team's conventions.

Integrity and structure tests verify the DAG as an orchestration artifact — they check the container without checking the contents. Unit tests verify the contents: the Python functions that tasks execute. A transformation function that should filter negative daily_kw values but accidentally filters positive values is invisible to integrity and structure tests — the DAG loads correctly, has the right parameters, and follows naming conventions. Only a unit test that passes known data through the function and checks the output catches the logic error. Unit testing task logic is the layer of the pyramid that prevents business-logic bugs from reaching production.
Separating Logic from Orchestration

The key to testable task code is separating the business logic (the transformation, the validation, the computation) from the Airflow context (the task decorator, XCom, connections). A function that accepts a DataFrame, applies quality filters, and returns the filtered DataFrame is a pure Python function that can be tested with pytest and no Airflow installation. A function that reads from XCom, queries a Connection, and writes to an Airflow Variable is coupled to the Airflow runtime and requires mocking to test. SaulėGrid's convention is that every task's business logic lives in a function in the saulegrid_common package, and the @task decorator in the DAG file is a thin wrapper that calls the business function.
The Separation in Practice

The separation pattern has a concrete directory structure: business logic lives in saulegrid_common/transformations/ (testable without Airflow), DAG definitions live in dags/ (thin wrappers that compose business functions), and tests live in tests/ (pytest files that import from saulegrid_common directly). A developer writing a new quality filter starts in the transformations module, writes the function with its unit tests, verifies they pass locally, then writes the DAG wrapper that calls the function. The DAG wrapper is typically 10-15 lines — just enough to read the input, call the business function, write the output, and return metadata via XCom. The business function can be 50-100 lines of tested, documented logic.

# saulegrid_common/transformations/meter_transform.py
# Pure business logic — no Airflow imports, no decorators

def filter_quality(records: list[dict], rejection_threshold: float = 2.0) -> tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]:
    """Filter meter records by quality rules.
    
    Returns (valid_records, rejected_records).
    
    Quality rules:
    1. meter_id must not be null
    2. daily_kw must not be negative for non-prosumers
    3. date must not be in the future
    4. region must be a known SaulėGrid region
    """
    valid = []
    rejected = []
    
    for record in records:
        rejection = _validate_record(record)
        if rejection:
            rejected.append({**record, 'rejection_reason': rejection})
        else:
            valid.append(record)
    
    return valid, rejected


def _validate_record(record: dict) -> str | None:
    """Validate a single record. Returns rejection reason or None."""
    if not record.get('meter_id'):
        return 'NULL_METER_ID'
    if record.get('daily_kw', 0) < 0 and record.get('customer_type') != 'PROSUMER':
        return 'NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER'
    # ... additional validations ...
    return None


# dags/dag_silver_meters.py
# Thin wrapper — calls the business function
from airflow.decorators import task
from saulegrid_common.transformations.meter_transform import filter_quality

@task
def transform_meters(data_date: str) -> dict:
    records = read_bronze_meters(data_date)
    valid, rejected = filter_quality(records)
    write_silver(valid, data_date)
    write_dead_letter(rejected, data_date)
    return {'valid': len(valid), 'rejected': len(rejected)}

The filter_quality function is a pure Python function: it takes a list of dictionaries, applies validation rules, and returns two lists. No Airflow imports, no decorators, no connections, no XCom. This purity makes it directly testable with standard pytest assertions. The @task wrapper in the DAG file calls the business function and handles the Airflow-specific concerns (reading from Bronze, writing to Silver, returning XCom metadata). The separation is the architectural enabler for unit testing — without it, testing the quality filter requires mocking the entire Airflow runtime.
Writing the Unit Tests

# tests/test_meter_transform.py
import pytest
from saulegrid_common.transformations.meter_transform import filter_quality


# Test fixtures — known data with expected outcomes
VALID_RECORD = {
    'meter_id': 'M-001', 'daily_kw': 15.2, 'date': '2026-03-24',
    'region': 'VILNIUS', 'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL',
}

PROSUMER_NEGATIVE = {
    'meter_id': 'M-002', 'daily_kw': -8.5, 'date': '2026-03-24',
    'region': 'KAUNAS', 'customer_type': 'PROSUMER',
}

NULL_METER_ID = {
    'meter_id': None, 'daily_kw': 15.2, 'date': '2026-03-24',
    'region': 'VILNIUS', 'customer_type': 'RESIDENTIAL',
}

NEGATIVE_NON_PROSUMER = {
    'meter_id': 'M-003', 'daily_kw': -5.0, 'date': '2026-03-24',
    'region': 'KLAIPEDA', 'customer_type': 'COMMERCIAL',
}


def test_valid_records_pass():
    """Valid records should appear in the valid list."""
    valid, rejected = filter_quality([VALID_RECORD])
    assert len(valid) == 1
    assert len(rejected) == 0
    assert valid[0]['meter_id'] == 'M-001'


def test_prosumer_negative_kw_is_valid():
    """Prosumers with negative daily_kw are valid (generation)."""
    valid, rejected = filter_quality([PROSUMER_NEGATIVE])
    assert len(valid) == 1
    assert len(rejected) == 0


def test_null_meter_id_rejected():
    """Records with null meter_id should be rejected."""
    valid, rejected = filter_quality([NULL_METER_ID])
    assert len(valid) == 0
    assert len(rejected) == 1
    assert rejected[0]['rejection_reason'] == 'NULL_METER_ID'


def test_negative_kw_non_prosumer_rejected():
    """Non-prosumer records with negative daily_kw should be rejected."""
    valid, rejected = filter_quality([NEGATIVE_NON_PROSUMER])
    assert len(valid) == 0
    assert len(rejected) == 1
    assert rejected[0]['rejection_reason'] == 'NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER'


def test_mixed_batch():
    """A batch with mixed records should correctly separate valid and rejected."""
    records = [VALID_RECORD, PROSUMER_NEGATIVE, NULL_METER_ID, NEGATIVE_NON_PROSUMER]
    valid, rejected = filter_quality(records)
    assert len(valid) == 2   # VALID_RECORD + PROSUMER_NEGATIVE
    assert len(rejected) == 2  # NULL_METER_ID + NEGATIVE_NON_PROSUMER
Test Organization

SaulėGrid organizes unit tests to mirror the source code structure: saulegrid_common/transformations/meter_transform.py has a corresponding tests/test_meter_transform.py. This one-to-one mapping makes it easy to find the tests for any source file and ensures that code review includes both the implementation and its tests. The tests directory also contains shared test utilities (tests/conftest.py for fixtures, tests/assertions.py for custom assertions) and test data (tests/fixtures/ for fixture files). The mirrored structure is enforced by convention, not by tooling — but the monthly test audit verifies that every source module has a corresponding test module.

Each test has a single responsibility: test_valid_records_pass verifies the happy path, test_prosumer_negative_kw_is_valid verifies the prosumer exception, test_null_meter_id_rejected verifies null handling, and test_mixed_batch verifies correct separation when valid and invalid records are mixed. The test fixtures at the top of the file define known records with predictable validation outcomes. A developer reading these tests understands the validation rules without reading the implementation — the tests serve as executable documentation of the business logic. The thin wrapper pattern keeps the DAG file readable — a reviewer can see the task structure without wading through transformation logic.
Testing Edge Cases

The most valuable unit tests cover edge cases — inputs that are technically valid but boundary-level. SaulėGrid's edge case tests include: a meter record with daily_kw exactly equal to 0 (valid — zero consumption is legitimate for vacant properties), a prosumer with daily_kw equal to 0 (valid — neither consuming nor generating), a record with an empty string for region (rejected — empty string is not the same as a valid region), and a batch with zero records (returns two empty lists without error). These edge cases are the scenarios that production data produces unexpectedly — the transform works correctly for 99% of records and silently mishandles the 1% that hit a boundary condition.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's unit test convention: every quality gate function must have at least one positive test (known-good data passes), one negative test per rejection rule (each rejection code is triggered), one edge case test per boundary condition, and one batch test that mixes valid and invalid records. The convention ensures that both the acceptance path and the rejection path are tested — a quality gate that accepts everything is as dangerous as one that rejects everything. The test count per function typically ranges from 6 to 12, depending on the number of validation rules and boundary conditions.

Unit tests verify pure business logic — functions that take data in and return data out. But many task functions interact with external systems: SFTP servers, HTTP APIs, PostgreSQL databases, and S3 storage. Testing these functions requires either a real external system (slow, unreliable, potentially costly) or a simulated one (fast, deterministic, free). Mocking replaces real external system interactions with controlled simulations that return predetermined responses. The test verifies that the task logic handles the responses correctly without actually connecting to the external system. SaulėGrid mocks all external systems in their unit and structure tests, reserving real system interaction for the integration tests at the top of the pyramid.
Mocking Hooks and Connections

# tests/test_bronze_ingestion.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock
from saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion import MeterIngestionOperator


@patch('saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion.SaulegridSFTPHook')
def test_ingestion_operator_success(mock_hook_class):
    """Verify the ingestion operator processes a successful download."""
    # Configure the mock to return a successful download result
    mock_hook = MagicMock()
    mock_hook.download_file.return_value = {
        'filename': 'saulegrid_meters_2026-03-24.csv',
        'file_size': 1024000,
        'download_seconds': 12.3,
        'status': 'success',
    }
    mock_hook_class.return_value = mock_hook
    
    # Create the operator and execute with a mock context
    operator = MeterIngestionOperator(
        task_id='test_ingest',
        source_type='meters',
        sftp_conn_id='sftp_saulegrid_meters',
        min_row_count=100,
        validation_mode='strict',
    )
    
    # Mock the file operations
    with patch('os.path.getsize', return_value=1024000), \
         patch('os.makedirs'), \
         patch('os.rename'), \
         patch.object(operator, '_count_rows', return_value=1200000):
        
        result = operator.execute(context={'ds': '2026-03-24'})
    
    # Verify the result
    assert result['status'] == 'success'
    assert result['row_count'] == 1200000
    
    # Verify the hook was called with correct parameters
    mock_hook.download_file.assert_called_once()
    call_args = mock_hook.download_file.call_args
    assert 'meters' in call_args.kwargs['file_type']

The @patch decorator replaces the SaulegridSFTPHook class with a MagicMock for the duration of the test. When the operator instantiates the hook inside its execute() method, it gets the mock instead of the real hook. The mock's download_file method returns a predetermined success result — no SFTP server is contacted, no file is downloaded, and the test completes in milliseconds. The assertions verify two things: that the operator's return value contains the expected metadata, and that the hook was called with the correct parameters. This dual verification ensures both the output correctness and the interaction correctness.
Mocking Airflow Connections

Tasks that use Airflow Connections (via hooks or operators) need the connection to be resolved during testing. Rather than creating real connections in a test metadata database, SaulėGrid mocks the connection resolution at the hook level. The @patch.object decorator replaces the hook's get_connection method with a mock that returns a MagicMock with the expected connection fields (host, login, password, extra). This approach is faster than creating real connections (no database setup) and more controlled (the test specifies exactly what credentials the hook receives), while still exercising the full code path from connection resolution through hook interaction to task output.
Testing Failure Scenarios

@patch('saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion.SaulegridSFTPHook')
def test_ingestion_operator_low_row_count_strict(mock_hook_class):
    """Verify strict validation rejects files with too few rows."""
    mock_hook = MagicMock()
    mock_hook.download_file.return_value = {'status': 'success', 'file_size': 100}
    mock_hook_class.return_value = mock_hook
    
    operator = MeterIngestionOperator(
        task_id='test_ingest',
        source_type='meters',
        min_row_count=100000,  # Expect at least 100K rows
        validation_mode='strict',
    )
    
    with patch('os.path.getsize', return_value=100), \
         patch('os.makedirs'), \
         patch.object(operator, '_count_rows', return_value=50):  # Only 50 rows
        
        # Strict mode should raise ValueError for low row count
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match='has 50 rows, minimum is 100,000'):
            operator.execute(context={'ds': '2026-03-24'})


@patch('saulegrid_common.operators.ingestion.SaulegridSFTPHook')
def test_ingestion_operator_connection_failure(mock_hook_class):
    """Verify the operator propagates SFTP connection errors."""
    mock_hook = MagicMock()
    mock_hook.download_file.side_effect = ConnectionError('SFTP server unreachable')
    mock_hook_class.return_value = mock_hook
    
    operator = MeterIngestionOperator(
        task_id='test_ingest',
        source_type='meters',
    )
    
    with pytest.raises(ConnectionError, match='SFTP server unreachable'):
        operator.execute(context={'ds': '2026-03-24'})

Failure scenario tests are at least as important as success tests. The test_ingestion_operator_low_row_count_strict test verifies that the operator raises a ValueError when the downloaded file has fewer rows than the threshold — catching the scenario where the mock returns successfully but the data is incomplete. The test_ingestion_operator_connection_failure test verifies that SFTP connection errors propagate correctly — the operator should not catch and silently swallow connection errors, because Airflow's retry mechanism needs the exception to trigger a retry. These tests prevent the anti-pattern of over-broad exception handling that masks failures.
Mocking HTTP APIs

import responses

@responses.activate
def test_weather_api_success():
    """Verify weather hook handles successful API response."""
    # Mock the OAuth token endpoint
    responses.add(
        responses.POST,
        'https://auth.weather-provider.eu/oauth2/token',
        json={'access_token': 'test-token', 'expires_in': 3600},
        status=200,
    )
    
    # Mock the forecast endpoint
    responses.add(
        responses.GET,
        'https://api.weather-provider.eu/v2/forecast',
        json={'region': 'VILNIUS', 'temp_c': 12.5, 'cloud_cover': 0.3},
        status=200,
    )
    
    hook = SaulegridWeatherAPIHook(weather_conn_id='api_weather_forecast')
    # Mock the connection resolution
    with patch.object(hook, 'get_connection') as mock_conn:
        mock_conn.return_value = MagicMock(
            login='client_id', password='client_secret',
            extra_dejson={'token_url': 'https://auth.weather-provider.eu/oauth2/token'}
        )
        result = hook.fetch_forecast(region='VILNIUS', date='2026-03-24')
    
    assert result['region'] == 'VILNIUS'
    assert result['temp_c'] == 12.5

The responses library provides a convenient decorator for mocking HTTP endpoints. The @responses.activate decorator intercepts all HTTP requests made within the test and routes them to the registered mock responses. The test registers two mock endpoints: the OAuth token endpoint (returns a test token) and the forecast endpoint (returns test weather data). The test then calls the hook's fetch_forecast method and verifies that the returned data matches the mock response. No HTTP request leaves the test process — the entire API interaction is simulated locally, making the test fast and deterministic.
When to Mock vs When to Use Real Systems

rows: Hook tests, operator tests, transform tests
DagBag loading only — no tasks execute
DAG import, parameter validation, naming checks
Real test endpoints or local containers
Docker Compose with test databases and mock APIs
End-to-end Silver transformation against test data
Full pipeline run against staging SFTP and staging DB

blocks.content: Mock Verification Patterns

Mocks provide two types of verification: output verification (did the function return the expected result?) and interaction verification (did the function call the right methods with the right arguments?). SaulėGrid uses both in every mock-based test. The assert_called_once() method verifies that the hook was invoked exactly once — catching bugs where the task calls the hook zero times (logic error) or multiple times (retry bug). The call_args inspection verifies that the hook received the correct file path, connection ID, and other parameters — catching bugs where the task passes the wrong argument to the hook. Both verifications are essential because a test that only checks the output misses incorrect interactions that produce coincidentally correct results.

blocks.title: The mocking boundary
blocks.content: The mocking boundary defines what is real and what is simulated in each test. Moving the boundary too far toward 'everything mocked' produces tests that pass even when the real system interaction is broken. Moving the boundary too far toward 'everything real' produces tests that are slow, flaky, and dependent on external system availability. SaulėGrid's boundary is deliberate: unit and structure tests mock everything (fast, reliable), integration tests use Docker containers for databases and mock servers for APIs (slower but realistic), and staging tests use real provider endpoints (slowest but highest confidence). Each layer catches different failure classes, and together they provide comprehensive coverage.

Unit tests with mocks verify that each component works correctly in isolation. Integration tests verify that the components work correctly together — that the operator uses the hook correctly, that the hook interacts with the database correctly, and that the end-to-end data flow produces the expected output. SaulėGrid's integration tests run the Silver transformation task against a small fixture dataset using Airflow's test utilities, verifying that the complete pipeline — read Bronze data, apply quality filters, write Silver output, route rejected records to the dead-letter table — produces the expected output files with the expected content.
Airflow's dag.test() Method

Airflow 2.5+ provides a dag.test() method that executes a DAG in a test context — a lightweight mode that runs the DAG's tasks sequentially without the scheduler, executor, or metadata database. The method is useful for quick validation during development: a developer can call dag.test() from a Python script or notebook to verify that the DAG's tasks execute without errors. For CI integration testing, SaulėGrid uses a more controlled approach: they run individual tasks with test-mode configuration that points to a fixture dataset and a temporary output directory.

# Quick DAG validation during development using dag.test()
# Run from a Python script or Jupyter notebook — not in CI

from dags.dag_silver_meters import dag_silver_meters

# Execute the DAG in test mode (sequential, no scheduler)
dag_silver_meters.test(
    execution_date='2026-03-24',
    # Optional: override connections for testing
    conn_file_path='tests/connections.yaml',
)

# tests/integration/test_silver_transform.py
import pytest
import os
import shutil
from airflow.models import DagBag


FIXTURE_DIR = 'tests/fixtures/bronze/meters/'
OUTPUT_DIR = '/tmp/test-silver/meters/'
DEAD_LETTER_DIR = '/tmp/test-dead-letter/meters/'


@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def clean_output():
    """Clean output directories before and after each test."""
    for d in (OUTPUT_DIR, DEAD_LETTER_DIR):
        if os.path.exists(d):
            shutil.rmtree(d)
        os.makedirs(d, exist_ok=True)
    yield
    for d in (OUTPUT_DIR, DEAD_LETTER_DIR):
        if os.path.exists(d):
            shutil.rmtree(d)


def test_silver_transform_end_to_end():
    """Integration test: Bronze fixture → Silver transform → verify output."""
    from saulegrid_common.transformations.meter_transform import filter_quality
    
    # Read the fixture data (100 records: 95 valid, 5 with known issues)
    import csv
    with open(os.path.join(FIXTURE_DIR, 'test_meters.csv')) as f:
        records = list(csv.DictReader(f))
    
    # Run the transformation
    valid, rejected = filter_quality(records)
    
    # Verify counts
    assert len(valid) == 95, f'Expected 95 valid records, got {len(valid)}'
    assert len(rejected) == 5, f'Expected 5 rejected records, got {len(rejected)}'
    
    # Verify specific rejections
    rejection_codes = [r['rejection_reason'] for r in rejected]
    assert 'NULL_METER_ID' in rejection_codes
    assert 'NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER' in rejection_codes
    
    # Verify output data integrity
    valid_meter_ids = {r['meter_id'] for r in valid}
    assert None not in valid_meter_ids  # No null IDs in valid output
    assert '' not in valid_meter_ids    # No empty IDs in valid output

The integration test uses a fixture file (test_meters.csv) containing 100 records with known characteristics: 95 valid records and 5 records with specific quality issues (null meter_id, negative daily_kw for a non-prosumer, future date, unknown region, and empty region). The test runs the transformation against this fixture and verifies both the aggregate counts (95 valid, 5 rejected) and the specific rejection codes. If a developer modifies the validation logic, the integration test catches any change in rejection behavior — even changes that the unit tests might miss because the unit tests use individual records while the integration test uses a realistic mixed batch.
Fixture Data Design

The quality of integration tests depends on the quality of the fixture data. SaulėGrid's fixture design follows three principles. First, the fixture must be small enough to run in seconds but large enough to exercise all code paths — 100 records is sufficient for the Silver transformation. Second, the fixture must contain known-good and known-bad records with predictable outcomes — the test asserts exact counts because every fixture record's validation result is predetermined. Third, the fixture must be version-controlled alongside the test code — changes to the fixture require the same review process as changes to the test assertions. SaulėGrid maintains fixtures in tests/fixtures/ with a README documenting the expected characteristics of each fixture file.

SaulėGrid extends the fixture design principles with expected output files: alongside the input fixture (test_meters.csv), the fixture directory contains expected output files (silver_meters_expected.csv and dead_letter_expected.csv). The integration test runs the transformation against the input fixture, writes the output to a temporary directory, and compares the output file against the expected output file — byte-for-byte for deterministic transformations or row-count-and-schema for transformations with non-deterministic ordering. The expected output files are generated once by running the transformation manually on the fixture, verifying the output visually, and committing the verified output as the expected baseline. Any code change that produces different output causes the comparison to fail, alerting the developer to the behavioral change.

# tests/fixtures/ directory structure
# Every pipeline stage has its own fixture directory

tests/fixtures/
├── bronze/
│   ├── meters/
│   │   ├── test_meters.csv          # 100 records: 95 valid, 5 known issues
│   │   └── README.md                 # Documents expected characteristics
│   ├── generation/
│   │   └── test_generation.csv       # 50 records: 48 valid, 2 known issues
│   └── weather/
│       └── test_forecast.json        # 10 regions, 7 days forecast
├── expected_output/
│   ├── silver_meters_expected.csv    # Expected Silver output for test_meters.csv
│   └── dead_letter_expected.csv      # Expected rejections for test_meters.csv
└── sftp/
    └── saulegrid_meters_2026-03-24.csv  # Mock SFTP file for download tests
The Idempotency Integration Test

def test_silver_transform_idempotent():
    """Verify that running the transformation twice produces identical output.
    
    This is the idempotency test from Module 3, lesson 1 —
    implemented as an automated integration test.
    """
    import csv
    with open(os.path.join(FIXTURE_DIR, 'test_meters.csv')) as f:
        records = list(csv.DictReader(f))
    
    # First execution
    valid_1, rejected_1 = filter_quality(records)
    
    # Second execution (same input — simulates a retry)
    valid_2, rejected_2 = filter_quality(records)
    
    # Outputs must be identical
    assert len(valid_1) == len(valid_2)
    assert len(rejected_1) == len(rejected_2)
    
    # Compare record-by-record (order-sensitive)
    for r1, r2 in zip(valid_1, valid_2):
        assert r1 == r2, f'Idempotency violation: records differ between runs'

The idempotency integration test is the automated version of the manual idempotency test described in Module 3. It runs the transformation twice with the same input and asserts that the outputs are identical — record count, record content, and record order. This test catches a specific class of bugs: transformations that use random number generation, current timestamps, or other non-deterministic operations that produce different results on each execution. SaulėGrid runs this test in CI and in the staging deployment pipeline, ensuring that the idempotency guarantee holds across environments.
Integration Test Performance Budget

Integration tests are slower than unit tests because they involve file I/O, potentially database connections, and larger data volumes. SaulėGrid budgets 60 seconds for their integration test suite — enough time for 8 integration tests that read fixture files, run transformations, verify outputs, and clean up temporary directories. Tests that exceed the budget are either optimized (reduce fixture size, use in-memory processing) or moved to the nightly test run (which has a 5-minute budget). The daily CI pipeline runs the full test suite — integrity, structure, unit, and integration — in under 90 seconds, fast enough that developers do not skip tests to save time.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's integration test convention: every pipeline stage (Bronze ingestion, Silver transformation, Gold aggregation) must have at least one integration test that runs the stage against a fixture dataset and verifies the output. The test fixture must include at least one record for each validation rule (both passing and failing). The test must verify both the valid output and the rejected output (dead letters). The test must include the idempotency check (run twice, compare outputs). This convention ensures that every code path — including the rejection path — is exercised by at least one test.

Unit tests verify that the quality gate function correctly classifies records as valid or rejected. Data quality tests go further: they verify that the quality gate's behavior is correct for the business context — that the rejection thresholds are appropriate, that the rejection codes are meaningful, that the dead-letter routing produces auditable output, and that the rejection rate is within the expected range for realistic data. A quality gate that rejects 0% of records is suspicious (the gate may be misconfigured or bypassed). A quality gate that rejects 50% of records is also suspicious (the gate may be too strict or the data may have a systemic quality issue). Data quality tests establish the expected behavior and alert when the actual behavior deviates.
Fixture Files with Intentional Quality Issues

The dirty fixture is the most important test artifact because it defines the contract between the quality gate's implementation and the business requirements. Each record in the dirty fixture represents a specific business rule: 'null meter IDs must be rejected' is encoded as a record with a null meter_id and an expected rejection code of NULL_METER_ID. If the quality gate is modified and this record unexpectedly passes validation, the test fails — catching a business rule violation before it reaches production. The fixture is the business specification in executable form, and the tests are the verification that the implementation matches the specification.

SaulėGrid maintains two types of fixture files: a clean fixture (all records pass quality checks) and a dirty fixture (records with specific, documented quality issues). The dirty fixture is more valuable for testing because it exercises the rejection path — the code path that production data triggers frequently but that clean test data never reaches. Each dirty record has a comment in the fixture file documenting the expected rejection reason, making the fixture self-documenting and reviewable. The fixture file is treated as a specification: each record documents a business rule, and the test suite verifies that the implementation matches the specification.

# tests/fixtures/bronze/meters/dirty_meters.csv
# 10 records with intentional quality issues for testing
# Each record has an inline comment explaining the expected rejection

meter_id,daily_kw,date,region,customer_type
,15.2,2026-03-24,VILNIUS,RESIDENTIAL          # NULL_METER_ID: empty meter_id
M-002,-8.5,2026-03-24,KAUNAS,COMMERCIAL        # NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER: commercial with negative kw
M-003,15.2,2029-01-01,VILNIUS,RESIDENTIAL       # FUTURE_DATE: date in 2029
M-004,15.2,2026-03-24,ATLANTIS,RESIDENTIAL      # UNKNOWN_REGION: 'ATLANTIS' not in valid regions
M-005,-12.0,2026-03-24,KAUNAS,PROSUMER          # VALID: prosumer negative kw is generation
M-006,0,2026-03-24,SIAULIAI,RESIDENTIAL         # VALID: zero kw is legitimate
M-007,15.2,2026-03-24,,RESIDENTIAL              # UNKNOWN_REGION: empty region
M-008,15.2,2026-03-24,VILNIUS,                   # VALID: empty customer_type not a rejection rule
M-009,-0.01,2026-03-24,KLAIPEDA,INDUSTRIAL      # NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER: barely negative
M-010,999999,2026-03-24,VILNIUS,RESIDENTIAL      # VALID: high kw is unusual but not rejected

The dirty fixture contains 10 records: 4 records that should be valid (M-005 prosumer generation, M-006 zero consumption, M-008 missing customer type, M-010 high consumption) and 6 records that should be rejected (empty meter ID, negative kW for non-prosumer, future date, unknown region, empty region, barely negative kW). The fixture is designed to test both the obvious cases (null meter_id) and the subtle cases (barely negative kW at -0.01 for an industrial customer, high consumption at 999999 kW). The in-line comments make the expected outcome explicit — a reviewer can verify the fixture without running the tests.
Testing Rejection Code Distribution

from collections import Counter

def test_rejection_code_distribution():
    """Verify that each rejection code is exercised by the dirty fixture."""
    import csv
    with open('tests/fixtures/bronze/meters/dirty_meters.csv') as f:
        records = list(csv.DictReader(f))
    
    valid, rejected = filter_quality(records)
    codes = Counter(r['rejection_reason'] for r in rejected)
    
    # Every known rejection code must appear at least once
    expected_codes = {'NULL_METER_ID', 'NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER', 
                      'FUTURE_DATE', 'UNKNOWN_REGION'}
    missing_codes = expected_codes - set(codes.keys())
    assert not missing_codes, (
        f'Rejection codes not exercised by fixture: {missing_codes}. '
        f'Add records to dirty_meters.csv that trigger these codes.'
    )
    
    # Verify expected rejection counts
    assert codes['NULL_METER_ID'] == 1
    assert codes['NEGATIVE_KW_NON_PROSUMER'] == 2  # M-002 and M-009
    assert codes['FUTURE_DATE'] == 1
    assert codes['UNKNOWN_REGION'] == 2  # M-004 (ATLANTIS) and M-007 (empty)

The rejection code distribution test verifies that every known rejection code is exercised by the dirty fixture. If a developer adds a new validation rule with a new rejection code but forgets to add a corresponding record to the dirty fixture, the test fails with a message identifying the untested code. This test acts as a living inventory of the quality gate's rules — it cannot pass unless the fixture covers every rule. SaulėGrid extends this pattern to every quality gate in the pipeline: meters, generation, battery, and weather each have their own dirty fixtures with documented expected outcomes.
Testing the Dead-Letter Output

The dead-letter routing from Module 3, lesson 6 produces structured rejection records with the original data, rejection code, pipeline stage, and timestamp. Data quality tests verify the dead-letter output structure: every rejected record must include all required fields, the rejection code must match a known code from the validation rules, and the original record must be preserved without modification. SaulėGrid's dead-letter test reads the rejection output, parses the structure, and asserts that each field is present and correctly typed — catching the subtle bug where a dead-letter writer serializes the original record incorrectly or omits the rejection code.
Regression Tests for Quality Gates

Quality gate regression tests verify that previously-caught issues continue to be caught after code changes. SaulėGrid maintains a regression fixture — a collection of real meter records that caused production quality issues in the past, anonymized and added to the test suite. The regression fixture grows over time: each production quality incident adds a new record that represents the specific data pattern that caused the issue. The regression test runs the gate against this fixture and asserts that every historical issue is still correctly identified. This ensures that future code changes cannot accidentally re-introduce a quality gap that was previously fixed.

SaulėGrid's data quality test suite serves a dual purpose: it verifies the quality gate implementation and it documents the quality rules. A new team member who reads the dirty fixture and its corresponding test file learns every validation rule, every rejection code, and every edge case that the quality gate handles. The tests are more reliable documentation than a written specification because they are executable — if the specification and the implementation drift apart, the tests fail. SaulėGrid's quality engineering practice is 'specification as code': the dirty fixture is the specification, the validation function is the implementation, and the test suite verifies that they match.

blocks.title: Fixture maintenance
blocks.content: Dirty fixtures must be updated when validation rules change. If a new rule is added (for example, rejecting records with daily_kw above 10,000), the dirty fixture must include a record that triggers the new rule and the distribution test must expect the new rejection code. SaulėGrid treats fixture updates as a mandatory part of any PR that modifies validation logic — the PR checklist includes 'fixture updated for new/modified validation rules.' A CI check verifies that every rejection code in the validation function has a corresponding entry in the dirty fixture's comments, catching mismatches between code and test data.

The previous lessons taught each layer of the testing pyramid in isolation. This lesson presents the pyramid as a complete system: how the layers interact, how the test suite is organized, how the CI pipeline orchestrates the layers, and how the execution time budget determines how many tests belong at each layer. The pyramid is not just a metaphor — it is a concrete engineering decision about where to invest testing effort for maximum return. This approach ensures consistency across all environments and eliminates the class of failures caused by environment-specific differences.
SaulėGrid's Test Suite Inventory

rows: Import errors, duplicate IDs, parse time
Missing SLA, wrong naming, missing outlets
Wrong filter conditions, edge cases, business rule violations
Operator behavior, hook interaction, failure handling
End-to-end data flow, idempotency, fixture validation
Real connection verification, provider endpoint compatibility

blocks.content: The inventory shows the pyramid shape in action: 45 fast tests at the base (integrity + structure, 8 seconds), 40 medium tests in the middle (unit + mock, 20 seconds), and 8 slow tests at the top (integration, 45 seconds). The total CI execution time is 73 seconds — under the 90-second budget that SaulėGrid set as the maximum acceptable CI wait time. The staging smoke tests run post-deployment and are not part of the CI pipeline — they verify real connection availability and provider endpoint compatibility, which cannot be tested in CI because CI does not have access to the external systems.
CI Pipeline Configuration

# .github/workflows/dag-tests.yml
name: DAG Test Suite
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Set up Python 3.10
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install apache-airflow==2.8.1 pytest responses
          pip install -e ./saulegrid_common
        env:
          AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES: 'false'
      
      - name: DAG integrity tests
        run: pytest tests/test_dag_integrity.py -v --tb=short
      
      - name: Structure validation tests
        run: pytest tests/test_dag_structure.py -v --tb=short
      
      - name: Unit tests
        run: pytest tests/test_*.py -v --tb=short --ignore=tests/integration
      
      - name: Integration tests
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: pytest tests/integration/ -v --tb=short
      
      - name: Report test results
        if: always()
        run: echo "Tests completed"

visual-recommendation.description: Annotated screenshot of a GitHub Actions CI pipeline run showing the sequential test stages (integrity, structure, unit, integration) with pass/fail indicators and execution times for each stage.

blocks.content: The CI pipeline runs tests in order of increasing cost. Integrity tests run first — if a DAG cannot be imported, there is no point running structure or unit tests. Structure tests run second — if DAG parameters are misconfigured, unit test results may be misleading. Unit tests run third on every push. Integration tests run fourth, but only on pull requests (not on every push) because they take 45 seconds and are most valuable before merging, not during development. This conditional execution keeps the per-push CI time under 20 seconds while providing full test coverage on PRs.
Test Failure Handling

# Example test failure output — designed for fast diagnosis

FAILED tests/test_dag_structure.py::test_gold_dags_have_sla[dag_gold_billing]

  AssertionError: dag_gold_billing has no sla_miss_callback.
  Gold DAGs require SLA monitoring (Module 3, lesson 5).
  
  Fix: Add sla_miss_callback to the @dag decorator.
  Example:
    @dag(
        dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
        sla_miss_callback=sla_alert,
        ...
    )

When a test fails in CI, the pipeline stops and the PR cannot merge. The developer's workflow is: read the failure message (which includes the test name, the assertion that failed, and the expected vs actual values), diagnose whether the failure is a real bug or a test that needs updating, fix the code or the test, and push again. SaulėGrid's error messages are designed for fast diagnosis — every assertion includes context about what the test is checking, why the requirement exists (with a module reference), and what the expected value should be. A developer should be able to understand a test failure in under 30 seconds without reading the test source code.
The Gate That Blocks Deployment

A test suite that does not block deployment is a reporting tool, not a quality gate. SaulėGrid's GitHub repository is configured with branch protection rules that require the dag-tests CI job to pass before a PR can be merged to the main branch. The main branch is the deployment source — merging to main triggers the deployment pipeline (Module 7). This configuration creates an unbreakable chain: code changes go through PR review, the CI pipeline runs all tests, the PR cannot merge unless all tests pass, and only merged code is deployed. A developer who wants to bypass the tests must convince a repository administrator to override branch protection — a deliberate, auditable action that requires explicit justification.
When Tests Are Not Enough

Tests catch predictable failures — failures whose causes can be anticipated and encoded as test assertions. Tests cannot catch unpredictable failures: a provider changing their API response format, a data volume spike that exceeds the processing capacity, or a network partition between the worker and the metadata database. For these unpredictable failures, SaulėGrid relies on the production monitoring layer (SLA monitoring from Module 3, executor health monitoring from Module 4) and the operational runbooks (Module 3, lesson 7). Tests prevent deployment of known-broken code. Monitoring detects runtime failures that no test predicted.

SaulėGrid's CI pipeline is configured to run all test layers sequentially and stop at the first failure. If integrity tests fail, structure and unit tests do not run — there is no point testing logic when the DAG cannot be loaded. This fail-fast approach provides the fastest possible feedback: a circular import is diagnosed in 3 seconds, not 73 seconds. The CI output clearly indicates which layer failed and which test within that layer, so the developer knows immediately whether the issue is structural (fix the import), configurational (fix the parameter), or logical (fix the business function).

blocks.title: The testing investment
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's test suite took 3 weeks to build: 1 week for integrity and structure tests (the highest-value investment), 1 week for unit tests, and 1 week for mock-based and integration tests. The suite runs 123 tests in 73 seconds. Since deployment, the test suite has blocked 18 PRs that contained errors — 11 structural errors, 4 logic errors, and 3 configuration errors. Each blocked PR would have become a production incident if deployed. The 3-week testing investment has prevented 18 potential incidents in two quarters, making it the highest-ROI engineering project the team has undertaken.

Tests need an environment to run in: a Python installation with the correct Airflow version and provider packages, a temporary directory for output files, an Airflow configuration that does not interfere with the developer's local Airflow installation, and optionally a database for integration tests that require metadata persistence. Managing this environment consistently across developer laptops, CI runners, and staging servers is a prerequisite for reliable test results. A test that passes on one developer's machine and fails on another is worse than no test — it erodes trust in the test suite and teaches the team to ignore test failures.
The Lightweight Airflow Test Configuration

Airflow's default configuration assumes a production deployment: PostgreSQL metadata database, CeleryExecutor, and a persistent home directory. For tests, SaulėGrid uses a minimal configuration that eliminates production dependencies: SQLite as the metadata database (no PostgreSQL required), SequentialExecutor (no Celery or Redis), and a temporary directory for AIRFLOW_HOME. This lightweight configuration allows tests to run on any machine with Python installed — no Docker containers, no external services, and no persistent state between test runs. This lightweight configuration allows tests to run on any machine with Python installed — no Docker containers, no external database servers, and no persistent state between test runs.

# conftest.py — shared test configuration
import os
import pytest
import tempfile


@pytest.fixture(scope='session', autouse=True)
def airflow_test_config():
    """Configure Airflow for testing: SQLite, SequentialExecutor, temp home."""
    # Create a temporary Airflow home directory
    with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
        os.environ['AIRFLOW_HOME'] = tmpdir
        os.environ['AIRFLOW__CORE__EXECUTOR'] = 'SequentialExecutor'
        os.environ['AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN'] = f'sqlite:///{tmpdir}/airflow.db'
        os.environ['AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES'] = 'false'
        os.environ['AIRFLOW__CORE__DAGS_FOLDER'] = os.path.abspath('dags/')
        os.environ['AIRFLOW__CORE__UNIT_TEST_MODE'] = 'true'
        
        # Initialize the test database
        from airflow.utils.db import initdb
        initdb()
        
        yield
        
        # Cleanup is automatic — tempdir is deleted

The conftest.py fixture runs once per test session (scope='session') and configures the Airflow environment before any test executes. The AIRFLOW__CORE__UNIT_TEST_MODE setting disables features that are irrelevant for testing (like the scheduler heartbeat) and enables test-friendly behavior (like more verbose error messages). The SQLite database is created in the temporary directory and destroyed when the session ends, ensuring no state persists between test runs. Every developer and every CI runner starts from the same clean state. This lightweight configuration allows tests to run on any machine with Python installed — no Docker containers, no external database servers, and no persistent state between test runs.
Docker Compose for Integration Tests

Some integration tests need services that SQLite cannot provide: a PostgreSQL database for testing warehouse writes, or a mock SFTP server for testing file downloads. SaulėGrid uses Docker Compose to spin up these services for the integration test suite. The Docker Compose file defines a PostgreSQL container and a mock SFTP server container that start before the tests and stop after. The integration tests connect to these containers using localhost ports, making the tests self-contained and reproducible on any machine with Docker installed.

# tests/docker-compose.yml — services for integration tests
version: '3.8'
services:
  test-postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: test_warehouse
      POSTGRES_USER: test_user
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test_password
    ports:
      - '5433:5432'  # Non-standard port to avoid conflicts
    healthcheck:
      test: pg_isready -U test_user -d test_warehouse
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  test-sftp:
    image: atmoz/sftp
    command: test_user:test_password:::upload
    ports:
      - '2222:22'  # Non-standard port
    volumes:
      - ./fixtures/sftp:/home/test_user/upload:ro
CI Docker Compose Integration

The CI pipeline starts Docker Compose services before running integration tests using GitHub Actions' services feature or a manual docker-compose up step. SaulėGrid's CI workflow waits for the PostgreSQL healthcheck to pass before running the integration tests, preventing the race condition where tests start before the database is ready. The Docker Compose services run only during the integration test step — they are not started for the faster integrity, structure, and unit tests, keeping the per-push CI time minimal. The compose file is identical in CI and on developer machines, ensuring that integration test results are reproducible across environments.

The Docker Compose file uses non-standard ports (5433 for PostgreSQL, 2222 for SFTP) to avoid conflicts with any local services the developer might be running. The SFTP container mounts the fixture directory as a read-only volume, so the fixture files are immediately available for download tests. The PostgreSQL healthcheck ensures the database is ready before tests start — a missing healthcheck causes intermittent test failures when the test runner connects before PostgreSQL finishes initialization. SaulėGrid's CI pipeline starts Docker Compose before the integration test step and stops it after.
Fixture Management

Test fixtures are the test suite's data layer — their quality directly determines the test suite's value. SaulėGrid's fixture management follows four rules. First, fixtures are version-controlled in the tests/fixtures/ directory alongside the test code. Second, every fixture file has a header comment documenting its purpose, record count, and expected quality characteristics. Third, fixture changes require the same PR review as code changes — a modified fixture can change test outcomes and must be reviewed by a team member. Fourth, fixtures are never generated at test time — they are static files created by the developer with known, predetermined characteristics. Dynamic fixture generation introduces non-determinism that makes test failures unreproducible.
Test Database Reset

Integration tests that write to a database must reset the database state between tests. SaulėGrid uses pytest fixtures with function scope (autouse=True) that truncate all test tables before each test function. This ensures that test_A's output does not affect test_B's assertions, even if tests run in a different order. The reset is a TRUNCATE TABLE command on each test table — fast for the small volumes used in tests (under 1000 rows). For larger integration tests that create complex state, SaulėGrid uses a snapshot-restore pattern: the test database is initialized to a known state from a SQL dump, the test runs and modifies the state, and the next test's fixture restores the snapshot.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's test environment principle: every test must be runnable on a fresh machine with nothing installed except Python and Docker. The conftest.py configures Airflow with no external dependencies (SQLite, SequentialExecutor). The Docker Compose file provides the services that integration tests need (PostgreSQL, SFTP). The fixtures provide the data. A new team member can clone the repository, run pip install and docker-compose up, and execute the full test suite on their first day — no configuration, no database setup, no environment variable juggling.

This module has covered five layers of testing — DAG integrity validation, structure validation, unit testing, mock-based testing, and integration testing — plus data quality testing and test environment management. This lesson synthesizes every layer into SaulėGrid's testing standards: the minimum test requirements for every DAG, the CI pipeline configuration that enforces them, the test maintenance practices that keep the suite current, and the metrics that measure testing effectiveness. Every standard traces to a specific test type from this module, and every test type was motivated by a production incident that the test would have prevented.
The Testing Standards

rows: Every DAG file must load without import errors
The expected number of DAGs must be loaded
Every DAG must have a unique dag_id
Total DAG parse time must be under 3 seconds
DAG IDs and task IDs must follow team conventions
