Gold DAGs must have sla_miss_callback configured
All conn_ids must exist in the connection registry
Every quality gate function must have positive and negative tests
Every rejection code must be exercised by a dirty fixture
Every write task must pass the run-twice-compare test

blocks.content: The PR Checklist

# SaulėGrid PR checklist for DAG changes — testing section

## Testing Requirements

- [ ] All existing tests pass (CI green)
- [ ] New DAG has integrity test coverage (appears in dag_count assertion)
- [ ] New tasks have structure validation (naming, tags, SLA, retries)
- [ ] New business logic has unit tests (positive + negative cases)
- [ ] New external system interactions have mock-based tests
- [ ] Modified quality gates have updated dirty fixtures
- [ ] New write tasks have idempotency integration test
- [ ] Test execution time within budget (< 90 seconds total)

## Test Evidence
- CI pipeline link: _______
- New test count: ___ tests added
- Coverage change: ___ → ___ %

The checklist has two sections: automated checks (verified by CI — the developer links the passing pipeline) and manual checks (verified by the reviewer — the developer provides evidence). The automated section covers integrity, structure, and execution time. The manual section covers test quality: does the test actually verify the behavior it claims to verify, does the fixture cover the relevant edge cases, and does the test failure message provide enough context for diagnosis? The reviewer does not just verify that tests exist — they verify that the tests are meaningful.

The PR checklist ensures that every DAG change includes the corresponding test updates. A new DAG must appear in the DAG count assertion (otherwise the test will fail when the expected count does not match). New tasks must have structure validation tests. New business logic must have unit tests. The checklist is a pull request template in GitHub — every PR starts with the checklist pre-populated, and the developer fills in the evidence before requesting review. The reviewer verifies each checkbox independently, not just trusting the developer's self-assessment.
Test Maintenance Practices

Test suites decay if they are not maintained. SaulėGrid prevents test decay with three practices. First, the monthly test audit: a team member reviews the test inventory against the DAG inventory to ensure every DAG has the required test coverage — missing tests are added as a maintenance task. Second, the test execution time review: if the total CI time approaches the 90-second budget, the team identifies slow tests and either optimizes them or moves them to the nightly run. Third, the flaky test policy: any test that fails intermittently (passes on retry without code changes) is immediately investigated and fixed. A flaky test is worse than a missing test because it teaches the team to ignore failures.
Testing Effectiveness Metrics

SaulėGrid tracks three metrics to measure testing effectiveness. First, the incident prevention rate: the number of PRs blocked by test failures that would have caused production incidents. In two quarters, 18 of 142 PRs were blocked — a 12.7% catch rate that prevented 18 potential incidents. Second, the coverage metric: statement coverage of the saulegrid_common package, maintained at 85% with 100% coverage on quality gate functions. Third, the test execution time: tracked weekly to detect gradual slowdowns before they exceed the budget. These metrics are reported in the quarterly platform retrospective and used to justify continued investment in the test infrastructure.

The most revealing metric is the incident prevention rate broken down by test layer: integrity tests blocked 11 PRs (61% of catches), structure tests blocked 3 PRs (17%), unit tests blocked 3 PRs (17%), and integration tests blocked 1 PR (5%). The distribution confirms the pyramid's design: the base layers (integrity + structure) provide the highest catch rate because structural and configuration errors are the most common mistake category. The upper layers catch fewer but more subtle errors — the one integration test catch was a data corruption bug that would have been invisible to unit tests because it only manifested when processing a batch with a specific mix of record types.
Testing ROI
Continuous Improvement

The testing standards, like the pipeline design standards from Module 3, represent the team's collective learning encoded in executable form. Each standard was motivated by a specific incident, each test was designed to prevent a specific class of failure, and each metric tracks the effectiveness of the prevention. The testing pyramid is not a theoretical ideal — it is a practical engineering tool that has prevented 18 production incidents in two quarters and reduced the team's on-call burden from multiple incidents per month to a baseline of infrastructure-only alerts. The investment in testing is the investment that makes every other investment — in scheduling, in operators, in configuration management — reliable.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: A DAG without tests is a pipeline waiting to fail at midnight — and SaulėGrid's testing pyramid, from the 3-second integrity tests that catch import errors to the 45-second integration tests that catch data corruption, transforms the question from 'will this DAG work in production?' to 'this DAG has been verified against 123 assertions covering structure, logic, mocking, integration, and idempotency — it will work in production because it already has.'

chapters.title: Deployment and CI/CD
chapters.description: Master automated deployment of Airflow DAGs: Git-based DAG management, CI pipeline design for validation and testing, deployment strategies for zero-downtime DAG updates, environment promotion from development through staging to production, and rollback procedures.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's first deployment process was simple: Matas copied DAG files from his laptop to the Airflow server using scp. The process worked for one developer and three DAGs. When the team grew to three engineers and twelve DAGs, the scp approach created three problems in the same week. On Monday, Ieva deployed a DAG that referenced a Connection not yet created in production — the task failed at midnight with an authentication error. On Wednesday, two developers deployed different versions of the same DAG within an hour — the second deployment silently overwrote the first, and the first developer's changes vanished without any record. On Friday, a deployment introduced a circular import that prevented all twelve DAGs from parsing, stalling the entire pipeline for six hours.
Why Manual Deployment Fails at Scale

Manual deployment fails because it relies on human discipline for tasks that should be automated: remembering to run tests before copying files, verifying that connections exist in the target environment, coordinating with other developers who might be deploying simultaneously, and checking that the scheduler can parse the new DAG before the next processing cycle. Each of these steps is trivial individually, but the combination — performed correctly every time, by every developer, under time pressure — creates a compounding failure probability. SaulėGrid's three incidents in one week were not caused by incompetence; they were caused by a process that required perfection from humans in conditions that make perfection unlikely.

rows: Developer forgets, or tests pass on laptop but fail in production Python version
CI pipeline runs tests automatically on every push
Developer assumes production has the same connections as dev
CI validates connection references against the registry (Module 6)
No coordination mechanism — last scp wins
Git branching prevents simultaneous conflicting changes
Developer does not have a production-equivalent scheduler to test against
CI runs DagBag import test against production Airflow version
No record of what was deployed or what the previous version looked like
Git history provides exact rollback target

blocks.content: The Requirements for Production Deployment

A production deployment process must satisfy five requirements. First, validation: every deployment must pass the test suite before reaching any environment. Second, auditability: every deployment must be traceable to a Git commit, a pull request, an approver, and a timestamp. Third, reproducibility: any previous deployment must be reproducible from the Git history. Fourth, environment promotion: changes must flow from development through staging to production, with validation at each stage. Fifth, rollback: any deployment must be reversible within minutes, not hours. These five requirements are the acceptance criteria that SaulėGrid used to design their CI/CD pipeline.
What This Module Covers

The deployment pipeline interacts with every module in the mid-level track. It runs the test suite from Module 6 as the primary validation gate. It deploys the configuration files from Module 5 through the initialization script. It respects the scheduling architecture from Module 1 by deploying during the pipeline's quiet period. It enables the pipeline versioning patterns from Module 3 by maintaining a complete Git history of every code change. Without a reliable deployment pipeline, every improvement from other modules — better operators, better testing, better configuration management — cannot reach production consistently.

This module teaches the complete deployment lifecycle for Airflow DAGs. Git-based DAG management (lesson 2) establishes the repository structure that makes deployment automatable. The CI pipeline (lessons 3-4) runs validation and tests on every code change. Deployment strategies (lesson 5) cover how DAG files reach the Airflow workers. Environment promotion (lesson 6) covers the workflow from development through staging to production. Rollback procedures (lesson 7) cover how to reverse a bad deployment. Provider and dependency upgrades (lesson 8) cover the special case of upgrading the Airflow platform itself. Infrastructure as code (lesson 9) covers managing the Airflow deployment configuration alongside the DAG code. Lesson 10 synthesizes into SaulėGrid's complete deployment pipeline.
From Manual to Automated

The deployment pipeline interacts with every module in the course: it runs the tests from Module 6, it deploys the configuration from Module 5, it respects the scheduling architecture from Module 1, and it enables the pipeline versioning patterns from Module 3. Deployment is not a standalone topic — it is the mechanism that delivers every other module's work from a developer's laptop to the production cluster. A broken deployment pipeline means that every improvement the team makes — better operators, better testing, better configuration — cannot reach production reliably.
The Five Deployment Requirements

The five requirements — validation, auditability, reproducibility, environment promotion, and rollback — are not independent. Validation without auditability means the team cannot verify which checks passed for a given deployment. Auditability without reproducibility means the team can see what was deployed but cannot recreate the deployment if the environment is rebuilt. Environment promotion without rollback means changes flow forward but cannot flow backward when problems are discovered. The five requirements form a complete system where each requirement supports the others, and removing any one requirement creates a gap that undermines the remaining four.

# SaulėGrid's original deployment process (before CI/CD)
# Every deployment was a manual scp from a developer's laptop

# Step 1: (Hopefully) run tests locally
pytest tests/  # Sometimes skipped under time pressure

# Step 2: Copy DAG files to the server
scp dags/*.py airflow-prod:/opt/airflow/dags/

# Step 3: Copy the shared package
scp -r saulegrid_common/ airflow-prod:/opt/airflow/saulegrid_common/

# Step 4: (Hopefully) check the scheduler logs for import errors
ssh airflow-prod 'tail -20 /opt/airflow/logs/scheduler.log | grep import_errors'

# Problems with this approach:
# - No validation that tests actually passed
# - No record of who deployed what when
# - No protection against simultaneous deployments
# - No rollback mechanism (what was the previous version?)
# - No environment promotion (dev → staging → production)

The scp deployment script looks simple, but it encodes five assumptions that fail under pressure: the developer will remember to run tests before deploying, the developer's local Python environment matches production, no other developer is deploying simultaneously, the scheduler will parse the new files without errors, and if something goes wrong, the developer can remember what the previous working version looked like. Each assumption is reasonable for a single developer deploying once a week. None of them hold reliably for a three-person team deploying multiple times per week under delivery pressure.

blocks.title: The deployment maturity shift
blocks.content: The shift from scp to CI/CD is the shift from 'I deployed the code' to 'the pipeline deployed the code.' When a human deploys, the deployment quality depends on that human's attention to detail on that specific day. When a pipeline deploys, the deployment quality depends on the pipeline's configuration, which is reviewed, tested, and consistent across every deployment. SaulėGrid's three-incident week could not happen with their current pipeline because the CI validates tests, checks connections, and prevents conflicting changes — automatically, on every push, regardless of who is deploying.

The foundation of automated deployment is a Git repository that contains everything needed to deploy the Airflow platform: DAG files, the shared Python package, test suites, configuration files, and infrastructure definitions. The repository is the single source of truth for the entire platform — if a file is not in the repository, it does not exist in production and cannot be deployed. This principle eliminates the 'works on my machine' category of deployment failures because every environment is built from the same repository, and it provides the audit trail, versioning, and collaboration mechanisms that manual file-copying deployment fundamentally lacks.
Repository Structure

saulegrid-airflow/
├── dags/                              # DAG definitions
│   ├── dag_bronze_meters.py
│   ├── dag_bronze_generation.py
│   ├── dag_bronze_weather.py
│   ├── dag_silver_meters.py
│   ├── dag_silver_generation.py
│   ├── dag_gold_billing.py
│   ├── dag_gold_forecast.py
│   ├── dag_regulatory_report.py
│   ├── dag_battery_soc_monitor.py
│   ├── dag_bronze_reprocess.py
│   ├── dag_sla_monitor.py
│   └── dag_maintenance.py
├── saulegrid_common/                  # Shared Python package
│   ├── hooks/
│   ├── operators/
│   ├── transformations/
│   ├── datasets.py
│   └── xcom.py
├── plugins/                           # Airflow plugins
│   └── saulegrid_timetables.py
├── config/                            # Configuration as code (Module 5)
│   ├── connections.yaml
│   ├── connection_registry.yaml
│   ├── pools.yaml
│   ├── dev.yaml
│   ├── staging.yaml
│   └── production.yaml
├── tests/                             # Test suite (Module 6)
│   ├── test_dag_integrity.py
│   ├── test_dag_structure.py
│   ├── test_meter_transform.py
│   ├── integration/
│   └── fixtures/
├── helm/                              # Kubernetes deployment (lesson 9)
│   ├── values-staging.yaml
│   └── values-production.yaml
├── scripts/                           # Deployment and maintenance scripts
│   ├── init_config.py
│   └── deploy.sh
├── .github/workflows/                 # CI/CD pipeline definitions
│   ├── ci.yml
│   ├── deploy-staging.yml
│   └── deploy-production.yml
├── pyproject.toml                     # Package metadata
├── requirements.txt                   # Python dependencies (pinned)
└── README.md

The repository has seven top-level directories, each with a distinct role. The dags/ directory contains only DAG definition files — thin wrappers that compose operators and set dependencies. The saulegrid_common/ directory contains the shared package with hooks, operators, and transformation logic — this is the code that is tested and reused across DAGs. The plugins/ directory contains Airflow plugin classes. The config/ directory contains the configuration-as-code files from Module 5. The tests/ directory contains the test suite from Module 6. The helm/ directory contains deployment configuration. The .github/workflows/ directory contains the CI/CD pipeline definitions that automate everything.
Branching Strategy

SaulėGrid uses a trunk-based development model with short-lived feature branches. The main branch represents the production-ready state — every commit on main has passed CI and is deployable. Developers create feature branches from main, make changes, open pull requests, and merge back to main after CI passes and a reviewer approves. Feature branches live for 1-3 days; long-lived branches are discouraged because they accumulate merge conflicts and delay integration. The branching strategy is simple because Airflow DAGs do not have the complex release management of application software — there are no version numbers, no release branches, and no hotfix branches. The current main branch is always the deployment target.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's branching workflow
blocks.content:
main ──────●──────●──────●──────●──────●──────●──── (always deployable)
            \              \             /
             \              \           /
              ●──●──●        ●──●──●──●
              feature/        feature/
              add-battery     fix-sftp-retry
              (2 days)        (1 day)

Workflow:
1. Create feature branch from main
2. Make changes, push commits
3. CI runs on every push (tests, lint)
4. Open PR → reviewer approves
5. CI runs integration tests on PR
6. Merge to main → triggers staging deployment
7. Verify in staging → approve production deployment
blocks.caption: Trunk-based development with short-lived feature branches. Every merge to main triggers the deployment pipeline. No long-lived branches, no release branches.

blocks.content: The Monorepo Decision

SaulėGrid uses a monorepo — DAGs, shared package, tests, and configuration all live in the same Git repository. The alternative is a multi-repo approach: one repository for DAGs, one for the shared package, and one for configuration. The monorepo provides atomic changes (a DAG change and its corresponding shared package change are committed and deployed together), simpler CI (one pipeline tests everything), and easier onboarding (one repository to clone and understand). The trade-off is that any change to any file triggers the full CI pipeline, even if the change is a README edit. SaulėGrid accepts this trade-off because the CI pipeline runs in under 90 seconds — the cost of running unnecessary tests is negligible compared to the complexity of coordinating deployments across multiple repositories.
The .airflowignore File

The Airflow scheduler parses every Python file in the DAGs directory on every heartbeat. Files that are not DAGs — utility modules, test files accidentally placed in the DAGs directory, or __pycache__ directories — add unnecessary parsing overhead. The .airflowignore file tells the scheduler which files and patterns to skip during parsing, similar to .gitignore for Git. SaulėGrid's .airflowignore excludes test files, __pycache__ directories, and any Python file that starts with an underscore (the convention for helper modules that are imported by DAGs but are not DAGs themselves). This reduces the scheduler's file scan from 20+ files to the 12 actual DAG files.
Dependency Pinning

# requirements.txt — ALL dependencies pinned to exact versions
# Updated quarterly during the dependency upgrade cycle (lesson 8)

apache-airflow==2.8.1
apache-airflow-providers-sftp==4.8.0
apache-airflow-providers-postgres==5.10.0
apache-airflow-providers-hashicorp==3.6.0
apache-airflow-providers-amazon==8.16.0
pendulum==3.0.0
hvac==2.1.0
responses==0.25.0
pytest==8.0.0
ruff==0.3.0

Every Python dependency is pinned to an exact version in requirements.txt. Version ranges (>=2.8.0, ~=2.8) are prohibited because they can produce different installed versions on different machines or CI runs, creating the 'works in CI but fails in production' class of bugs. Pinned versions guarantee that every developer, every CI runner, and every production worker installs exactly the same packages. SaulėGrid updates pinned versions quarterly through a deliberate upgrade process (lesson 8), not through automatic version resolution. The requirements.txt file is the dependency contract: if a version is listed, it is tested and known to work together.
Repository Access Control

The Git repository's access control reflects the team's deployment authority. All three engineers have write access to feature branches and can open PRs. Only the team lead and the senior engineer have permission to merge to main — the branch that triggers deployments. Only the team lead can override branch protection rules for emergency deployments. This tiered access ensures that every production-impacting change is reviewed by an experienced team member and that emergency overrides are traceable to a specific person with explicit authority. The access control configuration is documented in the repository's CODEOWNERS file and reviewed during onboarding.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's repository convention: the README.md contains setup instructions that a new developer can follow to go from 'cloned the repo' to 'running the test suite' in under 10 minutes. The instructions include: install Python 3.10, run pip install -r requirements.txt -e ., run pytest — three commands. If a new developer cannot set up the development environment in 10 minutes, the repository structure needs simplification. The README also links to the team's conventions document, the architecture decision records, and the onboarding checklist.

The CI pipeline is the automated quality gate between a developer's code change and the deployment target. Every push to any branch triggers the CI pipeline, which runs linting, DAG integrity tests, structure validation, unit tests, and — for pull requests — integration tests. If any step fails, the pipeline fails and the developer is notified. If all steps pass, the code is eligible for merging to main. The pipeline is the enforcement mechanism for every standard from Modules 3, 5, and 6 — without CI, those standards are documented guidelines that can be bypassed. With CI, they are automated checks that cannot be circumvented without administrator intervention.
Pipeline Stages

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's CI pipeline stages
blocks.caption: Four stages run sequentially, each gating the next. Lint catches formatting issues in 5 seconds. Integrity catches structural errors in 8 seconds. Unit tests catch logic errors in 20 seconds. Integration tests catch end-to-end issues in 45 seconds. Total: under 90 seconds.

visual-recommendation.description: CI pipeline flow diagram showing four sequential stages with timing, what each catches, and the PR-only gate for integration tests, styled as a GitHub Actions pipeline visualization.

blocks.content: Stage 1: Linting

The linting stage runs ruff — a fast Python linter — against all Python files in the repository. Ruff checks for syntax errors, unused imports, style violations, and the custom rules that SaulėGrid configures: no Variable.get() at the top level of DAG files (the parsing performance trap from Module 5), no bare except clauses in operators (the failure masking anti-pattern from Module 3), and no print() statements in production code (use logging instead). The linting stage completes in under 5 seconds and catches the most mechanical code issues before any Airflow-specific testing begins.

# ruff.toml — SaulėGrid's linter configuration
[lint]
select = ['E', 'F', 'W', 'I', 'N', 'UP', 'B']

[lint.per-file-ignores]
# DAG files: custom rules for Airflow-specific patterns
'dags/*.py' = ['E402']  # Allow late imports (Airflow patterns)

# Custom rule: no Variable.get() at module level
# Enforced by scripts/check_top_level_variable.py
# (ruff does not have a built-in rule for this)

The ruff configuration selects rule categories that are relevant for Airflow development: E (pycodestyle errors), F (pyflakes — unused imports, undefined names), W (pycodestyle warnings), I (import sorting), N (naming conventions), UP (Python upgrade suggestions), and B (flake8-bugbear — common gotchas). The per-file-ignores entry allows late imports in DAG files because Airflow's module-level DAG registration pattern sometimes requires imports after function definitions. SaulėGrid's configuration is deliberately conservative — adding new rule categories requires a team discussion to ensure the rules do not produce false positives that erode trust in the linter.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI Pipeline
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'
      - run: pip install ruff
      - run: ruff check dags/ saulegrid_common/ tests/
      - run: ruff format --check dags/ saulegrid_common/ tests/

  test:
    needs: lint  # Only runs if linting passes
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt -e . pytest
        env:
          AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES: 'false'
      - name: DAG integrity + structure tests
        run: pytest tests/test_dag_integrity.py tests/test_dag_structure.py -v
      - name: Unit tests
        run: pytest tests/ -v --ignore=tests/integration
      - name: Integration tests (PR only)
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: pytest tests/integration/ -v

The CI configuration uses GitHub Actions with two jobs: lint and test. The test job depends on lint (needs: lint), so test only runs if linting passes — there is no point running DAG tests on code with syntax errors. Within the test job, the stages run sequentially: integrity and structure first (fastest, catches structural errors), then unit tests (catches logic errors), then integration tests (slowest, only on PRs). The AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES environment variable prevents Airflow's example DAGs from loading, which would interfere with the DAG count assertion and add 10 seconds of unnecessary parsing.
Branch Protection

The CI pipeline becomes a quality gate through GitHub's branch protection rules. SaulėGrid configures the main branch with three protection rules: the CI pipeline must pass before merging, at least one code review approval is required, and direct pushes to main are prohibited. These rules ensure that every change to main — the deployment source — has been validated by both the CI pipeline and a human reviewer. A developer who wants to bypass the pipeline must convince a repository administrator to override the protection — a deliberate, auditable action that requires explicit justification. In SaulėGrid's first six months with branch protection, the override has been used once (for an emergency hotfix) and required team lead approval.
Notification and Feedback

When the CI pipeline fails, the developer receives a GitHub notification linking to the failed run. The notification includes the stage that failed (lint, integrity, unit, or integration), the specific test or check that triggered the failure, and the error message. SaulėGrid configures the CI to produce concise, actionable error output: instead of dumping the full pytest output (which can be hundreds of lines for a test suite), the workflow uses --tb=short for shortened tracebacks and -q for quiet mode, showing only the failed tests and their error messages. A developer should be able to diagnose a CI failure from the notification email without opening the GitHub Actions log.
CI Environment Parity

The CI runner must match the production environment closely enough that test results are predictive. SaulėGrid's CI uses the same Python version (3.10), the same Airflow version (2.8.1), and the same provider package versions (pinned in requirements.txt) as production. The only differences are the executor (SequentialExecutor in CI vs CeleryExecutor in production) and the metadata database (SQLite in CI vs PostgreSQL in production). These differences are acceptable because they do not affect DAG parsing, operator behavior, or transformation logic — they only affect execution scheduling, which is not tested in CI. If a test passes in CI, it will pass in production for the same reasons. If a test fails in CI, the failure is genuine and must be fixed before deployment.

blocks.title: CI anti-pattern
blocks.content: The most common CI anti-pattern is a pipeline that is too slow to run on every push. If CI takes 10 minutes, developers push less frequently, batch more changes per push, and are more likely to skip running CI locally before pushing. SaulėGrid's 90-second target ensures that CI is fast enough to be a natural part of the development workflow — not a bottleneck that developers work around. If the test suite grows beyond 90 seconds, the team splits slow tests into a nightly job rather than slowing the per-push pipeline.

The CI pipeline from lesson 3 runs the test suite from Module 6. This lesson covers the additional DAG-specific validations that CI performs beyond general Python testing: Airflow version compatibility checks, deprecated API detection, schedule validation, and the custom CI checks that SaulėGrid has added based on their operational experience. These validations form a second layer of quality assurance — the first layer (Module 6 tests) checks business logic and DAG structure, and this second layer checks platform compatibility and operational readiness.
Airflow Version Compatibility

Airflow evolves across versions: APIs are deprecated, new features are added, and behavior changes. A DAG that uses schedule_interval (deprecated in Airflow 2.4) instead of schedule will produce a deprecation warning in Airflow 2.8 and may break in a future version. SaulėGrid's CI includes a compatibility check that scans DAG files for deprecated patterns and fails the pipeline if any are found. The check uses a curated list of deprecated APIs maintained in the repository — updated during the quarterly dependency upgrade cycle (lesson 8).

The deprecation checker scans DAG files for string patterns that match known deprecated APIs. When a pattern is found, the checker reports the file, line number, deprecated pattern, and the recommended replacement. The check runs in CI as part of the lint stage — it adds less than 1 second to the pipeline. SaulėGrid caught a DAG using schedule_interval during a routine code change — the developer had copied an example from an outdated tutorial. The CI check blocked the merge with a clear message pointing to the replacement API, preventing a deprecation warning in production logs.
Schedule Validation

Schedule validation verifies that every DAG's scheduling configuration is valid and follows the team's conventions. The check verifies that cron expressions are syntactically correct, that Dataset references use URIs from the shared datasets.py registry (Module 1), and that custom timetables are registered in the plugin system. SaulėGrid's schedule validation also checks for scheduling anti-patterns: a DAG with both a cron schedule and ExternalTaskSensors (the sensor-cron overlap from Module 1), or a Dataset-scheduled DAG with catchup=True (which would create backfill DAG Runs for every historical Dataset update).

The schedule validation tests encode the scheduling conventions from Module 1 as automated checks. The test_dataset_scheduled_dags_no_catchup check prevents a common misconfiguration: a Dataset-scheduled DAG with catchup=True would create a backfill DAG Run for every historical Dataset update event, potentially flooding the executor with hundreds of unwanted runs. The test_no_sensor_cron_overlap check catches the anti-pattern where a DAG uses both old-style sensors and cron scheduling — a sign that the DAG has been partially migrated to Datasets but the migration is incomplete. Both checks would have caught real issues that SaulėGrid encountered during their Module 1 migration.
Connection and Pool Reference Checks

The connection reference check from Module 6 is part of the CI pipeline, but two additional reference checks are specific to the deployment context. The pool reference check validates that every task's pool parameter matches a pool defined in config/pools.yaml — a task assigned to pool='gpu_pool' when no GPU pool exists will queue indefinitely in production with no error message. The queue reference check validates that every task's queue parameter matches a queue in the known queue list — a task assigned to queue='gpu' when no worker subscribes to that queue will never execute. Both checks read the authoritative configuration files and compare them against the DAG code, catching references to infrastructure that does not exist.
Custom CI Checks

Beyond the standard test suite and validation scripts, SaulėGrid runs three custom CI checks specific to their operational standards. First, the pool reference check validates that every task's pool parameter matches a pool defined in pools.yaml — catching the scenario where a developer assigns a task to a pool that does not exist. Second, the queue reference check validates that every task's queue parameter matches a queue in the known queue list — catching the orphaned queue problem from Module 4. Third, the Variable reference check scans task code for Variable.get() calls and verifies that the referenced keys exist in the environment YAML files — catching typos in variable names before deployment.
The CI Check Inventory

rows: Syntax errors, style violations, top-level Variable.get()
Circular imports, missing modules, syntax errors
Missing SLA, wrong naming, missing outlets
References to non-existent pools or queues

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's CI check evolution: every production incident that could have been prevented by a CI check results in a new check being added. The check inventory table documents each check's origin — the specific incident or migration that motivated it. This traceability ensures that checks are not added arbitrarily (which leads to slow CI and false positives) but are each justified by a concrete failure. The team reviews the check inventory quarterly and removes checks that have not caught anything in 6 months — keeping the CI lean and focused on real risks.

The deployment strategy determines how DAG files reach the Airflow workers after CI validation and merge to main. The choice affects four operational characteristics: deployment speed (how quickly changes take effect after merge), rollback capability (how easily a bad deployment can be reversed), version consistency (whether all workers run the same code version simultaneously), and the risk of disrupting in-progress DAG Runs. Each strategy makes different trade-offs among these characteristics, and the right choice depends on the environment's priority — speed for development, consistency for production.
Strategy 1: File Sync

The file sync strategy uses rsync or S3 sync to copy DAG files from the Git repository to the Airflow DAGs folder. A CI job runs after merge to main, syncs the DAG files to an S3 bucket, and the workers' file systems are configured to mount or sync from that bucket. Changes take effect on the next scheduler parse cycle (default: 30 seconds). This strategy is simple and fast — changes are live within a minute. The drawback is that the DAG files and the Python environment are not versioned together: a DAG change that depends on a new version of the shared package may deploy before the package is installed, causing an import error until the package installation catches up.
Strategy 2: Docker Image Build

The Docker image strategy bakes DAG files, the shared package, and all dependencies into a Docker image that is tagged with the Git commit hash. Deployment means updating the worker containers to use the new image. Changes take effect when the containers restart — typically 1-5 minutes for a rolling restart. This strategy guarantees version consistency: the DAG files and the Python environment are locked together in the same image. The drawback is deployment speed — building and pushing a Docker image takes 2-5 minutes, and restarting containers adds another 1-5 minutes. SaulėGrid uses this strategy for their scheduled workers because version consistency is more important than deployment speed for production pipeline execution.

# Docker image build and deploy workflow
# .github/workflows/deploy-staging.yml (excerpt)

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          docker build -t saulegrid/airflow:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker tag saulegrid/airflow:${{ github.sha }} saulegrid/airflow:latest
      
      - name: Push to container registry
        run: |
          docker push saulegrid/airflow:${{ github.sha }}
          docker push saulegrid/airflow:latest
      
      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          helm upgrade airflow-staging helm/airflow \
            --set images.airflow.tag=${{ github.sha }} \
            --values helm/values-staging.yaml \
            --namespace saulegrid-staging

The deployment workflow builds a Docker image tagged with the Git commit SHA, pushes it to the container registry, and updates the Helm release to use the new image. The commit SHA tag provides traceability: given any running container, the team can identify exactly which Git commit it was built from and view the corresponding code, tests, and CI results. The Helm upgrade performs a rolling restart of the worker pods, replacing old pods with new ones running the updated image. During the rollout, some workers run the old image and others run the new — Airflow handles this gracefully because DAG parsing and task execution are independent per-worker operations.
Version Pinning with Image Tags

The Docker image tag is the commit SHA — not 'latest', not a version number, and not a branch name. The SHA tag is immutable: image saulegrid/airflow:abc123 always contains exactly the code from commit abc123, and it will never be overwritten by a different build. This immutability is essential for rollback: when the team rolls back to a previous revision, Helm pulls the previous SHA-tagged image, which is guaranteed to contain the exact code that was running before the failed deployment. A 'latest' tag would be overwritten by every build, making it impossible to roll back to a specific previous state. SaulėGrid's container registry retains all SHA-tagged images for 90 days, providing a 3-month rollback window.
Strategy 3: Git-Sync Sidecar

The Git-sync strategy uses a sidecar container that runs alongside each Airflow component (scheduler, workers, web server). The sidecar periodically pulls the latest DAG files from the Git repository and writes them to a shared volume that the Airflow container reads. Changes take effect on the next scheduler parse cycle after the sync completes — typically 1-2 minutes depending on the sync interval. This strategy combines the speed of file sync (no image rebuild) with the version tracking of Git (every sync is traceable to a Git commit). SaulėGrid uses Git-sync for their development and staging environments because the faster deployment cycle supports rapid iteration.
SaulėGrid's Hybrid Strategy

left.label: Dev + Staging (Git-Sync)
points: Git-sync sidecar pulls from main every 60 seconds
pip install -e during container startup
1-2 minutes (next sync cycle)
DAG files may update before package installs
Revert the Git commit, wait for sync
Rapid iteration, testing deployment changes

right.label: Production (Docker Image)
points: DAG files baked into Docker image
Package installed during Docker image build
5-10 minutes (build + push + rolling restart)
DAG files and package are locked in the same image
Helm rollback to previous image tag
Stable production deployment with guaranteed consistency

blocks.content: The strategy choice also affects how the team handles the shared Python package. With Docker image deployment, the package is installed during the image build and is frozen with the DAG files — guaranteed consistency. With Git-sync, the package must be installed separately, either during container startup (adds 30-60 seconds to startup time) or pre-installed in the base image (requires a separate image build for package changes). SaulėGrid's staging environment uses Git-sync for DAG files but bakes the shared package into the base image, updating the base image only when the package changes. This hybrid minimizes startup time while providing rapid DAG iteration.

The hybrid strategy uses different deployment mechanisms for different environments because the requirements differ. Development and staging prioritize deployment speed — a developer needs to see their changes in staging within minutes, not after a 10-minute image build. Production prioritizes version consistency — a DAG file and its shared package must be from the same commit, guaranteed by the Docker image. The hybrid approach is operationally more complex (two deployment mechanisms to maintain) but matches each environment's dominant requirement. SaulėGrid's deployment pipeline automatically selects the strategy based on the target environment.

blocks.title: Deployment during active runs
blocks.content: Deploying DAG changes while tasks are running requires caution. If a running task uses a function from the shared package and the deployment changes that function, the running task still uses the old code (it was loaded before the deployment). This is generally safe — the running task completes with the old code, and the next run uses the new code. However, if the deployment changes the DAG structure (adds or removes tasks, changes dependencies), the scheduler may see a mismatch between the running DAG Run's task instances and the new DAG definition. SaulėGrid mitigates this by deploying during the quiet period (10:00-14:00) when no DAG Runs are in progress.

SaulėGrid operates three Airflow environments: development (each engineer's local machine), staging (a shared environment that mirrors production configuration), and production (the live platform serving downstream teams). Code changes flow through these environments in a defined sequence — development to staging to production — with validation at each stage. The promotion workflow ensures that code is tested in an increasingly production-like environment before reaching the live platform, catching issues that only manifest with real connections, real data volumes, or real scheduling interactions.
The Promotion Workflow

blocks.title: Environment promotion flow
blocks.content:
Developer laptop            Staging                    Production
(LocalExecutor,             (CeleryExecutor,           (CeleryExecutor,
 SQLite, mock data)          real connections,           real connections,
                             test data volumes)          full data volumes)

  ┌──────────┐   merge to   ┌──────────────┐  approved  ┌──────────────┐
  │ Feature  │   main       │  Auto-deploy  │  deploy    │  Manual      │
  │ branch   │─────────────▶│  to staging   │──────────▶│  deploy to   │
  │          │              │               │           │  production  │
  └──────────┘              └──────────────┘           └──────────────┘
       │                          │                          │
       ▼                          ▼                          ▼
  CI tests pass              Staging validation          Production
  + code review              checklist (manual)          verification
  + PR approved              + smoke tests               + SLA monitoring
blocks.caption: Development → Staging is automatic (every merge triggers staging deployment). Staging → Production is manual (requires validation checklist approval). Production is verified by the SLA monitoring system.

blocks.content: Automatic Staging Deployment

Every merge to main automatically triggers a staging deployment. SaulėGrid's staging deployment pipeline builds the Docker image (or triggers Git-sync, depending on the strategy), runs the configuration initialization script with staging parameters, and waits for the scheduler to parse all DAGs. The deployment is complete when the staging Airflow UI shows the expected DAG count with no import errors. This automatic deployment means that staging always reflects the current main branch — there is never a gap between 'what is in Git' and 'what is in staging.' Developers who need to verify their changes in staging simply merge their PR and wait 5-10 minutes for the deployment to complete.
The Staging Validation Checklist

# SaulėGrid staging validation checklist
# Complete before approving production deployment

## Automated Checks (CI pipeline)
- [ ] All CI tests passed on the merged commit
- [ ] Docker image built and pushed successfully
- [ ] Staging deployment completed without errors

## DAG Verification (Airflow UI)
- [ ] Expected DAG count visible in staging UI (12 DAGs)
- [ ] No import errors in the scheduler log
- [ ] New/modified DAGs appear with correct scheduling

## Connection Verification
- [ ] airflow connections test passes for all connections
- [ ] No authentication errors in recent task logs

## Smoke Test (if applicable)
- [ ] Trigger a manual run of the modified DAG
- [ ] Verify task completion and output correctness
- [ ] Check XCom values and Dataset events

## Approval
- [ ] Validation completed by: ________
- [ ] Date: ________
- [ ] Approved for production deployment: Yes / No

The staging validation checklist combines automated checks (CI passed, deployment succeeded) with manual verification (DAGs visible in UI, connections work, smoke test passes). The manual steps exist because some validation cannot be automated: verifying that a DAG's scheduling configuration makes sense in context, confirming that a smoke test's output looks correct, and checking that no unexpected side effects occurred. The checklist is a GitHub issue template — the developer creates a validation issue, fills in each checkbox, and links the issue to the production deployment PR. The checklist provides an audit trail: for any production deployment, the team can find the corresponding validation issue and verify what was checked.

The staging validation checklist distinguishes between automated checks (which passed in CI and should still pass in staging — a failure here indicates an environment issue, not a code issue) and manual checks (which require human judgment). The automated checks are a sanity verification: they confirm that what worked in CI also works in the staging environment. The manual checks are the value-added step: the developer verifies that the DAG's behavior in a production-like environment matches their expectations, that the output data looks correct, and that no unexpected side effects occurred. The checklist takes 30 minutes for routine changes and up to a full day for complex changes that require a smoke test run and output verification.
Manual Production Deployment

Production deployment is always manual — it requires a human to approve the deployment after completing the staging validation checklist. SaulėGrid's production deployment workflow is a separate GitHub Actions workflow that is triggered manually (workflow_dispatch) by a team member with deployment permissions. The workflow takes the Git commit SHA as input (defaulting to the latest main commit), builds or retrieves the Docker image for that SHA, and deploys it to the production Kubernetes namespace. The manual trigger ensures that production deployments are deliberate, traceable, and never accidental — an automatic deployment to production would mean that every merge to main immediately affects the live platform.
Environment Parity

Staging must be similar enough to production that staging validation predicts production behavior. SaulėGrid's staging environment uses the same Airflow version, the same executor type (CeleryExecutor), the same provider packages, and the same configuration structure as production. The differences are intentional and documented: staging uses the provider's test endpoints (not production endpoints), staging has smaller worker capacity (2 workers vs 3), and staging processes a subset of the data (100,000 meters vs 1,200,000). These differences keep staging costs low while preserving the configuration and behavioral parity that makes staging validation meaningful.
Promotion Audit Trail

Every production deployment is traceable through three artifacts: the Git commit SHA (which code was deployed), the staging validation issue (what was verified before promotion), and the production deployment workflow run (who triggered the deployment and when). The three artifacts form an unbroken audit chain that the team can reference during incident investigation ('what changed before the failure?'), security review ('who approved this deployment?'), and compliance reporting ('can you demonstrate that every production change was tested and reviewed?'). SaulėGrid retains deployment workflow logs for 12 months, matching the retention period for their regulatory audit requirements.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's deployment cadence: staging deployments happen automatically on every merge (multiple times per week). Production deployments happen during the 10:00-14:00 window on weekdays, typically 1-2 times per week. Emergency hotfix deployments can happen outside the window with team lead approval. The regular cadence means that production is never more than a few days behind the latest code, which reduces the risk of large batch deployments where many changes are deployed simultaneously and a failure is harder to diagnose.

Every deployment carries the risk of introducing a regression — a change that passes all tests and staging validation but causes a failure in production. The rollback procedure is the safety net that restores the previous working state when a deployment goes wrong. SaulėGrid's rollback policy is that any production deployment must be reversible within 15 minutes. The 15-minute target is driven by the SLA architecture: if a rollback takes longer than 15 minutes, the cascading impact on the Bronze-Silver-Gold chain risks an SLA breach that is harder to recover from than the original deployment issue.
Rollback Mechanisms by Deployment Strategy

rows: Helm rollback to previous image tag: helm rollback airflow-prod 1
Running tasks complete with old code; new runs use rolled-back code
Revert the Git commit: git revert HEAD && git push
Sync picks up the reverted code on the next poll
Re-sync previous version: aws s3 sync s3://dags-v-prev/ s3://dags/
Requires maintaining a backup of the previous version

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's production environment uses Docker image deployment, so rollback is a Helm rollback command that reverts to the previous image tag. Helm maintains a history of releases, and each release is tagged with the Docker image's Git commit SHA. The rollback restarts the worker pods with the previous image — the same image that was running before the failed deployment. The rollback is atomic in the sense that all workers eventually run the same (previous) image, but during the rolling restart, some workers temporarily run the new (broken) image while others run the old (working) image. Tasks scheduled during this transition may experience different behavior depending on which worker executes them.
The Rollback Decision Framework

Not every production issue requires a rollback. SaulėGrid uses a decision framework to determine the appropriate response. If the issue is a single task failure caused by a code bug (for example, a typo in a column name), the fix is a forward-fix: deploy a corrected version rather than rolling back. If the issue affects multiple DAGs or the scheduler (for example, an import error that prevents DAG parsing), the response is an immediate rollback — the impact is too broad to wait for a forward-fix. If the issue is a data quality regression (the pipeline runs but produces incorrect output), the response depends on whether the incorrect output has already been consumed by downstream systems — if yes, rollback plus data correction; if no, forward-fix is sufficient.

# SaulėGrid's production rollback procedure

# Step 1: Verify the issue justifies rollback
# (Use the decision framework: broad impact → rollback, narrow impact → forward-fix)

# Step 2: Identify the target rollback version
helm history airflow-prod --namespace saulegrid-prod
# Shows: REVISION  UPDATED                  STATUS      DESCRIPTION
#        5         2026-03-24 10:30:00      deployed    Upgrade to abc123
#        4         2026-03-21 11:00:00      superseded  Upgrade to def456

# Step 3: Execute the rollback
helm rollback airflow-prod 4 --namespace saulegrid-prod
# Rolls back to revision 4 (the previous working version)

# Step 4: Verify the rollback
kubectl get pods -n saulegrid-prod -w
# Watch pods restart with the previous image

# Step 5: Verify DAG parsing
kubectl logs -n saulegrid-prod deployment/airflow-scheduler | grep 'import_errors'
# Should show: import_errors={}

# Step 6: Verify the next scheduled DAG Run succeeds
# Monitor the Airflow UI for the next Bronze run

# Step 7: Communicate the rollback
# Post in #saulegrid-engineering: 'Production rolled back from abc123 to def456.
# Reason: [description]. Next steps: [fix plan].'

The rollback procedure is a seven-step process that SaulėGrid rehearses quarterly in staging. Step 1 (verify the issue) prevents unnecessary rollbacks for issues that are better addressed with a forward-fix. Step 2 (identify the target) ensures the rollback goes to a known-good version, not just 'the previous version' (which may have been a bad version that was quickly superseded). Steps 3-5 execute and verify the rollback. Step 6 waits for confirmation that the platform is functional. Step 7 communicates the rollback to the team — a rollback without communication leads to confusion when other engineers see production running a different version than main.
Post-Rollback Recovery

A rollback restores the code to the previous version but does not restore the data. If the failed deployment ran tasks that produced incorrect output before the rollback, that output persists in the Bronze, Silver, or Gold layers. The post-rollback recovery procedure determines whether the incorrect output needs correction: check the output timestamps to determine which DAG Runs used the broken code, compare the output against expected values to determine whether the data is actually incorrect (sometimes the code change does not affect the output), and if correction is needed, clear the affected task instances and let the retry produce correct output using the rolled-back code. Because all SaulėGrid tasks are idempotent (Module 3), the rerun overwrites the incorrect output with correct output.
Communication During Rollback

A rollback affects every team member and every downstream consumer. SaulėGrid's rollback communication protocol has three stages. Immediate notification (within 5 minutes of the rollback decision): a Slack message in #saulegrid-engineering stating that a rollback is in progress, the reason, and the expected duration. Completion notification (within 5 minutes of rollback completion): a follow-up message confirming that the rollback is complete, the platform is functional, and the next DAG Run is expected at [time]. Post-mortem notification (within 24 hours): a brief write-up documenting the deployment failure, the rollback decision rationale, the rollback execution, and the preventive measures being added. The communication protocol prevents the confusion that occurs when team members discover production is running a different version than expected.
Rollback Testing

SaulėGrid tests their rollback procedure quarterly by performing a simulated rollback in staging: deploy a known change, execute the rollback procedure, and verify that the platform returns to the pre-deployment state. The quarterly test serves three purposes: it verifies that the procedure documentation is accurate (documentation drifts if it is not exercised), it ensures that the team remembers the procedure (a rollback at 2 AM is not the time to read the documentation for the first time), and it catches environmental changes that may have invalidated the procedure (for example, a Helm version upgrade that changed the rollback command syntax). SaulėGrid's last quarterly test caught an issue where a new Kubernetes RBAC policy prevented the deployment service account from executing helm rollback — the fix was applied before any production rollback was needed.

SaulėGrid's rollback history provides data for improving the deployment pipeline. The team has executed two production rollbacks in six months. The first was caused by a schema change in the Silver transformation that broke the Gold aggregation — the CI did not catch it because the integration tests used a fixture that did not exercise the specific column the schema change affected. The preventive measure was adding a cross-layer integration test that runs the Silver-to-Gold path against a fixture containing the affected column types. The second rollback was caused by a misconfigured pool size that was correct in the values file but overridden by a manual UI change in staging that was not reflected in the production values file. The preventive measure was the configuration drift detection from Module 5, lesson 6.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: A rollback procedure that has never been tested is not a rollback procedure — it is a hope. SaulėGrid treats rollback rehearsal with the same seriousness as fire drills: the procedure is documented, tested quarterly, and every team member has performed a rollback in staging at least once. The 15-minute rollback target is achievable only because the team has practiced the procedure and knows each step without consulting documentation under pressure. The quarterly rehearsal is a 30-minute investment that provides confidence that the safety net actually works.

SaulėGrid's requirements.txt pins every dependency to an exact version — Apache Airflow 2.8.1, the SFTP provider 4.8.0, the Postgres provider 5.10.0, and 30 other packages. These pins guarantee reproducibility but create a maintenance obligation: new versions fix bugs, close security vulnerabilities, and add features that the team may need. Ignoring updates leaves the platform on increasingly outdated versions with known security issues. Updating too aggressively risks introducing breaking changes that destabilize production. The dependency upgrade process balances these concerns through a quarterly upgrade cycle with structured testing.
The Quarterly Upgrade Cycle

SaulėGrid upgrades dependencies quarterly during the months of January, April, July, and October. The cycle is deliberately scheduled during a low-pressure sprint that does not overlap with regulatory reporting deadlines or major data migration projects. The upgrade process has four structured phases: assessment (identify available updates, review their changelogs, and categorize by risk), testing (update requirements.txt and run the full test suite to detect incompatibilities), staging validation (deploy the updated dependencies to the staging environment and monitor for one full week), and production deployment (promote to production using the standard promotion workflow with extended monitoring).
Phase 1: Assessment

# Phase 1: Identify available updates
# Run pip-outdated to compare current pins against latest versions

pip install pip-audit pip-outdated

# Check for security vulnerabilities in current pins
pip-audit -r requirements.txt

# List outdated packages
pip list --outdated --format=columns

# Example output:
# Package                          Version  Latest   Type
# apache-airflow                   2.8.1    2.9.0    wheel
# apache-airflow-providers-sftp    4.8.0    4.9.1    wheel
# apache-airflow-providers-postgres 5.10.0  5.11.0   wheel
# pendulum                         3.0.0    3.0.0    wheel  (up to date)

# Review changelogs for each outdated package:
# - Major version bumps (2.8→3.0): breaking changes expected, defer unless necessary
# - Minor version bumps (2.8→2.9): new features, possible deprecations
# - Patch version bumps (2.8.1→2.8.2): bug fixes, security patches

The assessment phase categorizes each available update by risk level. Security patches (pip-audit findings) are prioritized regardless of the quarterly schedule — a critical vulnerability is patched immediately as an emergency update. Major version bumps are evaluated individually: Airflow 3.0 would require a dedicated migration effort, not a quarterly update. Minor version bumps are the standard quarterly update scope: they may introduce deprecation warnings or behavior changes that require DAG code adjustments. Patch version bumps are low-risk and are included in every quarterly update. SaulėGrid documents the assessment in a GitHub issue that lists every package, its current and target version, the risk level, and the relevant changelog entries.
Phase 2: Testing

# Phase 2: Update pins and run the full test suite

# Create an upgrade branch
git checkout -b chore/quarterly-dependency-upgrade-q2-2026

# Update the target packages in requirements.txt
# (manually — review each version change against its changelog)
vim requirements.txt

# Install the updated dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Run the full test suite
pytest tests/ -v

# If tests fail, investigate:
# - Read the changelog for the updated package
# - Check if the test failure is a genuine incompatibility or a test that needs updating
# - Adjust DAG code or test code as needed

# Commit the updated requirements.txt AND any code adjustments
git add requirements.txt dags/ saulegrid_common/ tests/
git commit -m 'chore: quarterly dependency upgrade Q2 2026'
git push origin chore/quarterly-dependency-upgrade-q2-2026

The testing phase is deliberately manual rather than automated because it requires judgment at each step. A developer reviews each changelog to understand what changed, runs the test suite to detect incompatibilities, and adjusts code when necessary. The code adjustments — updating a deprecated API call, changing a parameter name, adjusting an assertion that checks version-specific behavior — are committed alongside the requirements.txt update so that the upgrade is an atomic change. If the test suite passes without code changes, the upgrade is a simple pin update. If code changes are needed, they are reviewed in the same PR, providing context for why the changes were made.

The testing phase updates requirements.txt with the target versions and runs the full test suite. The process is deliberately manual rather than automated: a developer creates a branch, updates the version pins, runs the test suite locally, investigates any failures, adjusts DAG code if needed to accommodate API changes, and opens a PR with the updated pins and any code changes. This manual approach ensures that every version change is reviewed and that any required code adjustments are committed alongside the version update — maintaining the atomic change principle.
Phase 3: Staging Validation

After CI passes, the updated dependencies are deployed to staging through the standard promotion workflow. SaulėGrid runs the staging environment with the updated dependencies for one full week before promoting to production. During this week, the team monitors: scheduler parsing time (some provider updates affect parsing performance), task execution behavior (checking that all DAGs run successfully through at least 5 daily cycles), and memory usage (some library updates change memory allocation patterns). If any anomaly is detected, the upgrade is revised or deferred. The one-week staging window is longer than a typical code deployment (which validates in staging for 1-2 days) because dependency changes can have subtle effects that only manifest after multiple execution cycles.
The Canary Approach for Major Updates

For Airflow minor version upgrades (2.8 → 2.9) or major provider updates that change operator behavior, SaulėGrid uses a canary approach: the new version is deployed to one production worker while the remaining workers continue running the old version. Tasks that execute on the canary worker use the new code; tasks on the other workers use the old code. The canary runs for 48 hours while the team monitors for errors, performance changes, or behavioral differences. If the canary is clean, the remaining workers are updated. If the canary shows issues, only the canary worker is rolled back — the impact is limited to the tasks that happened to execute on that worker.

The canary approach requires that the old and new Airflow versions can coexist in the same cluster — both versions must be able to read the same metadata database schema and process the same DAG files. This is generally true for patch and minor version upgrades within the same major version (2.8.1 → 2.9.0) but may not hold for major version upgrades (2.x → 3.x) that change the database schema or the DAG API. SaulėGrid evaluates version coexistence as part of the assessment phase: if the changelog indicates database schema changes, the canary approach is not suitable and a full-cluster upgrade with a maintenance window is required instead.

blocks.title: Emergency security patches
blocks.content: Security vulnerabilities discovered by pip-audit do not wait for the quarterly cycle. SaulėGrid's emergency patch process: assess the vulnerability severity (CVSS score and exploitability), update the affected package to the patched version, run the full test suite, deploy to staging for a 24-hour validation window (not the usual one-week window), and deploy to production. The compressed timeline is acceptable because the scope is narrow (one package, one version bump) and the risk of not patching (an exploitable vulnerability) outweighs the risk of a regression. SaulėGrid has executed two emergency patches — both for transitive dependencies with critical CVEs — and both were deployed within 48 hours of discovery.

The previous lessons covered deploying DAG code and Python packages to the Airflow platform. This lesson covers deploying the platform itself — the Airflow scheduler, workers, web server, metadata database, and message broker — as version-controlled infrastructure. SaulėGrid manages their Airflow deployment using Helm charts on Kubernetes, with environment-specific values files that define the executor configuration, worker count, resource limits, and scheduler parameters. Every infrastructure change — adding a worker, adjusting a configuration property, upgrading the Airflow version — goes through the same PR review and CI validation process as DAG code changes.
Helm Charts for Airflow

Helm is the package manager for Kubernetes: it defines the desired state of a Kubernetes application as a set of templates and values, and the helm upgrade command reconciles the running cluster with the desired state. The Apache Airflow community maintains an official Helm chart that deploys all Airflow components — scheduler, web server, workers, database migration job, and optional components like Flower and StatsD. SaulėGrid uses this official chart with custom values files that override the defaults for their specific requirements: CeleryExecutor, Redis broker, 3 workers with queue-specific configuration, and the saulegrid_common package installed as a pip extra.

# helm/values-production.yaml — production-specific Helm values
# Overrides the official Airflow Helm chart defaults

airflowVersion: 2.8.1

executor: CeleryExecutor

redis:
  enabled: true
  password: ${REDIS_PASSWORD}  # Injected from Vault at deploy time

scheduler:
  replicas: 1
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: '1'
      memory: 2Gi
    limits:
      cpu: '2'
      memory: 4Gi

workers:
  replicas: 3
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: '2'
      memory: 4Gi
    limits:
      cpu: '4'
      memory: 8Gi
  extraInitContainers:
    - name: init-config
      image: saulegrid/airflow:${GIT_SHA}
      command: ['python', 'scripts/init_config.py', '--env', 'production']

webserver:
  replicas: 1
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: '500m'
      memory: 1Gi

config:
  core:
    dags_folder: /opt/airflow/dags
    load_examples: 'false'
    parallelism: '24'
  scheduler:
    scheduler_heartbeat_sec: '5'
    scheduler_zombie_task_threshold: '120'
  secrets:
    backend: airflow.providers.hashicorp.secrets.vault.VaultBackend
    backend_kwargs: '{...}'  # Vault configuration

extraPipPackages:
  - -e /opt/airflow/saulegrid_common

The values file defines every aspect of the production Airflow deployment: the Airflow version, executor type, component resource allocations, Airflow configuration overrides, and the init container that runs the configuration initialization script from Module 5. The ${GIT_SHA} and ${REDIS_PASSWORD} variables are injected at deployment time — GIT_SHA from the CI pipeline and REDIS_PASSWORD from Vault. The values file is version-controlled alongside the DAG code, so infrastructure changes go through the same PR review process: a change to worker resource limits requires a PR, a code review, and a staging validation before it reaches production.
Environment-Specific Values

SaulėGrid maintains separate values files for staging and production. The staging values file uses smaller resource allocations (1 worker vs 3, 2Gi memory vs 8Gi), a smaller metadata database instance, and the staging Vault path for secrets. The production values file uses production-scale resources and the production Vault path. Both files reference the same Helm chart version and the same Airflow image — only the resource allocations and environment-specific configuration differ. When the team needs to test a Helm chart upgrade or a new configuration property, they apply it to staging first using the staging values file, validate it for a week, and then apply the same change to the production values file.
Docker Compose for Development

# docker-compose.yml — local development environment
# Mirrors production topology but with minimal resources

version: '3.8'
services:
  airflow-scheduler:
    image: apache/airflow:2.8.1
    environment:
      AIRFLOW__CORE__EXECUTOR: LocalExecutor
      AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN: postgresql://airflow:airflow@postgres:5432/airflow
      AIRFLOW__CORE__LOAD_EXAMPLES: 'false'
    volumes:
      - ./dags:/opt/airflow/dags
      - ./saulegrid_common:/opt/airflow/saulegrid_common
      - ./plugins:/opt/airflow/plugins
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy

  airflow-webserver:
    image: apache/airflow:2.8.1
    ports:
      - '8080:8080'
    command: webserver
    volumes:
      - ./dags:/opt/airflow/dags
    depends_on:
      - airflow-scheduler

  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: airflow
      POSTGRES_USER: airflow
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: airflow
    healthcheck:
      test: pg_isready -U airflow
      interval: 5s

The Docker Compose development environment serves as the onboarding entry point for new team members. The setup instructions in the README are three commands: git clone, pip install, docker-compose up. Within 5 minutes, the new developer has a running Airflow instance with all 12 DAGs visible in the web UI at localhost:8080. The DAG files are mounted as volumes, so changes made in the developer's editor are immediately visible in the Airflow UI on the next parse cycle. This instant-feedback loop accelerates learning: a new developer can modify a DAG, see the change reflected in the UI, and observe the scheduler's behavior — all within minutes of their first code edit.

The Docker Compose file provides a local development environment that mirrors the production topology at minimal scale: one scheduler, one web server, and one PostgreSQL database, all running on the developer's laptop. The DAG directory and shared package are mounted as volumes, so code changes take effect immediately on the next scheduler parse cycle — no rebuild required. The development environment uses LocalExecutor (no Redis or Celery needed), which simplifies the local setup while maintaining the same DAG parsing and task execution behavior as production. A developer can start the environment with docker-compose up -d and have a running Airflow instance with the current DAGs in under 30 seconds.
Infrastructure Change Management

Infrastructure changes follow the same change management process as DAG code: create a branch, modify the values file, open a PR, get review approval, merge, and deploy. SaulėGrid classifies infrastructure changes by impact: low-impact changes (adjusting a log level, changing a monitoring threshold) can be deployed during the regular deployment window. Medium-impact changes (adding a worker, adjusting parallelism) are deployed during a maintenance window with monitoring. High-impact changes (changing the executor type, upgrading the Airflow version, modifying the database configuration) require a dedicated migration plan with rollback procedures and are deployed during an extended maintenance window with the full team available.
Drift Between Helm Values and Docker Compose

The Helm values (production) and Docker Compose (development) must be kept in sync for the properties that affect DAG behavior: Airflow version, provider package versions, configuration properties that affect parsing or execution, and environment variable names. SaulėGrid maintains a sync check script that compares the Airflow version in the Helm values against the Docker Compose image tag and the requirements.txt Airflow pin — all three must match. The check runs as part of the CI pipeline and fails if any version diverges. Configuration properties that differ between development and production (executor type, parallelism, database URL) are documented in the Helm values file with comments explaining why they differ.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's infrastructure principle: every component of the Airflow deployment is defined in version-controlled files. The Helm values define the cluster configuration. The Docker Compose file defines the development environment. The requirements.txt defines the Python dependencies. The config/ directory defines the Airflow configuration. If any of these files are lost, the team can reconstruct the entire platform from the Git repository. This 'cattle not pets' approach means the production Airflow instance is disposable — it can be destroyed and recreated in under an hour from the repository, making infrastructure failures recoverable rather than catastrophic.

This module has covered seven deployment topics: Git-based DAG management, CI pipeline design, DAG validation checks, deployment strategies, environment promotion, rollback procedures, dependency upgrades, and infrastructure as code. This lesson synthesizes every topic into SaulėGrid's complete deployment pipeline: how a code change flows from a developer's laptop to the production cluster, what validations occur at each stage, and how the pipeline has evolved since the team's first scp deployment. Every step in the pipeline traces to a concept from this module, and every automated check was motivated by a specific incident that the check prevents.
The End-to-End Pipeline

The deployment pipeline in this diagram represents the final state of SaulėGrid's CI/CD system after three months of incremental development. Every arrow in the diagram is an automated step — no manual file copying, no ad-hoc scp commands, no reliance on individual developer discipline. The automation ensures that the pipeline behaves identically regardless of who is deploying, what time of day it is, or how much delivery pressure the team is under. The consistency of automated deployment is the foundational guarantee that makes every other module's patterns — scheduling, operators, configuration, testing — reliable in production.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's complete deployment pipeline
blocks.content:
Developer                    GitHub                         Staging                 Production
    │                           │                              │                       │
    │  create branch            │                              │                       │
    │  make changes             │                              │                       │
    │  push                     │                              │                       │
    ├──────────────────────────▶│                              │                       │
    │                           │  CI Pipeline                 │                       │
    │                           │  ├─ Lint (ruff)              │                       │
    │                           │  ├─ Deprecated API check     │                       │
    │                           │  ├─ DAG integrity tests      │                       │
    │                           │  ├─ Structure validation     │                       │
    │                           │  ├─ Unit tests               │                       │
    │                           │  └─ Integration tests (PR)   │                       │
    │                           │                              │                       │
    │  open PR                  │  Code review                 │                       │
    │◀─────────────────────────▶│  + CI must pass              │                       │
    │                           │                              │                       │
    │  merge to main            │  Auto-deploy to staging      │                       │
    │                           ├─────────────────────────────▶│  Build image          │
    │                           │                              │  Deploy (Helm)        │
    │                           │                              │  Init config          │
    │                           │                              │  Verify DAG count     │
    │                           │                              │                       │
    │  staging validation       │                              │                       │
    │  checklist completed      │                              │                       │
    │                           │                              │                       │
    │  approve prod deploy      │  Manual deploy trigger       │                       │
    │                           ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶│
    │                           │                              │  Build/retrieve image │
    │                           │                              │  Deploy (Helm)        │
    │                           │                              │  Init config          │
