    │                           │                              │  Verify DAG count     │
    │                           │                              │  SLA monitoring active│
blocks.caption: Every code change flows through the same pipeline: branch → CI → review → merge → staging → validation → production. No shortcuts, no exceptions, no manual file copying.

visual-recommendation.description: Full deployment pipeline diagram showing the complete flow from developer branch through CI, staging, and production with all validation gates and timing annotations.

blocks.content: Pipeline Timing

The timing breakdown also reveals the deployment pipeline's sensitivity to each component. If the CI pipeline slows from 90 seconds to 5 minutes, the per-push feedback loop degrades but the end-to-end time barely changes because code review dominates the timeline. If code review slows from 4 hours to 24 hours, the end-to-end time doubles and deployment frequency drops. If staging validation slows from 1 day to 1 week, deployments batch up and each deployment contains more changes — increasing the risk per deployment. SaulėGrid monitors each component's timing weekly to detect trends before they impact deployment cadence.
Deployment Cadence and Metrics

SaulėGrid deploys to production 1-2 times per week, typically on Tuesday and Thursday during the 10:00-14:00 quiet window. This cadence balances two competing goals: deploying frequently enough that each deployment contains a small, manageable set of changes with reduced risk per deployment, and deploying infrequently enough that each deployment receives adequate staging validation and thorough code review. The team tracks four deployment metrics borrowed from the DORA research on software delivery performance: deployment frequency (target: 1-2 per week), lead time from merge to production (target: under 8 business hours), change failure rate requiring rollback (target: under 5%), and mean time to recovery from a failed deployment (target: under 15 minutes). These metrics are reviewed monthly alongside the platform health metrics from Module 4.

The end-to-end time from 'developer pushes code' to 'code is running in production' is approximately 2-8 hours: 90 seconds for CI, 1-4 hours for code review, 5-10 minutes for staging deployment, 30 minutes to 1 day for staging validation, and 5-10 minutes for production deployment. The bottleneck is code review — the automated steps add less than 20 minutes total. SaulėGrid's code review SLA is 4 hours during business hours, which means most changes reach production within the same business day. Emergency hotfixes can bypass the staging validation window (with team lead approval) and reach production in under 30 minutes from merge.
The Pipeline's Evolution

SaulėGrid's deployment pipeline evolved over three months from the initial scp approach. Month 1: established the Git repository and CI pipeline with integrity tests only — the minimum investment that caught the circular import class of failures. Month 2: added the staging environment, Docker image deployment, and the promotion workflow — eliminating the 'works on my machine' class of failures. Month 3: added the structure validation tests, deprecation checks, and the complete rollback procedure — achieving the full pipeline described in this lesson. Each month's additions were motivated by specific incidents from the previous month, and each addition was justified by the incidents it would have prevented.

The pipeline continues to evolve. The quarterly CI check review adds or removes checks based on their incident prevention rate. The annual dependency upgrade process adjusts the quarterly schedule based on the volume and severity of available updates. The rollback procedure is refined after every rollback event (SaulėGrid has executed two production rollbacks — both within the 15-minute target). The pipeline is a living system that adapts to the platform's changing risk profile, not a static configuration that was designed once and left unchanged.
Continuous Pipeline Improvement

The pipeline's prevention record is its strongest justification. In the six months before CI/CD adoption, SaulėGrid experienced 14 deployment-related production incidents. In the six months after, they experienced 2 — both caught within the 15-minute rollback window and both leading to new CI checks that prevent recurrence. The 86% reduction in deployment-related incidents was achieved with a 3-month investment in pipeline infrastructure. The ongoing maintenance cost — CI pipeline updates, quarterly dependency upgrades, rollback rehearsals — is approximately 4 hours per month of engineering time, offset many times over by the engineering hours no longer spent on midnight incident response.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: Automated deployment transforms DAG code from files on a developer's laptop into a production-running pipeline through a chain of automated validations — linting, integrity tests, structure checks, unit tests, integration tests, staging validation, and production verification — ensuring that every change is tested, reviewed, traceable, and reversible, and that the three-incident week that motivated the pipeline can never happen again.

chapters.title: Security and Access Control
chapters.description: Master Airflow's security model for multi-team environments: role-based access control, DAG-level permissions, audit logging, API authentication, and the security policies that enable safe shared access to a production orchestration platform.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Airflow instance was originally built for one team — Matas and Ieva managing 12 DAGs. When the analytics team and the operations team were granted access to monitor their downstream data, the platform became a shared resource serving three teams with different responsibilities and different risk profiles. On March 8th, an analytics team member investigating a data delay accidentally paused the dag_bronze_meters DAG from the Airflow UI. The pause prevented the midnight ingestion run, which cascaded through Silver and Gold, causing a 4-hour data freshness breach that affected billing calculations. The analyst had no malicious intent — they were exploring the UI to understand why their dashboard data was late, and the pause toggle was a single click away from the DAG list view.
Why Single-Team Security Fails at Multi-Team Scale

In the junior-level track, security was implicit: only the data engineering team had Airflow access, so every user was trusted to perform any action. At mid-level scale — 3 teams, 12 people, 12 DAGs — this trust model breaks. The analytics team needs to view DAG status and read logs to diagnose data freshness issues, but they should not be able to pause, trigger, or modify DAGs. The operations team needs to monitor platform health metrics and trigger reprocessing workflows, but they should not be able to modify Bronze or Silver DAGs that they do not own. Each team has a legitimate need for access, but the scope of that access must match their responsibility — not exceed it.

rows: Full control: create, modify, trigger, pause, delete DAGs
View DAG status, read task logs, inspect XCom values
Pause, trigger, modify, or delete any DAG
Trigger reprocessing DAGs, view monitoring DAGs, read logs
Modify Bronze/Silver DAGs, change connections or variables
Trigger smoke tests, check DAG health via API
Access the web UI, modify DAGs, read connection credentials

blocks.content: The access requirement differs not just by team but by individual. Within the operations team, the manager needs to view overall platform health but should not trigger reprocessing workflows — that is the operations engineers' responsibility. Within the analytics team, the lead analyst may need broader visibility than the junior analysts to support cross-domain investigations. SaulėGrid's role model starts with team-level roles (analytics_viewer, operations_user) and can be refined with individual permissions when the team-level granularity is insufficient. The current role model serves 12 users across 3 teams; if the user count grows to 30 or more, the team plans to introduce sub-team roles that provide finer-grained access within each team.
The Three Security Requirements

Multi-team Airflow security must satisfy three requirements. First, visibility control: each team should see only the DAGs relevant to their work — the analytics team does not need to see the maintenance DAG that vacuums the metadata database. Second, action restriction: each team should be able to perform only the actions appropriate to their role — viewing is different from triggering, which is different from modifying. Third, accountability: every action must be traceable to a specific user — when a DAG is paused, the audit trail must record who paused it and when, enabling incident investigation and compliance reporting.
What This Module Covers

The security mechanisms covered in this module form a layered architecture where each mechanism addresses a different aspect of the multi-team security problem. Authentication (lesson 5) establishes identity before any Airflow interaction begins. RBAC (lessons 2-3) determines what actions the authenticated user can perform globally. DAG-level access control (lesson 4) determines which specific DAGs the user can see and act on. API security (lesson 6) applies the same controls to programmatic access. Audit logging (lesson 7) records every action for accountability and compliance. Network security (lesson 8) protects the infrastructure that hosts all the other mechanisms.

This module teaches the security mechanisms that transform Airflow from a single-team tool into a multi-team platform. RBAC (lessons 2-3) provides role-based access control that maps team responsibilities to permission sets. DAG-level access control (lesson 4) restricts which roles can see and act on specific DAGs. Authentication backends (lesson 5) control how users prove their identity. API security (lesson 6) protects the REST API from unauthorized access. Audit logging (lesson 7) records every security-relevant action. Network security (lesson 8) protects the Airflow deployment at the infrastructure level. Security anti-patterns (lesson 9) catalogs the common mistakes. Lesson 10 synthesizes into SaulėGrid's complete security framework.
Implications

The security model interacts with every previous module. The Secrets Backend from Module 5 stores credentials outside the metadata database — a security improvement that this module's audit logging verifies. The CI/CD pipeline from Module 7 uses a service account that this module's API security configures. The executor architecture from Module 4 creates network boundaries that this module's network security hardens. Security is not a standalone concern — it is a cross-cutting property that touches every component of the platform.
The Security Investment

The security investment also includes ongoing maintenance: quarterly role audits (2 hours), quarterly token rotation (1 hour), monthly audit log review (30 minutes), and weekly temporary access review (15 minutes). The total ongoing cost is approximately 12 hours per quarter — a modest investment for a platform that serves three teams and processes billing-critical data. SaulėGrid's security cost is proportional to the platform's risk profile: a single-team platform with non-critical data would need less investment, while a platform serving external customers or processing PII would need more.

SaulėGrid's security framework was implemented over two sprints — approximately 4 weeks of engineering effort. The investment included configuring OAuth authentication (3 days), designing and implementing custom RBAC roles (2 days), adding DAG-level access control to all 12 DAGs (1 day), configuring API authentication and rate limiting (2 days), setting up audit logging and the monitoring task (2 days), and hardening network security (3 days). The investment was motivated by the March 8th incident and the recognition that the platform's growing user base required explicit access controls. The payback period was immediate: no unauthorized actions have occurred since the framework was deployed.

blocks.title: The security maturity shift
blocks.content: The shift from single-team to multi-team security is not about adding restrictions — it is about making the implicit trust model explicit. When only two engineers have access, the trust model is 'everyone can do everything because we trust each other.' When twelve people across three teams have access, the trust model must be codified: who can do what, on which DAGs, and how is that access verified and audited. The March 8th incident was not a trust violation — the analyst was a trusted colleague. It was a permission model failure — the UI gave the analyst the ability to perform an action that was outside their responsibility.

Airflow's role-based access control (RBAC) system is built on Flask-AppBuilder (FAB), the web framework that powers the Airflow UI. FAB provides a permission model based on three concepts: resources (what can be accessed — DAGs, connections, variables, the configuration page), actions (what can be done — read, create, edit, delete, menu access), and roles (named collections of resource-action permissions assigned to users). When a user logs into the Airflow UI, their role determines which pages they can see, which buttons are visible, and which actions they can perform. A user without the 'can_read on DAG' permission sees an empty DAG list. A user with 'can_read on DAG' but without 'can_edit on DAG' sees the DAG list but cannot modify any DAG's configuration.
The Built-in Roles

rows: All permissions on all resources — full platform control
Platform administrators who manage users, connections, and configuration
Matas (team lead) — manages users, security settings, and platform configuration
Read and execute permissions on DAGs, connections, variables, pools — but cannot manage users or security settings
Engineers who operate the platform daily
Ieva, Jonas — can trigger, pause, clear tasks, manage connections
Read and limited execute permissions on DAGs — can view DAG status, read logs, trigger manual runs
Team members who interact with DAGs but do not manage the platform
Operations team — can trigger reprocessing DAGs and view monitoring
Read-only permissions — can view DAG status, task logs, and execution history but cannot perform any action
Stakeholders who need visibility without action capability
Analytics team — can view DAG status and read logs for debugging
No permissions — no access to any page
Not used — all access requires authentication

blocks.content: The built-in roles provide a starting point, but they are too coarse for SaulėGrid's needs. The Viewer role gives the analytics team read access to all DAGs — including the maintenance DAG, the SLA monitor, and the CI smoke test DAG that are irrelevant to their work. The User role gives the operations team the ability to trigger any DAG — including Bronze and Silver DAGs that they should not control. The Op role gives engineers access to connection management — which should be restricted to the platform team through the Secrets Backend (Module 5). Custom roles solve these mismatches by providing fine-grained permission sets tailored to each team's actual responsibilities.
Understanding Resource-Action Pairs

The RBAC model's granularity comes from the combination of resources and actions. Airflow defines approximately 30 resources (DAG, Connection, Variable, Pool, Task Instance, Task Log, XCom, Configuration, and others) and 6 actions (can_read, can_create, can_edit, can_delete, menu_access, and can_access). A single permission is one resource-action pair — for example, can_read on Connection. A role is a collection of these pairs: the analytics_viewer role might have 8 pairs (read access to DAGs, task instances, task logs, and XCom, plus menu access to the DAGs and Task Instances pages). The platform_engineer role might have 40 pairs covering read and write access to most resources.

The resource-action model means that permissions can be tuned independently for each resource. A user can have read access to DAGs (to view status) without read access to Connections (which would reveal credentials). A user can have read access to Variables (to see configuration values) without edit access to Variables (which would allow changing pipeline behavior). This independence is what makes the custom roles from lesson 3 possible — each role is assembled from exactly the resource-action pairs that match the team's operational needs, with no bundled permissions that grant more than intended.
How Permissions Work

blocks.title: Airflow's permission model
blocks.content:
User (e.g., analyst_anna)
     │
     │  assigned to
     ▼
Role (e.g., analytics_viewer)
     │
     │  grants permissions
     ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Permission = Resource + Action       │
│                                      │
│ can_read on DAG                  ✓   │
│ can_read on Task Instance        ✓   │
│ can_read on Task Log             ✓   │
│ can_read on XCom                 ✓   │
│ can_edit on DAG                  ✗   │
│ can_delete on DAG                ✗   │
│ can_create on DAG Run            ✗   │
│ can_read on Connection           ✗   │
│ can_read on Variable             ✗   │
│ can_read on Configuration        ✗   │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: Each user is assigned a role. Each role grants a set of permissions. Each permission is a (resource, action) pair. The UI renders only the elements that the user's permissions allow.

visual-recommendation.description: Permission model diagram showing the relationship between users, roles, and resource-action permission pairs, with checkmarks and crosses indicating granted and denied permissions for the analytics_viewer role.

blocks.content: Listing Current Permissions

# List all permissions available in the Airflow instance
airflow roles list

# Show permissions for a specific role
airflow roles list-permissions --role-name Op

# Example output (abbreviated):
# Action                  | Resource
# can_read                | DAG
# can_edit                | DAG
# can_delete              | DAG
# can_create              | DAG Run
# can_read                | Task Instance
# can_read                | Task Log
# can_read                | XCom
# can_read                | Connection
# can_edit                | Connection
# can_read                | Variable
# can_edit                | Variable
# can_read                | Pool
# can_edit                | Pool
The Permission Audit Process

The permission audit script is stored in the repository alongside the security policy and runs as part of the quarterly security audit. The script outputs a table showing each role, its expected permissions (from the policy), its actual permissions (from the running instance), and any discrepancies highlighted in red. The script also checks for orphaned permissions — permissions that exist in the instance but are not assigned to any role, which may indicate a past role modification that was incompletely reverted. SaulėGrid runs the script against both staging and production to verify that both environments have consistent role configurations.

SaulėGrid audits role permissions quarterly by running airflow roles list-permissions for each custom role and comparing the output against the documented permission set in the security policy. The comparison is automated: a Python script reads the policy YAML file, queries the Airflow CLI for the actual permissions, and reports any discrepancies. A permission that exists in the running Airflow instance but not in the policy document is flagged as an unauthorized addition — it may have been added through the UI during incident response and not reverted. A permission in the policy that does not exist in the running instance is flagged as a missing enforcement — the deployment script may have failed to apply it. Both directions of mismatch are investigated and resolved during the audit.
Implications

The airflow roles list-permissions command shows every permission granted to a role. SaulėGrid uses this command during security audits to verify that each custom role has exactly the permissions documented in the security policy — no more, no less. A permission that appears in the role but not in the policy document is an unauthorized escalation that must be investigated and removed. The command output serves as the ground truth for the security audit: it reflects the actual permissions enforced by the running Airflow instance, not the intended permissions documented in a policy file.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: The most common RBAC mistake is assigning the Admin role to all users because it is the easiest way to resolve 'I cannot access X' complaints. Every Admin user has unrestricted access to the entire platform — including the security settings, the connection credentials, and the configuration page. SaulėGrid's policy: Admin is assigned to exactly one person (the team lead). All other users receive custom roles with the minimum permissions required for their work. If a user reports an access issue, the response is to review and potentially expand their custom role — never to escalate them to Admin.

The built-in roles are templates, not mandates. SaulėGrid creates custom roles that precisely match each team's responsibilities — no more permissions than needed, no fewer than required. The principle of least privilege governs every custom role: a user should have exactly the permissions necessary to perform their documented tasks, and no additional permissions 'just in case.' This principle is not about distrust — it is about limiting the blast radius of mistakes. The March 8th incident would not have occurred if the analytics team's role did not include the can_edit on DAG permission that enables the pause toggle.
Creating Custom Roles

# Create the analytics_viewer role with read-only permissions
airflow roles create analytics_viewer

# Add specific permissions to the role
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action can_read --resource DAG
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action can_read --resource 'Task Instance'
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action can_read --resource 'Task Log'
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action can_read --resource XCom
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action can_read --resource Dataset
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action menu_access --resource 'DAG Runs'
airflow roles add-perms analytics_viewer \
  --action menu_access --resource 'Task Instances'

# Verify the role's permissions
airflow roles list-permissions --role-name analytics_viewer

The analytics_viewer role grants read access to DAGs, task instances, task logs, XCom values, and Datasets — everything the analytics team needs to investigate data freshness issues and understand pipeline behavior. The role explicitly omits can_edit on DAG (prevents pausing or modifying DAGs), can_create on DAG Run (prevents triggering manual runs), can_read on Connection (prevents viewing credentials), and can_read on Variable (prevents viewing configuration values that may contain sensitive information). Each omission is a deliberate security decision that limits what an analytics team member can accidentally or intentionally affect.
SaulėGrid's Custom Role Inventory

rows: DAG read/edit/delete, Connection read/edit, Variable read/edit, Pool read/edit, trigger DAG Runs
Cannot manage users or security settings
DAG read, trigger DAG Runs for specific DAGs (reprocess, monitoring), read task logs
Cannot edit DAGs, connections, or variables; cannot trigger Bronze/Silver DAGs
DAG read, task log read, XCom read, Dataset read
Cannot edit anything, cannot trigger any DAG, cannot view connections or variables
Trigger specific DAGs via API, read DAG status via API
No web UI access, cannot modify anything, limited to API endpoints

blocks.content: Assigning Users to Roles

# Assign a user to a custom role
airflow users add-role --username anna.analytics --role analytics_viewer

# Remove a user from a role (if they change teams)
airflow users remove-role --username anna.analytics --role analytics_viewer

# List users and their roles
airflow users list

# Example output:
# Username          | Email                    | Roles
# matas.engineer    | matas@saulegrid.lt       | platform_admin
# ieva.engineer     | ieva@saulegrid.lt        | platform_engineer
# jonas.engineer    | jonas@saulegrid.lt       | platform_engineer
# anna.analytics    | anna@saulegrid.lt        | analytics_viewer
# petras.operations | petras@saulegrid.lt      | operations_user
# ci_deploy         | ci@saulegrid.internal    | ci_service

User-role assignments are managed through the Airflow CLI and documented in the security policy. SaulėGrid's onboarding checklist includes a step where the team lead assigns the appropriate role based on the new team member's team. When a team member changes roles (an analyst moving to the data engineering team), the old role is removed and the new role is assigned in the same session — there is no transition period where the user has both roles simultaneously. SaulėGrid manages role assignments as code using the initialization script from Module 5: a users.yaml file defines each user's role, and the deployment script applies the assignments during environment initialization.
Roles as Code
Role Maintenance

Custom roles require maintenance as the platform evolves. When a new Airflow feature introduces a new resource (for example, the Datasets UI added in Airflow 2.4), the team must update custom roles to grant or deny access to the new resource. When a team's responsibilities change (the analytics team now needs to trigger quality report DAGs), the team must update the corresponding role with the new permissions. SaulėGrid reviews custom role definitions quarterly during the security audit: the review compares each role's actual permissions against the documented policy, identifies any permissions that were added without updating the policy, and removes any permissions that are no longer needed. The quarterly review has caught two unintended permission escalations — both added during incident response when temporary access was granted and not revoked.

Role maintenance also includes handling edge cases: users who belong to multiple teams (assigned the union of both roles' permissions through role stacking), temporary access grants during incident response (documented with an expiration date and reverted after the incident), and service account role changes when the CI/CD pipeline's scope changes. SaulėGrid tracks temporary access grants in a Slack thread that the team lead reviews weekly — any temporary grant older than 7 days is reverted or converted to a permanent role modification through the standard PR process.
Implications

SaulėGrid's role inventory documents the rationale for each custom role's permission set. The analytics_viewer role has 8 permissions (read access to 4 resources plus menu access to 4 pages) because the analytics team needs to view DAG status, read task logs for debugging, inspect XCom values to understand data lineage, and browse Dataset events to track data freshness. Each permission has a documented justification: 'can_read on Task Log — analysts need to read error messages when investigating data delays.' This documentation ensures that the rationale survives team turnover — a future engineer reviewing the role definition can understand why each permission was included and whether it is still needed.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's role management principle: roles are defined in a YAML file (config/roles.yaml), managed by the initialization script, and versioned alongside the DAG code. Changes to roles go through the same PR review process as DAG changes — a role modification requires a PR with the justification, the security policy reference, and team lead approval. This prevents ad-hoc role modifications through the UI that bypass the review process. The initialization script overwrites role assignments on each deployment, ensuring that the YAML file is the source of truth for who has which permissions.

The custom roles from lesson 3 control what actions a user can perform across all DAGs. DAG-level access control adds a second dimension: which DAGs a user can see and act on. Without DAG-level control, the analytics_viewer role grants read access to every DAG on the platform — including maintenance DAGs, CI smoke tests, and internal monitoring workflows that are irrelevant to the analytics team and create visual noise in their UI. With DAG-level control, the analytics team sees only the Gold and reporting DAGs whose output they consume, and the operations team sees only the monitoring and reprocessing DAGs they are responsible for.
The access_control Parameter

DAG-level permissions are set using the access_control parameter on the DAG definition. The parameter accepts a dictionary mapping role names to permission sets. When access_control is set, only users whose role appears in the dictionary can see the DAG — users with roles not listed see the DAG as if it does not exist. Within the listed roles, the permission set controls which actions are allowed: a role with {'can_read'} can view the DAG but not trigger or modify it, while a role with {'can_read', 'can_edit', 'can_create'} has full control.

from airflow.decorators import dag
from pendulum import datetime

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
    schedule='0 6 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['gold', 'billing', 'saulegrid'],
    access_control={
        'platform_admin': {'can_read', 'can_edit', 'can_delete', 'can_create'},
        'platform_engineer': {'can_read', 'can_edit', 'can_create'},
        'analytics_viewer': {'can_read'},  # Analysts can view but not modify
        'operations_user': {'can_read'},   # Operations can view but not trigger
    },
)
def dag_gold_billing():
    # ... task definitions ...
    pass

dag_gold_billing()

The access_control dictionary on dag_gold_billing grants four roles different permission levels. Platform administrators and engineers have full control — they can read, edit, trigger, and delete the DAG. Analytics viewers can only read — they can see the DAG in the UI, view its run history, and read task logs, but the pause toggle, trigger button, and delete option are hidden from their UI. Operations users can also only read this specific DAG — Gold billing is not their responsibility, but they need to see its status when diagnosing downstream data freshness issues. Users with any role not listed in the access_control dictionary (for example, a hypothetical intern_viewer role) cannot see the DAG at all.
SaulėGrid's DAG Access Matrix

The access matrix reflects each team's operational role. Engineers have full access to all DAGs because they build and maintain them. Operations users can view most DAGs (for monitoring context) and can trigger the reprocessing DAG (their primary operational task). Analytics viewers can see only Gold DAGs (whose output they consume). The maintenance DAG is invisible to everyone except engineers because its function (metadata database cleanup) is not relevant to monitoring or analytics. The access matrix is documented in the security policy and implemented through access_control parameters on each DAG — the CI pipeline validates that every DAG has an access_control parameter matching the documented matrix.
The Visibility Benefit

DAG-level access control is not just about preventing unauthorized actions — it is about reducing cognitive load. The analytics team members see 2-3 Gold DAGs in their Airflow UI instead of 12 DAGs across all pipeline layers. The cleaner view helps them find the DAGs they care about faster and eliminates the confusion of seeing infrastructure DAGs (maintenance, SLA monitoring) that are irrelevant to their work. SaulėGrid's analytics team reported that the filtered view reduced their 'time to understand why data is late' from 15 minutes of UI exploration to 3 minutes of checking the visible Gold DAGs — a direct operational improvement from the security configuration.
Implementing the Access Matrix as Code

Centralizing access control profiles in the shared package ensures consistency across all DAGs and makes the access matrix auditable from a single file. When the security audit reviews DAG-level permissions, the reviewer examines security.py rather than scanning 12 individual DAG files. When a new role is added or an existing role's permissions change, the update happens in one place and propagates to all DAGs through the import. SaulėGrid's CI pipeline includes a check that verifies every DAG file uses an access_control profile from security.py rather than defining inline permissions — preventing ad-hoc permission sets that deviate from the documented policy.
Testing Access Control in CI

SaulėGrid's structure validation tests (Module 6) include a check that every DAG file uses an access_control parameter imported from saulegrid_common/security.py. The test scans each DAG file's AST for the access_control keyword argument in the @dag decorator and verifies that the value is a reference to a profile from the security module — not an inline dictionary. This enforcement ensures that access control definitions are centralized, consistent, and reviewable in a single file. A DAG with an inline access_control dictionary would pass the functional test but fail the centralization check, preventing ad-hoc permission sets that deviate from the documented security policy.

blocks.title: access_control gotcha
blocks.content: When access_control is not set on a DAG (the default), the DAG is visible to all authenticated users regardless of their role. This means a new DAG deployed without an access_control parameter is immediately visible to the analytics team, the operations team, and any other role — even if the DAG is not ready for their consumption. SaulėGrid's structure validation tests (Module 6) include a check that every DAG file has an access_control parameter set. A DAG without access_control fails CI, preventing uncontrolled visibility from reaching production.

RBAC controls what authenticated users can do. Authentication controls how users prove their identity before RBAC applies. Airflow supports multiple authentication backends — each determines how the login process works, where credentials are verified, and how user identities are managed. The choice of authentication backend affects the user experience (do users log in with a username/password or with their corporate SSO?), the security posture (are credentials managed by Airflow or by a dedicated identity provider?), and the operational overhead (does the team manage user accounts manually or are they synchronized from the corporate directory?).
Backend Options

rows: Username and password stored in Airflow's metadata database
Manual — create users with airflow users create
Simple but insecure for multi-team — passwords in the metadata database, no SSO, manual account management
Username and password verified against an LDAP/Active Directory server
Automatic — users from the LDAP directory are recognized by Airflow
Good for enterprises with existing LDAP infrastructure; SaulėGrid does not have LDAP
Users redirected to an identity provider (Google, Okta, Azure AD) for authentication
Automatic — users authenticated by the IdP are created in Airflow on first login
Selected — SaulėGrid uses Google Workspace as their identity provider
Ticket-based authentication via the Kerberos protocol
Automatic — Kerberos principals map to Airflow users
Enterprise-grade but complex; not needed for SaulėGrid's scale

blocks.content: Configuring OAuth with Google

SaulėGrid chose OAuth with Google Workspace because every team member already has a Google account through the company's workspace. The OAuth configuration redirects users to Google's login page, where they authenticate with their existing credentials (including two-factor authentication if enabled). After successful authentication, Google redirects back to Airflow with an identity token that contains the user's email address and group memberships. Airflow creates a local user record on first login and maps the user to a role based on their Google Workspace group — members of the saulegrid-engineering group are assigned the platform_engineer role, members of saulegrid-analytics are assigned analytics_viewer, and so on.

# webserver_config.py — OAuth configuration for Google Workspace
from flask_appbuilder.security.manager import AUTH_OAUTH
import os

AUTH_TYPE = AUTH_OAUTH

# OAuth provider configuration
OAUTH_PROVIDERS = [{
    'name': 'google',
    'icon': 'fa-google',
    'token_key': 'access_token',
    'remote_app': {
        'client_id': os.environ.get('GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID'),
        'client_secret': os.environ.get('GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET'),
        'api_base_url': 'https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v2/',
        'client_kwargs': {'scope': 'email profile'},
        'request_token_url': None,
        'access_token_url': 'https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/token',
        'authorize_url': 'https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth',
    },
}]

# Map Google Workspace groups to Airflow roles
AUTH_ROLES_MAPPING = {
    'saulegrid-engineering': ['platform_engineer'],
    'saulegrid-analytics': ['analytics_viewer'],
    'saulegrid-operations': ['operations_user'],
    'saulegrid-platform-admin': ['platform_admin'],
}

# Auto-register users on first login
AUTH_USER_REGISTRATION = True
AUTH_USER_REGISTRATION_ROLE = 'analytics_viewer'  # Default role for new users

The AUTH_ROLES_MAPPING dictionary maps Google Workspace group names to Airflow roles. When a user authenticates through OAuth, Airflow checks their Google group memberships and assigns the corresponding roles. If a user belongs to saulegrid-engineering, they receive the platform_engineer role. If a user is not a member of any mapped group, they receive the default registration role (analytics_viewer) — a safe default that provides read-only access while the team lead reviews and adjusts the assignment. The OAuth client_id and client_secret are stored in environment variables (not in the configuration file) and injected from the Secrets Backend during deployment.
Group-to-Role Mapping

The AUTH_ROLES_MAPPING configuration is the bridge between the corporate identity system and Airflow's RBAC model. When a user authenticates through Google OAuth, Airflow reads their Google Workspace group memberships and looks up each group in the mapping dictionary. If the user belongs to saulegrid-engineering, they are assigned the platform_engineer role. If they belong to saulegrid-analytics, they receive analytics_viewer. If they belong to multiple mapped groups, they receive all corresponding roles — the permissions are the union of all assigned roles. This group-based mapping means that role assignments are managed in Google Workspace (by the IT administrator) rather than in Airflow (by the platform team), reducing the operational burden of user management.

The group-to-role mapping also handles team changes automatically. When an analyst moves to the data engineering team, the IT administrator updates their Google Workspace group membership — removing them from saulegrid-analytics and adding them to saulegrid-engineering. On their next Airflow login, the OAuth flow reads the updated groups and maps them to the new role. No Airflow-side action is required. This automation eliminates the manual role update step that is frequently forgotten during team transitions, preventing the security gap where a former analyst retains engineering permissions for weeks until someone notices.
Authentication and RBAC Interaction

Authentication and RBAC work as two sequential gates. The authentication gate verifies identity: 'you are anna.analytics@saulegrid.lt.' The RBAC gate enforces permissions: 'anna.analytics has the analytics_viewer role, which grants can_read on DAGs but not can_edit.' A user who passes authentication but has no Airflow role sees an empty interface — they are authenticated (their identity is verified) but not authorized (they have no permissions). SaulėGrid's configuration ensures that every authenticated user has at least the analytics_viewer role through the AUTH_USER_REGISTRATION_ROLE setting, preventing the confusing experience of a blank Airflow UI after successful login.
Emergency Access

OAuth authentication depends on the Google Workspace identity provider being available. If Google experiences an outage, no user can log into Airflow — including the platform team that needs to manage the platform during the outage. SaulėGrid maintains a break-glass account: a local database user (the default admin created during Airflow initialization) with a cryptographically random password stored in Vault. The break-glass account bypasses OAuth and authenticates directly against the metadata database. The password is known only to the team lead and is changed after every use. The audit log records any login using the break-glass account, and such logins trigger an immediate security alert requiring justification. The break-glass account has been used once — during a Google Workspace maintenance window that coincided with a pipeline incident.

The break-glass account represents a deliberate trade-off between security and availability. A pure OAuth-only configuration is more secure (no local credentials to compromise) but less available (a Google outage prevents all access). SaulėGrid's risk assessment concluded that the availability risk of OAuth-only authentication outweighed the security risk of maintaining one local account with a random, rotated, Vault-stored password that triggers alerts on use. The break-glass account is reviewed during every quarterly security audit: the password is verified to be in Vault, the audit log is checked for any unauthorized usage, and the account's role is verified to be platform_admin (the minimum role needed for emergency platform management).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's authentication principle: the identity provider (Google Workspace) manages user identity and group membership. Airflow consumes the identity and maps it to roles. When a team member leaves the company, their Google account is deactivated — which immediately prevents Airflow login because OAuth authentication requires a valid Google session. The team does not need to remember to deactivate the user in Airflow separately. This delegation of identity management to the corporate IdP eliminates an entire class of 'forgotten account deactivation' security risks.

The Airflow REST API provides programmatic access to the same operations available in the web UI: triggering DAGs, reading DAG status, clearing task instances, and managing connections. SaulėGrid's CI/CD pipeline uses the API to trigger smoke tests after staging deployment. The operations team's monitoring scripts use the API to check DAG health and data freshness. An unsecured API is a critical vulnerability: anyone with network access to the Airflow web server can trigger arbitrary DAGs, read connection credentials, or modify pipeline configurations through API calls — bypassing the RBAC controls that protect the web UI.
API Authentication Methods

Airflow's REST API supports three authentication methods. Basic authentication sends the username and password in every request header — simple but insecure because credentials are transmitted with every call (even over HTTPS, the credentials are present in server logs and proxy caches). Session authentication uses a cookie obtained from a login endpoint — more secure but complex for automated scripts. Token-based authentication uses a long-lived or short-lived token that represents a service account's identity — the recommended approach for automated access because it does not expose the service account's password and can be revoked independently.

import requests

# API authentication with a service account token
# The token is stored in Vault and retrieved by the CI/CD pipeline

# Method 1: Basic authentication (simple but less secure)
response = requests.get(
    'https://airflow.saulegrid.internal/api/v1/dags',
    auth=('ci_deploy', '<service-account-password>'),
)

# Method 2: Token-based authentication (recommended)
response = requests.get(
    'https://airflow.saulegrid.internal/api/v1/dags',
    headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer <service-account-token>'},
)

# Method 3: Session-based authentication
session = requests.Session()
session.post(
    'https://airflow.saulegrid.internal/api/v1/login',
    json={'username': 'ci_deploy', 'password': '<password>'},
)
response = session.get('https://airflow.saulegrid.internal/api/v1/dags')

SaulėGrid uses token-based authentication for all API access. The CI/CD pipeline's service account (ci_deploy) has a token stored in Vault that is injected as an environment variable during pipeline execution. The operations team's monitoring scripts use a separate token for the ops_monitor service account, also stored in Vault. Each service account has its own token with its own permissions and its own revocation capability. If the CI token is compromised, it can be revoked without affecting the operations monitoring — the blast radius is limited to the compromised account.
Service Account Permissions

Service accounts follow the same RBAC model as human users — they are assigned roles with specific permissions. SaulėGrid's ci_service role grants minimal API permissions: trigger DAG Runs for the smoke test DAG (can_create on DAG Run for a specific DAG), read DAG status (can_read on DAG), and read task instance status (can_read on Task Instance). The role explicitly denies access to connections, variables, and configuration — the CI pipeline should never read credentials or modify the platform configuration through the API. The ops_monitor service account has a broader read scope (all DAGs and task instances) but no write permissions — it monitors but does not modify.
API Rate Limiting

API rate limiting prevents a misconfigured script or a compromised token from overwhelming the Airflow API with requests. SaulėGrid configures rate limiting at the reverse proxy level (Nginx): each API client is limited to 60 requests per minute, with a burst capacity of 10 requests. The CI pipeline's API calls are well within this limit (3-5 calls per deployment), and the monitoring scripts call the API once per minute. The rate limit protects against two scenarios: a script bug that makes API calls in a tight loop (which would consume API processing capacity and potentially slow the web server for human users), and a compromised token being used for reconnaissance (enumerating all DAGs, connections, and variables through rapid sequential API calls).
API Access Logging

Every API call is logged with the client identity, the endpoint accessed, the HTTP method, and the response status. SaulėGrid's reverse proxy logs API access separately from web UI access, enabling targeted analysis of programmatic access patterns. The API access log is forwarded to the ELK stack alongside the Vault audit logs, providing a unified view of who accessed what through which channel (UI or API) and when. The security audit reviews the API access log for anomalies: calls from unexpected IP addresses, requests to endpoints that the service account's role should not allow (which indicate a permission misconfiguration), and unusual request volumes that suggest a compromised token.
API Endpoint Restrictions

Endpoint-level restrictions provide defense-in-depth beyond RBAC. Even if a service account's RBAC role is misconfigured to grant broader permissions than intended, the Nginx endpoint restrictions prevent the account from accessing endpoints outside its allowed set. The reverse proxy acts as a coarse-grained firewall for API access: RBAC provides fine-grained control within the allowed endpoints. SaulėGrid maintains the endpoint restriction rules in the Nginx configuration file, which is version-controlled alongside the Helm values and reviewed through the same PR process. A change to the API endpoint restrictions requires security review because it directly affects which automated systems can perform which operations.

The layered API security model means that adding a new automated consumer is a three-step process: create a service account with a dedicated role in RBAC (specifying which resources and actions are permitted), generate an authentication token stored in Vault (providing the credential the consumer uses to authenticate), and configure the reverse proxy endpoint restrictions (specifying which API endpoints the consumer can access). All three steps are documented in the API consumer onboarding checklist and require security review. Removing a consumer follows the reverse process: revoke the token, remove the endpoint restriction, and delete the service account — ensuring no residual access remains.
Consumer Lifecycle Management

Not all API endpoints should be accessible to all service accounts. SaulėGrid's reverse proxy configuration restricts API endpoint access by client identity: the CI service account can only access /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns (to trigger smoke tests) and /api/v1/dags (to check DAG status). The operations monitoring service account can access /api/v1/dags and /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns (read only) but cannot access /api/v1/connections or /api/v1/variables. The endpoint restrictions are implemented as Nginx location blocks with IP-based and header-based access rules, providing a network-level enforcement layer in addition to the RBAC-level enforcement within Airflow.

blocks.title: API security anti-pattern
blocks.content: The most dangerous API security anti-pattern is using a human user's credentials for automated API access. When a developer's personal credentials are embedded in a CI script, the script has all of that developer's permissions (typically platform_engineer or Admin). If the developer leaves the company and their account is deactivated, the CI pipeline breaks. If the script is compromised, the attacker has the developer's full permission set. Service accounts with dedicated tokens and minimal permissions eliminate both risks — the token is not tied to a human's employment status, and its permissions are limited to exactly what the automation needs.

SaulėGrid's security model has three layers: authentication verifies identity, RBAC controls permissions, and audit logging records every security-relevant action. Without audit logging, the team can define who should be able to do what, but they cannot verify who actually did what. When the dag_bronze_meters pause incident occurred on March 8th, the team could see that the DAG was paused in the UI but could not determine who paused it, when, or from which IP address. The investigation required interviewing team members to reconstruct the timeline. Audit logging eliminates this detective work by recording every action with the actor's identity, the timestamp, and the action details.
What Airflow Logs

Airflow's audit logging records security-relevant actions in the metadata database and optionally in the web server's access log. The logged events include: DAG pauses and unpauses, DAG Run triggers (manual and automatic), task instance clears, connection creates, reads, and updates, variable creates, reads, and updates, user role changes, and login events. Each event record includes the user's username, the action performed, the resource affected, and the timestamp. The events are stored in the log table in the metadata database and are viewable in the Airflow UI under the Audit Logs page (available to Admin users).

The security monitoring task queries the audit log for sensitive actions performed in the last 24 hours and flags any that were performed by non-engineer users. A connection edit by an operations user would be flagged because operations users should not have connection edit permissions — the alert indicates either a permission misconfiguration (the operations_user role has an unintended permission) or a social engineering attack (someone obtained another user's credentials). The monitoring task runs daily as part of the maintenance DAG and sends alerts to the #saulegrid-security Slack channel.
What the Audit Log Captures

rows: login success/failure, role changes, permission changes
Daily automated check + immediate alert on failure
All REST API calls with client identity and endpoint

blocks.content: Forwarding Audit Logs to Central Logging

Storing audit logs only in the Airflow metadata database has two limitations: the logs are only accessible through the Airflow UI or database queries (not through the team's central logging platform), and the logs can be modified by anyone with database access (undermining their integrity for compliance purposes). SaulėGrid forwards Airflow audit logs to their ELK stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) by configuring the Airflow logging system to write audit events to both the metadata database and a structured log file that Filebeat ships to Elasticsearch. The central logging platform provides long-term retention (12 months versus the metadata database's 90-day retention), cross-platform search (correlating Airflow actions with Vault access and application logs), and tamper resistance (logs in Elasticsearch are append-only).

The ELK-based audit trail enables historical analysis that the Airflow UI's audit log page cannot provide. SaulėGrid's Kibana dashboards show security trends over time: login frequency by user (detecting dormant accounts that should be deactivated), action frequency by role (detecting roles that are more active than expected, which may indicate overly broad permissions), and failed login attempts over time (detecting brute-force attempts or credential issues). The trend analysis is reviewed during the quarterly security audit and has identified two patterns that led to security improvements: a service account that was making 10x more API calls than expected (caused by a polling loop bug in the monitoring script, not a security issue, but worth investigating) and a user account that had not logged in for 60 days (the employee had left the company but the Google account was not yet deactivated — the finding accelerated the offboarding process).
Compliance Reporting

SaulėGrid's quarterly security report includes an audit log section that demonstrates compliance with the team's security policy. The report answers five questions: were all user accounts assigned to the correct roles (verified by comparing the user-role assignment against the security policy), were any sensitive actions performed by users outside their authorized role (verified by the daily security monitoring task), were all connection credential accesses logged and attributable to a specific user or service account (verified by correlating Airflow audit logs with Vault audit logs), were any login attempts from unrecognized IP addresses or at unusual times (verified by analyzing the web server access log), and were all role or permission changes approved through the documented change process (verified by correlating role change events with the Git history of the roles.yaml file).
Log Integrity

Audit logs must be tamper-resistant to be useful for compliance. If a malicious actor can modify the audit log to remove evidence of their actions, the log provides false assurance. SaulėGrid addresses log integrity through two mechanisms. First, logs in the metadata database are supplementary — the authoritative audit trail is in the ELK stack, which is managed by a separate team (the infrastructure team) with separate credentials. A compromise of the Airflow metadata database credentials does not grant access to the ELK cluster. Second, the ELK cluster is configured with append-only indices — existing log entries cannot be modified or deleted, only new entries can be added. This append-only property ensures that the audit trail is a complete, unmodified record of every action.

SaulėGrid verifies log integrity during the quarterly security audit by comparing the event count in the Airflow metadata database against the event count in ELK for the same time period. A discrepancy indicates either a forwarding failure (events were not shipped from Airflow to ELK) or a tampering attempt (events were deleted from one system but not the other). The integrity check has never detected tampering — but it has detected two forwarding failures where the Filebeat agent crashed silently, missing approximately 6 hours of events that were present in the metadata database but not in ELK. Both failures were resolved by restarting Filebeat and backfilling the missing events from the metadata database.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Audit logging is not about catching bad actors — it is about demonstrating that the security model works. The quarterly compliance report proves that RBAC is correctly configured (no unauthorized actions), that authentication is effective (no unrecognized access), and that credential management is secure (all access is logged and attributable). The value of audit logging is not the alerts it generates (which are rare in a well-configured system) but the confidence it provides that the security model is functioning as designed. Without audit logging, the security model is an assertion; with audit logging, it is a verified property.

RBAC and authentication protect the Airflow platform at the application level — they control who can log in and what they can do once logged in. Network security protects the platform at the infrastructure level — it controls which machines can communicate with the Airflow components and how that communication is encrypted. A perfectly configured RBAC system is ineffective if the metadata database is accessible from the public internet, if the Redis broker accepts connections from any IP address, or if the web UI serves traffic over unencrypted HTTP. Network security is the foundation that application security builds upon.
The Network Architecture

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's network security zones
blocks.caption: Three network zones with decreasing accessibility: the reverse proxy faces the internet, the application components are in a private subnet, and the data stores are in an isolated subnet accessible only from the application subnet.

visual-recommendation.description: Network architecture diagram showing three security zones (DMZ, application subnet, data subnet) with firewall rules, TLS termination at the reverse proxy, and the allowed communication paths between components.

blocks.content: HTTPS and TLS Configuration

All external access to the Airflow web UI and API is encrypted with TLS 1.3. The TLS certificate is managed by the reverse proxy (Nginx), not by the Airflow web server itself — the web server runs on HTTP internally while the reverse proxy handles encryption for external traffic. This pattern simplifies certificate management (one certificate on the proxy versus certificates on every Airflow component) and centralizes the TLS configuration in a single location. SaulėGrid uses Let's Encrypt certificates with automatic renewal, ensuring that the certificate never expires and the TLS configuration always uses current best practices.

The Nginx configuration implements three security controls in a single file: TLS encryption (all traffic is encrypted with TLS 1.3), rate limiting (API requests are capped at 60 per minute per client IP with a burst of 10), and IP restriction (API access is limited to the corporate VPN and internal service networks). The web UI location block proxies requests to the Airflow web server without rate limiting because human interaction is naturally rate-limited. The API location block applies stricter controls because automated scripts can generate requests at rates that overwhelm the web server.
Firewall Rules

SaulėGrid's network security is enforced through AWS security groups that implement a deny-by-default policy: all traffic is blocked unless an explicit rule allows it. The reverse proxy's security group allows inbound HTTPS (port 443) from the corporate VPN's IP range — not from the public internet. The application subnet's security group allows inbound HTTP (port 8080) only from the reverse proxy's security group. The data subnet's security group allows inbound PostgreSQL (port 5432) and Redis (port 6379) only from the application subnet's security group. This layered approach means that even if an attacker gains access to the DMZ, they cannot directly reach the metadata database or the Redis broker.
Securing the Metadata Database

The metadata database contains connection credentials (unless migrated to a Secrets Backend), user account information, DAG run history, and XCom values. SaulėGrid secures the database with four measures. First, network isolation: the database is in a private subnet accessible only from the application subnet. Second, authentication: the database requires a password that is stored in Vault and rotated quarterly. Third, encryption at rest: AWS RDS encrypts the database storage using AES-256. Fourth, encrypted connections: the database requires SSL for all client connections, preventing credential interception on the internal network. These four measures address the database's four attack surfaces: network access, authentication, storage, and transmission.

SaulėGrid's firewall rules are implemented as AWS security groups attached to each component's network interface. The security group rules are defined in Terraform configuration files that are version-controlled alongside the Helm values and DAG code. A security group change — adding a new IP range, opening a new port, or modifying an access rule — goes through the same PR review process as a DAG change. The Terraform state file records the current security group configuration, and the quarterly network scan verifies that the running configuration matches the Terraform state. This infrastructure-as-code approach prevents the configuration drift that occurs when security group rules are modified through the AWS console without updating the Terraform configuration.
Securing the Redis Broker

The Redis broker carries task dispatch messages between the scheduler and workers. A compromised broker could inject malicious task messages or intercept task results. SaulėGrid secures Redis with three measures: network isolation (Redis is in the data subnet, accessible only from the application subnet), password authentication (the Redis AUTH password is stored in Vault), and TLS encryption for client connections (preventing message interception on the internal network). Redis's maxmemory configuration is set with an eviction policy that prevents memory exhaustion attacks — a compromised client that sends large payloads cannot fill Redis's memory and cause a denial-of-service.

SaulėGrid verifies network security quarterly with an automated scan that tests each security assumption: can the Airflow web UI be reached from outside the VPN (should not be possible), can the metadata database be reached from the application subnet (should be possible), can the metadata database be reached from the internet (should not be possible), and are all connections to the database encrypted with SSL (should be enforced). The scan is implemented as a CI job that runs against the staging infrastructure, verifying that the security group rules and TLS configurations match the documented security policy. The scan has caught one drift: a staging security group that was temporarily broadened during debugging and not reverted.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's network security principle: every communication channel between Airflow components is encrypted, authenticated, and restricted to the minimum necessary network scope. External access goes through the reverse proxy with TLS. Internal access uses private subnets with security group rules. Database and broker access requires both network-level and application-level authentication. The principle is verified quarterly by a network scan that checks for any unexpected open ports or unencrypted connections — the scan is automated as a CI job that runs against the staging environment's infrastructure.

The previous lessons taught the security mechanisms — RBAC, DAG-level access control, authentication, API security, audit logging, and network security. This lesson presents the mistakes: the security configurations that appear reasonable but create vulnerabilities. Each anti-pattern is drawn from common Airflow deployment practices that SaulėGrid either encountered directly or identified during security audits of their own platform. Understanding these anti-patterns is defensive knowledge — recognizing them during code review, infrastructure setup, or security audit prevents the vulnerabilities before they are exploited.

Security anti-patterns are particularly dangerous because they often appear in tutorials, quick-start guides, and Stack Overflow answers. A developer following a tutorial that uses the default admin account for all users is implementing an anti-pattern that the tutorial author accepted for simplicity but that has no place in a production deployment. SaulėGrid's security review process explicitly checks new code and configuration against the anti-pattern list: the code reviewer verifies that no anti-pattern has been introduced, even if the code 'works' and the developer's intent is benign. The anti-patterns in this lesson are the team's 'never do this' checklist — the inverse of the design patterns from Module 3.
Anti-Pattern 1: Admin Role for Everyone

The most common security anti-pattern in Airflow deployments is assigning the Admin role to all users. This happens when a new user reports that they cannot access a page or perform an action, and the quickest fix is to give them Admin instead of investigating which specific permission they need. Within a few months, every user is Admin and the RBAC system provides no protection. SaulėGrid prevents this with a documented policy: Admin is assigned to exactly one person (the team lead), and any access request is resolved by creating or modifying a custom role — never by escalating to Admin.
Anti-Pattern 2: Credentials in DAG Code

Hardcoding credentials in DAG files — database passwords, API keys, or tokens — is a security vulnerability that persists in the Git history even after the credentials are removed. A developer who commits a password, realizes the mistake, and removes it in the next commit has not actually removed the password — it exists in the Git history forever (unless the history is rewritten, which is a destructive operation). SaulėGrid's CI pipeline includes a secret scanning step that checks every commit for patterns matching credentials (API keys, passwords, tokens) and blocks the merge if any are found. All credentials are stored in Vault and accessed through Connections (Module 5).
Anti-Pattern 3: Default Admin Password

Airflow's setup process creates a default admin user with a password specified during initialization. If this password is weak, default, or shared across environments, it provides an easy entry point for unauthorized access. SaulėGrid mitigates this by using OAuth authentication (lesson 5), which means the default admin password is irrelevant for web UI login — users authenticate through Google. However, the default admin account still exists in the metadata database for CLI access and emergency recovery. SaulėGrid sets this password to a cryptographically random string stored in Vault, rotates it quarterly, and monitors its usage in the audit log — any login using the default admin credentials triggers an immediate security alert.
Anti-Pattern 4: Exposed API Without Authentication

Airflow's REST API is enabled by default and, in some deployment configurations, is accessible without authentication if the auth_backend configuration is not explicitly set. An unauthenticated API endpoint allows anyone with network access to trigger DAGs, read connection credentials, and modify the platform configuration. SaulėGrid's deployment configuration explicitly sets the API authentication backend to reject unauthenticated requests, and the network security rules restrict API access to the corporate VPN and the CI/CD service account's IP address. The quarterly network scan verifies that the API endpoint is not accessible from outside the allowed IP range.
Anti-Pattern 5: Neglected Audit Log Review

The neglect anti-pattern is the most insidious because it does not create an immediate vulnerability — it creates a delayed one. The audit logs accumulate data that would reveal a security issue, but nobody reviews them. The issue persists for months until an incident forces an investigation, at which point the audit logs reveal that the vulnerability was detectable from the beginning. SaulėGrid prevents neglect through two mechanisms: the automated daily monitoring task that checks for specific patterns (sensitive actions by unauthorized users, failed login attempts, unusual API access), and the quarterly manual review that examines a broader set of events with human judgment. The automated check catches known patterns; the manual review catches novel patterns that the automated check was not designed to detect.

Configuring audit logging without regularly reviewing the logs provides a false sense of security. The logs accumulate events, but nobody checks them — so a permission misconfiguration that allows unauthorized actions goes undetected for months. SaulėGrid prevents this with two mechanisms: the daily automated security monitoring task (lesson 7) checks for sensitive actions by non-authorized users, and the quarterly manual security audit reviews a sample of audit log entries against the security policy. The automated check catches immediate policy violations; the manual review catches subtle patterns that the automated check is not configured to detect.
Anti-Pattern Summary

rows: Any user can modify anything — RBAC provides no protection
Admin limited to one person; custom roles for all others
Passwords in Git history — accessible to anyone with repo access
Secret scanning in CI; all credentials in Vault
Weak or shared password provides easy unauthorized access
OAuth for web UI; random CLI password in Vault; usage monitoring
Anyone with network access can control the platform via API
API auth required; network restricted to VPN and CI IPs
Security violations go undetected for months
Daily automated check + quarterly manual review
All users see all DAGs — including sensitive maintenance workflows
access_control on every DAG; CI check enforces

blocks.title: The compound risk
blocks.content: Security anti-patterns compound: an Airflow deployment with Admin for everyone, credentials in DAG code, no API authentication, and no audit log review has no effective security — any user can access any credential, perform any action, and do so without any accountability. Fixing one anti-pattern while leaving the others creates the illusion of security without the reality. SaulėGrid's security framework addresses all six anti-patterns simultaneously because partial security is often worse than no security — it creates confidence without justification.

SaulėGrid's security policy is a version-controlled document stored at config/SECURITY_POLICY.md that defines every aspect of the security framework: the custom role definitions with their complete permission sets, the DAG access matrix mapping roles to DAGs and permission levels, the authentication configuration including OAuth provider settings, the API service accounts with their tokens and endpoint restrictions, the audit log review schedule and escalation procedures, and the network security architecture including firewall rules and TLS configuration. The document serves as both the implementation specification and the audit baseline — every security control in the running platform traces back to a section in this document.
The Security Stack

rows: Infrastructure access — who can reach the platform
Identity — who the user claims to be
Permissions — what actions the user can perform
Visibility — which DAGs the user can see and act on
Token authentication, rate limiting, IP restriction
Programmatic access — which automated systems can call the API
Platform team via reverse proxy config
Audit logging + ELK forwarding + daily monitoring
Accountability — who did what and when
Platform team via logging config + monitoring DAG
Credentials — where passwords and tokens are stored

blocks.content: How the Layers Interact

The seven security layers work as a defense-in-depth system: each layer catches threats that the previous layers miss. Network security prevents unauthorized machines from reaching the platform. Authentication prevents unauthorized users from logging in. RBAC prevents authenticated users from exceeding their permissions. DAG-level access prevents users from seeing DAGs outside their scope. API security prevents automated systems from exceeding their authorized operations. Audit logging detects any action that slipped through the previous layers. Secrets management ensures that even if a layer is compromised, credentials are not exposed in the metadata database or Git history.

The defense-in-depth model means that the failure of any single layer does not compromise the platform. If the OAuth provider is briefly unavailable (authentication layer failure), the network security layer still prevents unauthorized machines from reaching the platform. If a role is misconfigured with excessive permissions (RBAC layer failure), the DAG-level access control still limits visibility to the appropriate DAGs. If the audit log forwarding fails (audit layer failure), the metadata database still retains the events for manual inspection. SaulėGrid designs each layer to be independently effective — the layers complement each other but do not depend on each other. This independence is verified during the quarterly security audit, which tests each layer's effectiveness in isolation.

blocks.title: Defense-in-depth security model
blocks.content:
External request
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Network Security │ ← Can this machine reach the platform?
│ (Security Groups)│   Only VPN IPs allowed
└────────┬────────┘
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Authentication   │ ← Is this user who they claim to be?
│ (OAuth / Google) │   Valid Google account required
└────────┬────────┘
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ RBAC             │ ← Can this user perform this action?
│ (Custom Roles)   │   Role-specific permissions enforced
└────────┬────────┘
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ DAG Access Ctrl  │ ← Can this user see this specific DAG?
│ (access_control) │   Per-DAG visibility enforced
└────────┬────────┘
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Audit Logging    │ ← Record: who did what, when
│ (ELK + Monitoring)│  Every action is logged
└─────────────────┘
blocks.caption: Each layer adds a security check. A request must pass all layers to succeed. The audit layer records every request regardless of whether it was allowed or denied.

blocks.content: The Security Policy Document

SaulėGrid's security policy is a version-controlled document (config/SECURITY_POLICY.md) that defines every aspect of the security framework: the role definitions and their permissions, the DAG access matrix, the authentication configuration, the API service accounts and their permissions, the audit log review schedule, and the network security architecture. The document serves three purposes: it is the reference for implementing security controls (the roles.yaml and security.py files are derived from the policy), it is the baseline for the quarterly security audit (the auditor compares the running configuration against the policy), and it is the onboarding resource for new team members (the policy explains what access they will have and why).
Quarterly Security Audit

SaulėGrid performs a quarterly security audit that verifies every layer of the security framework. The audit checks: are all user-role assignments correct (compare airflow users list against the policy), do custom roles have exactly the documented permissions (compare airflow roles list-permissions against the policy), does every DAG have the correct access_control parameter (CI check verification), are all API service accounts using least-privilege permissions (review API access logs), are all audit log alerts investigated and resolved (review the alert history), and are all network security rules current (network scan against the infrastructure). The audit takes approximately 4 hours and produces a report that the team lead signs and archives for compliance records.

The quarterly audit has caught four security issues in two quarters: a role that retained a connection-edit permission after a policy change (the roles.yaml was updated but the deployment script had not been run), a DAG that was deployed without an access_control parameter (the CI check was not yet implemented for that DAG), an API service account whose token had not been rotated within the quarterly window (the rotation alert was missed during a busy sprint), and a security group rule that allowed inbound traffic from an IP range broader than the current VPN CIDR (the VPN IP range had been narrowed but the security group was not updated). Each finding was resolved within the audit sprint and led to a preventive measure (automated check or process improvement) that prevents recurrence.
Maintenance and Evolution

The security framework requires ongoing maintenance on three cadences. Weekly: the team lead reviews any temporary access grants and reverts those that have expired. Monthly: the automated security monitoring task results are reviewed for trends — a gradual increase in flagged events may indicate a misconfiguration or a policy that needs updating. Quarterly: the full security audit is performed, covering role permissions, DAG access control, API tokens, audit log integrity, and network security rules. The maintenance schedule is documented in the operations runbook alongside the credential rotation schedule from Module 5 and the test suite maintenance from Module 6.

The security framework evolves with the platform. When SaulėGrid onboards a new team (the forecasting team is planned for Q3), the evolution includes: defining a new custom role with appropriate permissions, adding the team's Google Workspace group to the OAuth mapping, updating the DAG access matrix to include the new team's visibility requirements, creating a service account if the team needs API access, and updating the security policy document to reflect all changes. The onboarding security checklist is a PR template that ensures every security aspect is addressed before the new team receives access. The checklist is reviewed by the team lead and approved as part of the onboarding PR — providing an auditable record of what access was granted and why.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
