blocks.content: Multi-team security transforms Airflow from a single-team tool where everyone trusts everyone into a governed platform where access is controlled by roles, visibility is controlled by DAG-level permissions, identity is verified by the corporate identity provider, programmatic access is secured by tokens and rate limits, every action is audited, and the entire framework is verified quarterly — ensuring that the March 8th DAG-pause incident cannot happen again because the analytics team's role structurally prevents the action.

chapters.title: Performance and Scheduler Tuning
chapters.description: Master Airflow performance diagnostics and tuning: scheduler throughput optimization, DAG parsing performance, metadata database maintenance, configuration tuning for mid-level workloads, and the monitoring patterns that detect performance degradation before it becomes an outage.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's scheduler started falling behind during the overnight processing window. Tasks that should have been dispatched within seconds of becoming eligible were waiting 45 seconds or more in the 'scheduled' state before moving to 'queued.' The on-call engineer's first instinct was to increase the parallelism setting — more concurrent tasks should mean faster throughput. The increase had no effect because the bottleneck was not concurrency — it was metadata database query latency. The scheduler was spending 80% of each loop iteration waiting for database responses, and increasing parallelism only added more tasks competing for the same slow database. The fix was a database connection pool increase, not a parallelism increase.
Measure Before You Change

The parallelism misdiagnosis illustrates the most common performance tuning mistake: changing a setting without first measuring the actual bottleneck. Airflow's performance is determined by the interaction of five components — the scheduler loop, the DAG parser, the metadata database, the executor, and the workers — and a bottleneck in any component affects the overall throughput. Increasing parallelism when the database is the bottleneck is like adding more lanes to a highway when the bottleneck is a single-lane bridge: the additional lanes are unused because traffic backs up at the bridge. The diagnostic mindset requires identifying the bridge before adding lanes.

rows: Scheduler loop is slow due to DB query latency
Increase DB connection pool; optimize slow queries
DAG Runs start late (past expected time)
DAG parsing takes too long — scheduler can't keep up
Reduce top-level code in DAG files; use .airflowignore
Pool slots exhausted — tasks wait for pool, not workers
Increase pool slot count or review pool assignments
All tasks slow on specific days
Database maintenance overdue — XCom table bloat
Run airflow db clean; implement weekly maintenance DAG

blocks.content: The Measure-Diagnose-Fix-Verify Cycle

SaulėGrid's performance tuning follows a four-step cycle. Measure: collect the relevant metric (scheduler loop duration, DAG parse time, task queue time, database query latency) using Airflow's built-in logging or StatsD metrics. Diagnose: compare the measured value against the expected baseline and identify which component is responsible for the deviation. Fix: apply the targeted change to the identified component — a configuration adjustment, a code optimization, or an infrastructure change. Verify: re-measure the same metric after the fix and confirm that it improved to the expected level without degrading other metrics.

The verification step is critical because performance fixes can have side effects. Increasing the database connection pool to fix scheduler query latency may exhaust the database's maximum connection limit, causing worker tasks to fail with 'too many connections' errors. Reducing DAG parse time by deferring imports may increase task startup time because the imports happen at execution rather than parse time. Every fix must be verified against the target metric and monitored for side effects on related metrics. SaulėGrid's performance tuning is always a controlled experiment: one change at a time, measured before and after, with rollback if the side effects outweigh the improvement.
SaulėGrid's Performance Baseline

Before optimizing anything, SaulėGrid established a performance baseline: the measured values of all key metrics under normal operating conditions. The baseline provides the reference point for detecting degradation: a scheduling delay of 25 seconds is concerning only if the baseline is 10 seconds — the same value would be healthy if the baseline were 30 seconds. SaulėGrid's baseline was established over a two-week measurement period during which no configuration changes were made and no unusual workloads ran. The baseline values are documented in the performance standards (lesson 10) and displayed as reference lines on the Grafana dashboard.

The baseline also reveals the platform's capacity headroom: the difference between the current metric value and the threshold. If the scheduler loop takes 1.0 seconds and the threshold is 5.0 seconds, the headroom is 4.0 seconds — enough for the DAG count to increase by approximately 4x before the scheduler reaches its limit. SaulėGrid uses headroom calculations for capacity planning: when headroom drops below 50%, the team begins evaluating scaling options (more parsing processes, faster database, additional workers) before the threshold is breached.
Performance vs Correctness

Performance tuning must never compromise correctness. Increasing parallelism to speed up task throughput is safe. Reducing retry counts to speed up failure resolution is dangerous — the task may need those retries to handle transient errors. Shortening the zombie detection threshold to speed up failure detection may cause false positives — marking healthy but slow tasks as zombies. SaulėGrid's performance tuning rule: every change must be evaluated for both its performance benefit and its correctness risk. A change that improves throughput by 10% but introduces a 1% chance of incorrect task state is rejected. Performance is valuable only when the pipeline's correctness guarantees from Module 3 remain intact.
What This Module Covers

This module teaches the diagnostic approach to Airflow performance tuning. Scheduler internals (lesson 2) explains how the scheduler loop works so that symptoms can be traced to root causes. DAG parsing performance (lesson 3) covers the most common bottleneck — slow DAG file parsing that delays the entire scheduling cycle. Reducing scheduler load (lesson 4) covers configuration and design patterns that minimize parsing overhead. Metadata database performance (lessons 5-6) covers the second most common bottleneck — database query latency and table bloat. Concurrency tuning (lesson 7) covers the configuration properties that control task execution throughput. Task execution performance (lesson 8) covers optimizing individual task overhead. Performance monitoring (lesson 9) covers the StatsD and Grafana integration that makes bottlenecks visible. Lesson 10 synthesizes into SaulėGrid's performance standards.

blocks.title: Performance bottleneck diagnostic flow
blocks.content:
Tasks slow?
    │
    ├── Tasks stuck in 'scheduled'?
    │       │
    │       ├── Scheduler loop duration high? ──▶ DB latency or parsing
    │       │       ├── Parse time > 3s? ──▶ Lesson 3: DAG parsing
    │       │       └── DB query > 100ms? ──▶ Lesson 5: DB performance
    │       │
    │       └── Scheduler loop normal? ──▶ Executor dispatch issue (Module 4)
    │
    ├── Tasks stuck in 'queued'?
    │       ├── Pool slots exhausted? ──▶ Lesson 7: Pool/concurrency tuning
    │       └── Workers at capacity? ──▶ Module 4: Worker scaling
    │
    └── Tasks running but slow?
            ├── Task startup overhead? ──▶ Lesson 8: Task execution
            └── Task logic slow? ──▶ Optimize task code (not an Airflow issue)
blocks.caption: The diagnostic flow traces symptoms to root causes. Each branch leads to a specific lesson in this module or a reference to the executor module for runtime architecture issues.

visual-recommendation.description: Decision tree diagram showing the performance diagnostic flow from symptoms (tasks stuck in scheduled, queued, or running slowly) through diagnostic checks to root causes and the corresponding lesson references.

blocks.title: The diagnostic principle
blocks.content: Performance tuning without measurement is guesswork. The scheduler loop duration, DAG parse time, database query latency, and task queue time are the four metrics that diagnose 90% of Airflow performance issues. Every lesson in this module begins with a measurement, explains what the measurement reveals, and presents the fix that targets the specific bottleneck the measurement identified. SaulėGrid never changes a performance-related configuration property without first measuring the metric it affects and verifying the improvement after the change.

The scheduler is the engine that drives Airflow: it parses DAG files, creates DAG Runs, evaluates task dependencies, dispatches ready tasks to the executor, detects zombie tasks, and processes callbacks. Understanding how the scheduler works internally is the prerequisite for diagnosing performance issues because every scheduler performance problem maps to a specific phase of the scheduler loop. A slow parse phase manifests as delayed DAG Run creation. A slow dependency evaluation phase manifests as tasks stuck in 'none' state longer than expected. A slow dispatch phase manifests as tasks stuck in 'scheduled' state. Knowing which phase is slow tells you where to look for the root cause.
The Scheduler Loop

blocks.title: The scheduler loop phases
blocks.content:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SCHEDULER LOOP                         │
│                                                          │
│  1. Parse DAG files                                      │
│     ├── Scan dags/ directory for .py files               │
│     ├── Import each file, execute top-level code         │
│     └── Collect DAG objects into the DagBag              │
│     Duration: 0.3-3.0 seconds (depends on file count)    │
│                                                          │
│  2. Create DAG Runs                                      │
│     ├── Check which DAGs need new runs (schedule/Dataset)│
│     └── Insert DAG Run records into metadata database    │
│     Duration: 10-100 milliseconds                        │
│                                                          │
│  3. Evaluate task dependencies                           │
│     ├── For each DAG Run, check task upstream status     │
│     └── Move eligible tasks from 'none' to 'scheduled'  │
│     Duration: 10-200 milliseconds (depends on task count)│
│                                                          │
│  4. Dispatch to executor                                 │
│     ├── Send 'scheduled' tasks to the executor           │
│     └── Executor moves tasks to 'queued'                 │
│     Duration: 5-50 milliseconds                          │
│                                                          │
│  5. Heartbeat and cleanup                                │
│     ├── Detect zombie tasks (no heartbeat)               │
│     ├── Process callbacks (on_success, on_failure)       │
│     └── Update scheduler heartbeat timestamp             │
│     Duration: 5-20 milliseconds                          │
│                                                          │
│  Total loop: 0.4-3.5 seconds per iteration               │
│  Loop interval: scheduler_heartbeat_sec (default: 5)     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The scheduler loop runs every scheduler_heartbeat_sec seconds. Each phase has a typical duration range. The parse phase dominates — it is 80-90% of the total loop duration for most deployments.

visual-recommendation.description: Scheduler loop diagram showing the five phases with timing annotations, highlighting the parse phase as the dominant cost and the database interactions in each phase.

blocks.content: The Parse Phase Dominates

In SaulėGrid's deployment, the parse phase takes 0.8 seconds and the remaining four phases take 0.2 seconds combined. The parse phase is 80% of the total loop duration — and this ratio is typical for Airflow deployments with 10-50 DAG files. The parse phase is expensive because it imports every DAG file as a Python module: the Python interpreter reads the file, compiles it to bytecode, executes the top-level code, and collects the DAG objects. Any code that runs at the top level of the DAG file — imports, Variable.get() calls, database queries, API calls — executes on every parse cycle. This is why DAG parsing optimization (lesson 3) is the highest-impact performance improvement for most deployments.
Key Scheduler Metrics

rows: Total time for one scheduler loop iteration
Time to parse all DAG files
Parse time per individual DAG file
Number of loop iterations per minute
Time to evaluate task dependencies for one DAG Run
Number of tasks currently in 'running' state
Tasks eligible to run but waiting for executor slots

blocks.content: SaulėGrid monitors these metrics through StatsD (lesson 9) and displays them on the Grafana performance dashboard. The key diagnostic metric is scheduler_heartbeat_duration: if it exceeds the scheduler_heartbeat_sec interval (5 seconds), the scheduler cannot keep up — it spends more time processing each loop than the interval between loops, creating a backlog. SaulėGrid's current loop duration of 1.0 seconds provides a 4-second buffer — the scheduler uses 20% of its time budget, leaving 80% for handling increased DAG count or data volume without degradation.

The scheduler loop count metric (scheduler.scheduler_loop_count) reveals how many complete loop iterations the scheduler performs per minute. With a 5-second heartbeat and a 1-second loop duration, the scheduler completes 12 loops per minute — one loop every 5 seconds. If the loop count drops below 10 per minute, the scheduler is spending more than 6 seconds per loop, indicating that either the parse phase or the database query phase has slowed. The loop count is the simplest health indicator: a steady count means the scheduler is keeping pace, and a declining count means the scheduler is falling behind.
The Scheduling Delay Metric

Scheduling delay is the time between when a task becomes eligible to run (dependencies satisfied) and when it begins executing on a worker. This metric captures the entire scheduling pipeline: dependency evaluation, dispatch to executor, queue wait, and worker pickup. SaulėGrid targets a scheduling delay under 30 seconds for Bronze tasks (which have SLA implications) and under 60 seconds for monitoring tasks (which have lower urgency). A scheduling delay above the target indicates a bottleneck somewhere in the pipeline — the diagnostic flow from lesson 1 traces the delay to the responsible component.
Scheduler Resource Allocation

The scheduler needs dedicated CPU and memory resources that are not shared with workers or the web server. SaulėGrid allocates 2 CPU cores and 4GB of memory to the scheduler container — enough for 12 DAG files with the current parse profile. The CPU allocation determines how many parsing processes can run in parallel (parsing_processes=2 matches the 2 cores). The memory allocation must cover the Python interpreters for all parsing processes plus the scheduler's own memory for the DagBag, the task instance cache, and the executor's dispatch queue. Insufficient memory causes the scheduler process to be OOM-killed — a hard failure that stops all scheduling until the process restarts.

SaulėGrid monitors the scheduler's resource usage alongside the functional metrics to detect infrastructure constraints before they affect scheduling throughput. The scheduler CPU utilization should stay below 70% during normal operation, leaving 30% headroom for burst activity such as backfill DAG Run creation and large dependency evaluation cycles. The memory usage should stay below 80% of the container's allocation, leaving a buffer for temporary spikes during the DAG parsing phase when multiple Python processes load DAG files simultaneously. If either resource approaches its limit during normal operation, the scheduler container's resource allocation should be increased before the limit causes a performance degradation or an out-of-memory process kill.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid runs the scheduling delay measurement as a daily task in the monitoring DAG. The results are stored as XCom values and trended over time on the performance dashboard. A gradual increase in scheduling delay — from 10 seconds to 15 to 20 — is an early warning that the scheduler is approaching its capacity limit. The trend is more valuable than any single measurement because it reveals whether the platform is stable, improving, or degrading over time. SaulėGrid reviews the scheduling delay trend weekly and investigates any increase that persists for more than 3 consecutive days.

DAG parsing is the most common performance bottleneck in Airflow because it runs on every scheduler heartbeat — every 5 seconds by default. When a DAG file takes 0.5 seconds to parse, the scheduler spends 10% of its time budget parsing that single file. With 12 files at 0.5 seconds each, the parse phase consumes 6 seconds — more than the 5-second heartbeat interval. The scheduler cannot keep up: it finishes one parse cycle and immediately starts the next, with no time for dependency evaluation or task dispatch. The result is tasks stuck in 'scheduled' state because the scheduler never reaches the dispatch phase.
Why DAG Parsing Is Expensive

Every DAG file is a Python module that the scheduler imports on each parse cycle. When the scheduler imports a file, Python executes all top-level code: import statements, function definitions, class definitions, variable assignments, and any other statements at the module level. Import statements are particularly expensive because they trigger the import of the referenced module and all of its dependencies. Importing pandas at the top level of a DAG file loads pandas (0.3-0.5 seconds), numpy (0.2-0.3 seconds), and dozens of transitive dependencies. This import happens every 5 seconds — approximately 17,000 times per day — even though pandas is only needed when the task actually executes.
Measuring Parse Time

# Measure DAG parsing time from the Airflow logs
# The scheduler logs each file's parse duration
grep 'dag_processing' /opt/airflow/logs/scheduler/latest/*.log | grep 'import_duration'

# Example output:
# dag_bronze_meters.py: import_duration=0.052s
# dag_bronze_generation.py: import_duration=0.048s
# dag_silver_meters.py: import_duration=0.065s
# dag_gold_billing.py: import_duration=0.071s
# dag_battery_soc_monitor.py: import_duration=0.812s  ← SLOW!

# The battery DAG takes 812ms — 10x longer than the others
# Investigation: the file imports pandas at the top level

# Measure total parse time for all files
python3 -c "
import time
from airflow.models import DagBag
start = time.time()
bag = DagBag(dag_folder='dags/', include_examples=False)
print(f'Total parse time: {time.time() - start:.2f}s')
print(f'DAGs loaded: {len(bag.dags)}')
print(f'Import errors: {len(bag.import_errors)}')
"

The measurement reveals that dag_battery_soc_monitor.py takes 812ms to parse — 10x longer than the other DAG files. The file imports pandas at the top level because the battery state-of-charge analysis uses a DataFrame. The fix is to defer the pandas import to inside the task function, where it executes only at task runtime (once per DAG Run) rather than at parse time (once per heartbeat). This single change reduced the total parse time from 1.2 seconds to 0.4 seconds — a 67% improvement from moving one import statement inside a function definition.
Identifying Slow DAG Files

The scheduler logs the parse duration for each DAG file, but finding the slow files in a sea of log output requires targeted analysis. SaulėGrid uses a simple script that parses the scheduler log, extracts the import_duration value for each file, and sorts by duration — revealing the outliers immediately. The script runs as part of the quarterly performance standards audit and produces a ranked list of DAG files by parse time. Any file that exceeds the 250ms budget is flagged for investigation. The most common causes are heavy top-level imports (pandas, numpy, sqlalchemy), top-level Variable.get() calls, and accidental top-level computation (a DAG that builds a lookup dictionary from a file read at import time).
The Top-Level Code Anti-Pattern

The fast version defers both the pandas import and the Variable.get() call to inside the task function. At parse time, the file defines the DAG and the task — lightweight operations that take milliseconds. At task runtime (when the scheduler dispatches the task to a worker), the function body executes: pandas is imported, the Variable is read, and the analysis runs. The performance cost of the import and Variable query is the same — but it happens once per DAG Run instead of once per heartbeat. For a daily DAG, this is 1 execution versus 17,280 executions per day.
Common Heavy Imports

rows: Automatically deferred when pandas is deferred
Used in 2 DAGs for warehouse writes
Use Airflow hooks instead of direct SQLAlchemy
Used in 1 DAG for API calls
Use Airflow's HttpHook instead, or defer import
Acceptable — lightweight package with no heavy dependencies

blocks.content: The DAG Parsing Budget

SaulėGrid's parsing budget is 3 seconds for all 12 DAG files combined — an average of 250ms per file. Files that exceed 250ms are investigated and optimized. The budget is derived from the scheduler_heartbeat_sec setting (5 seconds): the parse phase should consume at most 60% of the heartbeat interval, leaving 40% for the remaining scheduler phases (DAG Run creation, dependency evaluation, dispatch, cleanup). With 3 seconds of parsing and 2 seconds for everything else, the scheduler completes each loop well within the 5-second budget. If the DAG count doubles to 24 files, the budget per file drops to 125ms — which is achievable only if no file has heavy top-level imports.

blocks.title: The hidden cost of top-level code
blocks.content: Top-level code in DAG files has three hidden costs beyond import time. First, Variable.get() calls at the top level trigger metadata database queries on every parse — 12 queries per heartbeat across 12 DAG files. Second, any exception raised by top-level code produces a DAG import error that prevents the DAG from being scheduled — a transient database timeout during Variable.get() temporarily removes the DAG from the scheduler. Third, top-level code that writes to files, sends HTTP requests, or performs any side effect executes on every heartbeat — potentially sending thousands of unintended API calls per day. SaulėGrid's lint rule (Module 6) flags all top-level code that is not a function or class definition.

DAG parsing optimization (lesson 3) reduces the cost per file. Scheduler load reduction reduces the number of files the scheduler must process and the frequency of processing. SaulėGrid's DAGs directory contained 12 DAG files and 33 non-DAG files: __init__.py, utility modules imported by DAGs, conftest.py from a misplaced test, __pycache__ directories, and helper scripts. The scheduler scanned all 45 files on every heartbeat, spending time on 33 files that would never produce DAG objects. Adding a .airflowignore file that excluded non-DAG files reduced the scheduler's file scan from 45 files to 12 files — a 73% reduction in files parsed.
The .airflowignore File

# dags/.airflowignore
# Patterns to exclude from the scheduler's DAG file scan
# Follows the same syntax as .gitignore

__pycache__
*.pyc
__init__.py
conftest.py
test_*
_*.py          # Convention: files starting with _ are helper modules
README.md
.git

The .airflowignore file tells the scheduler which files and directories to skip during the DAG directory scan. The file uses the same pattern syntax as .gitignore: glob patterns match filenames, directory names exclude entire directories, and lines starting with # are comments. SaulėGrid's convention is that DAG files are named dag_*.py and helper modules are named _*.py (with a leading underscore). The .airflowignore excludes the underscore-prefixed files, __pycache__ directories, test files, and other non-DAG artifacts that accumulate in the DAGs directory during development.
Scheduler Configuration Properties

rows: Interval between scheduler loop iterations — lower = faster scheduling, higher CPU usage
Minimum seconds between re-parsing the same file — prevents excessive re-parsing of unchanged files
How often the scheduler rescans the DAGs directory for new or removed files (seconds)
Number of processes the scheduler uses for parallel DAG parsing
Maximum DAG Runs created per scheduler loop — limits the burst of new runs
Maximum concurrent DAG Runs per DAG — prevents backfill from overwhelming the executor

blocks.content: The min_file_process_interval property is the key to reducing redundant parsing. With the default value of 30 seconds, the scheduler waits at least 30 seconds before re-parsing a file it has already parsed — even though the heartbeat runs every 5 seconds. This means each file is parsed at most twice per minute instead of 12 times per minute. The setting trades freshness (DAG changes take up to 30 seconds to be recognized) for reduced parse overhead. SaulėGrid keeps the default 30-second interval because their deployment pipeline deploys changes to the DAGs directory only during the 10:00-14:00 window, so the 30-second recognition delay is inconsequential.
Parallel Parsing with parsing_processes

The parsing_processes configuration controls how many processes the scheduler spawns to parse DAG files in parallel. With parsing_processes=2 and 12 DAG files, the scheduler divides the files across 2 processes, each parsing 6 files. This halves the wall-clock parse time compared to a single process. SaulėGrid uses 2 parsing processes — matching the number of CPU cores available to the scheduler container. Setting parsing_processes higher than the available CPU cores causes contention: the processes compete for CPU time and the parse duration increases rather than decreases. The optimal setting is equal to the number of CPU cores dedicated to the scheduler.
Reducing DAG Complexity

The scheduler's dependency evaluation phase scales with the number of tasks and dependencies in each DAG. A DAG with 50 tasks and 200 dependencies takes longer to evaluate than a DAG with 10 tasks and 20 dependencies — the scheduler must check each task's upstream status against the metadata database. SaulėGrid's design convention limits DAGs to 20 tasks maximum: if a pipeline requires more than 20 tasks, it is split into multiple DAGs connected by Datasets (Module 1). This convention bounds the per-DAG evaluation cost and keeps the total dependency check phase under 200 milliseconds for all 12 DAGs.

# Monitoring the scheduler's file scan count
# Run periodically to detect non-DAG files accumulating in the DAGs directory

import os
from pathlib import Path

def audit_dags_directory(dags_dir: str = 'dags/') -> dict:
    """Count files in the DAGs directory that the scheduler will scan."""
    all_files = list(Path(dags_dir).rglob('*.py'))
    dag_files = [f for f in all_files if f.name.startswith('dag_')]
    non_dag_files = [f for f in all_files if not f.name.startswith('dag_')]
    
    ignored = []
    ignore_path = Path(dags_dir) / '.airflowignore'
    if ignore_path.exists():
        patterns = ignore_path.read_text().strip().splitlines()
        # Simplified pattern matching for audit
        ignored = [f for f in non_dag_files 
                   if any(p in str(f) for p in patterns if not p.startswith('#'))]
    
    scanned = len(all_files) - len(ignored)
    return {
        'total_py_files': len(all_files),
        'dag_files': len(dag_files),
        'non_dag_files': len(non_dag_files),
        'ignored_by_airflowignore': len(ignored),
        'files_scanned_by_scheduler': scanned,
    }

DAG serialization interacts with parsing performance: when serialization is enabled, the scheduler parses DAG files and writes the serialized structure to the database. The web server reads the serialized structure from the database instead of parsing files. The parse overhead remains on the scheduler (lesson 3's optimizations still apply), but the web server's resource usage decreases because it no longer imports DAG files. For SaulėGrid's deployment, enabling serialization reduced the web server's memory usage from 2GB to 800MB and eliminated the occasional import errors that the web server experienced when DAG files were being deployed (the scheduler handles deployment-time import errors gracefully, but the web server's error handling was less robust).
The .airflowignore Maintenance Trap

The .airflowignore file must be maintained as the DAGs directory evolves. A new helper module added without updating .airflowignore will be scanned by the scheduler — adding unnecessary parse overhead. SaulėGrid's CI pipeline includes a check that counts the files in the DAGs directory that are scanned by the scheduler (not excluded by .airflowignore) and compares the count against the expected DAG file count. If the scanned count exceeds the expected count, the CI check fails with a message listing the unexpected files — prompting the developer to either add the file to .airflowignore or update the expected count if the file is a new DAG.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's scheduler optimization checklist (reviewed quarterly): verify .airflowignore is up to date (no new non-DAG files bypassing it), verify parsing_processes matches the scheduler's CPU allocation, verify no DAG file exceeds the 250ms parse time budget, verify min_file_process_interval is appropriate for the deployment cadence, and verify that the total scheduler loop duration stays under 60% of the heartbeat interval. The checklist takes 15 minutes to review and has caught two performance regressions — a new helper module that was not excluded by .airflowignore and a DAG that added a top-level API call during a refactoring.

The metadata database is Airflow's coordination layer: every component reads from and writes to it. The scheduler queries it to check task states and create DAG Runs. Workers update it to record task progress and results. The web server reads it to render the UI. XCom values are stored in it. Every connection resolution that falls through the Secrets Backend hits it. A slow metadata database slows every component simultaneously — the scheduler waits for query responses, workers wait for state updates, and the UI becomes unresponsive. SaulėGrid's metadata database is the single most important infrastructure component for Airflow performance.
The XCom Table Bloat Problem

SaulėGrid's metadata database grew from 2GB to 40GB over 6 months. The growth was almost entirely in the xcom table: every task that returned a value via XCom stored that value as a serialized blob in the database. SaulėGrid's quality reporting tasks returned detailed validation results — 500KB per task execution. With 5 quality tasks running daily for 180 days, the xcom table accumulated 450MB of quality results alone. Combined with other XCom-using tasks, the table reached 35GB. Query performance degraded: task instance lookups that took 5ms at 2GB took 200ms at 40GB because the database's query planner was scanning the bloated xcom table during joins.
Identifying the Bloated Table

The first diagnostic step when database performance degrades is identifying which table is responsible. The pg_statio_user_tables system view shows the size of each table and its indexes. A healthy Airflow metadata database has the task_instance table as the largest (typically 40-60% of total size), followed by dag_run and log tables. If the xcom table is the largest table, XCom bloat is the primary issue. If the log table is the largest, audit log retention is too long. If no single table is disproportionately large but total size is high, the retention period for all tables may need shortening. SaulėGrid's weekly maintenance DAG records the top 5 table sizes and trends them on the performance dashboard.
Database Connection Pool Sizing

Every Airflow component that communicates with the metadata database uses a connection pool — a set of pre-established database connections that are reused across queries. The pool size determines how many concurrent database operations the component can perform. If the pool is too small, queries queue behind each other and the component stalls. If the pool is too large, the database exhausts its maximum connection limit and rejects new connections. SaulėGrid's connection pool configuration balances these limits: the scheduler gets 10 connections (it makes the most queries), each worker gets 5 connections, and the web server gets 5 connections. The total is 10 + (3 workers × 5) + 5 = 30 connections — well within PostgreSQL's default limit of 100.

# airflow.cfg — database connection pool configuration
[database]
sql_alchemy_pool_size = 10        # Connections per Airflow component
sql_alchemy_max_overflow = 5      # Additional connections allowed during burst
sql_alchemy_pool_recycle = 1800   # Recycle connections after 30 minutes
sql_alchemy_pool_pre_ping = True  # Verify connection health before use

# Monitor database connections from PostgreSQL
# Run on the database server to check connection usage
psql -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE datname='airflow';"
Query Performance Diagnostics

When the metadata database is slow, the diagnostic approach is to identify the slow queries — not to blindly increase resources. SaulėGrid enables PostgreSQL's slow query log (log_min_duration_statement = 100) to capture any query taking longer than 100ms. The most common slow queries in Airflow are: task instance lookups that join the dag_run and task_instance tables (slow when tables are large), XCom reads that scan the xcom table for a specific dag_id and task_id (slow when the table is bloated), and audit log inserts that contend with reads on the log table. Each slow query type has a specific fix: table maintenance for bloated tables, index verification for missing indexes, and connection pool tuning for contention.

-- Diagnostic queries for the Airflow metadata database
-- Run on the PostgreSQL server during performance investigation

-- Check table sizes (identify bloat)
SELECT
    relname AS table_name,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS table_size,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid) - pg_relation_size(relid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_catalog.pg_statio_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Expected output for a healthy database:
-- table_name     | total_size | table_size | index_size
-- task_instance   | 500 MB     | 300 MB     | 200 MB
-- dag_run         | 100 MB     | 60 MB      | 40 MB
-- xcom            | 50 MB      | 40 MB      | 10 MB   ← Should be small!
-- log             | 200 MB     | 150 MB     | 50 MB

-- Check for missing indexes (common cause of slow queries)
SELECT
    schemaname, tablename, indexname
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename IN ('task_instance', 'dag_run', 'xcom', 'log')
ORDER BY tablename, indexname;
Connection Pool Tuning

The connection pool is the interface between Airflow components and the database. Each pool has a fixed number of pre-established connections (sql_alchemy_pool_size) and a burst capacity (sql_alchemy_max_overflow) for temporary spikes. SaulėGrid's pool configuration allocates 10+5 connections to the scheduler (the heaviest database user), 5+3 to each worker, and 5+3 to the web server. The total maximum connections (10+5 + 3×(5+3) + 5+3 = 47) stays well within PostgreSQL's default limit of 100. If the pool is consistently exhausted (visible in the database's pg_stat_activity view), either the pool size should be increased or the component's database query pattern should be optimized to reduce connection hold time.

The pool_pre_ping setting verifies each connection's health before using it — preventing the 'connection reset' errors that occur when a connection has been idle long enough for the database or a network device to close it. The pool_recycle setting forces connections to be recreated after 30 minutes, preventing stale connection state from accumulating. Both settings add a small overhead per query (the pre_ping health check takes 1-2ms) but prevent the intermittent connection failures that are difficult to diagnose because they occur randomly and resolve on retry.
Database Connection Pool Configuration

The table size query reveals the database's storage distribution. A healthy Airflow database has the task_instance and dag_run tables as the largest, with the xcom table small relative to the others. If the xcom table is the largest table, XCom bloat is the primary issue — the fix is to migrate large XCom values to an external backend (S3, covered in Module 2) and clean the existing xcom table. If the log table is disproportionately large, audit log retention is too long — the fix is to archive old log entries. SaulėGrid runs these diagnostic queries weekly as part of the maintenance DAG and trends the table sizes on the performance dashboard.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: The metadata database is not a data warehouse — it should stay small and fast. Airflow stores operational state (task instances, DAG runs, XCom values) in the database, not analytical data. Any pattern that causes the database to grow continuously (large XCom values, verbose audit logging, no record cleanup) will eventually degrade performance. SaulėGrid's database size target is under 5GB — enough for 90 days of operational history for 12 DAGs. The weekly maintenance DAG (lesson 6) enforces this target by removing records older than the retention period.

The metadata database accumulates records over time: a DAG Run record for every execution of every DAG, a task instance record for every task in every DAG Run, an XCom record for every task that returns a value, and a log record for every audit event. Without cleanup, these tables grow indefinitely. SaulėGrid's database grew from 2GB to 40GB in 6 months before the team implemented automated maintenance. The growth was predictable: 12 DAGs × 10 tasks × 365 days × overhead = approximately 7GB per year of task instance records alone, plus XCom, logs, and other tables. Automated maintenance keeps the database within its size target by removing records older than the retention period.
The airflow db clean Command

# Clean records older than 90 days from the metadata database
# Run as a scheduled maintenance task

# Dry run — show what would be deleted without deleting
airflow db clean --clean-before-timestamp '2025-12-24T00:00:00' --dry-run

# Actual cleanup — removes old DAG Runs, task instances, and logs
airflow db clean \
  --clean-before-timestamp '2025-12-24T00:00:00' \
  --tables dag_run,task_instance,log,xcom,task_fail,rendered_task_instance_fields \
  --yes

# Verify the size reduction
psql -c "SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_database_size('airflow'));"

The airflow db clean command removes records older than the specified timestamp from the specified tables. The --dry-run flag shows the record count that would be deleted without performing the deletion — always run a dry run first to verify the scope. SaulėGrid's retention policy is 90 days for all operational tables: records older than 90 days are not needed for day-to-day operations and can be archived or deleted. The 90-day window covers the quarterly regulatory audit period during which VERT may request data lineage documentation that references task execution history.
Retention Period Trade-offs

The 90-day retention period balances three concerns: operational utility (how far back the team needs to look when investigating incidents), compliance requirements (how long audit trails must be preserved for regulatory purposes), and database performance (how much data the database can hold without degrading query speed). Shorter retention (30 days) reduces database size but limits incident investigation to recent history. Longer retention (180 days) provides more historical context but increases database size and slows queries on large tables. SaulėGrid's 90-day retention covers the quarterly regulatory audit window while keeping the database under the 5GB performance target.

For compliance-critical data that must be retained beyond 90 days, SaulėGrid archives records to S3 before deletion. The archival task runs immediately before the cleanup task in the maintenance DAG: it exports records older than 90 days to a Parquet file in the archival S3 bucket, then the cleanup task deletes those records from the database. The archived data is queryable through Athena if needed for regulatory requests, but it does not consume metadata database capacity or affect query performance.
The Weekly Maintenance DAG

from airflow.decorators import dag, task
from pendulum import datetime, now
from datetime import timedelta
import subprocess

@dag(
    dag_id='dag_maintenance',
    schedule='0 8 * * 0',  # Every Sunday at 08:00
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['maintenance', 'platform', 'saulegrid'],
)
def dag_maintenance():

    @task
    def clean_old_records() -> dict:
        """Remove records older than 90 days from the metadata database."""
        cutoff = (now() - timedelta(days=90)).format('YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss')
        
        result = subprocess.run([
            'airflow', 'db', 'clean',
            '--clean-before-timestamp', cutoff,
            '--tables', 'dag_run,task_instance,log,xcom,task_fail,'
                        'rendered_task_instance_fields',
            '--yes',
        ], capture_output=True, text=True)
        
        return {
            'cutoff_date': cutoff,
            'stdout': result.stdout[-500:],  # Last 500 chars
            'return_code': result.returncode,
        }

    @task
    def measure_database_size() -> dict:
        """Report the current metadata database size."""
        import psycopg2
        from airflow.hooks.base import BaseHook
        
        conn_info = BaseHook.get_connection('airflow_db')
        conn = psycopg2.connect(
            host=conn_info.host, port=conn_info.port,
            dbname=conn_info.schema, user=conn_info.login,
            password=conn_info.password,
        )
        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute("SELECT pg_database_size('airflow')")
        size_bytes = cursor.fetchone()[0]
        conn.close()
        
        size_gb = size_bytes / (1024 ** 3)
        return {
            'database_size_gb': round(size_gb, 2),
            'target_max_gb': 5.0,
            'status': 'healthy' if size_gb < 5.0 else 'warning',
        }

    clean_old_records() >> measure_database_size()

dag_maintenance()

The maintenance DAG runs every Sunday at 08:00 — during SaulėGrid's quiet period when no pipeline processing is active. The DAG has two tasks: clean_old_records removes records older than 90 days, and measure_database_size reports the database size after cleanup. The size measurement is stored as XCom and trended on the performance dashboard. If the database size exceeds the 5GB target after cleanup, the monitoring task fires a warning alert indicating that either the retention period needs shortening or a table-specific investigation is needed (typically XCom bloat from large return values).
PostgreSQL VACUUM and ANALYZE

Deleting records from PostgreSQL does not immediately reclaim disk space — the deleted rows are marked as dead tuples but remain on disk until the VACUUM process reclaims the space. SaulėGrid configures PostgreSQL's autovacuum to run aggressively on the metadata database: autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor=0.05 (vacuum when 5% of rows are dead) and autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor=0.02 (update statistics when 2% of rows change). These settings are more aggressive than PostgreSQL's defaults (20% and 10%) because the metadata database has high write throughput — every task execution creates and updates multiple rows. The aggressive autovacuum settings ensure that dead tuples are reclaimed promptly and the query planner's statistics stay current.
Monitoring Maintenance Effectiveness

The maintenance DAG's measure_database_size task reports the database size after cleanup. SaulėGrid trends this metric weekly: a healthy trend shows the size staying flat or gradually decreasing as the retention window moves forward. An upward trend despite weekly cleanup indicates that the inflow rate exceeds the cleanup rate — either the platform's DAG count has grown (producing more records per day) or a specific table is growing faster than expected (typically XCom). The trend visualization on the performance dashboard provides early warning: if the size trend crosses the 4GB mark (80% of the 5GB target), the team investigates the growth source before the target is breached.
XCom Cleanup Strategy

The db clean command removes all XCom records older than the retention period, but SaulėGrid's real XCom size problem was large values — quality report results stored as 500KB JSON blobs. The long-term fix (implemented in Module 2) was migrating to an S3-backed XCom backend that stores values in S3 and keeps only a reference URL in the metadata database. The migration reduced the xcom table from 35GB to 200MB. For teams that have not yet migrated to an external XCom backend, the interim fix is to limit XCom value sizes: SaulėGrid's code review convention rejects any task that returns more than 10KB of XCom data, with a note to use the S3 backend or write the data to a file and return only the file path.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's database maintenance schedule: weekly cleanup via the maintenance DAG (removes records older than 90 days), continuous autovacuum via PostgreSQL configuration (reclaims dead tuples), monthly manual review of table sizes (identifies unexpected growth), and quarterly full VACUUM ANALYZE during the maintenance window (rebuilds statistics and reclaims all dead space). The combined maintenance keeps the database under 3GB — well within the 5GB target — and query latency under 50ms for the scheduler's most frequent queries (task instance lookups and DAG Run status checks).

Airflow provides three concurrency settings that control how many tasks execute simultaneously: parallelism (the global ceiling across all DAGs), max_active_tasks_per_dag (the per-DAG ceiling, formerly dag_concurrency), and max_active_runs_per_dag (the maximum concurrent DAG Runs for each DAG). These settings interact with the executor's capacity (worker count × concurrency per worker) and the pool slot counts from Module 4. A misconfiguration at any level creates a bottleneck that limits throughput regardless of other settings. SaulėGrid's initial deployment used Airflow's defaults, which were wrong for their workload — and the misfit caused two distinct performance issues.
The Three Concurrency Settings

rows: Maximum tasks running simultaneously across ALL DAGs
Maximum tasks running simultaneously within ONE DAG
Maximum concurrent DAG Runs per DAG (affects backfill)

blocks.content: The three settings form a hierarchy: parallelism is the global ceiling, max_active_tasks_per_dag limits each DAG's share of the ceiling, and max_active_runs_per_dag limits how many times a DAG can run concurrently. SaulėGrid set parallelism to 24 — matching their executor capacity (3 workers × 8 concurrent tasks per worker). Setting parallelism higher than executor capacity means the scheduler dispatches more tasks than workers can handle, causing tasks to accumulate in 'queued' state. Setting parallelism lower wastes executor capacity — workers sit idle while tasks wait for scheduler dispatch slots.
The Default Parallelism Problem

Airflow's default parallelism of 32 was too high for SaulėGrid's 3-worker cluster (24 executor slots). The scheduler dispatched up to 32 tasks, but only 24 could execute simultaneously. The remaining 8 tasks sat in 'queued' state waiting for worker slots. This was not a critical issue — queued tasks eventually executed when slots freed up. The real problem was the interaction with pools: the 32-parallelism setting allowed 32 tasks to be dispatched, but the sftp_pool had only 2 slots. If 5 SFTP tasks were dispatched simultaneously, 3 waited in the pool queue while occupying parallelism slots — preventing other non-SFTP tasks from being dispatched. Reducing parallelism to 24 (matching executor capacity) eliminated the slot waste.
The max_active_runs Problem

The default max_active_runs_per_dag of 16 caused a problem during backfills. When Jonas backfilled 30 days of the Silver meters DAG, the scheduler created 16 concurrent DAG Runs (the maximum). Each DAG Run had 5 tasks, producing 80 task instances competing for 24 parallelism slots. The backfill consumed all available executor capacity, preventing today's scheduled Bronze and Gold DAGs from executing. SaulėGrid reduced max_active_runs_per_dag to 2, which limits backfills to 2 concurrent DAG Runs — 10 task instances — leaving ample capacity for the regular scheduled workload.
Diagnosing Concurrency Issues

When tasks are stuck in 'queued' state, the diagnosis requires checking all four concurrency constraints in order. First, check parallelism: are there parallelism slots available? If the running task count equals the parallelism setting, the global ceiling is the constraint. Second, check max_active_tasks_per_dag: is the specific DAG at its per-DAG limit? Third, check executor capacity: are all worker slots occupied? Fourth, check pool slots: is the task's pool at capacity? SaulėGrid's diagnostic script queries the metadata database for the current values of all four constraints and reports which one is the binding limit — providing a definitive answer to 'why is this task queued?' in under 5 seconds.

# airflow.cfg — SaulėGrid's concurrency configuration
[core]
parallelism = 24                    # = 3 workers × 8 tasks/worker
max_active_tasks_per_dag = 4        # No single DAG monopolizes the cluster
max_active_runs_per_dag = 2         # Limits backfill concurrency

# The interaction:
# - 12 DAGs × 4 max tasks = 48 potential tasks
# - But only 24 can run simultaneously (parallelism cap)
# - So 2-3 DAGs can run their full complement while others wait
# - During backfill: 2 concurrent runs × 5 tasks = 10 backfill tasks
#   Leaves 14 slots for regular scheduled tasks

The configuration comments explain the interaction between settings. With parallelism=24 and 12 DAGs each limited to 4 concurrent tasks, the theoretical maximum is 48 tasks — but only 24 can actually run (the parallelism cap). This means at most 6 DAGs can run their full 4-task complement simultaneously, while other DAGs wait. During the overnight processing window when all 12 DAGs need to run, the scheduler dispatches tasks round-robin across DAGs, ensuring that every DAG makes progress rather than allowing one DAG to monopolize all 24 slots. The max_active_tasks_per_dag setting is the mechanism that enables this fair scheduling.
Pool and Concurrency Interaction

Pools (Module 4) add a fourth concurrency dimension that is independent of the three Airflow settings. The sftp_pool with 2 slots limits concurrent SFTP tasks to 2 regardless of parallelism, max_active_tasks_per_dag, or executor capacity. When diagnosing 'why are tasks queued?', the engineer must check all four constraints: is the task waiting for a parallelism slot (global ceiling reached), a dag_concurrency slot (DAG-level ceiling reached), an executor slot (all workers busy), or a pool slot (external resource limit reached)? The Airflow UI's task instance details page shows which constraint is active, but only if the engineer knows to check all four.

SaulėGrid's concurrency tuning principle is alignment: parallelism should equal executor capacity, max_active_tasks_per_dag should be set so that no single DAG can monopolize the cluster, max_active_runs_per_dag should limit backfills to a fraction of total capacity, and pool slots should match external system limits. When these four settings are aligned, tasks flow smoothly from 'scheduled' through 'queued' to 'running' without accumulating at any bottleneck. Misalignment at any level — parallelism exceeding executor capacity, or pool slots exceeding the external system's actual limit — creates queuing that degrades overall throughput.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: The most common concurrency tuning mistake is increasing parallelism to fix 'tasks are queued too long' without identifying the actual bottleneck. If tasks are queued because pool slots are exhausted, increasing parallelism has no effect — the pool is the constraint, not the global parallelism. If tasks are queued because workers are at capacity, increasing parallelism actually makes the problem worse — more tasks are dispatched into the queue, increasing queue depth without increasing throughput. The correct diagnostic sequence: check pool slot utilization first, then check executor slot utilization, then check parallelism utilization. Only increase the setting that is actually the binding constraint.

The previous lessons covered scheduler and database performance — the orchestration overhead that exists before a task's Python code runs. This lesson covers the performance of the tasks themselves: the startup overhead before the business logic begins, the patterns that keep tasks lightweight, and the anti-pattern of performing heavy computation inside Airflow tasks. Airflow is an orchestration platform, not a processing engine. Tasks should coordinate work — triggering Spark jobs, calling APIs, copying files — not perform the work itself. A task that spends 20 minutes processing data inside the Airflow worker is consuming a worker slot for 20 minutes when the task could have submitted the work to a dedicated processing cluster and completed in 30 seconds.
Task Startup Overhead

Every task execution has a fixed startup overhead: the worker imports the DAG file, locates the task, initializes the task instance, resolves connections and variables, and begins executing the task's Python code. For CeleryExecutor, the startup overhead includes the message broker dispatch (1-2 seconds) and the worker's DAG import (50-200ms per file). For KubernetesExecutor, the overhead includes pod creation (1-2 seconds), image pull (5-30 seconds if not cached), and container initialization (2-5 seconds). This startup overhead is fixed per task — it does not scale with the data volume. For a task that runs for 20 minutes, 5 seconds of startup is negligible. For a task that runs for 5 seconds, 5 seconds of startup doubles the execution time.
The Thin Task Pattern

# ANTI-PATTERN: Heavy computation inside the Airflow task
# The worker processes 1.2M rows directly — consuming worker resources for 20 minutes
@task
def process_meters_heavy():
    import pandas as pd
    df = pd.read_parquet('/data/bronze/meters/')  # 3GB file
    # 20 minutes of processing on the Airflow worker
    filtered = df[df['daily_kw'] > 0]
    aggregated = filtered.groupby('region').agg({'daily_kw': 'sum'})
    aggregated.to_parquet('/data/silver/meters/aggregated.parquet')

# CORRECT: Thin task that triggers an external processing engine
# The worker submits a Spark job and waits — the processing happens on the Spark cluster
@task
def process_meters_thin():
    from airflow.providers.apache.spark.operators.spark_submit import SparkSubmitOperator
    # Submit the job to Spark — takes 5 seconds
    # Spark cluster processes 1.2M rows — takes 3 minutes
    # Worker slot is occupied for 3 minutes, not 20
    submit_spark_job(
        application='/opt/spark/apps/meter_transform.py',
        arguments=['--date', '{{ ds }}'],
        spark_conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )

The thin task pattern delegates data processing to a dedicated engine (Spark, Databricks, BigQuery, dbt) and uses the Airflow task only for orchestration: submit the job, wait for completion, and report the result. The worker slot is occupied for the job submission time (seconds) plus the polling interval (seconds), not for the data processing time (minutes to hours). This pattern frees worker slots for other tasks and keeps the Airflow cluster sized for orchestration throughput rather than processing capacity. SaulėGrid's convention: if a task processes more than 10,000 rows or runs longer than 2 minutes, the processing should be delegated to an external engine.
External Engine Patterns

SaulėGrid uses three external engine patterns depending on the processing requirement. For large-scale data transformations (Silver meter processing, Gold aggregation), the task submits a Spark job to the Spark cluster and polls for completion. For SQL-based transformations (reporting queries, data quality checks), the task executes SQL against the PostgreSQL warehouse using the PostgreSQLOperator. For API-based operations (weather data retrieval, regulatory submissions), the task calls the API using the appropriate hook. In all three patterns, the Airflow worker performs only the submission and polling — the actual data processing happens on the external system. The worker slot is occupied for seconds to minutes, not for the processing duration.
Reducing Task Dependency Overhead

Tasks that import heavy Python packages (pandas, scipy, tensorflow) at execution time incur a per-task import cost of 0.5-3 seconds. For the thin task pattern, this import is unnecessary — the task only needs to submit a job and poll for completion. SaulėGrid's thin tasks import only the lightweight libraries they need: the Airflow hooks and providers for submitting jobs, the requests library for API calls, and the os/shutil libraries for file operations. Heavy analytical libraries are installed on the Spark cluster or the external processing engine, not on the Airflow workers. This separation keeps the Airflow worker's Python environment lean and task startup fast.
Batching Lightweight Tasks

The opposite problem from heavy tasks is too many lightweight tasks. If a DAG has 50 tasks that each run for 2 seconds, the total business logic time is 100 seconds but the total execution time is 100 seconds + (50 × 5 seconds startup overhead) = 350 seconds. The startup overhead is 250% of the business logic time. SaulėGrid addresses this by batching related lightweight operations into a single task: instead of 10 separate quality check tasks (one per region), a single quality check task iterates over all 10 regions internally. The single task has one startup overhead instead of ten, reducing the total execution time from 50 + 10 × 5 = 100 seconds to 50 + 5 = 55 seconds.
Task Duration Monitoring

SaulėGrid monitors task duration as a performance indicator. Each task has an expected duration range: the Bronze SFTP download takes 1-3 minutes (depending on file size), the Silver transformation takes 5-8 minutes (depending on data volume), and the Gold aggregation takes 2-4 minutes. A task that exceeds its expected range triggers a duration alert — not necessarily an error, but a signal that something changed (data volume increased, external system is slow, or the task code was modified). The execution_timeout parameter (Module 4, lesson 9) provides a hard ceiling: tasks that exceed the timeout are killed. The duration alert provides a soft warning before the hard ceiling is reached.
Measuring Task Overhead

SaulėGrid measures task overhead as the difference between the task's total duration (start to finish) and the business logic duration (the time spent on actual data processing). For a thin task that submits a Spark job, the total duration might be 3 minutes (submission + polling) while the business logic is 0 seconds on the worker — the processing happens on Spark. For a heavy task that processes data directly, the total duration equals the business logic duration because the worker is doing the processing. SaulėGrid's convention: if the business logic duration exceeds 2 minutes on the worker, the task should be refactored to use an external engine. The 2-minute threshold is calibrated to SaulėGrid's workload — a platform with more workers or less SLA pressure might use a higher threshold.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Airflow workers are orchestration resources, not compute resources. Every minute a worker slot is occupied by data processing is a minute that slot cannot be used for orchestration. SaulėGrid's thin task convention keeps worker slots available for rapid task dispatch — the scheduler can dispatch 24 tasks per heartbeat cycle because tasks complete quickly (submission + polling) rather than occupying slots for extended processing. The platform's throughput is measured by tasks dispatched per minute, not by data processed per task — and the thin task pattern maximizes dispatch throughput.

The previous lessons taught how to diagnose and fix specific performance issues. This lesson covers the monitoring infrastructure that detects performance degradation before it becomes an incident: the StatsD metrics that Airflow exports, the Prometheus or Grafana integration that visualizes them, and the alerting thresholds that notify the team when metrics deviate from their healthy ranges. Performance monitoring transforms the diagnostic approach from reactive (investigate after a user complains) to proactive (detect the degradation trend before it affects SLA compliance).
StatsD Metrics Export

# airflow.cfg — StatsD metrics configuration
[metrics]
statsd_on = True
statsd_host = statsd.saulegrid.internal
statsd_port = 8125
statsd_prefix = airflow

# Key metrics exported by Airflow to StatsD:
# airflow.scheduler_heartbeat               — Scheduler loop count
# airflow.dag_processing.total_parse_time   — Total DAG parsing duration
# airflow.dag_processing.import_duration.*  — Per-file parse duration
# airflow.dagrun.dependency-check.*         — Dependency evaluation duration
# airflow.executor.queued_tasks             — Tasks waiting for workers
# airflow.executor.running_tasks            — Tasks currently executing
# airflow.pool.open_slots.*                 — Available pool slots
# airflow.pool.used_slots.*                 — Occupied pool slots
# airflow.ti.finish.*.*                     — Task instance completion (by state)

Airflow exports over 50 metrics to StatsD when statsd_on is enabled. The metrics cover every phase of the scheduler loop, every executor state, every pool's utilization, and every task instance's lifecycle. StatsD collects these metrics and forwards them to a time-series database (Prometheus, Graphite, or InfluxDB) for storage and visualization. SaulėGrid uses the StatsD → Prometheus → Grafana pipeline: StatsD receives the metrics from Airflow, the Prometheus exporter scrapes StatsD, and Grafana dashboards visualize the time-series data with alert thresholds.
Setting Up the Metrics Pipeline

# docker-compose.yml excerpt — metrics pipeline components
services:
  statsd-exporter:
    image: prom/statsd-exporter:v0.26.0
    ports:
      - '9102:9102'   # Prometheus scrape endpoint
      - '8125:8125/udp'  # StatsD receive endpoint
    command: --statsd.mapping-config=/etc/statsd/mapping.yml

  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus:v2.48.0
    ports:
      - '9090:9090'
    volumes:
      - ./prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana:10.2.0
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    volumes:
      - ./grafana/dashboards:/etc/grafana/provisioning/dashboards
      - ./grafana/datasources:/etc/grafana/provisioning/datasources
The Performance Dashboard

The performance dashboard has 8 panels covering the five performance domains: scheduler health (loop duration, parse time), scheduling efficiency (scheduling delay, queue depth), database health (query latency, database size), task health (success rate), and resource utilization (pool slots). Each panel has a healthy range (green), a warning threshold (yellow), and a critical threshold (red). The thresholds are calibrated from SaulėGrid's operational data: the healthy ranges represent the platform's normal operating envelope, and the warning thresholds are set at the point where the metric begins affecting SLA compliance.
Alert Configuration

SaulėGrid configures alerts in Grafana that fire when metrics breach their thresholds. Warning alerts are sent to the #saulegrid-platform Slack channel for the team to investigate during business hours. Critical alerts are sent to PagerDuty and page the on-call engineer for immediate response. The alert hierarchy matches the impact hierarchy: a scheduler loop duration above 5 seconds means the scheduler cannot keep up (tasks will be delayed), which is a critical issue. A database size above 4GB means the database is approaching the cleanup threshold, which is a warning that requires attention within the week but not immediately. The alert configuration is version-controlled alongside the Grafana dashboard JSON and reviewed quarterly.

SaulėGrid's alert rules follow the same tiered severity model used throughout the course. Informational alerts (scheduling delay increasing but still within healthy range) are logged to the dashboard but do not notify anyone — they are visible during the weekly performance review. Warning alerts (metrics approaching thresholds) are sent to the Slack channel for the team to investigate during business hours. Critical alerts (metrics breaching thresholds that affect SLA compliance) are sent to PagerDuty and page the on-call engineer for immediate response. The tiering prevents alert fatigue: the on-call engineer is paged only for issues that require immediate action, while trends and gradual changes are addressed during scheduled review sessions.
Alert Severity Tiering

SaulėGrid's alert configuration is stored as Grafana provisioning files in the Git repository — the same repository that contains the DAG code, test suite, and infrastructure configuration. Changes to alert thresholds go through the standard PR review process: a developer who wants to relax a warning threshold (from 3 seconds to 4 seconds for scheduler loop duration) must justify the change in the PR description and get approval from the team lead. This prevents alert threshold drift — the gradual relaxation of thresholds to silence alerts rather than fixing the underlying performance issue.
Trend Analysis

Individual metric values are useful for diagnosing current issues. Metric trends over time are useful for predicting future issues. SaulėGrid's Grafana dashboard shows 30-day trends for all performance metrics. A scheduling delay that increases from 10 seconds to 20 seconds over a month indicates that the scheduler is approaching its capacity limit — even if 20 seconds is still within the healthy range. The trend predicts that the delay will breach the 30-second warning threshold within 2-3 months if the growth rate continues. Trend analysis enables proactive capacity planning: the team can add scheduler capacity (more parsing processes, faster database) before the metric breaches the threshold, rather than reacting to the breach.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's performance review cadence: daily automated alerts for threshold breaches (handled by on-call), weekly trend review in the platform operations meeting (10 minutes reviewing the performance dashboard for gradual changes), monthly capacity planning review (comparing current utilization against growth projections), and quarterly performance standards audit (verifying that all metrics are within their documented healthy ranges and adjusting thresholds if the platform's operating profile has changed). The four-cadence approach catches immediate issues (daily), gradual degradation (weekly), capacity limits (monthly), and standard drift (quarterly).

This module has covered seven performance topics: the diagnostic mindset for identifying bottlenecks before applying fixes, scheduler internals for understanding where performance problems originate, DAG parsing optimization for the most common bottleneck, scheduler load reduction through configuration and file management, metadata database performance and maintenance, concurrency tuning for task execution throughput, task execution patterns for keeping workers efficient, and performance monitoring for detecting degradation proactively. This lesson synthesizes every topic into SaulėGrid's performance standards — the documented targets that the team monitors, the maintenance practices that maintain them, and the diagnostic procedures that restore performance when it degrades.
The Performance Standards

rows: All 8 dashboard panels with alert thresholds

blocks.content: The Performance Maintenance Schedule

Performance maintenance runs on four cadences. Daily: the StatsD metrics and Grafana alerts detect threshold breaches automatically and page the on-call engineer for critical issues. Weekly: the maintenance DAG cleans the metadata database, and the operations meeting reviews the performance dashboard for gradual trends. Monthly: the team reviews database growth rate, scheduler capacity utilization, and executor queue depth trends to assess whether the platform is approaching its capacity limit. Quarterly: the performance standards audit verifies that all metrics are within their documented targets, that the monitoring thresholds are appropriate, and that the maintenance DAG is running successfully.
The Performance Review Process

SaulėGrid's weekly platform operations meeting includes a 10-minute performance review. The team reviews the performance dashboard, noting any metrics that have changed since the previous week. A metric that moved toward the threshold (even if still within the healthy range) is flagged for investigation. A metric that improved (moved away from the threshold) is noted as evidence that a recent optimization was effective. The review takes only 10 minutes because the dashboard provides all the information — the team does not need to run diagnostic queries during the meeting. Action items from the review (investigate a trend, plan a capacity increase, schedule a maintenance task) are tracked in the sprint backlog.
The Diagnostic Runbook

# SaulėGrid's performance diagnostic runbook
# Follow this sequence when investigating a performance degradation

1. CHECK SCHEDULER HEALTH
   - scheduler_heartbeat_duration > 5s? → Parser or DB bottleneck
   - Parse time > 3s? → Check for heavy imports, run .airflowignore audit
   - Parse time normal? → Check database query latency

2. CHECK DATABASE HEALTH
   - Database size > 5GB? → Run airflow db clean, check XCom table
   - Query latency > 100ms? → Check connection pool, run VACUUM ANALYZE
   - Database healthy? → Check executor

3. CHECK EXECUTOR HEALTH
   - Queue depth > 10? → Check worker count, pool utilization
   - Workers idle + tasks queued? → Pool slots or parallelism misconfiguration
   - Workers at capacity? → Scale workers or optimize task duration

4. CHECK TASK PERFORMANCE
   - Task duration above expected range? → Check data volume, external system
   - Task startup slow? → Check heavy imports at execution time
   - Many short tasks queuing? → Consider batching

The diagnostic runbook is the on-call engineer's primary reference during a performance investigation. The four-step sequence (scheduler → database → executor → tasks) mirrors the performance bottleneck hierarchy from lesson 1. Each step has a specific metric to check, a specific threshold that indicates a problem, and a specific action to take if the threshold is breached. The runbook is designed for 2 AM clarity: the engineer should be able to follow the steps mechanically, without needing to remember the diagnostic theory from this module. The runbook was tested by having each team member perform a simulated performance investigation using only the runbook — and refined based on the points where the tester needed clarification.

The performance standards, the diagnostic runbook, and the monitoring dashboard form a closed loop: the standards define the targets, the dashboard shows the current values relative to the targets, and the runbook provides the diagnostic procedure when values deviate from the targets. A new team member who reads the standards document, reviews the dashboard, and follows the runbook can diagnose and resolve most performance issues without prior experience — because the loop encodes the team's collective performance engineering knowledge in executable form.
Standards and Diagnostic Integration

The diagnostic runbook is the executable version of the diagnostic flow from lesson 1. The on-call engineer follows the numbered sequence, checking each component in order of likelihood (scheduler → database → executor → tasks). Each check has a specific metric to examine, a specific threshold that indicates a problem, and a specific action to take if the threshold is breached. The runbook is stored in the operations wiki alongside the rollback procedures from Module 7 and the security audit checklist from Module 8.
How the Standards Evolved

Every performance standard traces to a specific incident or capacity planning exercise. The 250ms per-file parse time target was established after the battery DAG's pandas import caused a parsing bottleneck. The 5GB database size target was established after the XCom bloat incident that degraded query performance. The parallelism-equals-capacity rule was established after the backfill incident that consumed all executor slots. The thin task convention was established after a data processing task occupied a worker slot for 45 minutes during the peak processing window. Each standard exists because the team experienced the consequences of not having it — and the standards document records the incident that motivated each one.

The standards are reviewed quarterly alongside the performance metrics. If a standard is consistently met without effort (the database has never approached 5GB since the XCom migration), the team considers whether the standard is still relevant or whether the target should be tightened. If a standard is difficult to maintain (the 250ms parse time target is challenging for DAGs that import the shared package), the team investigates whether the target should be relaxed or the underlying constraint should be addressed. The quarterly review ensures that the standards remain achievable, relevant, and aligned with the platform's current scale and workload.

blocks.title: The module in one sentence
blocks.content: Performance tuning is not about making Airflow faster — it is about identifying which component (scheduler parsing, database queries, executor dispatch, pool slots, or task execution) is the binding constraint for the platform's throughput and applying the targeted fix that addresses that specific constraint, verified by the metric that measures it, and monitored by the dashboard that detects when it degrades again.

chapters.title: Production Operations at SaulėGrid
chapters.description: Operate a complete Airflow platform in production: monitoring dashboards, incident management, operational runbooks, on-call workflows, platform evolution planning, and the transition from writing DAGs to operating an orchestration platform.

blocks.content: Nine modules ago, SaulėGrid had 3 DAGs, 1 developer, and a deployment process that consisted of copying files to a server. Now the platform has 12 DAGs, 3 engineers, 3 consuming teams, a CeleryExecutor cluster with 5 workers, a Secrets Backend in Vault, a CI/CD pipeline that blocks untested code, RBAC with custom roles for 12 users, a performance monitoring dashboard, and documented standards for pipeline design, testing, and security. The transition from 'I wrote some DAGs' to 'I operate a production orchestration platform' is the subject of this final module — the operational practices that keep the platform running reliably day after day, week after week, quarter after quarter.
Development vs Operations

Development and operations require different mindsets. Development asks: 'does this DAG produce correct output?' Operations asks: 'will this DAG produce correct output at 2 AM when the SFTP server is slow, the metadata database needs vacuuming, and the on-call engineer has been asleep for three hours?' Development focuses on the happy path — the scenario where everything works correctly. Operations focuses on the failure modes — the scenarios where networks partition, disks fill up, external systems return unexpected responses, and configuration drifts between environments. Every module in this course has taught operational thinking applied to specific components. This module teaches operational thinking as a discipline.
SLIs, SLOs, and Error Budgets

SaulėGrid borrows three concepts from site reliability engineering (SRE) to quantify operational quality. A Service Level Indicator (SLI) is a metric that measures a specific aspect of service quality — for example, the percentage of DAG Runs that complete within their SLA window. A Service Level Objective (SLO) is the target value for an SLI — for example, '99.5% of Gold DAG Runs complete before 06:00.' An error budget is the tolerable failure rate — 0.5% means SaulėGrid allows approximately 1-2 Gold SLA breaches per month before investigating systemic issues. The SLI-SLO-error budget framework transforms operational quality from a subjective assessment ('the platform feels reliable') into a measurable property ('the platform achieved 99.7% Gold SLA compliance this month').
What This Module Covers

Each operational discipline addresses a specific failure mode. The monitoring dashboard detects degradation before it causes SLA breaches. Incident management provides the structured response that resolves failures quickly and prevents escalation. Runbooks encode operational knowledge so that any engineer can perform any procedure correctly. The on-call rotation provides human coverage for issues that automation cannot resolve. SLA governance aligns the engineering team's priorities with the business teams' needs. Together, these disciplines form the operational immune system that keeps the platform healthy despite the constant pressure of growing data volumes, changing requirements, and inevitable component failures.
The Operational Cost

Operations is not free — it requires ongoing engineering time. SaulėGrid's operational overhead is approximately 15% of the team's capacity: 5 minutes per day for the morning check, 1 hour per week for the operations meeting, 4 hours per month for the SLA report and capacity review, and 12 hours per quarter for the security audit, rollback rehearsal, and credential rotation. The remaining 85% of engineering time is spent on development: new DAGs, pipeline improvements, and platform enhancements. The 15% operational overhead is the investment that keeps the 85% development work reliable in production. Without operations, the development work would spend more than 15% of its time dealing with production incidents.
