This module covers the five operational disciplines that keep the platform running. The monitoring dashboard (lesson 2) provides visibility into every component's health. Incident management (lesson 3) provides the structured response when things go wrong. Operational runbooks (lesson 4) provide the step-by-step procedures for common operations. The on-call rotation (lesson 5) provides the human coverage that automated monitoring cannot replace. Data freshness governance (lesson 6) provides the SLA framework that connects platform health to business impact. Platform health metrics (lesson 7) provide the aggregate indicators that predict capacity needs. Change management (lesson 8) provides the coordination process for changes that affect multiple teams. The final two lessons preview the senior track and synthesize the entire course.
The Four Operational Cadences

This module is different from the previous nine because it is not about building new capabilities — it is about sustaining the capabilities that modules 1-9 built. The scheduling architecture from Module 1, the pipeline design patterns from Module 3, the security framework from Module 8, and the performance standards from Module 9 all require ongoing attention to remain effective. Configuration drifts, new team members need onboarding, external systems change their behavior, and the platform's scale grows. Operations is the discipline that detects these changes, adapts the platform, and maintains the quality standards that the previous modules established.
The Operational Cadences

SaulėGrid's operational practices run on four cadences that structure the team's maintenance work. Daily: the on-call engineer performs the morning check, responds to alerts, and logs any incidents. Weekly: the team reviews the performance dashboard and the platform health score in the operations meeting. Monthly: the team produces the SLA compliance report and reviews capacity trends with stakeholders. Quarterly: the team performs the security audit, reviews performance standards, rehearses the rollback procedure, rotates credentials, and adjusts SLA targets. The four-cadence structure ensures that nothing is forgotten because every maintenance activity has a defined frequency and a defined owner.

blocks.title: The operations principle
blocks.content: A platform that is not actively operated degrades. Configuration drifts from the documented state. New team members bypass security controls because they were not onboarded properly. Performance thresholds are relaxed to silence alerts rather than fixing the underlying issues. Credential rotation is missed because the rotation schedule was forgotten. Runbooks become outdated because they are not exercised. Active operations — monitoring, auditing, maintaining, and reviewing — is the force that counteracts this natural degradation. SaulėGrid's operational practices are not overhead; they are the immune system that keeps the platform healthy.

SaulėGrid's monitoring dashboard is the on-call engineer's primary interface with the platform. The dashboard consolidates every operational metric from modules 3-9 into a single view: pipeline SLA compliance, scheduler health, executor utilization, database performance, security alerts, and data freshness. The engineer does not need to check the Airflow UI, the Grafana dashboard, the ELK stack, and the Vault audit log separately — the monitoring dashboard aggregates the critical indicators from all sources into one screen. If the dashboard shows green across all panels, the platform is healthy. If any panel shows yellow or red, the engineer knows exactly which component needs attention.
Dashboard vs Airflow UI

The monitoring dashboard is not a replacement for the Airflow UI — it is a complement. The Airflow UI shows the detailed state of individual DAGs, tasks, and DAG Runs. The monitoring dashboard shows the aggregate health of the entire platform across time. An engineer investigating a specific failed task uses the Airflow UI. An engineer checking whether the platform is healthy uses the monitoring dashboard. An engineer diagnosing a performance trend uses the monitoring dashboard. SaulėGrid's convention: the morning check starts with the monitoring dashboard (5 minutes for the aggregate view), and only drills into the Airflow UI if the dashboard indicates an issue that needs detailed investigation.
Dashboard Architecture

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's monitoring dashboard data flow

blocks.caption: The monitoring dashboard aggregates metrics from three sources: Prometheus (real-time component metrics via StatsD), the ELK stack (audit and access logs), and the custom monitoring DAG (business-level checks like data freshness and SLA compliance).

visual-recommendation.description: Full-color dashboard architecture diagram showing the three data collection pipelines (Prometheus, ELK, custom DAG) feeding into the Grafana dashboard with the eight panel categories.

blocks.content: The Eight Dashboard Panels

rows: Gold/Silver/Bronze SLA achievement rate (% on time)
Loop duration, parse time, scheduling delay
Queue depth, running tasks, worker count
Failed logins, sensitive actions, API anomalies
Any sensitive action by non-engineer → alert
Latest data date per layer (Bronze, Silver, Gold)
Success rate, failure count, retry rate (24h)
Parallelism utilization, pool utilization, worker CPU/memory

blocks.content: The eight panels are arranged in order of business impact. Pipeline SLA is top-left — the first thing the on-call engineer checks because SLA breaches directly affect downstream teams. Scheduler health is next because scheduler degradation causes SLA breaches if not addressed. Database health follows because database issues cause scheduler degradation. The cascade order mirrors the diagnostic flow from Module 9: pipeline symptoms trace to scheduler causes, which trace to database causes, which trace to capacity causes. The engineer reads the dashboard left-to-right, top-to-bottom, following the causal chain from effect to root cause.
The Morning Check Routine

The on-call engineer performs a 5-minute morning check at 07:00 every weekday. The check follows a fixed sequence: verify the Gold SLA panel is green (billing data was delivered before 06:00), scan the task health panel for overnight failures (any red cells indicate tasks that exhausted retries), check the data freshness panel for stale layers (all three layers should show today's date), and scan the security panel for overnight alerts (any flagged events require investigation). The morning check catches issues that occurred overnight but did not trigger critical alerts — for example, a single task failure that was retried successfully (visible in the retry rate) or a data freshness gap that resolved itself when a delayed Bronze run completed. The check is logged in the #saulegrid-ops Slack channel with a thumbs-up emoji if everything is green.
Alert Integration

Each dashboard panel is connected to a Grafana alert rule that fires when the metric crosses its threshold. The alert includes the panel name, the current metric value, the threshold that was crossed, and a direct link to the relevant runbook in the operations wiki. The on-call engineer receives the alert in PagerDuty for critical alerts or in Slack for warnings, clicks the runbook link, and follows the documented procedure. The alert-to-runbook link eliminates the diagnostic step of determining which runbook applies to the specific alert, because the answer is embedded in the alert payload itself. SaulėGrid maintains the alert-to-runbook mapping in a configuration file reviewed whenever a new alert rule or runbook is added.

The dashboard also serves as the incident investigation timeline during active incidents. When the on-call engineer is diagnosing a scheduler performance issue, they can zoom into the scheduler health panel and correlate the degradation timestamp with other panels: did the database size spike at the same time, did the executor queue depth increase, did a deployment occur? The cross-panel correlation reveals causal relationships that are invisible when looking at individual metrics in isolation. SaulėGrid arranges causally-related panels adjacent to each other on the dashboard — scheduler next to database, executor next to capacity — to facilitate this visual correlation during time-sensitive incident investigation.
Dashboard Maintenance

The dashboard evolves with the platform. When a new DAG is added, its SLA is added to the pipeline SLA panel. When a new external system is integrated, its pool utilization is added to the capacity panel. When a new security role is created, the corresponding audit patterns are added to the security panel. SaulėGrid treats the dashboard configuration as code — the Grafana dashboard JSON is version-controlled alongside the DAG code and deployed through the same CI/CD pipeline (Module 7). Dashboard changes go through PR review to ensure that new panels follow the established layout conventions and that alert thresholds are calibrated against the performance standards from Module 9.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's dashboard principle: every metric on the dashboard must have an owner (who investigates when it alerts), a threshold (what value triggers the alert), and an action (what the engineer does when the alert fires). A metric without all three is visual noise that dilutes the engineer's attention. The quarterly dashboard review removes panels that have never alerted and adds panels for metrics that have caused incidents despite not being monitored. The dashboard is a living document of what the team considers important enough to watch.

An incident is any unplanned event that degrades the platform's service quality below the defined SLOs. A Gold SLA breach is an incident. A scheduler crash that stalls all DAGs is an incident. A security breach where an unauthorized user modifies a DAG is an incident. A database outage that makes the UI unresponsive is an incident. Not every issue is an incident — a single task retry that succeeds is normal operations. The distinction matters because incidents trigger a structured response process (detection, triage, response, recovery, post-mortem) while normal operations are handled through standard monitoring and the morning check routine.
The Incident Lifecycle

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's incident management lifecycle
blocks.content:
Detection                  Triage                 Response
    │                        │                       │
    ▼                        ▼                       ▼

│ Alert    │          │ Classify  │           │ Diagnose  │
│ fires    │─────────▶│ severity  │──────────▶│ root cause│
│ (auto or │          │ (P1-P3)   │           │           │
│  manual) │          └───────────┘           │ Mitigate  │
└──────────┘                │                 │ (fix or   │
                            │                 │  rollback)│
                            ▼                 └─────┬─────┘
                    ┌───────────────┐               │
                    │ Communicate   │               ▼
                    │ to affected   │         ┌───────────┐
                    │ teams         │         │ Verify    │
                    └───────────────┘         │ recovery  │
                                              └─────┬─────┘
                                                    │
                Recovery              Post-Mortem    │
                    │                     │          │
                    ▼                     ▼          ▼
              ┌───────────┐        ┌───────────────────┐
              │ Confirm   │        │ Root cause analysis│
              │ SLO       │───────▶│ Prevention measures│
              │ restored  │        │ Standards update   │
              └───────────┘        └───────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The incident lifecycle has five phases. Detection and triage happen in minutes. Response and recovery happen in minutes to hours. The post-mortem happens within 48 hours of resolution.

visual-recommendation.description: Incident lifecycle diagram showing the five phases (detection, triage, response, recovery, post-mortem) with timing annotations, severity classifications, and the communication protocol at each phase.

blocks.content: Severity Classification

rows: Platform-wide outage or Gold SLA breach imminent/actual
Single pipeline broken, downstream team affected
Degraded performance, no SLA breach yet
Scheduling delay increasing, DB queries slow

blocks.content: The Incident Communication Protocol

SaulėGrid's incident communication follows a three-stage protocol. Stage 1 (within 15 minutes of detection): the on-call engineer posts in #saulegrid-incidents with the severity, the affected component, and the initial assessment. Stage 2 (within 30 minutes): the engineer posts an update with the diagnosed root cause and the mitigation plan — either a fix, a rollback, or a workaround. Stage 3 (within 1 hour of resolution): the engineer posts a resolution summary and confirms that the SLO is restored. For P1 incidents, the engineer also sends a direct message to the affected team's lead (billing lead, analytics lead, or operations manager) with a business-impact summary and an estimated time to resolution.

The communication protocol prevents two common failures. First, the 'silent incident' where the engineer fixes the problem without telling anyone — downstream teams do not know that their data was late or that a fix was applied, and they may take incorrect actions based on stale data. Second, the 'noisy incident' where the engineer floods the channel with raw diagnostic output — downstream teams cannot determine whether the incident affects them or what action they should take. The structured protocol provides exactly the information that affected teams need: what happened, how long it lasted, and whether it is resolved.
The Incident Template

# SaulėGrid Incident Report Template
# Filed within 48 hours of P1/P2 incident resolution

## Incident Summary
- Date/time: [detection to resolution]
- Severity: [P1/P2/P3]
- Duration: [total incident duration]
- SLAs affected: [which SLAs breached, by how much]

## Timeline
- HH:MM — [Event: what happened]
- HH:MM — [Detection: how was it detected]
- HH:MM — [Response: what action was taken]
- HH:MM — [Resolution: when was service restored]

## Root Cause
[Detailed analysis of why the incident occurred]

## Impact
- Teams affected: [which downstream teams]
- Data impact: [was any data incorrect or delayed?]

## Prevention Measures
- [ ] [Specific action to prevent recurrence]
- [ ] [CI check / monitoring alert / config change]
- [ ] [Runbook update / standards update]
The Post-Mortem Process

Every P1 and P2 incident triggers a post-mortem document within 48 hours of resolution. The post-mortem answers five questions: what happened (timeline of events from detection to resolution), why it happened (root cause analysis tracing the failure to its origin), what was the impact (which teams were affected, which SLOs were breached, what was the business cost), how was it resolved (what fix, rollback, or workaround was applied), and how do we prevent recurrence (what CI check, monitoring alert, configuration change, or process improvement will prevent this specific failure from happening again). The prevention measure is the most important section — it transforms the incident from a one-time cost into a permanent improvement. Every standard in modules 3, 6, 7, 8, and 9 traces to a post-mortem prevention measure.
Prevention Measures Tracking

The prevention measures from each post-mortem are tracked as engineering tasks in the sprint backlog with a 'post-mortem' label. The quarterly operations review checks the completion status of all post-mortem action items: completed items are verified (does the new CI check actually catch the failure class?), open items are prioritized for the next sprint, and items that have been open for more than one quarter are escalated to the team lead. SaulėGrid has completed 23 of 25 post-mortem prevention measures within the quarter they were identified — the remaining 2 required infrastructure changes that were scheduled for the following quarter.

The prevention measures have a cumulative effect: each post-mortem adds a new CI check, a new monitoring alert, a new runbook, or a new standard that prevents a specific failure class from recurring. After two quarters of consistent post-mortem follow-through, SaulėGrid's platform has 9 CI checks (each tracing to a specific incident), 8 monitoring alert rules, 10 runbook procedures, and 9 pipeline design standards — all documented, all automated where possible, and all verified quarterly. The incident prevention rate (PRs blocked by CI checks that would have caused incidents) demonstrates the cumulative value: 18 prevented incidents in two quarters from the standards and checks that post-mortems introduced.

blocks.title: The blameless post-mortem
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's post-mortems are blameless: they focus on system failures, not human failures. If an engineer deployed untested code that caused an incident, the post-mortem asks 'why did the deployment pipeline allow untested code to reach production?' not 'why did the engineer skip the tests?' The system (the CI pipeline, the branch protection, the deployment process) should have prevented the error. If it did not, the system needs improvement — not the person. Blameless post-mortems encourage honest reporting: engineers who fear blame hide incidents or minimize their role, which prevents the team from learning from failures.

A runbook is a step-by-step procedure for a specific operational task. SaulėGrid maintains runbooks for every recurring operational procedure: pipeline failure recovery, scheduler restart, database maintenance, credential rotation, new DAG deployment, capacity scaling, security audit, and incident response. The runbook is the operational equivalent of a unit test — it encodes the correct procedure in a documented, reproducible form so that any team member can perform the operation correctly, even if they have never performed it before. An operation without a runbook depends on tribal knowledge — the procedure exists only in the head of the engineer who performed it last.
Runbook Structure

# RUNBOOK: Pipeline Failure Recovery
# Owner: Platform Engineering Team
# Last reviewed: 2026-03-24
# Frequency: As needed (triggered by pipeline failure alert)

## Prerequisites
- Airflow CLI access to the production scheduler
- Vault access for credential verification (if auth failure)
- PagerDuty access to acknowledge the alert

## Procedure

### Step 1: Identify the failed task
- Open the Airflow UI at https://airflow.saulegrid.internal
- Navigate to the failed DAG Run (linked in the alert)
- Identify the failed task and read the task log
- Classify the failure: transient (retry likely to succeed) or
  permanent (code/config/data issue)

### Step 2: Resolve based on classification

#### Transient failure (timeout, rate limit, connection error)
- Clear the failed task: airflow tasks clear <dag_id> --start-date
  <date> --end-date <date> --task-regex <task_id> --downstream --yes
- Monitor the retry in the Airflow UI
- Verify the downstream chain completes

#### Permanent failure (wrong credentials, schema mismatch, code bug)
- Check the Connection: airflow connections test <conn_id>
- If auth failure: verify credential in Vault, re-test
- If code bug: escalate to the DAG author, consider rollback
- If data issue: route to dead-letter, notify data team

### Step 3: Verify recovery
- Confirm the task completed successfully
- Confirm downstream tasks triggered (check Dataset events)
- Confirm the SLA is on track (check the monitoring dashboard)

### Step 4: Communicate
- Post resolution in #saulegrid-incidents
- If P1/P2: notify affected team leads

## Rollback
- If the fix causes additional failures: helm rollback airflow-prod 1
- Follow the Rollback Procedures runbook

## Expected Duration: 15-45 minutes

The runbook structure follows a consistent template across all SaulėGrid runbooks: prerequisites (what access and tools are needed), procedure (numbered steps with decision points), rollback (what to do if the procedure makes things worse), and expected duration (how long the operation should take). The template ensures that every runbook is complete — a missing prerequisites section means the engineer may discover halfway through that they lack the necessary access. The expected duration helps the engineer calibrate their response: if the procedure is taking longer than expected, something unexpected is happening and escalation may be needed.
Decision Points in Runbooks

Well-designed runbooks include decision points — steps where the engineer must choose between different paths based on diagnostic information. The Pipeline Failure Recovery runbook has a decision point at Step 2: classify the failure as transient or permanent. A transient failure (timeout, rate limit) is resolved by clearing and retrying. A permanent failure (wrong credentials, schema mismatch) requires different resolution — credential verification, code fix, or escalation. Decision points are formatted as if-then-else blocks with clear criteria for each branch, ensuring that the engineer can make the correct choice without interpreting ambiguous instructions.

The expected duration in each runbook serves a dual purpose: it tells the engineer how long the procedure should take, and it provides a trigger for escalation. If the Pipeline Failure Recovery procedure (expected: 15-45 minutes) has taken 60 minutes without resolution, the engineer should escalate to L2 rather than continuing to investigate independently. The expected duration is calibrated from historical execution data — SaulėGrid measures how long each runbook execution actually takes and updates the expected duration quarterly. A consistently exceeded expected duration indicates that the runbook needs refinement or that the underlying procedure has become more complex.
The Runbook Library
Runbook Maintenance

Runbooks decay if they are not exercised. A runbook written six months ago may reference a CLI command that was renamed, a UI page that was reorganized, or a Vault path that was restructured. SaulėGrid prevents runbook decay with two practices. First, every runbook is exercised at least once per quarter — either during a real operational event or during a scheduled drill. The drill is performed in staging by a team member who follows the runbook step-by-step and reports any steps that are unclear, incorrect, or missing. Second, every runbook has an 'owner' and a 'last reviewed' date in the header. Runbooks not reviewed within 90 days are flagged in the quarterly operations review for update.

SaulėGrid's runbook library is stored in the operations wiki alongside the performance standards, the security policy, and the architecture decision records. The runbooks are linked from the monitoring dashboard: each alert includes a 'Runbook' link that takes the engineer directly to the relevant procedure. The link-from-alert pattern reduces the engineer's response time from 'read the alert → find the runbook → follow the procedure' to 'read the alert → click the runbook link → follow the procedure' — eliminating the search step that adds minutes to incident response when minutes matter.
The Runbook Drill

SaulėGrid drills each critical runbook quarterly in the staging environment. The drill is performed by a different team member each quarter — ensuring that every engineer has practiced every procedure at least once per year. The drill follows the runbook exactly as written, and the engineer notes any steps that are unclear, incorrect, or missing. The notes are used to update the runbook after the drill. The quarterly drill has caught runbook issues 5 times in two quarters: a CLI command that changed syntax in an Airflow update, a Vault path that was restructured, a Helm command that needed an additional parameter after a chart upgrade, a monitoring dashboard URL that changed, and a Slack channel that was renamed.

Well-designed runbooks include decision points — steps where the engineer must choose between different diagnostic or remediation paths based on the information they have gathered. The Pipeline Failure Recovery runbook has a critical decision point at Step 2: classify the failure as transient or permanent based on the error message and the failure pattern. A transient failure like a timeout or rate limit is resolved by clearing the failed task and letting Airflow retry it. A permanent failure like wrong credentials or a schema mismatch requires different resolution — credential verification in Vault, code fix deployment, or escalation to the DAG author. Decision points are formatted as clear if-then-else branches with specific criteria for each path.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's runbook principle: if an operation has been performed more than twice, it should have a runbook. The first time is exploration (learning how to do it). The second time is verification (confirming the approach). The third time should be documented (writing the runbook so anyone can do it). This principle prevents the 'bus factor' problem where only one engineer knows how to perform a critical operation. With runbooks, every team member can perform every operation — the knowledge is in the document, not in any single person's head.

SaulėGrid's platform runs 24/7 — the midnight Bronze ingestion, the overnight Silver and Gold processing, and the continuous monitoring DAGs do not pause for weekends or holidays. Automated monitoring detects most issues and automated retries resolve most transient failures, but some incidents require human judgment: deciding whether to roll back a deployment, communicating with an external provider about a service degradation, or making a triage decision about a novel failure mode. The on-call rotation provides the human coverage that automated systems cannot replace — a designated engineer who is reachable within 15 minutes of a critical alert, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
The On-Call Rotation

SaulėGrid's 3-person platform engineering team rotates on-call weekly: Matas covers week 1, Ieva covers week 2, Jonas covers week 3, then the rotation repeats. The on-call engineer is responsible for acknowledging critical alerts within 15 minutes, performing the morning check routine at 07:00, and being available for escalation during business hours. The rotation is published in a shared calendar that the operations and analytics teams can reference when they need to reach the on-call engineer. The handoff between on-call shifts happens every Monday at 10:00: the outgoing engineer briefs the incoming engineer on any open issues, ongoing investigations, or scheduled maintenance.
On-Call Tooling

The on-call engineer's toolkit includes: PagerDuty for alert routing and acknowledgment, the Grafana monitoring dashboard for platform health visibility, the Airflow UI for DAG-level investigation, the Vault CLI for credential verification, the Helm CLI for deployment operations, and the runbook library for procedure reference. The toolkit is documented in the on-call onboarding guide — a document that every engineer reviews before their first on-call shift. The guide includes the PagerDuty escalation configuration, the Grafana dashboard URL, the Airflow UI credentials, and the VPN connection procedure for accessing the production environment from outside the office.

SaulėGrid's on-call tooling is tested during the quarterly runbook drills. The engineer performing the drill verifies that every tool in the toolkit is accessible, correctly configured, and produces the expected output. A tool that is inaccessible during a drill (VPN token expired, PagerDuty schedule misconfigured, Grafana password rotated without updating the on-call credentials) is fixed immediately — because the same accessibility issue during a 2 AM incident would add minutes to the response time when minutes matter most. The toolkit verification takes 10 minutes per drill and has caught 3 accessibility issues that would have delayed incident response.
The Escalation Path

rows: Alert fires or morning check reveals issue
L1 cannot resolve within 30 minutes, or P1 severity
Authorize rollback, coordinate with other teams, make triage decisions
Swarming: each engineer takes a diagnostic track
SFTP provider, weather API, AWS support

blocks.content: The escalation path is designed for progressive engagement: most incidents are resolved by the L1 on-call engineer using the runbook library. Only incidents that exceed the runbook's scope or the engineer's authority escalate to L2. The team lead at L2 can authorize actions that the on-call engineer cannot: production rollbacks, emergency deployments outside the window, and direct communication with downstream team leads. L3 swarming is reserved for platform-wide incidents where parallel diagnosis is needed — for example, the scheduler is down and the cause could be the scheduler process, the database, or the broker, and each engineer investigates one possibility simultaneously.
On-Call Responsibilities

The on-call engineer has five responsibilities. First, alert response: acknowledge and investigate all critical and warning alerts within the SLA (15 minutes for critical, 1 hour for warning). Second, morning check: perform the 5-minute dashboard review at 07:00 and post the result in Slack. Third, availability: be reachable by phone and Slack during the on-call shift, with a maximum 15-minute response time for critical alerts. Fourth, documentation: log all incidents, their resolution, and any follow-up actions in the incident tracking system. Fifth, handoff: brief the next on-call engineer on all open items at the Monday 10:00 handoff meeting.
On-Call Sustainability

A 3-person rotation means each engineer is on-call for one week out of every three — a sustainable cadence that provides adequate rest between shifts. SaulėGrid monitors on-call health through three indicators: the number of alerts per on-call shift (target: fewer than 5 per week), the number of after-hours pages (target: fewer than 2 per week), and the mean time to acknowledgment (target: under 10 minutes). If any indicator trends above the target, the team investigates the root cause — typically either alert noise (too many low-severity alerts) or a systemic platform issue (a component that fails frequently). Reducing alert noise is as important as fixing platform issues because alert fatigue degrades the engineer's response quality.
Handoff Protocol

The Monday 10:00 handoff meeting is a 15-minute structured exchange between the outgoing and incoming on-call engineers. The outgoing engineer covers four topics: incidents that occurred during the shift (resolved and unresolved), alerts that fired but did not require action (explaining why they were benign), any ongoing investigations or maintenance tasks that the incoming engineer should be aware of, and any changes to the platform that were deployed during the shift. The incoming engineer confirms they have access to all on-call tools, reviews the current state of the monitoring dashboard, and acknowledges the handoff in the #saulegrid-ops Slack channel. The handoff log serves as a weekly operational record that the team references during the weekly operations meeting.

blocks.title: On-call anti-patterns
blocks.content: The two most dangerous on-call anti-patterns are alert fatigue and hero culture. Alert fatigue occurs when the on-call engineer receives so many alerts that they stop investigating each one carefully — leading to a real incident being dismissed as 'just another false alarm.' SaulėGrid prevents alert fatigue by keeping the alert count under 5 per week (achieved by fixing noisy sources rather than silencing alerts). Hero culture occurs when one engineer handles all incidents personally rather than delegating or escalating — leading to burnout, knowledge silos, and a single point of failure on the team. SaulėGrid prevents hero culture by requiring every incident response to follow the runbook (not the hero's personal knowledge) and by rotating the on-call role strictly.

SLA management was introduced in Module 3, lesson 5 as a mechanism for detecting when pipelines run late. This lesson elevates SLA management from a detection mechanism to a governance framework: how SLAs are defined with downstream consumers, how SLA compliance is measured and reported, how SLA targets are adjusted based on actual performance, and how the SLA framework drives platform investment decisions. SLA governance transforms the relationship between the data engineering team and the consuming teams from 'we hope the data is ready on time' to 'we commit to delivering data on time and can prove we meet that commitment.'
The SLA Agreement Process

SaulėGrid's SLA for each pipeline layer was negotiated with the consuming teams, not unilaterally declared. The billing team specified that Gold billing data must be available before 06:00 because their systems process billing at 06:30. The analytics team specified that Silver data must be available before 04:00 because their dashboards refresh at 04:30. The Bronze SLA of 01:30 was derived by working backward from the Silver SLA: Silver processing takes approximately 90 minutes, so Bronze must complete 90 minutes before the Silver deadline. Each SLA is documented in a shared agreement that both teams have signed off on — the data engineering team commits to the delivery target, and the consuming team commits to not querying data before the agreed time.
SLA Compliance Reporting

SaulėGrid generates a monthly SLA compliance report that shows the achievement rate for each pipeline layer: the number of days where data was delivered within the SLA window, the number of breaches, the root cause of each breach, and the corrective action taken. The report is shared with all consuming teams during the monthly data platform review meeting. The report builds trust through transparency — when the billing team can see that Gold SLA compliance was 99.7% (one breach in 30 days) and that the single breach was caused by an SFTP provider outage with a documented recovery procedure, they trust the platform more than when they simply observe that 'data is usually on time.'
SLA Target Adjustment

SLA targets are reviewed quarterly and adjusted based on three factors. First, actual performance: if Bronze consistently completes by 01:00 (30 minutes before the 01:30 SLA), the SLA has excessive buffer and could be tightened to 01:15 — freeing 15 minutes of buffer for the Silver SLA. Second, capacity changes: if a new data source is added to the Bronze layer and processing time increases from 20 minutes to 35 minutes, the Bronze SLA may need to be relaxed to 01:45. Third, consumer needs: if the billing team changes their processing window from 06:30 to 06:00, the Gold SLA must be tightened to 05:30 — which cascades backward to tighter Silver and Bronze SLAs. SLA adjustments are documented, communicated to all affected teams, and reflected in the monitoring dashboard's alert thresholds.
SLA-Driven Investment Decisions

The SLA framework drives platform investment decisions by quantifying the impact of capacity constraints. When Bronze SLA compliance drops from 99.5% to 98% because data volume growth has increased processing time, the SLA metric provides the business justification for adding worker capacity: 'Bronze SLA compliance has decreased to 98% due to volume growth. Adding one worker restores the processing buffer and returns compliance to the 99.5% target. The worker costs €150/month; each SLA breach costs approximately 2 hours of engineering time plus billing team impact.' The SLA framework translates infrastructure costs into business terms that stakeholders can evaluate.
Freshness Monitoring Integration

The data freshness monitoring from Module 3 (the dag_sla_monitor DAG that checks whether each layer's data is current) integrates with the SLA governance framework. The monitor fires warnings when data is approaching staleness and critical alerts when the SLA deadline passes without data delivery. SaulėGrid extends the freshness monitor with a consumer notification feature: when the Gold SLA is breached, the monitor automatically notifies the billing team's Slack channel with a message explaining that data will be late, the estimated delay, and the root cause (if known). This proactive notification gives the billing team time to adjust their workflow rather than discovering the delay when their batch process fails.
SLA Performance Archive

The SLA compliance report is the cornerstone document for the monthly data platform review meeting. The report shows not just whether SLAs were met, but why any breaches occurred and what was done to prevent recurrence. A month with zero breaches produces a brief report — 'all SLAs met, no action required.' A month with breaches produces a detailed report that traces each breach to its root cause, documents the corrective action, and assesses whether the SLA target remains achievable. The report's consistency over time builds stakeholder confidence: when the billing team sees 6 consecutive months of 99.5%+ Gold SLA compliance with documented incident response for every breach, they trust the platform's reliability.

SaulėGrid archives monthly SLA performance data in a persistent store (a dedicated PostgreSQL table separate from the Airflow metadata database). The archive contains the daily SLA compliance status for every pipeline layer: the delivery time, the SLA target, whether the target was met, and the root cause code if the target was missed. The archive enables year-over-year performance comparison: the team can demonstrate that SLA compliance improved from 97% in Q1 to 99.5% in Q3 as a result of specific platform investments (the CeleryExecutor migration, the database maintenance automation, the CI/CD pipeline). The archive data feeds the quarterly SLA target adjustment discussion with stakeholders.
Historical SLA Analysis

The SLA performance archive also supports capacity planning by revealing seasonal patterns. SaulėGrid's Bronze processing time increases by 15% in July and August when residential air conditioning drives higher meter reading volumes. The archive shows this pattern repeating across multiple years, allowing the team to proactively add worker capacity in June before the summer volume increase pushes processing time beyond the SLA window. Without the historical archive, the team would discover the seasonal pattern only when the SLA is breached — reactive rather than proactive.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's SLA governance principle: SLAs are contracts between teams, not aspirational targets. The data engineering team commits to delivering data within the SLA window, and in return, the consuming team commits to querying data only after the SLA window. This mutual commitment prevents the scenario where the analytics team queries Silver at 02:30 (30 minutes before the 03:00 SLA) and complains about incomplete data — the SLA defines when the data is guaranteed to be ready, and querying before that time is outside the agreement. The mutual commitment creates clear accountability: if data is late, the engineering team investigates. If data is queried too early, the consuming team adjusts their schedule.

The monitoring dashboard (lesson 2) shows individual component metrics in real time. Platform health metrics aggregate these individual metrics into higher-level indicators that reveal the platform's overall trajectory: is the platform becoming more reliable over time, is capacity approaching its limits, and are operational practices maintaining the quality standards established in modules 3-9? These aggregate metrics are reviewed weekly in the operations meeting and monthly in the data platform review with stakeholders, providing a long-term perspective that real-time monitoring cannot.
SaulėGrid's Platform Health Score

SaulėGrid combines five indicators into a weekly platform health score: SLA compliance (weighted 30%), task success rate (weighted 20%), scheduling delay (weighted 15%), security compliance (weighted 15%), and capacity headroom (weighted 20%). Each indicator is scored on a 0-100 scale based on its distance from the target: SLA compliance of 99.7% against a 99.5% target scores 100, while 98% scores 60. The weighted average produces a single score that the team reports to stakeholders. The score is not a precise measure — it is a communication tool that answers the question 'how healthy is the platform this week?' with a number instead of a paragraph.
Calculating the Score

Each indicator's 0-100 score is calculated using a linear interpolation between the minimum acceptable value (score = 0) and the target value (score = 100). For SLA compliance, the target is 99.5% (score = 100) and the minimum is 95% (score = 0). A compliance of 97% scores 44 ((97-95)/(99.5-95) × 100). Values above the target score 100 (no bonus for exceeding the target — the platform should not be over-engineered). The linear scoring makes the health score intuitive: a score of 50 means the indicator is halfway between unacceptable and target. The weighted average across all five indicators produces a single number between 0 and 100 that the team reports weekly.
Capacity Planning Metrics

Capacity planning uses four trend metrics to predict when the platform will need additional resources. First, parallelism utilization trend: if the percentage of parallelism slots used during peak hours increases by 5% per month, the team can predict when the slots will be fully utilized and plan a worker addition. Second, database size growth rate: if the database grows 200MB per month after maintenance cleanup, the team can predict when the 5GB target will be exceeded and plan retention adjustments. Third, DAG parse time growth: if total parse time increases 100ms per quarter (as new DAGs are added), the team can predict when the parse time budget will be exceeded. Fourth, scheduling delay trend: if the p95 delay increases 2 seconds per month, the team can predict when the 30-second target will be breached.

The capacity planning metrics connect directly to the SLA governance framework from lesson 6: if parallelism utilization trends toward 100%, the scheduling delay will eventually increase beyond the SLA target, causing SLA breaches. The capacity projection predicts when this will happen, and the SLA framework provides the business justification for the infrastructure investment needed to prevent it. This connection between capacity metrics and business impact makes capacity planning actionable — the team requests additional servers not because they might be needed someday, but because the SLA compliance metric will drop below the 99.5% target in 4 months without additional worker capacity.
The Monthly Health Report

SaulėGrid produces a monthly platform health report for the data platform review meeting attended by all three team leads (engineering, analytics, operations). The report includes: the weekly platform health scores for the month (with trend), the SLA compliance breakdown by layer, the incident summary (count, severity, root causes, resolution times), the capacity utilization trend, and the planned platform changes for the next month. The report takes approximately 1 hour to prepare (mostly automated from the monitoring data) and provides the stakeholders with a data-driven view of the platform's operational quality without requiring them to interpret the Grafana dashboard directly.

The monthly report also includes a forward-looking section: risks and planned mitigations. If the capacity headroom metric is trending downward, the report notes the projected date when the platform will reach capacity and the planned response (add workers, optimize tasks, or scale the database). If a consuming team has requested a tighter SLA, the report analyzes the feasibility and the infrastructure changes required. This forward-looking analysis prevents surprises: stakeholders are informed about approaching limits and planned changes months before they become urgent.
Stakeholder Communication

The stakeholder communication strategy uses different levels of detail for different audiences. The engineering team receives the full monitoring dashboard with every metric in real time. The team leads receive the weekly platform health score — a single number with trend. The executive stakeholders receive the monthly health report — a one-page summary with SLA compliance, incident count, and capacity outlook. Each level of abstraction is derived from the same underlying data, ensuring consistency across all communication. The abstraction levels prevent information overload: the executive does not need to know the scheduler loop duration, and the engineer does not need to see the monthly summary during incident response.

The monthly health report is the primary communication channel between the data engineering team and the platform stakeholders. The report translates operational metrics into business terms: 'Gold SLA compliance was 99.7%' becomes 'billing data was delivered on time every day this month except March 12th, when an SFTP provider outage delayed delivery by 45 minutes — the delay was detected within 10 minutes and the billing team was notified proactively.' The translation makes the report accessible to non-technical stakeholders who care about data availability, not about scheduler loop duration or database connection pools. The engineering detail is available in an appendix for stakeholders who want to understand the technical underpinnings.

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Platform health metrics serve two audiences: the engineering team (who need detailed metrics for diagnosis and optimization) and the stakeholders (who need aggregate indicators for decision-making). The monitoring dashboard serves the engineering team — it shows every metric in real time with full detail. The platform health score and monthly report serve the stakeholders — they compress the platform's operational quality into a single number and a one-page summary. Serving both audiences with the same raw data, presented at different levels of abstraction, is the key to effective operational communication.

SaulėGrid's platform serves three teams. A change to one DAG can affect another team's data: a schema change in Silver propagates to Gold and to the analytics team's dashboards. A performance optimization that increases parallelism may cause pool contention for the operations team's reprocessing DAGs. A security role change may accidentally remove access for a user who needs it. Change management is the process that coordinates changes across the multi-team platform, ensuring that every change is assessed for cross-team impact, communicated to affected stakeholders, and executed with a rollback plan.
The Change Request Template

# SaulėGrid Change Request Template
# Filed as a GitHub Issue before implementing any change

## Change Description
- What: [Describe the change in one sentence]
- Why: [Business or technical motivation]
- DAGs affected: [List all DAGs modified or impacted]
- Datasets affected: [List any Dataset URIs that change]

## Impact Assessment
- Downstream teams affected: [analytics / operations / none]
- Schema changes: [yes/no — if yes, describe backward compatibility]
- Scheduling changes: [yes/no — if yes, describe impact on chain timing]
- Performance impact: [yes/no — if yes, describe expected change]
- Security impact: [yes/no — if yes, describe role/access changes]

## Deployment Plan
- Deployment window: [date, 10:00-14:00]
- Rollback plan: [specific rollback steps]
- Verification plan: [how to verify the change worked]

## Communication Plan
- Pre-deployment: [who to notify before deploying]
- Post-deployment: [who to notify after deploying]
- SLA impact: [will this change affect any SLA?]

## Approval
- Team lead approval: [ ] (required for all changes)
- Downstream team acknowledgment: [ ] (required if downstream affected)

The change request template forces the engineer to assess cross-team impact before implementation. The impact assessment section checks five dimensions: downstream teams, schema changes, scheduling changes, performance impact, and security impact. A change that affects none of these dimensions (for example, a code refactoring that does not change behavior) can proceed with standard code review. A change that affects any dimension requires additional review: downstream team acknowledgment for schema or scheduling changes, security team review for access changes, and performance review for changes that affect executor utilization or scheduler load.
Change Communication Windows

SaulėGrid defines two communication windows for changes. Pre-deployment communication (24 hours before deployment for medium-impact changes, 1 week before for high-impact changes) gives downstream teams time to prepare: adjust their query schedules, update their dashboards, or test their processes against the changed schema in staging. Post-deployment communication (within 1 hour of deployment) confirms that the change was deployed successfully and the platform is operating normally. The communication happens in the #saulegrid-data-platform Slack channel, which all three teams monitor. For high-impact changes, the communication also goes to the affected team's lead via direct message.
Impact Classification

rows: No downstream impact, no schema change, no scheduling change
Affects one downstream team, or changes scheduling or performance
Code review + team lead approval + downstream notification
Affects multiple teams, or changes schema, security, or infrastructure
Code review + team lead + downstream acknowledgment + change advisory review

blocks.content: The impact classification determines the change's approval process and deployment window. Low-impact changes follow the standard CI/CD pipeline from Module 7 with no additional coordination. Medium-impact changes require team lead approval and notification to the affected downstream team — the notification gives the team time to prepare (for example, adjusting their dashboard queries for a schema change). High-impact changes require a change advisory review: a 30-minute meeting where the engineer presents the change, the impact assessment, the deployment plan, and the rollback plan to the team lead and affected stakeholders. The review is not a gate that blocks changes — it is a coordination mechanism that ensures everyone understands the change's implications.
Dataset Dependency Graphs for Impact Analysis

SaulėGrid uses the Airflow Datasets tab to trace the impact of changes through the DAG chain. When an engineer modifies dag_silver_meters, the Dataset graph shows that Silver meters feeds into dag_gold_billing and dag_gold_forecast — both of which are consumed by the analytics team. The graph provides the definitive answer to 'who is affected by this change?' without requiring the engineer to remember the full dependency chain. SaulėGrid's change request template requires the engineer to include a screenshot of the relevant Dataset dependency graph, ensuring that the impact assessment is based on the actual dependency structure rather than the engineer's memory of it.
The Change Advisory Review

High-impact changes require a change advisory review — a 30-minute meeting where the engineer presents the change to the team lead and affected stakeholders. The review covers four topics: what the change does and why it is needed (business justification), what the impact assessment revealed (which teams and DAGs are affected), what the deployment plan looks like (timing, rollback plan, verification steps), and what the communication plan covers (who is notified, when, and how). The review is not a gate that blocks changes — it is a coordination mechanism that ensures everyone understands the implications. In SaulėGrid's experience, the review has changed the deployment plan for 3 of 8 high-impact changes reviewed in the past two quarters — in each case, the review identified an impact that the engineer had not considered.

The change advisory review maintains a log of all reviewed changes, their impact assessments, and their outcomes. The log serves as a reference for future similar changes ('the last time we changed the Silver schema, we needed to coordinate with the analytics team two weeks in advance') and as evidence for the compliance audit ('every high-impact change in Q1 was reviewed, approved, and communicated before deployment'). The log is stored in the operations wiki alongside the incident post-mortems and the SLA compliance reports, forming a comprehensive operational record.
Graduated Overhead

The change management process scales with impact: low-impact changes take 5 minutes of additional overhead (the standard PR review). Medium-impact changes take 30 minutes (PR review + team lead approval + notification). High-impact changes take 2-3 hours (PR review + impact assessment + change advisory review + stakeholder notification). The graduated overhead ensures that routine changes flow quickly while significant changes receive proportionate attention. SaulėGrid processes approximately 8 changes per week: 5 low-impact (DAG logic changes), 2 medium-impact (scheduling or performance changes), and 1 high-impact every 2-3 weeks (schema changes, infrastructure upgrades, or security modifications).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's change management principle: every change is reversible, communicated, and traceable. Reversible: every change has a documented rollback plan (Module 7). Communicated: every change that affects downstream teams is notified before deployment and confirmed after deployment. Traceable: every change is linked to a change request, a PR, a CI pipeline run, and a deployment event — providing a complete audit trail from the business motivation through the technical implementation to the production deployment. The three properties ensure that the platform evolves predictably and that any issue can be traced back to the change that caused it.

sections.title: What Comes Next — The Senior Track

blocks.content: The mid-level track has equipped you with the skills to build and operate a production Airflow platform: data-aware scheduling with Datasets, custom operators and hooks, idempotent pipeline design, distributed execution with CeleryExecutor, secrets management with Vault, systematic testing, CI/CD deployment, RBAC security, performance tuning, and operational runbooks. The senior-level track builds on this foundation to address the challenges that emerge at enterprise scale: a platform serving not 3 teams but 10, not 12 DAGs but 200, not 1 country but 3. The senior track's scenario is the BalticSolar Alliance — a confederation of SaulėGrid (Lithuania), NordGrid (Latvia), and WestGrid (Estonia) — operating a shared Airflow platform across 4 million meters, 30 regions, and 10 teams.

The BalticSolar Alliance scenario is not hypothetical — it represents the actual growth trajectory of the Lithuanian energy sector's digitalization initiative, scaled to a realistic enterprise deployment. The scenario's complexity is calibrated to expose the patterns that mid-level practices cannot handle: 200 DAGs cannot be maintained individually (requiring factories), 10 teams cannot share a flat RBAC model (requiring namespaces), and 3 countries cannot operate under a single set of regulatory rules (requiring per-country governance). Each senior topic is motivated by a specific failure of the corresponding mid-level pattern at enterprise scale.
The BalticSolar Alliance Scenario

The senior track introduces the BalticSolar Alliance: SaulėGrid (Lithuania), NordGrid (Latvia), and WestGrid (Estonia) operating a shared Airflow platform across three countries. The Alliance has 4 million meters across 30 regions, 10 teams with different regulatory requirements per country, and 200+ DAGs generated from 20 configuration templates. The scale increase from mid-level (1.2M meters, 12 DAGs, 3 teams) to senior (4M meters, 200 DAGs, 10 teams) is not just quantitative — it introduces qualitative challenges: multi-timezone scheduling where Estonian DAGs depend on Lithuanian data that arrives in a different timezone, regulatory compliance where each country's data must be processed and stored according to its own regulations, and organizational governance where 10 teams must coordinate changes across a shared platform without blocking each other.
The Senior Prerequisites

The numbering alignment between mid-level and senior modules is intentional: Senior Module 1 extends Mid-Level Module 1 (scheduling), Senior Module 2 extends Mid-Level Module 2 (task patterns), and so on through all 10 modules. This alignment allows a student to review the specific mid-level module before starting the corresponding senior module, refreshing the foundational concepts before encountering their enterprise-scale extensions. The alignment also structures the senior track's pedagogy: each senior lesson can reference the specific mid-level lesson that taught the concept being extended, providing a clear learning lineage from junior through mid-level to senior.

The senior track assumes mastery of every mid-level concept. The prerequisite is not 'having read the mid-level material' — it is 'being able to apply the mid-level concepts in practice.' A senior-track student should be able to configure Dataset scheduling without referencing the documentation, write a custom operator with proper error handling and logging, design an idempotent pipeline with dead-letter routing, diagnose a performance bottleneck using the scheduler metrics, and operate the platform through the monitoring dashboard and runbook library. If any of these skills feel uncertain, the student should review the corresponding mid-level module before starting the senior track.
Senior Track Topics

rows: Config-driven DAG generation from YAML specifications (200+ DAGs from 20 templates)
3 teams sharing 1 Airflow instance with RBAC
10 teams across 3 countries with namespace isolation, quota management, and chargeback
Three timezones with cross-timezone data dependencies and SLA coordination
Custom executor plugins for hybrid workloads (Celery + Kubernetes + Spark-on-K8s)
Source-code-level understanding of the scheduler, DagFileProcessor, and TaskRunner
Pipeline design standards for one team
Platform governance for 10 teams: data contracts, schema registries, cost allocation

blocks.content: The Scale Transition

The transition from mid-level to senior is not just about more DAGs and more teams — it is about a qualitative change in the engineering challenges. At mid-level, the team writes and maintains every DAG individually. At senior, the DAG count makes individual authorship impractical — 200 DAGs maintained by hand would require 20 engineers. DAG factories solve this by generating DAGs from configuration files, reducing the maintenance surface from 200 Python files to 20 YAML specifications plus a factory module. The factory pattern introduces new challenges: configuration validation, factory versioning, and testing generated DAGs — all of which build on the testing and deployment foundations from modules 6-7.

At mid-level, RBAC with custom roles provides adequate access control for 3 teams. At senior, 10 teams require namespace isolation — each team's DAGs, connections, and variables are scoped to a namespace that prevents cross-team interference. Namespace isolation introduces quota management (limiting each team's resource consumption to prevent one team from monopolizing the cluster) and chargeback (attributing infrastructure costs to the team whose DAGs consumed the resources). These enterprise governance patterns build on the security foundations from Module 8 and the capacity planning from Module 9.
Preparing for the Senior Track

The senior track assumes mastery of every concept in the mid-level track. Before starting the senior track, verify that you can: configure Dataset scheduling and custom timetables (Module 1), write custom operators and hooks (Module 2), design idempotent pipelines with dead-letter routing (Module 3), evaluate and configure executors (Module 4), manage secrets and configuration as code (Module 5), write the full testing pyramid for a DAG (Module 6), operate a CI/CD pipeline (Module 7), configure RBAC with custom roles and DAG-level access control (Module 8), diagnose and fix performance bottlenecks (Module 9), and operate the platform using the monitoring dashboard and runbooks (Module 10). Each senior topic extends a specific mid-level module — the numbering alignment is intentional.

The transition from mid-level to senior mirrors SaulėGrid's own growth trajectory. SaulėGrid started with 3 DAGs and grew to 12 through the mid-level practices. The BalticSolar Alliance starts with 12 DAGs per country and grows to 200+ through the senior practices of configuration-driven DAG generation, automated governance, and multi-tenant operations. Each scale transition requires new patterns because the previous patterns' manual steps (individual DAG authoring, manual change coordination, per-user role assignment) become impractical at the new scale. The senior track teaches the automation and abstraction patterns that restore operational efficiency at enterprise scale.

blocks.title: The scale principle
blocks.content: Every mid-level pattern breaks at enterprise scale. RBAC breaks when 10 teams need different access to overlapping DAG sets. Individual DAG authorship breaks when 200 DAGs need consistent configuration. Manual change management breaks when 50 changes per week flow through the platform. The senior track teaches the patterns that restore order at enterprise scale — DAG factories for consistency, namespace isolation for access control, automated governance for change coordination. The mid-level track teaches you to operate a platform. The senior track teaches you to design the platform that others operate.

sections.title: From DAG Author to Platform Engineer

blocks.content: This course began with a question: how do you take a working collection of DAGs and transform it into a production orchestration platform that three teams depend on for billing, analytics, and operational decision-making? The answer is not one technique — it is ten modules of interconnected practices that address scheduling, task design, pipeline resilience, execution architecture, configuration management, testing, deployment, security, performance, and operations. Each module solved a specific category of production problems, and together they form a complete platform engineering discipline. This final lesson reviews the journey and marks the transition from DAG author to platform engineer.
The Module Journey

rows: Eliminated timing fragility in the Bronze→Silver→Gold chain
Custom operators, hooks, task groups, XCom backends
Reusable, testable components in the saulegrid_common library
Idempotency, incremental processing, SLAs, dead letters
Retries are safe, backfills are reliable, failures are contained
Local, Celery, Kubernetes executors, pools, queues
Distributed execution matching the workload's capacity needs
Vault Secrets Backend, config as code, rotation
Integrity, structure, unit, mock, integration tests
123 tests catching 18 potential incidents before deployment
Git-based management, CI pipeline, promotion, rollback
Automated deployment pipeline replacing manual scp
RBAC, DAG-level access, OAuth, audit logging
12 users across 3 teams with least-privilege access
Scheduler internals, parsing, DB maintenance, monitoring
Dashboard, incidents, runbooks, on-call, SLA governance
Structured operations that maintain platform quality over time

blocks.content: The Before and After

The contrast between SaulėGrid before and after the mid-level track illustrates the value of production engineering. Before: DAGs deployed by scp with no tests, no monitoring, no RBAC, no secrets management, and incidents discovered by downstream complaints hours after they occurred. After: DAGs deployed through a CI/CD pipeline with 123 automated tests, monitored by an 8-panel Grafana dashboard with automated alerting, secured with custom RBAC roles and quarterly security audits, and configuration managed as code with Vault-backed secrets. The transformation took 10 modules and 53 hours of learning plus implementation effort, but the operational improvements are permanent and compound over time as the team adds new DAGs, onboards new users, and scales the platform.
The Platform Engineering Identity

A DAG author writes code that processes data. A platform engineer builds and operates the system that makes data processing reliable, scalable, secure, and observable. The difference is not technical seniority — it is scope of responsibility. The DAG author is responsible for the correctness of their transformation logic. The platform engineer is responsible for the correctness of the transformation logic, the reliability of the scheduling system, the security of the access controls, the performance of the execution cluster, the accuracy of the monitoring, the completeness of the runbooks, and the quality of the incident response. Every module in this course expanded that scope of responsibility by one dimension.

The platform engineering identity is not a title — it is a set of habits. The habit of writing tests before deploying code (Module 6). The habit of checking the monitoring dashboard every morning (Module 10). The habit of documenting runbooks for every recurring procedure (Module 10). The habit of reviewing security roles quarterly (Module 8). The habit of measuring performance before optimizing (Module 9). The habit of communicating changes to downstream teams before deployment (Module 10). These habits, practiced consistently, are what transform a collection of DAGs into a production platform that teams trust with their most critical data workflows.
The Mid-Level Promise Fulfilled

The course tagline was 'From working DAGs to production-grade orchestration — reliability, testing, and operational discipline.' That promise is fulfilled through three transformations. The reliability transformation: DAGs that crashed on retry now run idempotently, DAGs that missed SLAs now have automated monitoring, and DAGs that failed silently now route rejected records to dead-letter tables. The testing transformation: code that was deployed by copying files now passes 123 automated tests before reaching any environment. The operational discipline transformation: a platform that was managed through tribal knowledge now has documented standards, automated monitoring, structured incident response, and a quarterly audit cycle that verifies every operational property.

The ten modules are not independent improvements — they are a system. Testing (Module 6) validates the pipeline design patterns (Module 3). The CI/CD pipeline (Module 7) deploys the tested code with the configuration from Module 5. The security framework (Module 8) protects the deployment. The performance monitoring (Module 9) detects when the deployed code degrades the platform. The operational practices (Module 10) coordinate the response when monitoring detects an issue. Each module reinforces the others, and removing any module weakens the entire system. The platform's reliability is the product of all ten modules working together — not the sum of ten independent improvements.
The Professional Transformation

The mid-level promise is not just about technical capabilities — it is about professional identity. A DAG author can be replaced by another developer who knows Python and basic Airflow. A platform engineer who understands scheduling architecture, pipeline design patterns, executor models, secrets management, systematic testing, automated deployment, security frameworks, performance tuning, and operational practices brings a depth of production knowledge that takes months to develop and represents genuine engineering expertise. The ten modules represent that depth: not just knowing how to write a DAG, but knowing how to ensure that the DAG runs correctly, securely, efficiently, and observably in a production environment that three downstream teams depend on for billing-critical data workflows.
The Continuous Journey

The course ends here, but the operational journey does not. SaulėGrid's platform will continue to evolve: new data sources will be integrated, new consuming teams will onboard, new Airflow versions will introduce capabilities that the team adopts. Each evolution creates new operational challenges that require the same diagnostic, testing, deployment, and monitoring practices taught in this course — applied to new contexts. The mid-level practices are not a destination; they are a foundation that the team builds on with every sprint, every incident, and every quarterly review. The platform engineer's job is never 'done' — it is continuously adapting to serve the teams that depend on the platform for their most critical data workflows.

blocks.title: The course in one sentence
blocks.content: You started this course knowing how to write DAGs that work. You finish this course knowing how to build, test, deploy, secure, tune, and operate a production orchestration platform that three teams trust with billing-critical data — and the ten modules, one hundred lessons, and fifty-three hours of content have equipped you with the patterns, standards, and operational habits that make that trust justified.
